Guo Chunsen
§YBorn:§! §L1876, in Wuxuan County, Guangxi Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §gYoung Guangxi Faction§!\n§YBackground:§! §LA scholar in the Qing Dynasty, Guo Chunsen started out passing the imperial exam at the age of 19. In 1905 he travelled to Japan to study law and politics, and during his time there joined the Tongmenghui. During the Xinhai Revolution, Guo participated in the Yunnanese Double Ninth Uprising, later serving as the Dean of Academic Affairs of the Yunnan Law and Politics School. Becoming a Senator in the first National Congress of the Beiyang Republic, Guo would travel south to Sun Yat-sen's Constitutional Protection Government in 1917, and after his arrival became ingratiated with Lu Rongting's Old Guangxi Clique.\n\nAppointed Secretary-General of the Guangxi Military Governor's Office, Guo would work with other senators to transition the Constitutional Protection Government's Generalissimo system to that of a Presidential one, and excluding Sun Yat-sen from office. Advising Mo Rongxin in the late 1910s, Guo would exile himself to Shanghai following Mo's deposition. Returning to Guangxi following Old Guangxi's revival under a rejuvenated yet ailing Lu Rongting, Guo would provide his amenable skillset to the new kingpins of the region, Lu Yuguang, Liu Zhilu, Lin Hu, and Ma Ji.§!
Zhang Gan
§YBorn:§! §L1897, in Guilin, Guangxi Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §LNeutral§!\n§YBackground:§! §LGraduating from the Guangxi Army Speed training School, Zhang Gan would start his military career as a major adjutant in Lu Rongting's Guangxi Army in 1923. After Lu's defeat, Zhang Gan's troops were absorbed into the New Guangxi Clique, were he served as a battalion commander under Li Zongren. Flexible and not too ideologically cohesive, Zhang did not particularly mind which patron he fought for as long as he could protect Guangxi. In 1926 he became a lieutenant colonel adjutant and colonel adjutant in the 7th Army of the National Revolutionary Army. Following the collapse of the Northern Expedition, Zhang would just as easily melt into the resurgent Old Guangxi's ranks as he did with New Guangxi, serving as a division commander under Lin Hu.\n\nIn 1928 Zhang would attend and graduate from the Nanjing Army University, and return to serve as Chief of Staff of the 2nd Guangxi Army, a role he has served dutifully ever since. Overseeing Lin Hu's expansion into the free territory of Liangguang, Zhang is a relatively neutral player in the Young Guangxi Faction, all things considered.§!
Ma Junwu
§YBorn:§! §LJuly 17th, 1881 in Gongcheng County, Guangxi Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §LIndependent§!\n§YBackground:§! §LClose ally of Sun Yat-sen, Ma Junwu would be an inquisitive and bright child, set on the path to becoming a scholar. Deeply influenced by Kang Youwei, who had travelled to Guilin to promote his ideas, Ma would believe that China needed westernisation to strengthen itself. Studying French in 1900, Ma would travel to Singapore to meet Kang, and then enter Shanghai's Aurora College.\n\nGranted a scholarship in Japan, Ma would meet Liang Qichao and Tang Rui, later meeting Sun Yat-sen in 1902. Transforming from Reformist to Revolutionary, Ma would become a close aide to Sun, being one of the first members of the Tongmenghui, and an enthusiastic revolutionary. Travelling to Germany in 1907, he would gain a bachelor's degree in engineering, and would see service in Xinhai, and then the new Republic.\n\nRemaining with Sun from Xinhai till his death, Ma would even be named Sun's preferred candidate for Guangxi Governor, albeit would be obstructed by the Old Guangxi Clique. Ma would oppose the 1924 Reorganisation, and as such would be expelled in 1926 for becoming Minister of Justice in Duan Qirui's provincial government; albeit would resign after the March 18th Massacre.\n\nCommitted to education reform, Ma would return to Guangxi to aid in the construction of the Guangxi University, his magnum opus. Working with Pan Zhuqi and the Old Guangxi Clique Reformists, Ma aimed to strengthen China through scientific research, ignoring political disputes, albeit has found himself the Guangxi Provincial Head of the developing Jingxing Society.§!
Shen Hongying
Son of a poor family, Shen Hongying had already joined a bandit gang at the age of 21. Becoming leader in 1905, Shen would expand the gang to include nearly 100 people, including his eldest son Shen Rongguang. Contacted by local Xinhai Revolutionaries such as Liu Zhenhuan, Shen decided to accept their offer of amnesty in exchange for service. Becoming increasingly powerful, he would ultimately betray the revolutionaries in the Second Revolution. Murdering and rampaging his way through Guangxi, Shen would ultimately end up under the employ of Lu Rongting.\n\nEngaging in the National Protection War under Lu Rongting, he would end up a close ally of Mo Rongxin, and de-facto warlord of Qiongya, and then in the East of Guangdong. On the eve of the First Yue-Gui War, Mo Rongxin floated Shen Hongying as his replacement for Governor of Guangdong, but following Ma Ji's interjection, he would be passed over. Underestimating Chen Jiongming's Guangdong Army, Shen would ultimately blunder his way into Hunan, wherein he would clash with Ye Kaixin and Ye Qi. Utilised as a tool by Wu Peifu to force the unification of the country, Shen would march into Guangxi in 1922, and would gain the support of Cen Chunxuan in Guangdong.\n\nRecommend by Cen to Sun Yat-sen, Shen's Army, the Yunnan Army in Guangdong, and the Guangxi Army under Liu Zhenhuan would fight Chen Jiongming for the KMT. Contacting Wu Peifu secretly, Shen was appointed Governor of Guangdong by the Zhili, but was ultimately ejected by Liu Zhenhuan and Yang Ximin. Clashing with Lu Rongting, Shen would ultimately square off against the nascent New Guangxi Clique alongside Li Jishen and Chen Jitang. Once again underestimating his opponent, he was utterly defeated by the KMT. Fleeing to Hong Kong and living a life of debauchery, Shen would return to prominence following the need of the Southern Zhili for their own man in Liangguang.
§YBorn:§! §L1871, in Luorong County, Guangxi Province, Great Qing§!\n§YBackground:§! §L$GXC_shen_hongying_bio$§!
§YBorn:§! §L1871, in Luorong County, Guangxi Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §PShen Army Faction§!\n§YBackground:§! §L$GXC_shen_hongying_bio$§!
Zhu Jiahua
Entering the Tongji German Medical School in Shanghai in 1908, and embroiled with revolutionary fervour, Zhu Jiahua would join the Revolutionary Dare-to-Die Corps in Shanghai during Xinhai. Returning in 1912, he would resume his studies as an Electrical Engineer. After graduating in 1914, Zhu would receive funding to study in Germany, where he learnt metallurgy at the Technical University of Berlin. In 1917 Zhu was unable to continue his studies due to the First World War, and so worked as a German Teacher at Beijing University.\n\nSoon afterwards he travelled to Switzerland to study and entered the University of Bern. In 1920 he returned to Germany and studied geology at the University of Berlin, receiving a doctorate in philosophy in 1922. Returning to China in 1924, he would become a professor and German instructor at the Department of Geology at Beijing University. In 1926 following the March 18th Massacre, Zhu was accused of being responsible for inciting the students, and was forced to flee to his hometown.\n\nBecoming head of the Department of Geology at the National Guangdong University, he would become acting chairman of the Sun yat-sen University Committee, and become a member of the Guangdong Provincial Government. Refusing to join the Kuomintang, Zhu would instead oversee the peaceful transition of power of the German Occupation of Guangzhou to the Chinese. Becoming a reasonably successful diplomat within the Chen Lianbo administration, he would move onto the retinue of Sun Chuanfang, and aid in negotiating the German Military Mission to China.\n\nHelping setup the Jingxing Society with individuals such as Tang Zaili, Li Linsi and Gu Weijun, Zhu would ultimately become the chief of the Guangdong branch of the Jingxing Society following the Shanghai Uprising; influential in Sino-German Circles, Zhu is an ambitious man with skill to match.
§YBorn:§! §LMay 30, 1893 in Wuxing County, Zhejiang Province, Great Qing§!\n§YBackground:§! §L$GXC_zhu_jiahua_bio$§!
§YBorn:§! §LMay 30, 1893 in Wuxing County, Zhejiang Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §PJingxing Society§!\n§YBackground:§! §L$GXC_zhu_jiahua_bio$§!
Shen Rongguang
§YBorn:§! §L1892, in Luorong, Guangxi Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §PShen Army Faction§!\n§YBackground:§! §LEldest son of esteemed bandit turned warlord and politician Shen Hongying, Shen Rongguang was recruited by his father at a young age to serve in his bandit gang. Participating in all endeavours of his father, he would serve as a sentry officer and captain in Lu Rongting's army following Shen Hongying's integration into the Old Guangxi Clique. In 1916 he entered Guangdong and served as a battalion commander, and later brigade commander under Long Jiguang. Fighting under his father, he would serve as a brigade commander in the Guangxi Frontier Defence Army, and later in 1922 under the Guangdong Army.\n\nServing in the 6th Army under Sun Yat-sen in 1924, he would ultimately be defeated by the New Guangxi Clique in 1925, and followed his father into exile in Hong Kong. Returning to prominence following his father's bargaining with the Southern Zhili Clique, Shen Rongguang acts as his father's ever faithful stooge. Not marred by any consciousness of pride or nationalism, he is a bandit through and through.§!
Li Yaohan
Briefly educated in a private school when he was young, he would ultimately lead a failed business before joining local bandits led by his fellow villager. Becoming a sentry officer and receiving an official rank, he gradually rose to the rank of Commander of the Guangdong Navy Patrol Battalion and the Garrison Commander of Dong'an county. After Xinhai, the Tongmenghui contacted Li and instigated him to join the revolution. Forming his own battalion, it eventually grew into a regiment.\n\nInitially supporting Yuan Shikai in 1912, he assisted Long Jiguang and was appointed garrison commander of Zhaoluoyang. Expanding his army to more than 10,000 people, Li would fight in the National Protection War against Yuan Shikai, and would inadvertently join the Old Guangxi Clique. Taking over as Governor of Guangdong from Chen Bingkun in 1917, Li did not actually take the position out of fear of his enemies. Refusing to join the Constitutional Protection Government, he would resign from his official position and return to Zhaoluoyang, but would lose his post to Lin Hu and his army to Mo Rongxin.\n\nLater deciding to join Chen Jiongming due to Old Guangxi's actions, he would still lose all of his troops and be relegated to an advisor position. Dissatisfied with Chen, he would defect to Shen Hongying in 1922, and would serve as commander of the 5th army of the Guangxi Army, but was defeated by Liu Zhenhuan and Yang Ximin. Considering defection, he would return to his hometown of Xinxing County, establishing his own fiefdom there. Pushed out briefly, he would return following the chaotic interregnum in the aftermath of the KMT. With lack of better allies available for either, Li would reluctantly enter into an alliance with Shen oncemore.
§YBorn:§! §L1876, in Xinxing County, Guangdong Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §PShen Army Faction§!\n§YBackground:§! §L$GXC_li_yaohan_bio$§!
§YBorn:§! §L1876, in Xinxing County, Guangdong Province, Great Qing§!\n§YBackground:§! §L$GXC_li_yaohan_bio$§!
Chen Lianbo
Born into a rich merchant family, despite being the eldest son he was adopted by his aunt. Chen Lianbo would initially study in a private school, at the age of 12 he would travel to Hong Kong to study English at Queen's College and obtain British citizenship. After graduating at the age of 16, Chen joined the Guangzhou Shamian Branch of HSBC Bank, and was later promoted to comprador due to his outstanding performance. He operated in own silk shop, and in 1908 founded the Guangdong Insurance Company. Becoming quite wealthy, he joined the Guangzhou General Chamber of Commerce.\n\nA celebrity within the Guangdong business community, he monopolised the local raw silk trade. Engaging in speculative business, he relied extensively on HSBC to control and manipulate the markets, and expanded into all sorts of economic endeavours. He served as Prime Minister of the Guangdong Export Association and President of the Guangdong Chamber of Commerce in 1915. Serving as president of various merchant associations, he had contacts directly with the Governorship. Soon in his lavish mansion he setup the \

Chen Gongshou
§YBorn:§! §L1877, in Nanhai County, Guangdong Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §cCanton Merchant Corps§!\n§YBackground:§! §LA graduate of the Guangdong Police School in 1907 who served as a patrol officer in Foshan, Chen Gongshou became the head of the Judicial Department of the Guangdong Police Department after the Xinhai Revolution. Climbing up the ranks and becoming director of the police department, Chen also lent his services as a consultant to the Nanyang Brothers Tobacco Company in 1920. In 1924 Chen established the Foshan Loyalty Association and served as the president of the Foshan Chamber of commerce and the Foshan Merchants Association. In 1924 he was elected Deputy Commander of the Guangdong Merchant Corps Joint Defence Headquarters alongside Deng Jieshi, and assisted Chen Lianbo in launching the Merchant Corps Incident, but after its failure fled to Hong Kong with Lianbo.\n\nBriefly flirting as a Buddhist monk, Chen would return to Foshan at the request of Chen Lianbo, he would build the Jiulian Shenghui temple and raise donations from believers, overseas Chinese, and businessmen. Returning to the position as Director of the Guangdong Police Department, Chen is effectively the main military arm within Guangzhou, operating its police force akin to a paramilitary.§!\n\n§YNotes: He is alleged for excessive abuse of power.§!
Deng Jieshi
§YBorn:§! §Lin Guangdong Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §cCanton Merchant Corps§!\n§YBackground:§! §LElected Deputy Commander of the Guangdong Merchant Corps Joint Defence Headquarters alongside Chen Gongshou in 1924, Deng would command the 8th Corps of the Merchant Corps, and see urban warfare against the Kuomintang and striking workers within Guangzhou during the Merchant Corps Incident. Fleeing to Hong Kong with Chen Lianbo and Gongshou, Deng would largely remain within Merchant Corps exile circles until Chen Lianbo's return to Guangzhou in 1927. Following his friend, he would once again take up a position of command within Chen Lianbo's military, alongside Chen Gongshou, and other hired generals.§!
Chen Lianzhong
§YBorn:§! §L1884, in Nanhai County, Guangdong Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §cCanton Merchant Corps§!\n§YBackground:§! §LThe younger brother of Chen Lianbo, Chen Lianzhong would end up the true heir of their wealthy merchant family's inheritance due to Lianbo's adoption by their aunt. At the age of 19, Chen began to enter the business world. In 1913 he was awarded a medal from the Beiyang Government for his enthusiasm in public welfare and meritorious service to local industry. In 1919 Chen Lianbo became the head of the Guangzhou Merchants' Association, and in 1921 was elected president of the Guangzhou General Chamber of Commerce. Similarly Lianzhong was appointed as director of the Guangdong Mint, but later he resigned.\n\nServing in various roles as manager of the Public Welfare Insurance company, director of the Dili Mining Company, director of Taihua Insurance, and founded several banks in Guangdong. Chen also served as the financial director of the Guangdong Food Relief Association, the Guangdong Charity Relief Association, and the Guangdong Merchants Association. In 1923 he was elected the president of the Guangzhou General Chamber of Commerce, replacing his brother, and in 1923 successfully maintained the value of the Guangdong Provincial Bank's banknotes.\n\nIn 1924 during the Merchant Corps Incident, Chen was forced to flee to Hong Kong, before returning in 1924 to serve as comprador of the HSBC Guangzhou branch in Shamian and head of the Merchant Corps, replacing his brother who was still in exile. Visited by Hu Hanmin in 1925, Chen would remit continuing to fund the Nationalist Government. Following the KMT's defeat, he would welcome his brother back into Guangzhou, handing the election of head of the Merchant Corps to him.§!
Jiang Kongyin
§YBorn:§! §L1864, in Nanhai County, Guangdong Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §LNeutral§!\n§YBackground:§! §LStudying under Kang Youwei, Jiang Kongyin would participate in the reform efforts of his teacher, including the infamous petition to the Emperor to prevent the signing of the Treaty of Shimonoskei by the Qing. Known for his literary talent, he passed the imperial exam in 1904 and served in various official capacities in Guangdong. As Governor, deputy admiral of the Guangdong Navy, and supervisor of the Guangdong-Guangxi Cleanup Campaign. A very popular man, he persuaded numerous officials to side with the Xinhai Revolutionaries, but thereafterwards quit politics almost entirely.\n\nLiving in Hong Kong for a time, in 1915 he was hired as the general agent of the British-American Tobacco Company in South China, participating in a fierce business battle with the Nanyang Brothers Tobacco Company. In the 1930s he returned to Guangzhou, renting more than 1000 acres of wasteland and established Jianglanzhai Bee Farm. Improving fruit varieties and introducing foreign bees, he also built various small-gauge railways and water conservancy projects§!.\n\n§YNotes: He has very strong ties to the Guangdong Grain Industry Maintenance Association, but remains passive in politics.§!
Qiu Jingyun
A major landlord in Haifeng, Qiu Jingyun became increasingly anxious like much of the gentry at the growing popularity of the Farmers' Association. In reaction, the Grain Industry Maintenance Association was formed with Chen Yuebo as chairman, and Wang Zuoxin as vice-chairman. This iteration of the association did not last long, however, as it was immediately dissolved when Chen Yuebo resigned and fled to Hong Kong.\n\nQiu soon became the County Magistrate on May 12th, 1923, much to the joint-opposition of the Farmers' Association and local academic community. His term as County Magistrate did not last long, however, as Wang was soon appointed county magistrate in May 27th. The Grain Industry Maintenance Association was soon restored by Wang Zuoxin, Qiu Jingyun, and Liu Douwen, only for it to dissolve yet again after Chen Jiongming stepped down and the KMT began their rule in Guangdong.\n\nAs the Northern Expedition soon fractured, the association was revived yet again under Qiu's chairmanship in collaboration with Ye Ju to reestablish the gentry's position and suppress peasant activities in Haifeng.\n\n§YNotes: An old mentor of Chen Jiongming, his relationship with his former student remains strong.§!

§YBorn:§! §Lin Guangdong Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §cGrain Industry Maintenance Association§!\n§YBackground:§! §L$GXC_qiu_jingyun_bio$§!
Lin Douwen
§YBorn:§! §Lin Guangdong Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §cGrain Industry Maintenance Association§!\n§YBackground:§! §LA member of the Zhengqi Society and Haifeng Tongmenghui, Lin Douwen was an early ally of Chen Jiongming, becoming Director of the County Security Force when Chen was governor of Guangdong. As Director, Lin protected the landed interests whilst collecting rent from the farmers through force, causing much resentment among the peasantry.\n\nThis burden enflamed tensions between gentry and peasantry, as more farmers flock to the Farmers' Association led by Peng Pai. After Chen Jiongming's tenure as governor ended with his defeat by the KMT, the Grain Industry Maintenance Association was forced to dissolve whilst the Haifeng General Farmers' Association aligned themselves with the KMT and became the Guangdong Provincial Farmers' Association.\n\nAs the Northern Expedition faltered and Guangdong was rid of the KMT, Lin reprised his role as Head of the Security Force when the Grain Industry Maintenance Association was restored. Chaos lingered in Haifeng as both the gentry and peasantry dish out retaliatory actions against each other, stopped only by Ye Ju's military presence within the county.§!\n\n§YNotes: He was once driven out of a village and subsequently beaten up in another village whilst collecting rent.§!

Zhong Xiunan
§YBorn:§! §L1877, in Haifeng County, Guangdong Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §cGrain Industry Maintenance Association§!\n§YBackground:§! §LGraduating from the Guangdong Normal University, Zhong Xiunan was one of Chen Jiongming's earliest followers, being a founding member of the Haifeng Tongmenghui and an early leader of the Public Interest Party. Alongside joining the Chinese Assassination Group and participation in the Second Guangzhou Uprising, Zhong also followed Chen into exile in Southeast Asia after Chen's unsuccessful rebellion against Yuan Shikai in 1913.\n\nServing as Chen Jiongming's head of finance in Southern Fujian, Zhong was appointed Acting Minister of Finance for Guangdong in November 1921 by Sun Yat-sen. When Chen stepped down in early 1923 as the KMT tookover Guangdong, Zhong refused to work for the KMT government and left with Chen to Hong Kong.\n\nAs the Northern Expedition began to collapse and Haifeng was reclaimed by Ye Ju in the chaos, Zhong was invited back to temporarily serve as the county magistrate under Ye Ju. During his tenure, he oversaw the suppression of the lingering KMT-aligned Farmers' Association, as well as the reconstruction process of the county with the assistance of the Grain Industry Maintenance Association.§!\n\n§YNotes: Indirectly associated with the Haifeng gentry, he remains loyal to his old friend Chen Jiongming.§!
Deng Benyin

He continued his service under Long up until 1916, when Deng's troops were reorganised into Zhu Qinglan's personal army. Rejected from affiliation with the Old Guangxi Clique, and following an arrangement with Sun Yat-sen to form the Aid Fujian army out of Guangdong Clique units, Deng came under the command of Chen Jiongming and Ye Ju.\n\nOrdered to clash with the Old Guangxi Clique in 1920, he fought alongside the likes of Ye Ju, Xiong Lue, Hong Zhaolin, Luo Shaoxiong and others. Occupying Qiongya in 1921, he was granted a practical fiefdom over the island. Hong Zhaolin
§YBorn:§! §LApril 3rd, 1876 in Ningxiang County, Hunan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §LIndependent§!\n§YBackground:§! §LSon of a poor family, Hong Zhaolin would join the Yongzi Battalion of the Guangdong Army at the age of 20, becoming a captain just before the Xinhai Revolution. Under instigation of Chen Jiongming and Deng Keng, he would join the revolutionaries in Huizhou. In 1912 he was appointed as the head of the Guangdong Army Mixed Corps and the Director of Huizhou Pacification. Fighting in the Second Revolution, he would ultimately be forced into exile in Japan.\n\nReturning to Huizhou in 1914, he plotted to overthrow Long Jiguang, but after failing he would flee to Hong Kong just to be arrested by the local authorities. Released by revolutionaries, he would again return to Huizhou in 1916 to overthrow Long, this time succeeding and being appointed as Assistan Director of Huizhou by the new Governor, Zhu Qinglan. Serving in the Guangdong-Fujian Army, he would fight against Li Houji in Fujian, and in the First Yue-Gui War under Chen Jiongming, becoming the local warlord of Chaomei.\n\nMaintaining parity with Chen Jiongming after June 16th, Hong would dupe the KMT, align with Cao Kun and the Zhili Clique, and then welcome Chen Jiongming back in 1923. Appointed Chaomei Protector by Beijing, Hong would fight in the Eastern Expedition, and bitterly hold onto the Chaoshan area. Seriously wounded in battle, Hong would be forced into retreat to recuperate. Briefly solicited by Sun Chuanfang to join him in his army, Hong would be dispatched by Sun to Guangdong during the scramble for Liangguang in 1927, rallying forces in Chaoshan, acting as an awkward ally of Shen Hongying under the same master, with ties to Ye Ju.§!\n\n§YNotes: He is notorious for running a rampant opium smuggling ring under the auspices of Chen Jiongming in the 1920s, and is on the outer rung of Haifeng and Yue politics.§!
Liu Zhenhuan
At a young age Liu Zhenhuan was surrounded by rich family and many friends. Enjoying a joyful childhood, he would join the Tongmenghui in 1911, and carry out revolutionary activities all the way until Xinhai. Immediately dispatching bandits such as Shen Hongying to aid the revolution, Liu organised his local militia, and in 1912 become an official commander within the Guangxi Army. Advocating immediate support of the Second Revolution, Liu would find himself in Hong Kong, and then Shanghai on behalf of the Kuomintang.\n\nReturning to Guangxi in 1916 to aid in the National Protection War, he fought against Long Jiguang in 1918, and aided the Guangdong Army in 1921 to fight the Old Guangxi Clique. Aiding Tang Jiyao in his return to Yunnan, Liu would ultimately side with Sun Yat-sen in the Second Yue-Gui War, and again during Shen Hongying's revolt.\n\nDissatisfied with the Reorganisation of the Kuomintang, Liu began to abuse his position, and his already lax discipline in his soldiers caused much outrage. In 1925 he endeavoured with Tang Jiyao to oust the New Guangxi Clique and Sun Yat-sen, but after its failure he formed an alliance with Yang Ximin, Deng Benying, Chen Jiongming, Duan Qirui and others and rebelled against the KMT in 1925, ultimately resulting in his own deposition. Lamenting in exile in Hong Kong, Liu would be hired by his old friend Chen Lianbo in 1927 to control the western approaches to Guangzhou.

Li Fulin
§YBorn:§! §L1874, in Panyu County, Guangdong Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §LIndependent§!\n§YBackground:§! §LIn the halls of the National Revolutionary Army, many of its top Army Commanders were some of the best officers of the nation; Cheng Qian, Li Zongren, Chiang Kai-shek, He Yingqin. And yet, for the Fifth National Revolutionary Army, they had only Li Fulin. A son of the peasantry, as a young child Li was inherently chivalrous by nature, adoring the martial arts. Gathering a variety of his compatriots, he would form a gang, and engage in banditry.\n\nFleeing to Singapore in 1907, Li would join the Tongmenghui, and would participate in the failed uprising of Huang Mingtang. Participating in Xinhai, Li would decide to align with Long Jiguang in 1913, and was particularly favoured by Yuan Shikai. With Long's defeat in 1916, Li would then join the Old Guangxi Clique, and in 1920 would defect to Sun Yat-sen and the Guangdong Army.\n\nFighting under Chen Jiongming, he would align with Sun during the Yue-Gui wars and ensuing expeditions, becoming chief of the Guangzhou police, and even the mayor. In 1925, he would fight against Liu Zhenhuan and Yang Ximin, and later that year would become the Commander of the Fifth Army of the NRA. Yang Ximin
§YBorn:§! §L1886, in Binchuan, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §mPolitical Science Clique§!\n§YBackground:§! §LSon of a peasant family, Yang Ximin would enter the Yunnan Military Academy in 1909, and participated in the Double Ninth Uprising, and later the Sichuan campaign. In 1912 he entered the Jiangxi Military Academy, and participated in the Second Revolution. Ultimately returning to Yunnan, he fought in the National Protection War under Cai E. Sent as a subordinate of Gu Pinzhen to Sichuan, he would participate in the 1920s coup against Tang Jiyao, and following Gu's death, Yang and the remaining plotters would retreat to Guizhou, and then to Guangdong.\n\nWith the Yunnan Army in Guangdong split between electing Lu Dao or Yang as their replacement for Commander-in-Chief, Yang would be appointed as acting Commander, and would fight against Chen Jiongming on behalf of Sun Yat-sen. Following their capture of Guangzhou, Yang would become a local warlord to finance his army, resulting in ruthless proliferation of corruption in his ranks.\n\nFighting hard against Shen Hongying's rebellion and their Zhili allies, Yang would emerge victorious. Opposing the Reorganisation of the Kuomintang, and the establishment of Whampoa, he would participate in the 1925 anti-Kuomintang plot with Liu Zhenhuan and others, being promptly ousted. Moving to exile in Hong Kong, Yang would be rehired by Chen Lianbo in order to bulk up his military prowess.§!\n\n§YNotes: Yang is viewed harshly by Yue natives for his treatment of them last decade.§!
Lin Zhengxuan
§YBorn:§! §L1883, in Wuchuan County, Guangdong Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §mPolitical Science Clique§!\n§YBackground:§! §LTaking the Imperial Exam, Lin Zhengxuan would be educated at Guangdong-Guangxi Higher School, and would serve in the Guangdong Provincial Council as Provincial Senator in 1911, and even as Director of the Guangdong Tongmenghui in 1912. Serving in the National Protection War as an aide to Yang Yongtai, Cai E, and Xiong Kewu, he would return to become the Speaker of Guangdong's Provincial Parliament.\n\nRepresenting Cen Chunxuan, he would cooperate with Dr. Sun to fight Chen Jiongming, albeit would be expelled by Chen Jiongming after the First Yue-Gui War. Exiling himself to Shanghai, he would decare the assembly suspended, only to return in January 1923 upon Shen Hongying's capture of parts of Guangzhou. Waltzing into the Provincial Assembly once more, he would thus declare it un-suspended, with himself presiding over it again as Speaker. After Shen Hongying's rebellion, Lin alongside the rest of the Political Science Clique would flee Guangzhou.\n\nAppointed to Duan Qirui's provisional 1925 senate, he would return to Guangdong with the fall of the Northern Expedition, occupying a premium space within the Guangzhou political scene. Aiding with negotiations with various Beiyang officials, and of securing at least tacit calm with Shen Hongying and Lin Hu, Lin has found himself indespensible within Guangdong's politics.§!\n\n§YNotes: Together with Yang Ximin and Liu Zhenhuan, the trio form the resident provincial branch of PSC Confederates, and much like Liu, is amenable to Third Party democrats.§!
Huang Dawei
§YBorn:§! §L1886, in Huangpi County, Hubei Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §LIndependent§!\n§YBackground:§! §LGraduating from the Royal Military Academy in Belgium, and the Meiji University in Japan, Huang Dawei joined the Tongmenghui during his time in Europe, and returned in 1911 to fight in the Xinhai Revolution. Serving as a military officer of the Provisional Presidential Mansion in Nanjing, he was promoted to Colonel of the Army Artillery and Major General in 1912. In 1917 he served as an officer of the Generalissimo's Office during the Constitutional Protection Government, and in 1920 served as commander of the first route of the Guangdong Army.\n\nIn 1922 he was serving in the aborted Northern Expedition, but after Chen Jiongming's June 16th Incident, he led his troops to retreat to Fujian with Xu Chongzhi, and became the local warlord of Chaoshan and Commander-in-Chief of the forces on the Fujian-Guangdong border. Later that year he served as commander of the first army under Xu Chongzhi, and in 1923 was promoted by the Beiyang General's Office.\n\nIn early 1923 Huang defected from the Pro-Sun camp to Chen Jiongming, and served as commander of Chen Jiongming's 7th Army, and director of Chaoshan Border Defence. From July 1923 to February 1924 he, alongside Lai Shihuang, Liu Zhilu, Lin Hu, and Wang Xianchen, established the United Forces Office Government in Zhangzhou, Fujian. Collapsing in 1924 due to defection and Pro-Sun advances, he would flee to Hong Kong with the rest of Chen Jiongming's allies. Returning in 1927 under Chen Lianbo's employment, he jockeys with Hong Zhaolin for control of the Chaoshan region.§!\n\n§YNotes: Noted for arrogance and disobeying orders due to ego.§!
Yang Chisheng
§YBorn:§! §L1891, in Shiping County, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §LIndependent§!\n§YBackground:§! §LGraduating from the cavalry department of the Yunnan Military Academy, and a close friend of Yang Ruxuan, Yang Chisheng would serve in the Double Ninth Uprising, and the National Protection War. Fighting under Cai E, he would be promoted in 1917 to regimental commander in the Huguojun, and later brigade commander. Supporting Gu Pinzhen's anti-Tang coup, he would be ejected from Yunnan and end up as a component of the Yunnan Army in Guangdong. Working with Yang Ximin and Yang Ruxuan to exclude Zhang Kairu from power, he would elect Ximin as Commander-in-Chief of the Yunnan Army in Guangdong.\n\nFighting against Chen Jiongming on behalf of the Kuomintang, he would ultimately be removed from his position in 1925 as divisional commander, and expelled from Guangdong due to collaboration with Wu Peifu and the Zhili Clique. Collaborating in-part due to Yang Ximin's manoeuvres, he would then fully defect and serve as commander of the 1st Division of the Yunnan Army under Wu Peifu, and garrison commander of southern Jiangxi.\n\nDuring the Northern Expedition he would fight against the 4th, 7th and 14th Armies of the National Revolutionary Army in Jiangxi, successfully triumphing as the National Revolutionary Army collapsed. Shuffling into Guangdong in pursuit of the retreating NRA, Yang would end up within Shen Hongying's sphere of control, as a warlord of his own calibre with Yang Ruxuan. With a tense relationship with Shen himself, Yang maintains distance with the Shen Army faction, forming their own sub-clique with Yang Ruxuan, stuck between a rock and a hard place.§!
Yang Ruxuan
§YBorn:§! §L1895, in Binchuan County, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §LIndependent§!\n§YBackground:§! §LJoining the army in his brother's place, Yang Ruxuan would enlist in the Yunnan Military Academy as a classmate of Zhu De, Zhu Peide, Jin Handing, Yang Chisheng, Fan Shisheng and others. Invited to study in the special class with 99 others, he would participate in the Double Ninth Uprising as a member of the Tongmenghui. Graduating in 1913, he would participate in the preparations for the National Protection War, and fought under Cai E's first army in the sixth regiment under Zhu De. Fighting bravely and fiercely, he would be seriously injured, and promoted for his merits.\n\nParticipating in Gu Pinzhen's coup attempt in 1920, he would fight the bandits in Southern Yunnan, effectively wiping them out. Following Sun Yat-sen's request, the Yunnan Army exiles travelled from Guizhou to Guangdong to fight against Chen Jiongming, and subsequently against the Guangxi Army led by Shen Hongying. Appointed as commander of the Second Division of the Yunnan Army in Guangdong, with Chisheng as commander of the First Division, he would be slighted by Yang Ximin, and begin communications with Wu Peifu.\n\nOuted by Yang Ximin as potential defectors to Wu Peifu, Ruxuan and Chisheng would be dismissed by Sun Yat-sen, and then subsequently defect to Wu properly as commanders of the Yunnan Army under the Zhili. Fighting against the National Revolutionary Army, he would be contacted by Zhu Peide to defect, but ultimately refuse follwing Kuomintang failures on the battlefield. Folding into Guangdong in pursuit of the Kuomintang, he would form a sub-clique with Yang Chisheng within Shen Hongying's area of control.§!\n\n§YNotes: He is sympathetic to the revolutionaries.§!
Li Qingwen
§YBorn:§! §Lin Gaozhou, Guangdong Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §LNeutral§!\n§YBackground:§! §LIn 1905 Li Qingwen was admitted to the first class of the Gaozhou Middle School, and after graduation was admitted to the 15th class of the Huangpu Naval Academy in 1911. Serving in the Guangdong Navy after graduation in 1916, he would serve dutifully and in 1927 be promoted to Deputy Commander of Guangdong Coastal Defence and Deputy Commander of the First Fleet. In 1932 he served as Deputy Director of the Navy Department of the Guangdong Army Headquarters, and Deputy Commander of the Provincial Fleet with Tang Tingguang as commander. In 1935 he became Chief of Staff of the Guangdong Fleet, and serves as Commander of the Guangdong River Defence Fleet alongside Principal of the Huangpu Naval School.§!
Tang Tingguang
§YBorn:§! §L1865, in Huaxian County, Guangdong Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §LNeutral§!\n§YBackground:§! §LGraduating from Huangpu Naval Academy, Tang Tingguang was sent to study the navy in the United Kingdom. Returning to China, he would fight in the Sino-Japanese War in 1894 as captain of the Dingyuan Ship. Due to his merit in service he was made a naval commander and served as captain of the crusier Haichen. Appointed captain of the Haiqi warship in 1909, he accompanied Cheng Biguang to serve as representatives to the coronation of George V of England, and later toured the White House and met President Taft.\n\nAwarded with a medal by Yuan Shikai in 1913, he would follow Cheng Biguang and other navy officers to the Constitutional Protection Government in 1917, and was appointed by Sun Yat-sen to serve as a military officer in the Generalissimo's Office, and then as Deputy Minister of the Navy. Fighting the Guangxi Clique in 1919, he would become Governor of Guangdong in the interim chaos and handed the position over to the Guangdong Army. Being rewarded as Minister of the Navy of the new Government.\n\nDuring the June 16th Incident, he wrote an open letter with Wei Bangping and Ye Ju demanding that the fighting cease, but his subordinates resolutely supported Sun Yat-sen, and he resigned in September. Appointed by Chen Xiru back into office, he would remain when Sun Yat-sen returned in 1923. From 1925 onwards he resigned and worked in his local county, before being recommissioned by Chen Lianbo to serve as Minister of the Navy within his Provincial Government.§!
Zhou Tianlu

From Chen Jiongming to exile to Chen Lianbo, Zhou has continued his service irrespective of anything bar revolutionary politics.§!
Wei Bangping
§YBorn:§! §L1884, in Xiangshan County, Guangdong Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §LIndependent§!\n§YBackground:§! §LFollowing his father to Japan at the age of 13, Wei Bangping would study in Kobe, before entering the Shikan Gakko to study in the military. Serving as an instructor at the Guangdong Military Academy, and participated in the Xinhai Revolution with distinction. Commander in the Guangdong Army and director of a police department, Wei would be ejected due to Long Jiguang.\n\nIn 1915 Wei fought in the National Protection War on the side of Cai E, and defeated Long Jiguang's forces in Guangdong, becoming commander-in-chief of the 5th National Protection Army under Mo Rongxin. Seeing that the Old Guangxi Clique opposed Sun Yat-sen, he would try to resign but was retained by Zhu Qinglan. Serving with Yang Yongtai, he worked within the Guangzhou Municipal Office, and in 1920 launched a Pro-Chen Jiongming rebellion with Li Fulin against Mo Rongxin. Fighting the Guangxi army further, he would become garrison commander of Guangzhou.\n\nEscorting Sun Yat-sen to safety on the eve of the June 16th Incident, he also managed to reunite Song Qingling with her husband, and became a ruling member of the 1923 Kuomintang Government, with Hu Hanmin, Li Liejun, Xu Chongzhi, and Zou Lu. Detained and deposed by Shen Hongying and Yang Ximin, he was released via Sun's intervention. Resigning that year, he was suspected in the Liao Zhongkai Case, and exiled himself to Japan. Returning in 1931 upon Chen Lianbo's request to serve as Director of the Guangzhou Police Department, he still maintains fondness for the Kuomintang, and is a known associate of Chen Jitang.§!\n\n§YNotes: He has consistently refused to join the Kuomintang, insisting that \
Ding Ying
§YBorn:§! §LNovember 25th, 1888 in Gaozhou City, Guangdong Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §LNeutral§!\n§YBackground:§! §LWith his father believing the poor were often abused by the gentry because they were uneducated, Ding Ying would thus be sent to private schooling, the first in his family. Excelling in school, he was exposed to revolutionary ideals in his institution, and actively joined student groups. Intending to study agriculture to improve the lives of farmers, missing an exam period resulted in his application to Guangdong Normal University's museology department.\n\nStudying in Japan in 1912, he learnt Japanese, and participated in Chinese student-led public protests in 1919. Returning that same year, he taught at local schools, but returned to Japan in 1921 to study at the Tokyo Imperial University. Graduating with a Bachelor's in Agriculture, he would teach in the Guangdong Public Agricultural School, later National Sun Yat-sen University, College of Agriculture. Establishing experimental rice cultivation farms, he bred a total of 110 rice varieties, and in 1934 achieved the world's first hybrid rice variety with wild rice.\n\nUnder Ding's efforts, Guangdong has become the rice breeding centre of Southern China, and he is considered something of a local icon and celebrity.§!\n\n§YNotes: Lives a particularly simple and frugal life, and lives a life as selfless as it is philosophical.§!
Ling Hongxun
§YBorn:§! §LApril 15th, 1894 in Panyu County, Guangdong Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §LNeutral§!\n§YBackground:§! §LTaught by his mother and studying the classics with his uncle, in 1910 Ling Hongxun was admitted to the Shanghai Higher Industrial School of the Ministry of Posts and Communications on Government tuition. Learning English, he was promoted to a junior college to major in civil engineering. After Xinhai, he cut off his braids, and participated in various student sports associations. In 1915 he graduated and was recommended to serve as an intern at the American Bridge Company in New York, and studied at Columbia University, similarly participating in the New York Chinese Alumni Association.\n\nIn 1918 he was assigned to be a civil servant of the Jingfeng railway due to a family connection, and he begun his interest in Chinese railways from this point onwards. Doing various oddjobs, he would becoming acting school administrator of the National Chiao Tung University, and returned to the Beijing-Hankou Railway to build the Yellow River Bridge. Chief Engineer in the Ministry of Communications, he would be dismissed by Gao Enhong in 1922 and taught at Nanyang University full-time. In 1924 he would become University President, and remained in his position until 1927.\n\nAt the invitation of the Guangxi Government, he went to Wuzhou to serve as Director of the Public Works Bureau, establishing ties with the Old Guangxi Clique. In 1929 he returned to serve in the Ministry of Railways of the Central Government, before returning to Liangguang to focus on the construction of local provincial railways.§!
Ding Jixu
§YBorn:§! §L1902, in Dongguan, Guangdong Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §LNeutral§!\n§YBackground:§! §LGraduating from Guangzhou Peiying Middle School, Ding Jixu would travel to France to study medicine before moving onto Germany to study in the aviation field. After graduation, he would marry a German woman, and return to Guangdong in 1926 with his family. First working in the Guangzhou Shijing Arsenal, he would later serve as a major flight instructor at Guangdong Aviation School. In February 1927 he was sent to France to study again and returned to Guangzhou in early December to a completely different province. Returning to the aviation school as a flight director, he would become a colonel of the Guangdong Provincial Air Force, and later a captain in the Guangdong Air Force Headquarters.§!\n\n§YNotes: Not particularly invested in politics.§!
Feng Huang

However he would be admitted to the Guangxi Army Ma Xiaojun Model Battalion as a cadet instead. In 1918 he completed his studies, and served as a squad leader, later being transferred to the Second Company of Xiawei as a sergeant clerk. In 1919 he was promoted to platoon leader, and was admitted to the Guangxi Army Academy. Seeing a smooth series of promotions throught the 1920s, he would fight in the Northern Expedition in the New Guangxi Clique's 7th army of the National Revolutionary Army.\n\nYoung enough to scrape by unscathed, Feng would fold into the Old Guangxi Clique's new army, and in 1927 would be sent to Seijo School in Tokyo to study Japanese. From 1928 to 1930 he studied at the Army Infantry School in Japan, and returned to China in 1931 to serve in the First Army of the Guangxi Army General Headquarters. In 1933 he served as the student captain of the Aviation Department of the Guangxi Army General Headquarters, and head of the General Affairs Section. The following year he was appointed deputy director of the Guangxi Aviation Department, and then the vice president of the Guangxi Aviation School.§!\n\n§YNotes: A young guard officer with a keen interest and knack for aviation, he has no concern for factional politics.§!

Zhang Zizhu
§YBorn:§! §L1896, in Xinhui District, Guangdong Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §LNeutral§!\n§YBackground:§! §LThough he grew up studying sericulture, after the Xinhai Revolution Zhang cut of his braids while still in school and began work as a teacher, politice officer, reporter, and tax collector. A protégé of Yang Yongtai, then director of Guangdong Provincial Finances, he was recommended to the Xinhui County Office which sponsored him to study abraod in France in 1920. There he entered the University of Paris studying economics and co-founded Herald Weekly with Hu Guowei. In 1924 he helped to found the Young China Party along with Zeng Qi, Li Huang, and others and was elected as their propaganda director. After etruning to China he taught economics at Shanghai Dasha University, Hosei University, and Wuchang Zhonghua University. He also briefly wen tto Yunnan to work as a senior advisor to Tang Jiyao, helping lead the newly established MinZhi College and also teaching at Donglu University and worked as a political instructor at Kunming Military Academy.\n\nAfter a stint teaching Overseas Chinese in Vietnam, he returned to China in 1928 to teach and write in Hong Kong. He also served a variety of roles in Cantonese civic organisations and minor government roles. With the majority of YCP senior leadership based in the North, Zhang has been free to manage southern affairs as he sees fit.§!

Nominally led by Russian Veteran Pyotr von Glazenap, serving as the main connection between Russia and China, the mission aims to integrate modern Russian methods to the battlegrounds of China.\n\nOther key figures within the mission include Georgy Elfvengren, an ideological army organiser intent on stressing ideological purity in warfare, and Vsevolod Ivanov, a famed cavalry reader, writer, and noted Eurasianist whom intends to import a \
MMIC - Liangguang Branch
§YMission Militaire Internationale en Chine's Liangguang Branch§!\n§YBackground:§! §LDeployed to China by the Third Internationale following the League War, the MMIC is the evolution of prior socialist efforts to aid the Chinese revolution. Initially consisting of Red Russian émigrés fleeing their civil war sent to aid Dr. Sun in 1924, it has since drawn officers from across the socialist world in an international effort befitting of its sponsors.\n\nThe current head of the Mission is Frenchman André Malraux, who serves a dual role of advising the National Revolutionary Army and promoting it back home. Other figures include David Ramsay of Britain, Manabendra Nath (M.N.) Roy of India, Jean Fontenoy of France, Ingvald Berentin Aase of Norway, and Jakob Rudnik (Paul Ruegg) of Ukraine.\n\nA mixture of political and military officers, their motivations are mixed. Some are drawn by the excitement of an adventure on the other side of the world, others by the tantalising prospect of fame and advancement they could not otherwise achieve at home. Some simply wish to fulfil a sense of internationalist obligation, while others have an affinity for Orientalism.\n\nMany younger NRA officers, particularly those trained abroad or sympathetic to syndicalism, are quite fond of their MMIC trainers. However, they have drawn criticism for their willingness to engage in Chinese politics - seen as undue meddling of internal affairs by many in the Old Guard. The MMIC on the other hand, believe themselves to be the necessary counterweight to reactionary influences in the NRA high command.§!
German Military Mission
§YBackground:§! §LRunning off of the background of the Sino-German Military Mission of the 1920s and 1930s, there has been an increasing cast of Pro-German cadets and officers educated in academies within Europe who have provided an ample landing ground for a further reigniting of Sino-German cooperation. Whilst the original Sino-German Military Mission found itself implemented sparingly in regions outside of the League of Eight Provinces, there has undoubtedly been hard lessons learned between the limits of which the Germans can exhert themselves on Chinese military-political spheres.\n\nLargely confining themselves to tacit pressure in politics, the rejuvenated Sino-German Military Mission focuses mostly on the sphere of war, bringing with them the attitudes of Germany's leading military groups; total war, mechanisation and mobility, or refinement of tried and true methods from the last World War. Either way, the renewed presence of German Officers in Chinese Headquarters may be a sign of a new dawn for Chinese Warfare.§!
Morris Abraham Cohen
Guangdong University
With the revival of the educational system within Guangdong that suffered under the Warlord period, the Guangdong University now serves as one of the frontiers of the patriotic Chinese Intelligentsia. Increasing the studies of technology that will help China not only in terms the future of the people but also in it's defence.
Guangzhou Hankou Railway
The new Guangzhou Hankou Railway company focuses on modernising the ways of transport on areas that we would originally had to rely on rivers on delivering supplies across, especially in Hankou which was a key example of this. However with the GHR, their help will make getting to these places like Hankou much easier.

Currency Instability
Our economic experts have recommended that we swiftly employ a programme of devaluation of the Liangguang Yuan in order to fight back against economic collapse, however such action has produce numerous bouts of ire and contention from the gentry and merchants, yet the broad farming peasantry supports our efforts, seeing their personal situations improve.
Economic Aid to Failing Industries
In order to save our local domestic industries and prevent even further unemployment within Liangguang, we have sought out local business leaders and merchants, offering them benevolent monetary aid in return for their continued functioning and support.
Pared Military Spending
Whilst our own independent military once shined as a beacon of provincial independence in the light of Zhili meddling, it has transformed into a bloated, inefficient mess. Withdrawing considerable funding in such critical times will hurt deeply, but sacrifices have to be made.
Struggling Literacy
By the turn of the 20th century, China's literacy rate reached a significant disparity with the rest of the world despite the formation and spread of modern education as a result of growing ties with the rest of the world. Irregardless, as a region, Guangdong finds itself as one of the most literate centers of China with some reports recording that the literacy rate in metropolitan areas might be close to 80-90%.\n\nNonetheless, rural areas in Liangguang such as Guangxi continue to struggle with widespread illiteracy and it will take some serious reforms in education in order to administer widespread provincial literacy.
Unfinished Business
Liangguang has never fully recovered from the political instability of the 1910s-1920s. A revolving door of governments took hold over Guangdong and Guangxi as warlords, revolutionaries, and others took turns seizing power from one another. The defeat of the Kuomintang's National Revolutionary government in Guangzhou by the Zhili backed by German support allowed for Old Guangxi Clique warlords to take power, but their externally aided rule has been shaky. Guangdong warlords have rallied around Guangzhou, with the diminished but not fully extinguished Federalist movement enduring. Meanwhile, Kuomintang and other revolutionary cells continue to operate across the two provinces, hoping one day to return to power.
German Style Army
Inspired by the works of Bauer and Seekt on their purposals of reforming the military; focusing on a smaller yet more mobile forces, set under a centralised command that is only loyal to the state with each assignee set to duties that relate to their expertese. This also allows resources to be under the control of the state to serve the army during times of war.
Japanese Style Army
Japan, though poses a threat to China, is a perfect model to reflect our military on, concidering they're the first in Asia to beat back an European power through full force of spirit, using the might of the infantry and skills of their airforce to both follow the code of Bushido to overcome the imperialistic Europeans.
Anglo-American Style Army
The Americans and their English kin are admired for a reason, focusing on tactics using artillery and making stronger use of the industry to supply the armies with an endless supply of equipment and not only to mention their commitment to be loyal to civilian governments, by mimicking them, we will be able to overcome our many foes.
Divided Regional Military
Liangguang is after all two provinces and with such a unique region naturally comes the problems of regionalism due to Guangxi and Guangdong's regional, cultural, and linguistic differences that have pervaded into the military forces of the province. The Guangdong Army is divided into a multitude of regional cliques and has fallen obsolete in comparison with forces such as the Fengtian Clique in the North while the Guangxi forces have been compared to be nothing more than organised bandit clans. As long as these forces remain divided and inadequate, Guangdong's potential to bid for a chance at national relevancy are but merely a fool's dream.
Divided Military

The Whampoa Spirit
Renowned for their sheer ferocity in the face of overwhelming odds, the cadres of Whampoa were like no other soldiers during the Northern Expedition, only being defeated by overwhelming technological and numerical advantage. With the reopening of Whampoa military academy, and the revival of its culture, a new generation of politically conscious, relentless and resilient soldier will be produced, for the ultimate unification and advancement of the Chinese nation.
The Whampoa Spirit

The Eliteheer Model
Following the natural conclusion of German attempts to professionalise the Northern and Southern Zhili armies of Wu Peifu and Sun Chuanfang respectively, we have sought to no longer be considered mere auxilleries to their elite forces, but to implement our own programme of a German styled military. With an elite core surrounded by lesser support forces, the emulation of the Victors of the Weltkrieg is sure to bring great successes to our government.
The Eliteheer Model

Local Officer Initiative
To ever maintain our advantage and cunning on the battlefield, we have delegated the tactical initiative to the officers actually foreseeing the combat, as opposed to some lofty general far from the frontlines. With this we hope to craft a culture of independent initiative in our individual units in conjuncture with the whole army, ultimately to reach the same mutual goal.
The Guangxi Model
Espoused by renowned Guangxi natives such as Bai Chongxi and Ma Ji, the hardy, bandit-filled, mountaineous landscape of Guangxi has bred a series of ingenious tactis to survive in such a region. Total mobilisation of the people, running parallel with a mindset and initiative aiming to circumvent the expectations of our opponents, our army is that of true Citizen-Soldiers.
The Guangxi Model
$GXC_guangxi_army_idea_desc$
The Guangxi Model
$GXC_guangxi_army_idea_desc$
The Threefold Self Reliance Policy
Taking the critical Three Principles espoused by Dr. Sun Yat-sen: Democracy, Welfare, Nationalism, the Guangxi model formulates the Three Principles of Self Reliance: self-government, self-sufficiency, self-defence. China must be united utilising Chinese arms, material and manpower only, and in such, the nation must also be reconstructed by the same men who fight for it.
Youth Paramilitarism
Unfortunately for a place as torn asunder like China, the youth's livelihood has been completely annihilated by the likes of perfidious warlords and bandits. In order to defend the nation from warlords and imperialists alike, the young boys and girls will be taught the skills necessary for the nation's survival from a young age, so that future generations shall never experience the humiliation that we have.
Rousing the People
In order to revive the spirit of our great nation, it is necessary that we unite all classes of the people in a common struggle for national defence, in preparation for a great bloodletting. Only out of this great bloodletting can our nation secure its existence and independence from foreign powers!
Creating Modern Reserves
The very precipice of our nation's survival could depend on its ability to muster sufficient reserves to reinforce our divisions in times of great strife, therefore in order for China to rise against foreign powers around the world we will implement a modern reserve system modelled on the contemporary military powers of the world.
Unified Militia Framework
We cannot simply rely upon imitating the old provincial militia system, rife with infractions against the common people, corruption, abuses of power, and above all else seemingly lacking in effectiveness and capability. Instead the Guangxi System should aim for a unified organisational framework, with extensive regulation and ideological motivation. Only then shall we truly reach the new horizons of modern warfare.
Chinese Warfare, Revitalised
Our militia system is not a new invention, nay in fact it is a way of waging warfare endemic to the very heart of the Chinese people. The dynasties of old all practised our system, we have merely reinvigorated it, bringing it to the new age of warfare. Our soldiers and citizenry are one in the same, like a sentient organism. May the unity in our peoples strike far and wide against our enemies!
Political Commissars
Inspired by the combat squads of the SZRS and the political commissars that make up the Russian Army as well as the former socialist influence on Whampoa, the resurrected National Revolutionary Army seeks to uphold the commissar model. Non-commissioned officers are instructed to lead their men in battle and in livelihood, demonstrating exceptional worth and commitment to the cause of national revolution as well as providing lectures on the infallible doctrine of the Three Principles of the People. If China is indeed to be saved, then the education of the masses must begin with the armed forces - so that they will be truly an army dedicated to the cause of revolution.
Dare to Die Corps
Salvation does not come to those who wait but rather to those who are willing to give it all, their livelihoods, their families, and most of all their lives for the freedom and liberty of China. As the bugle sounds and the revolutionary soldier rises to take his stand in this pivotal moment of Chinese history, bold and courageous individuals should seek to form dare to die corps in the mold of the martyrs of the Xinhai Revolution, so that they too can offer their lives in sacrifice for a free China.
Widespread Workers' Unrest
Since the start of Year of the Rat, a wave of working class unrest has spread rapidly through the city of Guangzhou - an unprecedented number of workers and seamen have been taking to the streets to clamour for reform since the failed 1932 Shanghai Uprising. Guangzhou's position by the sea and its existing urban networks have already been aroused several times with notable examples being the Canton-Hong Kong Strike of 1925 and the recent 1932 Uprising.\n\nThe League of Chinese Syndicalists and the Kuomintang are no doubt behind this recent wave of unrest that threatens to envelop and disrupt the province. Each day, the tones of the protestors continue to grow more and more belligerent and only time will tell whether or not the workers decide to take up arms on the barricades.

Expanded Social Services
As espoused by the third principle of Minsheng, the government must provide for the livelihood and welfare of the nation and its people. To this end we have sought to expand our social services to necessitate the support network that our people need in such trying times.
Mass Education Programs
With the expanding resources of the government, the Chinese nation has sought to implement multiple campaigns and programs seeking to expand education opportunities to the masses, modelled on the programs implemented within the militia system of the New Guangxi Clique. A large number of basic schools have been set up, proving remarkably successful.
The modern Chinese Federalist rose and fell across the first half of the 1920s. A coalition of constitutionalists, democrats, and regionalists - they banded together amid the continued North-South stalemate at the peak of the warlord era. Their calls for the various provinces to work towards implementing local self-government and then peacefully unite together into a federal republic achieved some traction, but after a promising start it was rejected by both northern and southern governments. The Zhili's successful unification by force discredited the movement, and many came to associate the movement as little more than a front for warlordism.\n\nRebuilding the momentum that was lost over a decade ago will be difficult, but the with the country once again seemingly hopelessly divided and the Zhili clique's liberalising efforts proving to be a sham, many hold out hope that the Federalist movement could be the solution to China's problems.

Lasting Legacy of the Cohong
The Cohong was the name granted to the Chinese merchant guild that solicited and operated the infamous import-export monopoly in Canton during the Qing dynasty, and for a time was the only point of access for Europeans within Chinese markets. Receiving immense profits and a practical monopoly on foreign trade by the Qing court, the Cohong would see a rapid expansion of finances within Canton, turning it into a true Pearl of the East. However, with the end of the First Opium War, the Cohong was dismantled, albeit this was not the end of the dominance of Cantonese Merchants.\n\nTransitioning from the Confucian bureaucratic merchant-to-merchant model of the Qing, the Cohong's successors would transform themselves into that of the British Empire's \

The primary agricultural developments of Guangxi province have not been for the benefit of the people, but for the creation of commodities and raw wealth accumulation, creating an economy entirely hinged on the ebbs and flows of its exports. Controlled by the local gentry through a web of powerful landowners, magistrates, and allied militias, unrest over rents and taxation has triggered conflicts for generations.\n\nDespite how essential exports are, both Guangdong and Guangxi often lack sufficient roads (or at least sufficiently maintained ones) for bulk agricultural transportation. Liangguang, as part of a China saddled with unequal treaties, cannot control import duties. A lack of capital due to poverty, political corruption, and endemic banditry (and the need to hire soldiers to guard against it) has prevented the two provinces (especially the inland Guangxi) from exploiting arable land, water, and mineral resources.

Scorched Earth

Republic of China Navy
The precursor to a modern Chinese republican navy dates back to the early days of the Xinhai Revolution when the Ministry of the Navy was formed in the Provisional Government of the Republic of China. The former Qing Dynasty admiral Liu Guanxiong became the first Minister of Navy during this turbulent period. Following the Warlord Era and the subsequent Xuantong Restoration, many elements of the Republic of China Navy fled south for the Kuomintang and Federalist strongholds, while many others chose to remain loyal to the government in Beijing. With the Chinese country finally nominally unified under one banner, the time has come once more to revitalise and rebuild the Republic of China Navy so that it is truly worthy of protecting China's great maritime interests.

Fallout of the Yue-Gui Split
The Nationalist Government has divided into Guangdong (Yue) based and Guangxi (Gui) based camps, with catastrophic results. Barely maintaining the facade of unity against external foes, unification has ironically eliminated any pretence of unity, a problem worsened in the aftermath of President Lin Sen's resignation. All of this has left the central government dangerously vulnerable to circling vultures.

The economists and planners of the National Reconstruction Commission adhere to the autarkic practices inspired from Russia's Aleksandr Voznesensky and the Supreme Economic Council. Encirclement and Annihilation
The doctrine of encirclement embraces two of the three principle aims of war stipulated by CLausewitz: first, destruction of the military power of the enemy, and second - the destruction of his will. Annihilation of enemy units means a reduction in the extent of enemy territory, which grants us greater capacity for mobilising popular support.

Progressive Tax Reforms

Part of this involves a thorough, nation-wide revision of the national tax system, incorporating a tiered, mean-based brackets for income, inheritance, and excessive profits taxes. Such a programme is bound to be unpopular among the wealthy, but it is a must if China is to survive in the modern age.

Experimental Rice Cultivation
Educated in some of the finest Faculties of Agriculture in China and Japan, Ding Ying has been a pioneer in experimentation in regards to prolific rice strains, having bred a total of 110 rice varieties, and by 1934 had bred the first hybrid rice strain with wild rice. Under his promotion, Guangdong became the rice breeding centre of Southern China, but with his methods now popularised nationwide, it is hoped that the whole of China will never have to experience starvation ever again.

The Charitable Halls, Qing Era Institutions that ebbed and flowed in significance in the Warlord Era, have been the bedrock of various Guangzhou based governments ever since 1910. Without railroads a nation cannot industrialise as efficiently, and within the inteior of China, many cities and villages hardly have roads let alone modernised track. Expositor of the Three Principles
The National Revolution and the Three Principles may be a uniquely Chinese endeavour, that is true. Nonetheless, the Three Principles of the People may serve as a template for all revolution's around the world, as China's own National Revolution pertains to be one of the youngest and most successful in recent history. Working towards this goal, it is imperative that the Party remind the Chinese people of the revolutionary faith by which it defends and espouses.
Tutelage Phase
In essence, the phase of Tutelage as described by the Guofu follows the principles of educating the Chinese people on their rights within a modern democracy, their duty to participate in it, and the eventual introduction of local self government as a preamble towards the Constitutional Rule stage. Through the processes of local training programs for excising self-governance, implementation of Land to the Tiller, the reduction of national illiteracy, and additionally staff training programs for the party as to allow its personnel to better accommodate Tutelage, it is hoped that China will be revolutionarily transformed. Marching forward towards Datong, Tutelage itself must forever maintain a spirit of benevolence and forgiveness.\n\nHowever the Party cannot become complacent, it cannot merely suffice to fill the void of the warlords, gentry and socialists before it. For the Guofu has left us an ideology of practical and scientific theory, based on the laws of human social evolution, not that of metaphysics or materialism.
Democratic Awakening
With the seizing of the levers of power by the more democratic elements of the KMT, the boisterous chorus of cries clambering for the democratisation of the state have been emboldened, and continue to grow ever louder, seriously hampering the new government's capacity to maintain the daily functions of the state.
Warlord Intransigence
Despite on paper striving for a centralised government ruling all regions of China, in reality the previous government's position necessitated the establishment of alliances and agreements with a variety of minor and major regional warlords in order to ensure the success of its military campaigns. With the rise of the [ROOT.GetRulingParty], the various warlords continue to exercise their full control of their regional forces, slashing the effectiveness of our armies.
Student Agitation
With the increasing political activity of the young Chinese populace, many students have taken to creating pressure groups in order to agitate against the government in their desire for a system that truly functions not just to stabilise a revolutionary state, but to provide stability and welfare for every individual no matter their creed.
Return of the Vanguard
Considered the elder membership of the Kuomintang, the Western Hills Clique has seen a resurgence in both vitality and popularity, no longer necessitating being the minor partner to Hu Hanmin, but now a force in its own right. Pressing forward, they hope to drive home the quest for a \

May the Republic of China stand as a shining example for the international community to emulate.

Empowered National Legislature
It is imperative, particularly pressing even, that the nation no longer be ruled by the rifle. For the promotion of constitutional government and the realisation of democratic politics must be the primary goals of nation-building. Officialdom is not a gateway to power, but a gateway to serving the people. A strong legislature, alongside the adoption of a general culture that stands for abiding by the law and cultivating the habit of rule of law, that will be the fundamental solution for the country's decades of chaos and bloodshed.
Constitution of the Republic of China
Forming the Fundamental Law by which the Republic governs, the Constitution of the Republic of China serves to be the final step on the long road to true constitutional rule within the Chinese nation. Drafted by the Political Consultative Council bringing together some of China's greatest legal minds from within the Kuomintang, namely the Reconstruction Faction of Sun Fo, the Western Hills Clique of Ju Zheng, the Elders Clique of Hu Hanmin, and even some minor participants such as the Political Science Clique of Huang Fu, it is a testament to the Kuomintang's duty, servitude, and diligence in China's state building project.\n\nHighlighting the Three Principles of the People as the guiding principles upon which the nation should be administered, and the concept of sovereignty of the people, the constitution also clearly stipualtes the protection of the people's freedoms and civil rights, the central government's own separation of powers into five governmental organs - the Five Yuans, and the local autonomy system.\n\nOutshining the previous attempts at constitutional rule within the Chinese nation, such as the Cao Kun Constitution, or the Temple of Heaven Draft Constitution, it is an inherently progressive piece of legislature. Composed of three parts, the Constitution of Liberty, the Constitution of Government, and the Constitution of Sovereignty, it retains 175 articles in total divided into 14 chapters.
Breaking their Hilts
Considered a revolutionary political-military structure that won the National Revolutionary Army its greatest victories, the time of Whampoa's political indoctrination, focus on martial spirit and developing radicalism has run its course. With the nation united, and the radical alumni of the academy alongside nefarious warlord cliques in strong opposition to [ROOT.GetLeader], it has been deemed necessary to excise indoctrination. For all it is worth, loyalty to ideology pales in patriotism and fervent nationalism for one's country, regardless of creed.
Flourish of the Liberals
Whilst once considered a spent force within the Kuomintang, the Liberal forces of the Reconstruction Faction have proven all naysayers wrong. With its great success, many are flooding to its ranks, in the hopes of eventual Chinese democracy, sooner, rather than later. Endowed with newfound passion, vigour, and purpose, the Reconstruction Faction shall surely see the conclusion of the National Revolution, for Tutelage is merely a matter of time.
Revolution by the Railway!
Driven forward from experiences garnered from his father, and his tenure as Minister of Railways, it has become increasingly clear to [ROOT.GetLeader] that national prosperity and the National Revolution can only be brought to fruition through infrastructure. Without infrastructure, would Britain have ruled the waves? Would Germany have found their place in the sun? Would American have tamed the west? It is time for China's own poverty to be solved by the train!
Consolidation of the Bureaucracy
Ostensibly an authoritarian party, the Kuomintang is rife with opposition to our more liberal minded outlook. To undo this, we have begun to embark on a gradual reorganisation of the party and government. Reshuffling ministers and appointees, it is hoped that the nation shall be a government free of bureaucratic evils. For China to achieve a democratic system, it must first have wise political leadership capable of enabling China and its people to move towards the goal of democratic politics.
Local Democratic Elections
The Kuomintang adheres to Dr. Sun Yat-sen's beliefs that the rights of the people are the ability to exercise election, recall, referendum, and initiative. These rights must, however, be trained and taught by the Party, under the leadership of the Kuomintang and the creation of local governments with the help of the Party - can such a democracy flourish? It is the goal of the Party that by training the masses - that one day the people will fully be capable of democracy.
