Reaffirm Inter-provincial Hegemony
Under Governor Lu Rongting, the twin provinces of Guangxi and Guangdong were briefly joined together under the rule of the Old Guangxi Clique, which did not last long, however, as Guangxi dominance over Liangguang disintegrated following the devastating Yue-Gui Wars. Although the twin provinces went through years of a tremendous power vacuum after the collapse of the Northern Expedition, Governor Lu Rongting returned to power in Guangxi and, through his pragmatic alliances, managed to secure himself the position of 'Viceroy of Liangguang'. As heirs to his legacy, with the dust having settled across the wartorn provinces, we shall reassert our dominance and remind those who oppose us who the true masters of Liangguang are.
Attract Foreign Support
Our control over the territories we rule is limited, to say the least. With us lacking allies within Liangguang, and those that we do have only doing so out of pragmatic self-preservation, we lack the means to overcome our precarious military and economic situation. Despite the rest of the country being either too busy dealing with their internal struggles, or outright hostile to our cause, we are fortunately still able to obtain foreign support through our contacts. By attracting their favour, our misfortunes shall be reversed, even if it comes at a price.
Harmony with the Business Class
For centuries, the merchant and business class have been the backbone to China's prosperity, accumulating wealth and building local economies through trade. Should our relations with them remain harmonious and cooperative, the prosperity and wealth brought on by these merchants would be of great use to us, assisting in our construction of Liangguang. Unlike the Kuomintang, which had needlessly antagonised and suppressed these merchant associations, we will continue to acknowledge their role within society and build a tranquil peace together.
Rebuilding A Southern Political Establishment
Through endless warfare and administrative fragmentation due to political infighting on all sides, Liangguang fell into near-anarchy. Swept away by the Kuomintang's takeover of the twin provinces, the Beiyang-friendly political establishment in the South was in ruins. Even as our control over Liangguang returned in the aftermath of the Northern Expedition, the political landscape has never been the same as the local bureaucracy stumbles to regain its grip. With the help of local administrative experts, the construction of a more efficient and well-oiled political machine will assist in the process of political centralisation.
Treading the Lawful Path
To rule lands through the iron grip and the army's strength is easy, but to maintain stability and social harmony is difficult if the rule of law is ignored, especially by the rulers who intend to govern their lands. Public perception of the governments in Liangguang has been at an all-time low, as crimes go unpunished and are, in many cases, encouraged as a path towards social mobility. With the calls for constitutionalism and democracy grow louder and louder, this is an opportune time for us to kill two birds with one stone by restoring public trust in our administration alongside calming democratic voices by demonstrating our commitment to a strict adherence to law.
Pressure Liangguang into Alignment
Our efforts in Liangguang have begun to bear fruit as those loyal to our government are starting to wield enough power to contest the current authorities in the region. With their mandate in the twin provinces on the verge of collapse, it is high time we apply pressure upon the region and mobilise loyalist forces within Liangguang to seize control!
Allure of Money and Flattery
The sorry state that Liangguang finds itself in is merely another opportunity for us to expand our influence in the South. Having recently repelled a devastating invasion by the Kuomintang alongside internal rebellions by bandits and local warlords, many within Liangguang are insecure about their positions, financially and militarily. By sending financial aid to assist in their reconstruction alongside flattering words of their leadership, they will become more receptive towards our government. After all, everyone has their price.
Bribing the Disaffected
Liangguang may be in a semi-stable state on the surface, but to those who look within, it is clear that not everybody is satisfied with the current arrangements. Whether it be out of petty grudges, ideological disagreements with their superiors, or simply willing to do anything for the sake of personal enrichment, many within Liangguang are willing to switch sides if bribed enough. Discreetly, we can dismantle the organisational structure of an opposing faction easily, allowing our preferred candidate in Liangguang to gain control without much bloodshed.
Issue Threats to Liangguang
With their military in tatters having recently come out of devastation, their inability to put up much of a resistance should another invasion march into Liangguang, and the need for time to rebuild their army has made Liangguang prone to appeasement. To further expand our influence within the region, we should pressure the leadership in Liangguang with threats, and hopefully, they will be scared enough to comply with our demands of accommodating our preferred side within their administration.
Accommodate Local Warlords
Aside from the incursions by Kuomintang forces, Liangguang was also plagued by rebellions staged by bandits and local warlords. Crushed by regional authorities along with the repelling of KMT forces, these warlords fled across the South, either retiring into obscurity or still hopelessly planning a comeback, intent on reclaiming the position they had lost in their rebellion. With them at our mercy, we could accommodate and use them to further expand our influence within the twin provinces.
Send Out Peace Feelers
The South has notoriously been the most rebellious region in the country, having been the host to Sun Yat-sen and the revolutionary Kuomintang, which had marched on the north in their Northern Expedition. Although the expedition collapsed and was unsuccessful, fears of a second Northern Expedition have yet to subside. Thus, it is necessary that we send peace feelers to Liangguang and ensure that conflict between our two administrations does not emerge, as well as strengthen those within their ranks who are sympathetic to our cause.
Expand the Guangxi System
Conceptualised as a fundamental framework in which local Guangxi leaders are able to organise their Guangxi Reconstruction Movement, the Guangxi Militia system finds its historical precedence in the Xia, Shang, and Zhou dynasties. Having no professional armies, no distinction existed between soldiers and peasants, with the army and people acting in unison. When the nation faced war, all in the nation took up arms. When peace returned, troops dispersed to till their lands. This system was referred to as \
Expand Guangxi System in [FROM.GetName]
Whilst the Guangxi system has found remarkable success within Guangxi itself, it has always been the ambitions of its progenitors to see it expanded to the whole of China. By dividing [FROM.GetName] into a series of militia regions, district magistrates will be indebted with the responsibilities of transplanting the Guangxi system to their local communities.
Add Reconnaissance Detachments
By incorporating various cavalry detachments into the Guangxi militia formations, we can ensure superior combat effectiveness and reconnaissance gathering capabilities within the paramilitaries. We shall be more than just the old provincial militia system, we shall be a new citizen's army.
Add Support Artillery
Expanding the firepower capabilities of our Guangxi militias are imperative, as the lone rifle cannot strike a semblance of the carnage or fear that a barrage of artillery shells can. Of course, it will be necessary to incorporate artillery training into the militia curriculum, but that is a simple matter.
Add Support Engineer Company
Including trained specialist engineer companies within the Guangxi militias will undoubtedly increase their effectiveness not only in field duty, but also in that of infrastructure efforts, and in the shaping of the new Chinese state.
Incorporate Additional Militia Formations
Whilst the initial militia's formation has proved themselves successful in all facets required of them, the time has come to advance from a mere militia to a full volunteer army. Utilising long term enlistments, and a working draft system, every able bodied male of military age shall be trained in the way of warfare. As when the inevitable final struggle dawns upon the Chinese people, every one will take up arms to fight for their nation.
Expand the Wehrstaat in [FROM.GetName]
Three Year Plan Decisions
Although Guangdong is a rich region in China, much work remains to be done if this province is to be brought into the modern age. The collapse of the League and Black Monday have laid bare our economic weaknesses, which has prompted the Guangzhou Government to lay out plans for an ambitious economic project that will modernise and develop our economy. The Three Year Plan will make use of Guangxi's material resources as well as Guangdong's industrial capacity to address both agricultural and urban issues in order to bring prosperity for all, and a beacon for the rest of China to emulate!
Wengjiang Hydroelectric Plant
Close in proximity to the city of Shaoguan is the Bei Jiang (North River), which flows south into Guangzhou. The Guangzhou Government's economic planners have decided that it is a prime spot to construct a hydroelectric plant, which will help with electrification efforts in Guangdong province.
Guangdong Beverage Factory
The Beverage Industry is a burgeoning part of Guangdong's economy. Many in China and indeed the world love to drink beers and soft drinks, and the architects of the Three Year Plan have laid out plans to seize on this opportunity by investing funds for a new beverage factory in Guangzhou, which will attract more commerce in Guangdong.
Guangdong Iron and Steel Mill
As part of our three year plan to modernize the economy of Guangdong, a plan has been formulated by the ministry of industry to develop Guangdong's iron and steel production. Despite being in abundance in China, steel output is low throughout the nation with the exception of the Anshan steel facility in the Northeast. Developing this steel and iron mill will help us reduce Japanese influence over our national economy and provide us a solid foundation for greater economic development.
Guangdong Chemical and Fertiliser Factory
Although a primarily agricultural nation, China still lacks a modern fertilising industry. Economic planners have outlined an ambitious project to develop a chemical and fertilising plant in Guangzhou which will hopefully kickstart China's fertilising modernisation.
Expand the Fuguo Coal Mine
We are still fighting shortages of important resources such as coal and the mines in Fuguo remain small. They should be expanded, for coal is one of the most important resources in the world and we must limit our imports of it.
Guangdong Farmer's Association Collectivisation
Whilst the Chinese nation may be united under the auspices of Peng Bodhisattva, day in and day out, the peasantry continue to lament under the perfidious gentry and landlord. No longer shall this be the case! Through any methods necessary, the peasantry will overthrow their chains and masters, rising to a new utopia!
Mass Collectivisation Efforts in [FROM.GetName]
The peasantry within [FROM.GetName] suffer under captialistic and feudalistic tyranny. Let [ROOT.GetLeader] and his loyal followers offer them their salvation.
China's Revolutionary New Culture Movement
Resolute in his beliefs, Tan Pingshan has seen the faults and fallacies in traditional Chinese culture. Such methods of living life has brought nothing but turmoil, pain and suffering to the Chinese people. This destructive influence has even destroyed the National Revolution of our forebearers. The true Chinese culture is that of a revolutionary one, a class conscious people exnoerating the Revolution.
Ensure Revolutionary Culture in [FROM.GetName]
[FROM.GetName] is rife with Counter-Revolutionary sentiment, and continues to remain unenlightened by the Third Party and our methods. Let us work towards their eventual integration into the mass Revolution.
Crumbling Hegemonies in China
With the Lunar Year approaching, League Marshal Sun Chuanfang has summoned the governors of the League of Eight Provinces to Nanjing for their annual conference. Notably not in attendance are Governor Zhao Hengti of Hunan, Viceroy Lu Yuguang of Liangguang, or his adopted-brother Governor Ma Ji of Guangxi. Shen Hongying, the quasi-recognised Governor of Guangdong has attended, ostensibly in the Old Guangxi Clique's stead.\n\nThough the \

Warring Factions in Eastern Guangdong
Eastern Guangdong has severely fragmented over the years, never quite healing from the conflicts of the 1920s. The furthest from the Young Guangxi Faction's centre of power in Nanning and bordering both the League and the MinGan insurgency, the Young Guangxi Faction's grip is weakest there, allowing for local factions to swell in size.\n\nTo the northeast is Shen Hongying and his Shen Army Faction, ruling from the city of Shaoguan. An ambitious Old Guangxi Clique warlord installed by Sun Chuanfang as Governor of Guangdong, he lost the power struggle for Liangguang with the Young Guangxi Faction of the Old Guangxi Clique in 1932 and was ejected from his traditional base in Guilin.\n\nShen now manages two loyalties. Forced to bend the knee to Lu Rongting's sons Lu Yuguang and Ma Ji, he retains considerable autonomy thanks to the patronage of Marshal Sun who uses him to retain influence over Liangguang during the Southern Zhili's power struggle with the North. Ma Ji, for his part, was a former lieutenant of Wu Peifu, successfully brokering an alliance with the North for his father to counteract the South. This proxy battle has prevented League forces from re-entering Guangdong, but the lack of cooperation has also allowed MinGan insurgents (who do not respect provincial borders) to infiltrate the area.\n\nTo the southeast are a string of Old Guangdong Clique (Yue Army) warlords, hostile to both Old Guangxi factions. Mayor Chen Lianbo recruited the exiled Chen Jiongming, former Governor of Guangdong, to raise an army to protect Guangzhou's autonomy, and Chen once again proved adept at gathering forces around his native Haifeng county. Chen has decided to remain mostly in Guangzhou as a Senator, leaving Ye Ju to manage this coalition of Yue warlords, including Yang Kunru of Huizhou and Hong Zhaolin in Chaomei. They are hardly alone however, with socialist peasants under Peng Pai and his Farmer's Association continuing to fight a brutal insurgency against the Yue-aligned gentry.
Lu Yuguang has arrived in Shaoguan to soothe tensions with Shen.
Chen Jiongming tours the southeast to rally Yue Army loyalists.
Peng Pai sends messengers to MinGan to coordinate operations.
Anti-Concessionist Violence in Canton
The ZheGan railway project, meant to be the ultimate showpiece of Sino-German cooperation, has utterly backfired. The AOG moved to consolidate control over many smaller railroads with the help of corrupt Zhili officials, giving them de facto control over what was supposed to be an equal partnership and prompting massive anti-concessionist protests around the country. This has triggered a greater wave of dissent across the nation as the Chinese people's repressed discontentment over economic inequality, Zhili authoritarianism, and continued foreign imperialism exploded into the open.\n\nIn Guangzhou, this has manifested in attacks against the merchants, who are seen as compradors for foreign conglomerates. The merchant elite, who have ruled Guangzhou since the German withdrawal in 1928 have called on Mayor Chen Lianbo to deploy his so-called Merchant Corps to restore order in the city. This paramilitary, initially formed to protect merchant interests from roving warlords and revolutionaries in the city, were reformed and turned into Chen's enforcers.\n\nWhile effective in breaking up smaller strikes, the turmoil engulfing the city has not been seen since the 1925 Canton-Hong Kong Strike. The Kuomintang, Chinese Syndicalist Party, and various anarchist groups are said to be stoking unrest through their agents in urban unions, but the tremendous outpouring of fury against foreign concessions indicates much deeper-rooted anger than all of them combined could muster on their own.\n\nAnti-concessionist beliefs extend to the intellectual class as well. Progressives and other members of the centre-left, most notably the Chinese Renaissance Society and the Public Interest Party, have also denounced the AOG. Dissatisfied with the reactionary warlords and conservative merchant government, they have begun to call for peaceful but decisive reforms in the Chinese political system. Their influence provides a distinct tug on student groups, pulling against more radical voices.
The radicals' demands for armed revolution inflame the masses.
The reformers' calls for measured changes take root among the protesters.

The Baise Borderlands
Ever since the death of Liu Rifu in 1933, western Guangxi has grown less and less stable. Kuomintang raids from Guizhou into the area have escalated amid national unrest. The poor, mountainous area has proven to be a porous border for the mixed band of insurgents, bandits, and militiamen launching probing attacks.\n\nThey are likely merely the prelude of the assault to come. The Young Guangxi War Council
The inner workings of the Young Guangxi Government are opaque. Guangxi is a poor, isolated province, especially compared to Guangdong. Many dismiss them as being merely the chiefs of Guangxi's bandit clans, but the truth is more complicated.\n\nThe Young Guangxi Clique is a quadrumvirate. Founded by Ma Ji, Lin Hu, and Liu Zhilu as the young guard of Lu Rongting's Old Guangxi Clique, after Lu's fall from power Ma Ji was able to leverage his good relationship with Wu Peifu and service under the Zhili during the Northern Expedition to successfully claim Guangxi in 1927.\n\nHe invited his adopted father Lu Rongting to return as a figurehead, and the YGF rallied the Guangxi clans to defeat Shen Hongying and assert hegemony over Liangguang. Prior to Lu's death in 1934, he had long groomed his adopted son (and child of his favourite concubine) Lu Yuguang as his heir. A fairly unsuccessful military commander and a decade younger than his peers, he is nevertheless the face of the government.\n\nThe YGF do tend to respect civilian governing structures on the whole. Lu Rongting set a precedent of sorts by cooperating with the Political Science Clique during the Constitutional Protection Junta era, and his heirs regularly convene the Guangxi Provincial Assembly. The Guangdong Provincial Assembly, based in Guangzhou, notably refuses to recognise Shen Hongying as Governor but did recognise Lu's suzerainty in the early 1930s.\n\nStill, in vital matters, only a select council deliberates. The violence in Wuhu seemed to be a turning point in the League, with Governor Chen Tiaoyuan of Anhui seceding from the League, which may trigger a war. Some fear this will shatter the three-way balance of power between the Northern Zhili, Southern Zhili, and the western provinces. Moving against either may expose the fragility of Liangguang's own balance of power, especially as political unrest extends even into the usually isolated, conservative Guangxi's military, political, and intellectual elite.
The council decides a show of strength will keep external problems away from Liangguang.
The council decides a cohesive message of anti-concessionism will win back defectors.
More councils and assemblies should be called to navigate this crisis.
Black Monday Devastates Canton
The precise cause of Black Monday is being debated and will probably be an extremely contentious topic in history books, but what is indisputable is the devastation it has wrought on the global economy. With the American economy still sluggish, the collapse of yet another vital Chinese trade partner has caused the shares sold at the Shanghai Stock Exchange to plunge in value.\n\nGuangzhou (often referred to as Canton by Westerners) has been severely affected by the crisis. The Smiling Tiger Falls
Sometimes, despite controlling provinces and armies and commanding vast webs of political patronage and hoarded wealth, all it takes is a single gunshot to fell a powerful man. And sometimes, all it takes is a single man to fall for an entire system to collapse.\n\nSun Chuanfang was shot, feared to be dead or dying. And while of course the League was already teetering from instability, Sun being out of the picture has made a bad situation much, much worse.\n\nRumours have been flying of a potential coup in the League, with Chen Tiaoyuan still in active rebellion and Qi Xieyuan said to be scheming from within Nanjing. Sun Chuanfang's loyalists insist he is still alive and will soon recover enough to resume control over the situation, but he has yet to be publicly available. The league is clearly on the verge of collapse.\n\nThe warlords that depended on Southern Zhili patronage are in utter shambles. \

A War on Two Fronts
Qi Xieyuan's seizure of power in Nanjing yesterday seemed to punctuate the collapse of the Zhili hegemony across China. With the two branches of the Southern Zhili Clique trading blows, the central government reeling from the crisis, and the MinGan insurgency rising up in a major rebellion, the post-1928 order has come to a fiery end.\n\nIn the east, General Chen Mingshu and his 10th Division have been deployed southwards on a vital mission: to secure the Kuomintang heartland in Guangdong for the revolution or at least to die protecting the insurgency's southern flank. With limited resources, it is unclear if they will be able to hold out long enough for reinforcements to arrive - if they ever do.\n\nThey are not alone, however. About a week ago, a large raid in Baise culminated with Li Zongren leading his Seventh Army into Guangxi to march on Nanning. It is unknown if or to what extent the dual invasions were coordinated, or if they simply had similar ideas the moment Sun was shot. Either way, what is clear is that there will be no retreat.\n\nNeither force can amount to more than 10,000 men. But victory is a strong magnet and Warlord Era armies have been known to rapidly expand and contract depending on who is winning and who is best able to pay. At the start of the Northern Expedition, for example, the National Revolutionary Army numbered around 100,000 men organised into \

Turning Point in Guangzhou
Whatever sense of law and order previously held in Guangzhou has bottomed out. With news of Chen Mingshu and Li Zongren's advances quickly making rounds across the two provinces, radical sleeper cells have awakened. KMT and CSP-backed strikers have paralysed the city in an eruption of rioting, joined by students and other protesters taking to the streets.\n\nAs the rabble begins to push back police forces, the Merchant Corps militia, and even military forces deployed against them, KMT sympathisers within the Guangdong Army have begun to plot against the government. At the centre of the conspiracy is Chen Jitang, a right-wing Cantonese regionalist who had defected to the winning side all those years ago and now must find common ground with the socialist agitators he betrayed.\n\nElsewhere in the city, Mayor Chen Lianbo was hoping to receive a show of support when he convened the Provincial Assembly but found himself walking into another headache. He was met by Senator Chen Jiongming, who demanded the floor immediately - brushing aside decorum and his political rival: Political Science Clique affiliated politician, former gubernatorial candidate, loose ally of Mayor Chen, and now Speaker of the Assembly Lin Zhengxuan.\n\nSenator Chen's \

Our Interests in Guangzhou
The history of Guangzhou, also known as Canton, is as rich as China itself. Resting upon the mouth of the Pearl River, for a century it was the only port foreign traders could access. This caused the broader Guangdong region (also referred to as Canton) to become a major centre of trade, enriching it significantly. Although other ports have since been opened to exports (often by force), Guangzhou's valuable nature has not changed.\n\nIt has, however, been occupied by a carousel of successive governments. National Protection, Constitutional Protection, and Sun Yat-sen's Guangzhou governments were major southern challengers to the northern establishment, the latter two anchored in the city. This, unsurprisingly, has not been good for the trade of the region, bringing a great economic downturn for business and the people. However, after the Northern Expedition, Chen Lianbo - through great political manoeuvring and extensive negotiations between the imperial powers that would form the Legation Cities - took power within the city as Mayor.\n\nChen's power rests on his democratic control of the Canton Merchant Corps, the armed militia of the local merchants. His rule has not been without conflict, pinned down by the Zhili-backed Old Guangxi Clique and their notoriously undisciplined troops. Socialist unrest has also worsened in the region, as Kuomintang remnants threaten Guangdong's stability. Feeling ignored and abandoned by both the Zhili and the Germans, Mayor Chenhas been secretly courting the Fengtian government and also seeking the protection of the Legation Powers, most notably the Japanese from their bases in Formosa.\n\nMayor Chen, in his desperation, has sent an envoy to us. The city of Guangzhou (Canton) sits at the mouth of the Pearl River and for many years was the only port foreign traders could access. As a result, the city and its surrounding province of Guangdong became a wealthy centre of trade.\n\nInstability in China has resulted in a carousel of successive governments occupying Guangdong and with turmoil once again threatening our eastern neighbor, it is essential that we protect our interests. Mayor Chen Lianbo of Guangzhou secured his rule over the city through cunning political manoeuvring and extensive negotiations between the imperial powers that would form the Legation Cities and his control over his Merchant Corps militias.\n\nThreatened by neighboring Zhili aligned warlords, he has been secretly courting the Fengtian government, the Legations, and Japanese officials stationed in Formosa. [FROM.GetNameDefCap] Supports Chen Lianbo
An envoy from [FROM.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] has informed Mayor Chen Lianbo of [FROM.GetNameDef]'s wholehearted support in his endeavours in attempting to maintain control over the Guangdong region. Presently seen as the most stable and legitimate high provincial official in the region, Chen's growing renown has led to increased tensions amongst Beiyang partners in Liangguang, namely the Old Guangxi Clique. Considered Chen's main rivals, the Merchant has long voiced his discontent and unhappiness with Old Guangxi's self-assertive behaviour in dealing with unrest in the region, and their purported claim of de-jure rulership in Liangguang.\n\nWhilst tensions have remained short of active conflict, considering that their mutual enemies are far more dangerous, Old Guangxi has continued a certain obstinant streak against the Merchant Corps. Feeling abandoned by the Germans, and ignored by the Central Zhili Government, and similarly losing his grip on his allied warlord subordinates alongside increasing pressure from Kuomintang insurgents, Chen's turn to [FROM.GetNameDef] was all but a natural evolution of events.\n\nHowever it has raised even further fury from Old Guangxi and their acolytes, as it both entangles further foreign players into regional power politics, alongside cementing Lianbo's position in the region - with particularly dubious financial, diplomatic, and material support.

Shen Hongying, Governor of Guangdong?
The history of Guangzhou, also known as Canton, is as rich as China itself. Resting upon the mouth of the Pearl River, for a century it was the only port foreign traders could access. This resulted in the broader Guangdong Province becoming inundated with trade and commerce, enriching it significantly. Although other ports have been opened to exports (often by force), Guangzhou's inherently valuable nature has not changed.\n\nLacking any true allies in the region, the Zhili Clique has long had to settle on auxiliary forces and opportunistic elements. With many ambitious warlords in the region, the North and South Zhili have largely split their support onto different horses, functioning as another extension of their split. For the North, the most charitable group is that of Lu Yuguang, Liu Zhilu, Lin Hu, and Ma Ji's Young Guangxi faction. For the South, they were left with Shen Hongying.\n\nThe nominal governor of Guangdong, at least from Nanjing's perspective, has been unable to secure control of much of the region fully. With his rival Mayor Chen Lianbo controlling a sizeable portion of the Pearl River Delta and Guangzhou, Shen has largely fallen by the wayside. Swiftly amassing an army in the interwar, the Merchant Corps have blunted any attempts for a full Old Guangxi takeover. Similarly, the Guangdong Provincial Assembly has utterly refused to recognise Shen, and his rivals in the Old Guangxi Clique. To make a bad situation worse,Young Guangxi managed to oust him from his primary base in Guilin in 1932.\n\nOnly quasi-recognised, Shen has largely languished within Shaoguan. Directly sustained through the support network stretching from Nanjing, his forces are sustained by Berlin. However, his fief is overflowing with MinGan insurgents, and if he is to stake his claim on Guangdong, he would necessitate funding. Naturally, Shen's position would endear him to any benefactor in perpetuity, and he could perhaps be more loyal than the Young Marshal of the South.
The city of Guangzhou (Canton) sits at the mouth of the Pearl River and for many years was the only port foreign traders could access. As a result, the city and its surrounding province of Guangdong became a wealthy centre of trade.\n\nInstability in China has resulted in a carousel of successive governments occupying Guangdong and with turmoil once again threatening our eastern neighbor, it is essential that we protect our interests. The region has been divided into quasi-fiefs since the German-Zhili victory in the Northern Expedition, and officers from our military mission deployed in China have identified the ambitious warlord Shen Hongying as our best avenue for maintaining our influence.\n\nGeneral Shen, based out of the city of Shaoguan, claims to be a Governor of the province though his legitimacy has been questioned. He has worked to bolster his power by courting German aid through the League and has worked with German advisors to suppress socialist insurgents operating nearby. Providing further support may help shore up Zhili rule in southern China and thus protect our northern flank.

[FROM.GetNameDefCap] Supports Shen Hongying
It would appear that Shen Hongying still retains a few tricks up his sleeve. Pulling on contacts outside of the region, namely his Zhili benefactors, Shen has managed to secure some additional material, financial, and military aid from [FROM.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] in exchange for some reaffirming of his allegiance to their interests and power. Pushed out of power by the Young Guangxi faction, circumstances have found the latter's independent streak pushing Shen forward as the Zhili's top-dog in the region, even in his isolated state.\n\nHolding a strong grudge against Young Guangxi for their ousting of Shen from Guilin, and similarly a rival with Chen Lianbo for Governorship of Guangdong, Shen has found himself in a difficult position. Relying exclusively on external support and aid, he had only tacitly recognised Lu Rongting's status as Viceroy in 1932, continuing to claim his position as Governor under him. With Rongting's death in 1934, however, Shen shifted his tone, utterly refusing to recognise Lu Yuguang as the new viceroy. Despite this, he presents himself as the lesser of two evils.\n\nWith Eastern Guangdong crawling with Kuomintang insurgents flowing in from the MinGan area, alongside Chen Jiongming and his Old Yue allies setting up their own power bloc in the south around Huizhou and Chaomei, the additional support from [FROM.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] would prove crucial in securing his position for the future.

Yunnan's Ambitions in Guangxi
The history of Yunnan, Guangxi, and Guangdong during the Warlord Era has largely been interlinked. Key events, such as Long Jiguang's deposition of Chen Jiongming, or Chen's deposition of the Old Guangxi Clique from Guangdong, the Guangxi Age of Heroes, or even Tang Jiyao's invasion attempt in 1925 contributed largely to a shared political dynamic. Most evidently is the case of Gu Pinzhen's coup against Tang in the early 1920s, seeing the eventual expulsion of large amounts of his followers into Liangguang - known as the Yunnan Army in Guangdong.\n\nIn 1925, after Sun's death, Tang suddenly reclaimed the title Vice Generalissimo of the Guangzhou Government, insisting that he was Sun's successor, and made a power play by marching on Guangzhou. In order to reach the city however, he had to enter Guangxi. First offering a polite threat to the New Guangxi Clique, he would engage in conflict with them, being beaten back, and losing power briefly in a subsequent coup by Long Yun.\n\nIn the aftermath of the Northern Expedition, Tang steered the Yunnan Clique inwards, albeit maintaining an eye on Guangzhou politics. However, he is not a man to make the same mistake twice, and in influencing Guizhou, he has ironically allowed New Guangxi to do it for him. Raiding through their shared border, Li Zongren's 7th Army has long yearned to return to their home province, seen as a rival by Zhu Peide and He Yingqin. In retaliation, the Guangxi generals have requested that Tang Jiyao increase his patrols within Guizhou and prevent further raids. An option not nearly as tantalising as maintaining the status quo.\n\nFor Tang personally, the best option to seize Guangzhou is to contact an old ally, Liu Zhenhuan. Having worked together in the 1925 invasion attempt, Liu has long desired the Governorship of Guangxi. A half-hearted ally to Federalism and democracy, perhaps Liu could prove the right player to finally wrangle Guangxi under the Federalist cause.
Dig up old channels to our friend Liu.
Perhaps Li may prove a useful pawn?
Our last bout in Guangxi nigh ruined us. Focus on Guizhou.
Liu Zhenhuan's Schemes in Guangxi
A noble and sincere revolutionary from a young age, Liu Zhenhuan has long been a regional power player within Liangguang. Aiding Lu Rongting, the National Protection Army, the Constitutional Protection Government, parleying with Sun Yat-sen, Chen Jiongming, Yang Ximin, Li Genyuan, and Tang Jiyao alike, he has long floated himself as an in-between, uniting revolutionaries of reform and revolution simultaneously. Elusively tied to the Political Science Clique in the North, he carries with himself a certain level of pompous middle-of-the-road liberalism, at least depending on the situation.\n\nSuffice to say, however, his reputation within Liangguang is far from perfect, with his soldiers committing numerous abuses a decade ago. In addition, his everlasting ambition for the Governorship of Guangxi has seen him ally with numerous contrasting allies in order to seize the position. First with Sun Yat-sen, and later Tang Jiyao alongside Chen Jiongming, Liu's scheme with Yang Ximin would be one of the last acts of regional resistance against the Kuomintang before the Northern Expedition. Following his defeat, Liu floated a half-hearted turn towards the Federalist cause and democratic activism, promoting the activities of the United National Construction League, and then the Federation of Chinese Democratic Parties.\n\nOnce again a local power player in the region, it would appear the ever ambitious Liu has reached out to Tang Jiyao yet again, and together have begun attempting to secure defectors amongst disaffected Guangxi Clique members. In particular, their presence has largely been felt amongst Lin Hu's garrison in Yulin - largely due to mutual Political Science ties, and association with Chen Jiongming. Albeit his influence has been disseminating across the province in general.
It is not like he succeeded the first time.
Defectors can be bought back.
Li Zongren Steps Up His Raids
One of the greatest military tacticians and strategists to have ever descended upon China in the Warlord Era, Li Zongren, commonly bundled together with his close friend and right hand, Bai Chongxi, has proven himself worthy time and time again for the revolutionary cause. Originally a gym teacher, Li would be emboldened by the duty and meritorious service present in the Chinese military. Seeking to transform its image from bullies, or a lowly career for glorified bandits, he would be thrust under the eventual employ of Lu Rongting, Governor of Guangxi. Rising through the ranks, he would first step onto the regional stage upon Lu's deposition, and the Guangxi Age of Heroes. Allying with Bai Chongxi, and fellow Lu subordinate Huang Shaohong, the trio would form the New Guangxi Clique - aligned with the Kuomintang in Guangdong through Bai Chongxi's connections.\n\nHaving maintained a moderate stranglehold on the province up until, and during, the Northern Expedition, the 7th Steel Army still posed a significant threat to Old Guangxi's power within the province, despite their utter ousting into neighbouring Guizhou. Acting akin to their own rival within the Guizhou Kuomintang remnant, they have largely struck out on their own, raiding Baise frequently and experimenting with their citizen-soldier model.\n\nWhilst usually kept on a relatively restrained leash due to fears of Governor Tang Jiyao of Yunnan finally crushing their administration in Guizhou, it would appear that the faithful Governor has turned a blind eye to New Guangxi's gallivanting across the border. Combined with the Kuomintang acting seemingly in eagerness to be rid of their \

Our Southern Neighbours
Governor Zhao is often defined by his friends to the North, however despite that he holds important ties to those in the twin Guangs, though his ties with Guangxi have faded with Lu Rongting's death. In Guangdong, however, there is an old ally of his in Chen Jiongming. During Chen's brief time in control of Guangdong as Governor, the two men worked together on the Federalist project, trying to negotiate unification with Wu Peifu. In the wake of the Northern Expedition, Governor Zhao decided not to join Chen's Grand Republican Alliance due to its underground nature, but has argued in its favour from within the Zhili many times.\n\nDespite having some old friends to the south, Hunan and its Governor haven't always gotten along with the twin Guangs. Due to Hunan's position between Guangzhou and Beijing, any north-south conflict would inevitably engulf Hunan in the flames of war, with the Northern Expedition having been the most recent example.\n\nHowever, the tide of history seems to be turning away from the Zhili, with Sun Chuanfang being incapacitated and the League embroiled in war, Hunan needs allies and friends. Chen Jiongming, who is using the chaos in the South to regain power, is exactly the friend Hunan may need. A man who believes in negotiation with the North, and a fellow Federalist just like Governor Zhao, Chen is our best option to prevent Hunan from being engulfed in war once more. That is, of course, assuming Zhao can convince the Assembly to allocate funds for such an extra-provincial affair.\n\nThere is another concern however, with small pockets of KMT control proliferating due to local support and their refusal to respect provincial borders. These pockets are being used to funnel troops and guns from MinGan and Guizhou into Liangguang. While not harming us directly, it may be best we use those funds to uproot the KMT in those regions instead of supporting Chen, given Governor Zhao's personal distaste for the KMT.
We should send our support to Chen, for Hunan's security.
We need to focus on ourselves, let the South sort itself out.
The Monroe Doctrine is in place for a reason.
Hunan aids a Federalist Resurgence?
In the early 1920s, Federalists Chen Jiongming and Zhao Hengti worked together as allies. However with Chen's expulsion from Guangdong and the Northern Expedition, the alliance has cooled notably, with Zhao refusing to outwardly endorse Chen's Grand Republican Alliance out of fear of Zhili reprisals.\n\nWith the cancellation of the 1933 elections however, Zhao found himself growing closer to his ideological allies in the South. Zhao Hengti and Chen Jiongming have maintained clandestine but increasingly open channels to one another, and as tensions rise in the south, Zhao has sent resources to Chen in order to help aid him in his attempts to secure Liangguang for the Federalist movement.
Chen sends a telegram to Zhao to thank him for his help.
Unfortunately for Chen, bandits seized those weapons before they could reach him.
Kuomintang Smuggling Operations in Hunan
The official Governor of Hunan, Zhao Hengti, has supported the Zhili and is personally aligned with the Federalist movement. Despite this, and the Kuomintang's loss in the Northern Expedition, insurgent cells and sympathetic figures keep control of a handful of counties in southern Hunan.\n\nIt is through these counties, especially with the help of the MinGan rebellion, that the KMT can smuggle weapons and men from Guizhou, Fujian and Jiangxi into the twin Guangs. Their hope is clear, that they wish to take control of the northernmost parts of the two Provinces, and use that as a staging ground to the south.
And through those trails, a northern base is established.
The supplies are stopped at the border and confiscated from their smugglers.
Chen Mingshu Requests Funds
Guangdong has long been the cradle of the Kuomintang revolution and has thus acted as the capital for various revolutionary governments, many of which were headed by the venerated father of the nation, Sun Yat-sen himself. As such, the loss of such a symbolic city has dealt a heavy blow to our ambitions to liberate China, but this new wave of chaos in the South may be our second wind in the region.\n\nEven after the debacle of the North Expedition and the KMT split, Guangdong still acts as the hotbed of revolutionary fervour. KMT and CSP rural cells continue to sabotage warlord infrastructure and repel local authorities. Likewise, the various unions in the city and its ports have been infiltrated by syndicalist agitators. Outside of Guangzhou's urban centres, Peng Pai has rallied peasant groups to the revolutionary cause. Meanwhile in MinGan, General Chen Mingshu, commander of the NRA 10th Division, advocates intensely for the reclamation of his home province.\n\nA political maverick of sorts, Chen's allegiance lies primarily with the KMT Left, finding allies within the Provisional Action Committee headed by Deng Yanda and Song Qingling. However, Chen also holds ties with the India Clique and Li Jishen due to the strong fraternal bonds of the 4th Army, whose officers all hail from Guangdong.\n\nRecently though, Chen Mingshu has been more adamant about his demands to go south and reports have reached high command regarding his impending departure for Guangdong over the following days. For such an endeavour, members of Chen's staff along with Chen himself have requested funds from the Central Committee along with permission to requisition some firearms from the provincial stockpiles for his units. The recent onset of our campaign against reactionary elements to the North however has rendered our capacity to aid General Chen tenuous at best and outright impossible at worst, but the ball is certainly in our court and our choice to make alone.
General Chen shall retake the revolutionary cradle!
We wish him good luck, but our hands are tied.
MinGan Reinforces Their Southern Flank
Ever since the Shanghai Uprising of 1932 and the further retreat of the Kuomintang from China's urban centres into the foothills of Jiangxi and Fujian, the establishment generals of Liangguang, and China in general for that matter, have considered them a spent force. But almost a decade of guerrilla warfare and rural agitation has proven the warlords wrong. With the collapse of the League, Kuomintang remnants in Guangdong, the home province of the KMT revolution, have only grown bolder in their strikes.\n\nOver the past few days, Chen Mingshu, the main commander of the Kuomintang on the Guangdong-Fujian border, has been observed by the regional garrisons of Meizhou and Shaoguan to be preparing to mobilise his men for an assault towards Guangzhou. Naturally, this news has spread southward, diffusing into the quiet hills and bustling streets of Guangdong alike. This spells further trouble for the tenuous hegemony that has held the twin provinces, as KMT agents have rallied to the cause, clashing with local authorities and bracing for the KMT's arrival. In Guangzhou, Mayor Chen Lianbo has ordered more rigorous patrolling in the outskirts of the city and officers of Shen Hongying's army take stock of their arms, preparing to weather the oncoming storm.
Chen Mingshu readies his men...
...but we will be ready for his next move!
The Kuomintang Receive Reinforcements
Much derided by the majority of China's power holders, and once thought to be nothing more than exiles languishing in Paris and Moscow, alongside obstinate guerrillas waging a futile death war against the ever slowly tightening noose of the encirclement campaigns, at least in MinGan, the Kuomintang appear to have found their opportunity. Staking it all, in a daring now or never strategy, they have pushed their fledgling units to their limits, motivated by revolutionary ferocity and sheer tenacity for a better China. With raids conducted in Guangxi orchestrated by Li Zongren and his 7th Army, they have been in conjunction with raids from MinGan, chiefly led by Chen Mingshu with support from Tang Shengzhi in Hunan.\n\nDoubling their efforts within the regions of Liangguang that they control, the call to action has rung true throughout their soldiers and martyrs. With the banners fluttering, their guns on their shoulders, their sabres at their waist, the blood of the revolution is blazing like a wild tide. Receiving extensive support, they have made preparations for a final daring operation to secure the birthplace of the revolution. Future struggles cast to the wayside, the National Revolutionary Army are following the warpath of their predecessors a decade ago.
Raise the Banner of the White Sun!
The Federalists Receive Reinforcements
Once thought to have been a relic of the past cast aside in light of Zhili and Fengtian's victories, along with the rise of the domineering Kuomintang, the Federalist cause in Guangdong had remained an undercurrent, supported by passionate intellectuals and gentry. Maintaining at least nominally aligned forces in Eastern Guangdong, their only true allies were those scant few voices in urban centres, alongside Governors Tang Jiyao and Zhao Hengti. Yet these ties would make themselves useful at the most opportune occasion. The Yue Army has managed to rebuff all opposition, reinforcing its hold within the Twin Provinces.\n\nReceiving support from its erstwhile allies in neighbouring provinces, they have redoubled their efforts, and entrenched their control. With confidence abounding, morale high, and men supplied, it was evident to most that the Federalist cause was on the recovery, perhaps even the upturn.\n\nConsidering its options, it has become clear that the time to press the advantage against the opposition was now. Establishing a communiqué with allied forces outside of the region, Chen Jiongming, Ye Ju, Ma Yuhang and others have launched an offensive against the Kuomintang and Old Beiyang forces, aiming to press them out once and for all.
Guangxi for the Guangxi People! Guangdong for the Guangdong People!
The Establishment Receives Reinforcements
Long the sustained rulers of the region, the Beiyang establishment forces (loosely centred around the Old Guangdong and Old Guangxi cliques) have managed to rebuff all opposition, and reinforce their present positions within the Twin Provinces. Receiving support from their erstwhile allies in Beijing and elsewhere, they were able to redouble their efforts to entrench their control and overcome the patchwork system of rulership they've established. With confidence abounding, morale high, and men supplied, it was evident to most that the Old Cliques were on the recovery, perhaps even the upturn.\n\nConsidering their options, it has become clear that the time to press their advantage against the opposition was now. Establishing a communiqué with Zhili forces outside of the region, Lu Yuguang, Ma Ji, Shen Hongying, Chen Lianbo and all other nominal subordinates were granted the official approval for an offensive against the Kuomintang and Old Federalist forces, aiming to press them out once and for all.
Stability and Reform rather than Radicalism and Revolution!
Our Efforts in Liangguang Quashed
Despite considerable investment on behalf of [ROOT.GetLeader], it would appear that our gamble within the Liangguang power struggle has ultimately been for naught. Lost in the miasma of warlords, both major and petty, our money and supplies found themselves in the wrong hands, or hardly leaving our territorial borders at all! With our efforts rebuked, it will take considerable time to regroup, and ultimately many within the administration have waned in passion for involvement within Guangxi or Guangdong, at least for the near future.
Let them fight amongst themselves.
A Shared Future for the Two Guangs
Liangguang today may be divided and resentful, but they share a considerable common history. The Viceroyalty of Liangguang, which literally translates to the Two Guangs (Guangdong means East Guang, Guangxi means West Guang), was first established in 1452 by the Jingtai Emperor of the Ming Dynasty. Both provinces toppled the Qing Viceroy in the 1911 Xinhai Revolution, but the following years saw different factions attempt to dominate the region.\n\nThe Old Guangxi Clique under Governor Lu Rongting of Guangxi, working with Political Science Clique aligned-Governor Cen Chunxuan of Guangdong, dominated the Constitutional Protection Government after driving out Beiyang-aligned Governor Long Jiguang and sidelining Sun Yat-sen. They attempted to negotiate with Beiyang in turn, but the poor behavior of their troops and fading political support gave an opening for Chen Jiongming and other Guangdong Army officers to raise an army in Fujian and drive out the junta, restoring Sun Yat-sen to power.\n\nThis led to the two Guangdong-Guangxi Wars, which Guangxi lost. The two Guangdong leaders then had a falling out, forcing them to retreat but in the power vacuum a Guangzhou aligned New Guangxi Clique junta emerged. After the Northern Expedition failed, Old Guangxi Warlords working with the Zhili dominated Liangguang but fought among themselves for power. In a particularly colourful incident in 1932, Lu Rongting ordered improvised bombers to attack Shen Hongying's forces in Guangdong. Meanwhile, the remaining Guangdong warlords and the Guangdong Provincial Assembly rallied around Mayor Chen Lianbo in Guangzhou.\n\nLu Rongting secured his hegemony over Liangguang in 1932, with Beijing, Nanning, and Guangzhou all recognising his position as Viceroy. With his death in 1934, his less competent sons have taken the reins. And now, Liangguang is divided and resentful, with many waiting for their chance to seize its future.
The future belongs to the heirs of Lu Rongting!
Sun Yat-sen's revolution continues to burn bright!
It is time for Chen Jiongming to get a fair chance to rule.
Liangguang Devolves Into Civil War
With Guangzhou in flames and news of the crisis quickly spreading up and down the river, Liangguang has been thrown once again into anarchy. Troops are being mobilised and militias raised as the three major coalitions - the Nationalists, the Federalists, and the Regionalists - gear up for battle. With the deep personal ties that connect the various figures in Liangguang, defections are expected but with the deep ideological divide between them, compromise is impossible.\n\nLiangguang is no stranger to political fragmentation but after the past few years of relative stability, there is no one who desires to return to a state of prolonged conflict. Therefore the fighting is expected to be fluid and chaotic. Momentum will be essential as local commanders will probably cast their lots with the winning side nearby, which will compound on a regional scale across the interconnected provinces.
May victory come soon.

Kuomintang Forces Make Random Gains in Guangdong
Federalist Forces Make Random Gains in Guangdong
Regionalist Forces Make Random Gains in Guangdong
As fighting criss-crosses the province, the battle for Guangdong has proven fluid. Armed bands of militia-men, mercenaries and insurgents battle for control over local hamlets and villages, while larger units of trained soldiers (and what passes for regulars these days) fight for cities and major roads. Little, unpredictable advantages slowly accumulate: a brilliant junior officer here, a fortuitous ambush there, an intrepid defence elsewhere. Their names will never reach the history books - some will not even be remembered as local legends - but these brave, nameless men and women can decide the fate of nations. The Kuomintang revolution has proven to be the benefactors of this randomness amid the fog of war, gaining ground through the blood of their martyrs.
As fighting criss-crosses the province, the battle for Guangdong has proven fluid. Armed bands of militia-men, mercenaries and insurgents battle for control over local hamlets and villages, while larger units of trained soldiers (and what passes for regulars these days) fight for cities and major roads. Little, unpredictable advantages slowly accumulate: a brilliant junior officer here, a fortuitous ambush there, an intrepid defence elsewhere. Their names will never reach the history books - some will not even be remembered as local legends - but these brave, nameless men and women can decide the fate of nations. The Federalist movement has proven to be the benefactors of this randomness amid the fog of war, gaining ground through the blood of their martyrs.

Liangguang's Morale Begins to Waver
Though divided by ideology and leadership, the people of Liangguang are united in their distaste for war on their soil. With a quick and commanding victory proving to be elusive on the battlefield, many have grown disenchanted with their leaders' slogans and exhortations in favor of practical realities on the ground. The hatred and bitterness from the fighting deters large-scale fraternisation between armed bands, the prospect of a negotiated surrender and possibly defection for better pay by soldiers who were neighbours not a few months ago has grown tempting. With many cities and towns growing frustrated by the need to maintain costly standing forces, calls for a quick resolution are growing and it seems like the war will be coming to an end soon.
Only the righteous and true will see this conflict through.
§rIf the conflict is not resolved in the next month, a penalty will be applied and the weakest faction overall will be eliminated and their resources distributed to the side they are most vulnerable to. The war will end one way or another within three months.§!
Regionalist Forces Lose Momentum
Federalist Forces Lose Momentum
Kuomintang Forces Lose Momentum
Truly multi-sided civil wars rarely last for long as most of the time wars tend to collapse into a more stable two sided conflict with minor additional actors. It would seem Liangguang has become such a case.
$GXC.introgame.210.d.base$ Though the war continues to rage, the hegemony Lu Rongting and his allies years piecing together has crumbled to dust. The stability and conservative reformism they have long promised but failed to deliver has found newer, more popular champions in Chen Jiongming and his coalition, with the young and largely ineffective Lu Yugguang being compared unfavorably to the older statesman Chen Jiongming. Likewise, the regionalism and provincial autonomy promoted by the Old Guangxi Clique has proven inferior to the patriotic federalism, which has drawn a larger crowd especially as the Old Guangxi Clique experiences battlefield defeats. The war will still be a fight to the finish, but now it is in the hands of the traditionally Guangdong dominated Federalist and Kuomintang movements.
$GXC.introgame.210.d.base$ Though the war continues to rage, Chen Jiongming and his Federalist alliance have effectively fallen out of contention. Federalism as a viable ideology has fallen considerably in the Chinese public imagination after the Zhili successfully conquered most of the south by force. Chen's move to fight against Zhili and Old Guangxi Clique domination had improved his fortunes, however defeats on the battlefield has largely sapped enthusiasm for his movement and most of his momentum has since gone to his more dynamic, radical rivals in the Kuomintang. The war will still be a fight to the finish, but it has become a dichotomy between the revolutionary Kuomintang and the reactionary Old Guangxi Clique.
$GXC.introgame.210.d.base$ Though the war continues to rage, the conflict has at this point become mostly defined as a continuation of the old Guangxi-Guangdong conflict as Lu Yugguang and his primarily Guangxi based forces battle Chen Jiongming and his primarily Guangdong centred alliance. Though Kuomintang, always an underdog, has once again been rendered largely impotent, with their cells driven underground and many of the New Guangxi Clique's forces being absorbed into the Old Guangxi Clique's ranks. The war will still be a fight to the finish, but it seems the Nationalists have taken their shot and missed.
One foe down, another to go.
Rival Forces Melt Away
With the two provinces exhausted by war and the winds blowing in their favor, their foes were forced to simply give way to the victorious invaders. The devastation of the conflict has rocked the stability of both provinces and the people have little appetite for further conflict. The same exhaustion that gave them their victory has robbed them of their chances of any further conquests in the meantime, as the apathetic locals turn towards rebuilding. The healing process will no doubt be difficult, and Liangguang may regret losing a step compared to their neighbours.
Lu Rongting spend years attempting to build a stable system in Liangguang, and it proved resilient amid Kuomintang and Federalist rebellions in the two provinces. Their enemies seemed to have withered away after months of indecisive seesawing, even if the Old Guangxi Clique failed to deliver a dramatic fatal blow to win the war. While enemy leaders have fled into hiding or exile, most of their loyalists returned home with their arms to guard their villages, even as they nominally accepted the new regime. For the few radicals willing to fight on as insurgents, they will simply have to hope that their comrades outside of Liangguang will come to liberate them soon.\n\n$GXC.introgame.220.d.base$
For the first time in over a decade, Chen Jiongming is master of Guangdong - successfully rallying a Guangdong dominated coalition to victory while promising a middle way between Kuomintang radicalism and Old Guangxi reactionaryism. The Liangguang Economic Outlook
In the aftermath of Black Monday, a collection of leading Liangguang scholars, economists, and bankers have presented their grim but honest report to [GXC.GetLeader], outlining the rapidly worsening economic situation in the two provinces. Heading the commission are leading educator and former diplomat Zhu Jiahua as well as bankers Zhang Gongquan and Chen Lianzhong, three of the largest names in Guangzhou politics.\n\nThe two provinces have hardly had time to recover from the fighting during the early warlord era, enjoying just four years of relative calm after Lu Rongting established his hegemony in 1932. Banditry and piracy was rampant, forcing local towns, gentrymen, and merchants to expand militias, draining local coffers and reducing commerce. With the cost of doing business rising and exports falling from the Great Depression, the economy was especially vulnerable to further downturns.\n\nBlack Monday has hit Guangdong's silk industry particularly hard, already struggling from the impact of the Great Depression on China. At the start of the decade, there were hundreds of silk firms employing over two million workers. Now, there are fewer than a hundred and all but half a dozen are on the verge of bankruptcy. The rapid decline in a traditional Chinese industry is seen by some as an indictment of the emotionless materialism of capitalism, and others as a symbol of Chinese backwardness.\n\nThe societal impact of Black Monday has also been severe. Average incomes in Guangdong (a comparatively wealthy province) have plunged to nearly a third of what they were in 1930 and are worsening. Families are said to be selling excess children and unemployed young men are joining bandit groups and \

The Issue of Military Consolidation
The outstanding victory of [ROOT.GetLeader] has snuffed out all dissent and potential rivals to our claim of regional leadership. With this great triumph, stability can finally be restored to Liangguang and the process of rebuilding can begin. Among other pressing matters currently is that of consolidating governmental power over our fledgling military. Whilst it received nominal training from Berlin, the Germans never thought of Liangguang's army as anything more than a colonial Askari to protect their Legation assets, and a broader anti-insurgency auxiliary for the central army. It is most pressing that we settle our military direction decisively, and with this in mind, we have three options to choose from.\n\nThere is, of course, the natural option of continuing to structure our army upon German lines. Furthering the concept of an elite core of units that would complement a broader, larger but less high quality army. Replicating German successes in the Weltkrieg, this would maintain stability within our own army and allow our forces to focus on preparing for the future.\n\nAlternatively, and perhaps unconventionally, our army could mold itself in line with the hit-and-run tactics of the Guangxi clans, institutionalising strategies deployed and used against the endemic, hardy bandits of Guangxi. To this end, we would transform our provincial army into that of a citizen-militia, blurring the lines between citizen and conscript.\n\nFinally, we could try to emulate the decisive victories produced a decade beforehand by recommissioning the concepts taught in Whampoa Academy. Featuring a smaller, devout and elite army built along Russian lines with concepts from Japanese Bushido and socialist revolutionary influence, the elite training of Whampoa could prove to be the crucial deciding factor in the future of [ROOT.GetLeader]'s government.
We build an army worthy of the Republic!
Fate of the Old Guangxi Clans
With [ROOT.GetLeader]'s success over his other rivals within the provinces, the consolidation of local power has continued. During the power struggle every contender had mustered their own forces to aid their claim, many of which have either fled or perished in the fighting. Most curiously, there have been reports of certain forces loyal to the Old Guangxi Clans approaching our commanders, requesting clemency in return for continuation of service. Known for their unorthodox tactics, the forces of Old Guangxi are resilient and enduring. However these requests for clemency are eyed suspiciously by our high command, as their disloyalty to their own cause has already been proven. Despite the contentiousness of accepting their offer, the knowledge, skill and connections their commanders possess could become integral to our success on the national scale.

To Unite the Peasants and the Army
A renowned Guangxi native and premier expert on waging war, Bai Chongxi had developed and theorised a unique set of tactics and stratagems during his time exiled in Guizhou. Through repeated stints leading an assortment of raids across the provincial border from Guizhou to Guangxi, he was finally able to concretely construct a new way of fighting conflict. Theorising that previous Chinese dynasties such as the Xia, Shang and Zhou had no professional armies, there existed no distinction between soldiers or peasants. This left the people and army to come into a union of one being. When war descended upon the populace all in the nation would take up arms and when peace returned, the troops would disperse to til the land. The Cadet's New Curriculum
Our premier military officers have developed a revolutionary new training programme, requiring many hours spent meticulously crafting an 18 month course for future cadets of the army.\n\nUpon entrance to the army at the average age of 17, recruits can be expected to deal with a curriculum of 33% military training, and 66% non-military training. The military side of the programme will include basic drilling, rifle training, light machine gun training alongside hand grenade training and battalion assault exercises. With this will also be a singular 3 hour night exercise for each week of the programme.\n\nOn the other hand non-military training will compose 12 months of the course, comprising of 120 hours each focused on agricultural science, law and economic reconstruction, 180 hours on political reconstruction, 100 hours on Sun Yat-sen's thought, 20 hours on the three self policies, 60 hours on general cultural reconstruction and 60 more hours on provincial regulations pertaining to primary schools, 60 hours on selected essays, 40 hours on teaching methods and additional short classes on diary keeping, holding discussions, giving awards and punishments, and various others subjects. Simultaneously, a preparatory course is offered for those who did not attend high school, in which 100 hours are dedicated to Chinese language, a separate 100 hours to history alongside geography, 80 hours to natural science and another 80 hours to maths, with 40 hours dedicated to citizenship.\n\nThe sheer scope and ambitions of the new programme has worried some within our Military High Command, with some key officers highlighting that the military portion is lacking emphasis, and that the non-military aspects are both expensive and make the programme more of a basic adult education rather than that for future soldiers.

The Influence of the Eagle
Since the dawn of foreigners setting foot in China, they've always maintained a consistent presence whether they were wanted or not. Whilst most of the spotlight remains on the negative aspects of foreign influence such as the treaty ports and occupations, there have been spurts of agreeable cooperation and mutual development to counter this. Many Chinese were able to be educated in Western universities, and industrialisation was bolstered by foreign machinery alongside their expertise. However with the recent chaos in Liangguang and in the broader world, many within our government are reconsidering our position and priorities into the future.\n\nAfter the first Weltkrieg, and partially due to the budding interests in the battlegrounds of China, the Reich had sent a plethora of German advisors to the region. Most were veterans of the Weltkrieg and were stubborn in that way of waging warfare, struggling to even conceptualise the realities of Warlord Era warfare. In order to accommodate for this, some of the even older personnel sent to China took inspiration from their experiences fighting colonial wars before the Weltkrieg. With such a mindset, a plan was developed that would be enacted throughout the whole of China: the Southern and Northern Zhili would both develop and cultivate an elite - yet smaller - 80 division plan which was to be supplemented by local warlords providing the equivalent of German Colonial Schutztruppe.\n\nWith the Chinese order all but shattered as of recent, key figures within our military have decided to emulate the aforementioned German plan, but with Guangdong at its heart. A truly premier core supplemented by auxiliary forces independent of Nanjing and Beijing, designed to finally reunite the Chinese people under one government - our government.
An Elite force for a new China!
Our Prospective Advisors
When it comes to foreign advisors and expertise, there is no shortage of help-for-hire to be found. The German Eagle hardly alone in trying to court Cantonese influence - the Japanese, Americans, and even remnants of the British Empire are all willing to lend a hand to whatever party can pay the right price. Whilst all capable have proud military histories that include expeditions to China, each option has increasingly evident strengths and weaknesses both politically and militarily.\n\nGerman influence is ubiquitous around the globe, and in China their guns, industry and personnel proliferate the cities. Having been the backers of the hegemonic Zhili factions in China, the Eagle was the kingmaker of the 1920s. However, since the Berlin stock market crash their influence has been receding and some believe that her feathers are fraying.\n\nVying with the Germans for influence, the Japanese have skyrocketed from feuding clans to a powerful empire, the only Asian nation capable of rivalling the Western powers. This meteoric rise could not have been done without their loyal and martial soldiers. Once considered a sister people, the Japanese provide the most accessible option, with a fierce reputation having successfully bested Europeans (and the Qing) before. Having eclipsed us in power, they have proven to be harsh neighbours and one would be wise to be wary of them in the future.\n\nFinally, the Anglo-American camp, a power that once stretched across the world, has had a storied history both within and without China. Their avaricious quest for Chinese markets proved to be the vanguard of China's humiliation, and other colonisers soon followed them. Their reign finally came to the end with shattering of the British empire and the American's catastrophic economic downturn. Despite past animosity, American exiles and British mercenaries flood into Liangguang through Hong Kong, and present a cheap option practically free of permanent foreign influence.
Go with the steadfast Germans.
Acquire Japanese expertise.
The Anglo-Americans have precedence.
Importing Equipment
Following our choice in maintaining foreign training and means of waging war in our armies, we have begun to slowly modernise our domestic equipment capabilities. To offset the gap however, we have begun to take a keen interest in the budding arms market, acquiring a multitude of contracts for foreign equipment. Individual arms dealers such as Hänschenklein Böcklin have made their presence known to us in Guangzhou, snapping up defence contract after defence contract. Whilst our ability to pay these sometimes ludicrous prices for equipment isn't easy, it could be the choice between winning or losing our future campaigns. Presently, there are a variety of arms plans available to us, including purchasing a small assortment of Anglo-American tankettes and armoured vehicles, buying German industrial production tools to aid our arsenals, or more practically we can acquire Japanese artillery and infantry equipment to supply our forces.
Acquire German Industrial aid.
Acquire Japanese arms.
Acquire Anglo-American vehicles.
It would appear nobody is willing to sell...
A New Academy Opens
The Whampoa Military Academy has a keen and storied history. Following the descent of China into warlord polities and chaos, Sun Yat-sen made multiple attempts to launch a Northern Expedition from his staging grounds in Guangdong. However, the fledgling KMT government remained militarily incapable of surpassing even the local warlords let alone Beiyang. To resolve this, the founding of a local military academy aimed at producing classes of revolutionary - and most importantly, loyal - officers to serve in the army, conforming to Dr. Sun's ideas. Concentrating all of the revolutionary military talents into one academy, the first commandant of Whampoa was the prodigy Chiang Kai-shek, with various other key figures such as Liao Zhongkai, Hu Hanmin and Wang Jingwei also serving in crucial roles at the academy. The cadets produced by Whampoa were some of the most fierce, loyal and capable soldiers to have ever descended onto the Chinese battlefield, willing to sacrifice everything and themselves for the liberation of the nation.\n\nWith this in mind our military high command has developed plans to replicate the tenacity and successes of Whampoa, reviving the concept of a smaller, higher quality and devout army to strike deep into the heart of our foes. With a primary influence from Red Russian and French advisors combined with aspects of Japanese Bushido, and now inspiration from modern Russian doctrine, the new Whampoa will produce revolutionary soldiers unheard of within China, bowing to no corrupt influence and fighting for the sincere future of the nation.
Fraternity, Devotion, Sincerity.

Foreign Arms Licensing
Whilst Liangguang's own arms industry is capable of producing a variety of small arms, as indicated by the capabilities of Guangdong Arsenal, the current capacity yielded by our domestic industries is rather lacking for our national ambitions of reunifying China. There is a growing fear that the arsenals of our rivals can yield far better equipment in larger quantities, no doubt with the help of foreign support. Therefore some of our military high command have suggested acquiring foreign contracts of our own to fill the gap.\n\nGerman arms have proven the test of time with their repeated victories in warfare and recent adoption across much of China since the restoration. Their competitors are the Japanese, who can produce Asian made armaments designed for regional theatres of war perhaps better suited for Chinese conflicts. Meanwhile, Anglo-American equipment has the benefits of being in surplus and inexpensive to acquire. Of course, there's always the ever present risk that these foreign arm deals aren't guaranteed to last forever, and so it might be wiser to support our domestic industry instead.
German arms won the Weltkrieg.
Japanese arms for Asian warfare.
Anglo-American surplus is plentiful, and cheap.
Prioritise our budding domestic industry.
Our Domestic Daredevils
Whilst not a completely foreign concept in Chinese warfare, the concept of a Chinese air force has never been truly realised. Despite this, multiple attempts have been made, from the Zhili to Fengtian, and more domestically, the various Guangxi and Guangdong warlords have all made their own versions of independent aviation programs. Most yielded very little actual impact on Chinese battlegrounds, with outdated planes and questionable pilots diminishing any effects they would have in battle. Despite this, the recent integration and combination of the various provincial aviation programs into one entity provides the blank slate needed to truly birth an admirable air fleet. However the various current air cadets are inexperienced, arrogant, and undisciplined - often owing their positions to being the sons of wealthy overseas Chinese families in the USA. Whilst an incredibly daunting task, some have suggested replacing the current array of pilots in order to acquire a more professional and experienced cadre, whilst others would propose that we merely tighten our standards and drill the current daredevils into proper condition.
Enforce Stricter Discipline.
Fire them all, start from scratch.
The Liangguang Air Force Programme
In our efforts to erect a sizeable and worthy air force that contest our domestic skies, we have decided to embark on an effort to attain foreign personnel with expertise in the air force. There has been precedent for such a move in Liangguang's recent history, with Guangxi warlords having recruited an assortment of British veterans supplied by the Far East Aviation Company in Hong Kong to train a fledgling air fleet. These British trainers were then partially replaced by Japanese military personnel after the Imperial collapse.\n\nMeanwhile, Guangdong warlords have prioritised purchasing American and British personnel and equipment for their attempts at an air force, though rising German influence has convinced some that it may be time for a change.\n\nHaving taken steps to consolidate power, [ROOT.GetLeader] feels confident enough to implement a most opportune reform: he has decisively concluded that the deal should only be conducted with a singular supplier, no middle men. He proudly argues that will save money and reduce corruption in the programme, while further bringing the air force under his control.
The Germans dominate the skies.
The Japanese may try again.
The Anglo-Americans have history.
Patrolling the South China Sea
For as long as anyone can remember, the South China Sea has been a hotbed of warfare and contested claims, stretching from before European influence reached into Asia til now in the current times. Recently, the South China Sea was contested between the Chinese on the mainland, the Japanese in Taiwan, the Americans in the Philippines, the British in Hong Kong, and now presently the Germans in formerly French Indochina. With all parties vying for total security and control, all have developed unique methods to contest the waters. Whilst we could never match any of the great powers in their attempts to control the sea, we can still attempt to secure contracts to produce a fleet utilising their methods of sea warfare. The Germans and their expert use of cruisers in their Asian fleet, the Japanese contesting the waters through Submarine warfare or the Anglo-American reliance on Battleships are all prospective options for our navy to adopt.
The Germans provide fresh ideas.
The Japanese know the seas well.
The Anglo-Americans have long dominated.
We must reinforce domestic efforts.

Bai heralded his new theory on warfare by arguing for utter mass mobilisation of the Chinese people.\n\nChampioning \
