Adult Education Women's Units
In our efforts to further the liberation of the Chinese people, we have directed our attention to the Chinese woman. Advancing the integration of the militia into Chinese society, each woman receives similar training to their male counterparts, with such egalitarian efforts being considered a prominent and permanent feature of the mobilisation programme.\n\nWhilst a nominal attempt at equality of the sexes, there are still numerous critiques brought up against the initiative. Men still receive a wider breadth of opportunities for employment and there are plentiful arbitrary rules about maintaining separation of men and women whilst in service. In a striking new step to try and further the programme, leadership within the party have suggested the concept of allowing women to also serve in various combat roles and duties alongside their militia work. Much discussion has been had over such a proposition, as the issue is multi-faceted, with some within the party applauding a noble step towards a truly egalitarian society, however others note that it may be a step too far, cautioning against such an expansion - with notably the progenitor of the concept, New Guangxi Clique, opposing such expansion, stating that the current implementation is worthy enough.
A worthy programme.
Expand it to combat roles.

Whilst intended to promote local self-governance, the Clique never intended for the militia they've cultivated to be independent like the warlord armies. The Hero of the 4th Army
In the age of the National Revolution, perhaps no army has contributed more to China's liberation than the 4th Army. Yet even in this assortment of heroes, one man stands above the rest. Cai Tingkai bravely stayed behind in Fujian following the necessary retreat of the Central Committee to France, awaiting a chance to ignite the Fourth Revolution. This chance came in Shanghai in 1932. The reactionary warlords Sun Chuanfang had bled Eastern China dry, selling out his dignity for German support.\n\nWhen the Chinese masses had had enough and launched uprisings in multiple major cities, Cai and his friend Jiang Guangnai wasted no time in sneaking into Xiamen and reaching Shanghai by boat. While there, Cai quickly took charge and personally fought against the encroaching League forces on the outskirts of Shanghai, organising the rebels into a more organised fighting force. Although unofficial, Cai and his men would be referred to as the 19th Route Army by the Central Executive Committee.\n\nWhen League troops under Qi Xieyuan finally entered the city, Cai and his men fought day and night on the streets in staunch resistance. When Qi came to the frontlines personally to observe the progress of his men, a stray bullet fired by Cai grazed his right arm. Despite the resolve of Cai, Jiang, and the 19th Route Army, Qi and the Jiangsu Army was eventually able to retake Shanghai and crack down harshly, leading to a general retreat into the mountains of Zhejiang.\n\nMaking their way down south, Cai and his men were able to evade Lu Xiangting's Zhejiang Army and linked up with the remnant NRA forces in Fujian under Li Jishen's command, who had the 19th Route Army remnants absorbed into the 4th Army. While the Shanghai Uprising ended in failure, Cai Tingkai and the 19th Route Army are forever remembered for their brave stand against German Imperialism and Beiyang despotism.
Three cheers for China's Foremost Revolutionary Hero!
The New Headmaster
During the heroic Northern Expedition and its harrowing collapse, many Whampoa cadets served and died. Among its fallen was its infamous Headmaster, Chiang Kai-shek, and with him the old academy dissolved. While many of its alumni retreated to Guizhou, others attached themselves to bandits or other republicans or retired to live quiet lives.\n\nDuring his tenure as Headmaster, Chiang made several attempts to increase the importance and magnitude of such a role within the revolution. Spurring on a flurry of deep interpersonal relations with the various cadets and faculty, he transformed it into a major political post that wielded considerable influence on the direction of not just the army, but the revolution as a whole.\n\nHaving revived this pivotal institution, the question of who to head it has emerged. While efforts have been made to curtail the role's influence, the competition for the position has nevertheless been fierce.
$GXC.earlygame.070.intro$ Two favoured candidates have emerged. General Li Zongren is the leader of the New Guangxi clique, a brilliant commander but with ambitions of his own. His rival is General Li Liejun, a renowned Southern revolutionary hero and veteran party member affiliated with the Western Hills clique.\n\nOther leading contenders have been excluded, Chen Jitang due to his governorship of Guangdong province while Bai Chongxi has withdrawn himself from candidacy to support the bid of his friend Li Zongren. Xu Chongzhi for his part has been appointed War Minister, and the party is reluctant to centralise military authority into one man to avoid another Chiang.\n\nThe final decision is sure to affect the future of Whampoa and the National Revolutionary Army's direction for the forthcoming wars, and so the final decision for Headmaster is...
$GXC.earlygame.070.intro$ Running off of the back of a relatively dire need for a suitable principal, or at least, an ideologically sound one, the race has fallen onto two candidates in particular.\n\nGeneral Ye Jianying is a prodigy emblematic of the new generation of Chinese soldiers, an associate of the late Chiang, albeit a leftist, he sports a past tenure as an instructor at Whampoa to reinforce his candidacy. His rival is that of General Cai Tingkai, a renowned hero of the Iron Army, a close confederate of Chen Mingshu, and one of the NRA's most skilled officers. Although his crippling lack of experience in the field of teaching has been his main drawback.\n\nOther contenders have largely withdrawn, or have been excluded, such as Chen Mingshu, or men such as Dai Ji, or Jiang Guangnai. The final decision is sure to affect the future of Whampoa and the National Revolutionary Army's direction for the forthcoming wars, and even that of the Liangguang sect's integration into the broader NRA. And thus, the final decision for headmaster is...

The Headmaster's Legacy
Chiang Kai-shek's reputation amongst the party is that of a troubled one, considered by some to be a failure with the right intentions, or an attempted dictator seeking to control the revolution, the failure of the Northern Expedition under his auspices has led to a disgraced legacy. Both those on the left and right of the party rarely praised Chiang's leadership, criticising his political machinations and his capacity to lead the National Revolutionary Army to victory. Having brewed a distasteful attitude towards him from most of the establishment within the party, many of our esteemed leading figures or commanders would share that negative sentiment. Yet for the lowly footsoldier or fresh cadet, the legacy of the first Headmaster is that of a positive one, a decisive leader whom would've successfully waged the Northern Expedition and revolution to a close had he not been taken away by assassination so soon. In fact, many of Chiang's old clique from within the party and army are spread throughout China, and his influence is still echoed in the halls of Whampoa to this very day. Truly some even argue that we should re-evaluate our perspective on the general, considering the Bushido concepts he had instilled in himself and his soldiers, producing a variety of soldier equal to none, his practical and pragmatic mindset enabling Chiang to make decisions bereft of ideological concerns, and the general loyalty to the revolution the Whampoa graduates exhibit. Embarking on the revival of Whampoa will require an official stance to be taken on the legacy of the first Headmaster, to set an example for all future cadets of the academy.
He was a wise mentor.
An attempted autocrat.

Clashing Personalities of the Guard
In an army as fierce, rowdy and ideological as the National Revolutionary Army, it is inevitable that disagreements and discontent blossom underneath the surface. Most disagreements are quickly snuffed out by their mentors, yet a very real conflict has begun to brew between the generals of our army.\n\nThe highly politicised nature of the NRA has allowed party grandees from the old revolution such as Xu Chongzhi and Li Liejun to retain considerable influence due to their experiences fighting the National and Constitutional Protection wars.\n\nYet for younger officers such as Li Zongren, Bai Chongxi and Chen Jitang, these old guard figures are hardly fit to lead a modern army. Of course these critiques are not without merit, Xu Chongzhi and Li Liejun have not led troops for a decade thanks to their long exile and even before that they were functionally sidelined by Chiang Kai-shek. The warlord officers claim to have a much stronger bond to Chinese warfare, having fought and bled on the local battlegrounds and honed local tactics.\n\nDespite this, Li Liejun and Xu Chongzhi's prior heroism should not be discounted and both have closely watched the development of the Russian army. Contributing to this dysfunction is the fact that few of our leading generals have a direct link to the old Whampoa, making them eager to entrench themselves in the new one. This disagreement in our methods has reached a point to which [ROOT.GetLeader] has had to deliberate on which side to favour, the reliable methods of the old guard or the unorthodox approach from the warlords.
Favour the veterans, Xu and Li.
New ideas for a new age.
The Soul of Whampoa
More than just a mere military school, the mythos and spirit of Whampoa develops in parallel to that of the revolutionary's own. Hand-in-hand, in complete and utter coherence, Whampoa advances bringing the revolution with it. The true bulwark of the national revolution, and a continued source of pride and strength for the Chinese nation, the cadets of Whampoa are a beacon of light in times of great darkness.\n\nThe continued development of spirit, dedication and camaraderie built in those very halls overcomes all odds opposing it, be it the opposing warlords, Germans or Japanese, nothing can stand in the way of the Whampoa alumni's march towards liberation. Spawning a multitude of close networks and relations for life, Whampoa cadets bond through triumph and tragedy no matter the situation and have maintained such bonds even during the tragedy of the Northern Expedition.\n\nThe new generation of Whampoa cadets have a bright future ahead of them, with the merit and lessons of the past generation imprinted onto the next. An opportunity has arisen in said generation, as we can focus our efforts on honing in on a particular revolutionary aspect for the cadets. Do we increase the Bushido influence in the curriculum, the Socialist Revolutionary influence, with their resolute devotion to the revolution, or perhaps a more broad expansion related to Chinese Nationalism, to further their true loyalty to the nation.
To make them true warriors, enhance the Bushido aspects.
Devotion to the revolution is absolutely required.
A Chinese soldier fighting for the Chinese nation!
Power Games in the Fleet
Despite the small size of the Guangzhou Navy, the disputes affecting it are unfortunately not of such a modest scale. Over time, Chen Jitang has become increasingly wary of Chen Ce, who has muscled his way into leadership of the fleet with the help of Chen Mingshu and other leftists.\n\nThis resentment has already led to the dismissal of cadets who were too close to Chen Ce, and the Governor has placed the Naval Academy under close surveillance. In order to control the Guangzhou Navy and further limit the commander's authority, Chen Jitang does not shy away from methods detrimental to the navy. It's becoming more and more common to see army officers as captains of the most important vessels, as the cases of the navy's newest ships show: four torpedo boats acquired from Germany and the Union of Britain two years prior were placed under the command of fresh graduates from the Guangdong Military Political Academy.\n\nThe command of the navy's most recent purchase, a smaller minesweeper, and of a river gunboat, built in the Guangnan Shipyard in Guangzhou, were given directly to Chen Jitang's brother, Chen Weizhou, a salt commissioner by profession. This has proven to be the last straw, with many sailors and officers affiliated with the left refusing to serve under Chen Jitang's appointees, in direct defiance of the Governor. Unsurprisingly, these petty squabbles have left their mark on the entire navy, and there's a long way to go if we want to get things back to normal.
Compromise with Chen Ce's loyalists.
Purge the navy of dissidents.
State of Affairs of the Naval Whampoa
Established under the zeitgeist of the Westernisation Movement following the Second Opium War, the Guangdong Naval and Land Academy would serve as the precursor institution to the modern day Whampoa Naval Academy. Under the authorisation of Zhang Zhidong, Governor of Guangdong and proponent of modernisation, the Academy was formed in 1887, and originally was staffed by three foreign teachers - one British, and two German. Divided into naval navigation and engineering students, the various students enrolled within the academy would come from across the nation. With the institution later split into Naval and Land schools, it would be renamed the Whampoa Naval Academy in the late 1800s.\n\nWith the addition of other institutions such as the Naval Industrial Academy and Naval Torpedo Academy, the curriculum would be revamped and the institution renamed to the Guangdong Naval Industrial Academy. With five years of preparatory courses and five years of undergraduate courses, graduates of the academy would be sent abroad thereafterwards to study further. Producing some of the nation's best naval officers, its subjects ranged from navigation, naval gun drills, and communications to mathematics, physics, and English. Renamed after Xinhai, it would see troubled finances in the Warlord Era, and ultimately be merged into the Whampoa Military Academy in 1924.\n\nIn 1930 local naval officers petitioned Mayor Chen Lianbo to reopen the institution following its closure after the Northern Expedition, but were refused. Now deeming it an appropriate time for the revival of the Academy, [ROOT.GetLeader] has been tasked with the appointment of the new institution's leading figure, its principal. Three options have presented themselves. There is the British educated Jiang Xiyuan, noted for his ties to Chen Jitang, the young guard alumni and graduate Li Qingwen, and finally the veteran, one-time Governor of Guangdong and Naval Minister Tang Tingguang.
Tang Tingguang should prove loyal.
Li Qingwen is a rising star!
The current principal is doing a good job.
A Comprehensive Study of the Art of War
Whilst China as a nation is perhaps particularly derided for the military strategies and tactics utilised in the Warlord Era — with notable incidents of vastly superior numbers being trumped by simple morale, or lack of coordination, and more — it is still a period of the nation's rich history wherein military science has advanced, and concepts proposed that rival even the greatest thinkers of the West. Perhaps most prevalent is that of the Social Darwinist adherence to Total War and considerations of the mobilisation of China's people for National Defence. One of the most famous adherents of such a philosophy being that of the late Cai E, and the iterations provided by the legendary Jiang Baili.\n\nYet the nation has borne numerous other strains of military theory, most notably Li Erkang's focus on science and modernisation to supplement insistence on Bushido and Warrior Spirit, and an emphasis on the tactical engagements of a battle, not the strategic mobilisation of a people for a war. Similarly there is the class of officers educated in the German theories of war, advocating the mobilisation of industry, or insistence upon officer initiative to win a war. Or even that of the Kuomintang, such as Bai Chongxi's Citizen-Soldier propensity, Zhu De's Guerrilla Mobile War, or Yang Jie's \
Our men march under great minds!
Composition of the Yue Army
Under the proposed Federalist Chinese project, the army has always been a matter of much debate. Provincialism inherently clashing with the concept of a national army, with proposals for provincial armies that constitute one national army considered fruitless due to inherent inefficiencies. Perhaps a symbolic microcosm of such a structure is the inherently feudalistic nature of the present Yue Army. Even before its reconstitution following [ROOT.GetLeader]'s return and victory over the other warlords of Liangguang, the Yue Army was similar to the majority of other military compositions within China. A disparate gathering of conscripts and professional soldiers all tied to their commanding officers as opposed to a national idea or concept.\n\nOf course, such an assortment was not out of the ordinary for the time, with units such as the National Revolutionary Army with an adherence to ideology and Sun Yat-sen being standouts from the norm. As indicated by the American Consulate, Chen Jiongming's army of the time consisted of 30,000 New Army Soldiers, 20,000 patrol guards, 2,000 police officers, all locally recruited. And in the present, not much has changed, or rather, perhaps it has deteriorated.\n\nPresently the Liangguang Armies are a composite force comprised of a mixture of warlord forces; ranging from auxiliaries attributed to the likes of Hong Zhaolin, the mixed forces of Liu Zhenhuan, Ye Ju, Yang Kunru, or even the comparitively elite forces recruited from Old Guangxi's divisions. With such a context in mind, and with redoubled efforts for military modernisation, a rather perplexing dilemma has been raised to the high command. Should they seek to reorganise and condense the military forces of their erstwhile allies, potentially incurring their wrath or insurbordination? Or should they focus on applying modern methodology and organisation only to new units, leaving the warlords their independence.
Streamline the division list for coherency.
Parleying with the warlords ensures their compliance.
National Reconstruction and the Military Arm
The struggle between an equal balance between the military and civilian spheres has undoubtedly been a vocal, violent, and vicious one within the Warlord Era. Warlords of local polities act akin both to Marshals and Presidents, with some in the upper echelons even carrying both positions legally. This has of course, raised much controversy, and there exists the ever continuing march towards nominal delimitation of suit and sword. Most prevalent within the Federalist movement would be the positions of Governor Zhao Hengti and Governor Tang Jiyao, who both liken themselves to elected civilians, not generals leading an army. And the case is no different with Chen. Preferring to act in a civilian capacity — no less due perhaps to his poor military record — he has left most military affairs in the hands of Ye Ju, his Commander-in-Chief.\n\nHowever during Chen's time as an active commander his greatest successes can be attributed to the conduct of his military, albeit not on the field. Instead Chen's successes emerge from his army's national reconstruction endeavours, such as in the Fujian Campaign. During his time in Shantou, he had set up an arsenal in Shanwei, established an extensive network of military supply stations, and a county road building programme — to facilitate both military and commercial transportation, and organised local militia and guerrilla units to suppress banditry.\n\nAnd it would appear, in much the same manner, that Chen's army are engaging in such national reconstruction efforts yet again. Albeit, unlike in Fujian, Ye Ju has seen more stubborn resistance to his attempts to prepare Guangdong and the local region for national unification. Both gentry and peasantry have not taken particularly kindly to the interfering of the military in their daily matters, even if only to solicit aid for provincial reconstruction. As such, complaints have reached [ROOT.GetLeader], insisting that he \
The people do not know what is best for them.
The military must avoid interference in state matters.
Vestiges of the Past and the Success of an Institution
With the ironic refounding of Whampoa under the pretenses of its original enemies, the Federalist movement and accompanying unreliable warlords, the Federalist army has entered into something of an odd situation. Whampoa was famed for producing perhaps the best military force in the whole nation. Their victories were many, such as in the First Eastern Expedition where the untested and swiftly trained Whampoa cadets managed to forge their reputation with blood in the Battle of Mianhu.\n\nFurther adding to the irony, the First Eastern Expedition was originally supervised by Yang Ximin and Liu Zhenhuan alongside the Whampoa Cadets and the Pro-Sun Guangdong Units, yet in the interim after the First Expedition, the former duo would revolt and defect, only to be utterly defeated by the infant National Revolutionary Army. In the Second Eastern Expedition, the Guangdong Army would almost kill Chiang, only to again be defeated, forcing Hong Zhaolin and Lin Hu into Fujian. Following the expulsion of Deng Benyin, the Pro-Chen forces in the region were utterly annihilated. And yet here they have returned to power, under new and old hands alike.\n\nWhilst palpably the turn of events is not lost on anyone within the high command, there still remains the issue of staffing the new Whampoa with proper faculty aligned to the values of the nation's new army. The position of principal has the capacity of immeasurable influence, and so has been jockeyed by numerous military cliques for the acquisition of the position. The three most viable contenders started with Lin Hu's candidacy. Moderately loyal to Chen, but a force of his own right, Lin's ties to the Old Guangxi Clique raise him as an object of suspicion. Secondly there is Wei Bangping, a man who remained neutral during the Sun-Chen power struggle, but found himself shuffled out of the Nationalist's ranks, and notably never joined the KMT. Lastly, there is Yang Ximin, making another bid for some influence and power.
Lin Hu can teach from the lessons of Mianhu.
Grant the position to Wei Bangping.
Yang Ximin has proven most resilient.

Zhang Junmai and the Politicised Army
With increasing efforts to reform, restructure, and modernise the military, many of the government's initiatives have received some spotlight attention in the media. Most recently there has been the rather heavily publicised case of Zhang Junmai, and his writings on the merits and faults of both the present reform efforts for the Federalist military, and the legacy of the National Revolutionary Army of which the former aims to emulate. Starting his piece, published in the party periodical, Zhang addresses the present situation of the once famed and renowned National Revolutionary Army, considering its downfall on the conclusion of the Northern Expedition not to be due to lack of merit, or inherent weakness in its organisational structure, but with German Intervention.\n\nConsidering the circumstances, Zhang even hypothesises that without Germany's landing at Guangzhou and the utter failure of the 1st and 4th Army to alleviate its capture, perhaps the Kuomintang would've managed to unite the nation. Praising the NRA for its great successes in triumphing against the majority of the warlord forces, Zhang draws up the strengths of the NRA to be in the heavily politicised academic curriculum in Whampoa, the subsequent absolute dedication of Whampoa cadets to the party's ideology, and its immeasurable popular support and discipline. Of course, Zhang levies none of these compliments onto the Kuomintang itself.\n\nAdvocating for increased politicisation of the army, and the construction of true adherents to Democratic ideology, Zhang's periodical has met much praise in ex-Kuomintang circles, yet much debridement in the cliques of the Old Federalists. The most notable opponent of such an initiative is none other than the core of the Yue Army, Ye Ju and his associates. Stipulating that such a move could result in an army holding the Republic at gunpoint for some sort of lofty ideal, or even a similar cult to that of the Guofu based around Chen Jiongming.

The Colourful Cast of the National Defence Army.

Ranging from Federalist loyalists, to Guangdong Army compatriots, Guangxi aficionados, and everything in-between, it is truly a motley force joined not too dissimiarly to say, the Zhili or Fengtian Cliques. United by bonds of mutual ties, loyalty, or otherwise financial investment, they nonetheless have fought side-by-side.\n\nIncluding a number of old-guard revolutionaries, the Federalist army sports the likes of the well-respected Wei Bangping. Role of the Zizhijun Model
With the reorganisation of the military on uniquely Guangxi origin doctrinal philosophy, there have been many attributions to the Old Guangxi Clique's Zizhijun model of division, wherein local guerilla militias would be supplemented by regular formations that retained enough mobility to swiftly avoid battles if necessary. Such an innovation has retained Old Guangxi's strength in the region even when they've been utterly forced out, a notable case being the rapid return of Lu Rongting following the National Revolutionary Army's collapse. Effectively acting as a precursor to the novel Citizen-Soldier militia model of the military's new doctrine, many of the Guangxi native commanding officers, especially the likes of Lin Hu, have advocated for a modernised take on the organisational model.\n\nRunning in contrary to this are the novel, westernised, and comparitively elite forces commanded directly by Lu Rongting and Ma Ji, otherwise known as the 1st Division, and the Model Battalion respectively. Focused on tactics learnt from the West, namely the German Military Mission, they have retained a serious edge on much of the local competition, albeit not without issue. With certain difficulties in supplying the 1st Division in Guangxi's largely rural environment, they have found the most usage on the few tracked roads of the interior, or otherwise urban centres.\n\nOf course, the Zizhijun model has its own critiques, notably those of the professional officer corps, who insist upon trained soldiers rather than county militia to form the bulk of the Liangguang military. Many of those aforementioned voices arise most prominently from officers not native to Guangxi, such as within Guangdong, who find no attachment to the Guangxi way of warfare.
Mobility and bravery can beat all odds!
The Model Battalion shall be retained as a shock unit.

Tasked with transforming a disparate force of hodgepodge warlord conscript forces into an army to rival not only the Japanese trained units of the Fengtian Clique, but to be comparable to a modernised military force. Auftragstaktik, and the Military-Industrial Victory
The deliberation of tactical and operational authority to the junior officers leading their soldiers in the field has long been proved to be the dominant form of warfare in the era of the German Empire, albeit not an entirely original theoretical concept belonging to them. It has seen adoption, construction, or utilisation in various militaries around the globe, and similarly China brought forth its own form of Mission-Type Tactics, notably within the Huguojun of the National Protection War, or the National Revolutionary Army.\n\nWhilst the aforementioned military forces preferred revolutionary spirit, bushido, isolated 1-on-1 engagements combined with manoeuvre warfare, the adoption of such a doctrine within the German-trained sphere of the Chinese military has largely come under the banner of the larger doctrinal dispute within the German high-command. As the various officer sent to train Chinese commanders and their soldiers bring with them different interpretations of the evolution of Germany's manoeuvre warfare. The Rice Glut
The aftershocks of Black Monday continue to reverberate across Liangguang. While the government struggled to put a lid over the initial crisis and the people managed the best they could, the disaster laid bare many of the core issues festering within our agriculture sector. Perhaps most concerning is the horrific underutilisation of provincial land and fixation on cash crop development which has compounded into Guangxi one of the most economically deprived provinces of China.\n\nThere is no place where this is more apparent than in the current rice glut plaguing the province, where in a tragically ironic turn of events some villages suffer from famine while others a glut of un-exportable rice. The certain cause of this crisis seems to be the poor quality of roads within Guangxi. For many years there existed but one motor road between Nanning to Wuming, said to lead directly to the personal residence of Lu Rongting for his own consumption.\n\nIn the post Northern Expedition environment, Guangxi authorities had worked to build 1000 miles of new highway whilst also operating several new bus lines from Guigang to Nanjing to Wuzhou. These subsequent road building initiatives had great promise but most have been mismanaged, unfunded, or generally inconsistent in implementation. Even now, many of our districts and villages remain disconnected by road, still relying upon an inefficient yet intricate system of inter-village ox, horse and handcart paths to help peasants move their grain from motor roads to distant markets.\n\nAll of this has compounded into the sluggish transport of vitally needed grains from the fields to the tables. [ROOT.GetLeader] and others within the government promise this year will be the last agricultural crisis the people will ever see, swearing they will ensure the completion of the long-promised highway constructions programs and moreover that this is but the first step in a permanent solution to the rural plight.

Youth Education Programme
Despite Liangguang's comparatively modern appearance and influx of wealth, education of the populace has remained a difficult mountain to climb. Despite the formation of modern educational methods within newly funded schools, these efforts remained largely directed towards urban areas and the metropolitan population. To truly bring Liangguang into the 20th century, we must strive to not only enlighten those within our cities, but simultaneously those within the rural areas - particularly in Guangxi. However, both funding and teachers are in drastic short supply and yet illiteracy seems likely to persist another generation.\n\nTo remedy this, some radical if unfailingly down-to-earth leaders in Guangxi have advocated for a completely new multi-stage approach, away from the traditional approaches of the intelligentsia. Firstly each village and ward will be required to turn one of their local temples into a primary school, or otherwise build a new one with community contribution. This will broadly expand education opportunities to the masses, as every settlement will have the capacity to teach their young. Secondly a new secondary school system will be established to offer a parallel academic curriculum to that of traditional secondary schooling. Instead of academic subjects aimed at preparing students for elitist universities, this new system will provide a more practical set of courses and subjects. Three year courses covering the sciences, including social sciences alongside natural sciences. This would simplify the mostly literature biased subject matter of current secondary schools and produce generations of future workers capable of progressing in the world.\n\nWhilst there are pragmatic voices within our government that raise valid issues such as the lack of teaching staff, or overriding the nurturing of future intellectuals, other officials are aware of the dire situation in the provinces, and see that decisive action is needed.

Guangxi's Crossroads
Straddled between Yunnan and Guangdong province, Guangxi province has a history stretching back as far as Chinese history. Sometimes called the Mountain Kingdom (shan-guo), mountainous ranges sprawl throughout the entirety of the province. Because of this, the province is home to many unique subcultures isolated by their unforgiving surroundings. Sporting only a few rich agricultural areas such as Yulin, Guangxi is one of China's poorest provinces, with the only consolation being its natural beauty. Indeed, Guangxi's beautiful landscape attracted many writers and artists, garnering a reputation for unorthodoxy.\n\nCoalescence of mountain poverty, apathy from Beijing, and a tradition of liberty with a heterodox messianic ideology created the spark for a conflict deadlier than the Weltkrieg, the Taiping Rebellion. Guangxi was the first of many provinces to be swept up by revolutionary fervour of the Heavenly King. Though Hong's crusade ended in disaster, Guangxi remained much the same: a province strife with poverty and riddled with banditry. Some of these bandits and former Taiping fighters crossed the border into French Vietnam, creating headaches for Paris for the latter half of the 19th century.\n\nThe opening of the west river jolted the province from its isolation into a new world of republicanism and militarism. Today, Guangxi's navigation and commerce is heavily dependent on rivers due to a lack of railroads, with 95.5% of the province's exports flowing downstream to Hong Kong and Canton. The economic woes of the province have not gone unnoticed, with one of the goals of the three-year plan being ending Guangxi's poverty. However, there are disagreements on why poverty exists in Guangxi. The prevailing theory among intellectuals is that deep-seated economic inequality is the cause, however many commercial leaders have argued that the true cause of the poverty is the province's failure to utilise available arable land and its vast mineral riches.
A fascinating question of the day.
Roading Building Competitions
With the Guangxi reconstruction project in full swing, we are already experiencing its boon. The most successful part of the project is the infrastructure initiative, which has seen so many roads built in Guangxi that it is now third only to Guangdong and Shandong in terms of numbers of roads. One downside though is that most of the construction benefited military needs of the government instead of the economy. In conjunction with the Guangxi infrastructure project, the Guangdong government has built the first major highway network system in Guangdong. These roads have helped greatly with our efforts in curtailing banditry throughout the two guangs, to the delight of the locals.\n\nThe government has also taken to organising road-building competitions. In the Yongning Experimental Subdistrict (Yongning Shi Yan Chu), various village militia units compete with each other on road building projects. The results have borne great fruit for our efforts in transportation modernisation. Now, the people of Guangxi can avoid having to use slow boats on rivers to travel into the interior. Guangxi rivers are notoriously unreliable, dropping around 50 to 60 feet in the west during the winter, only to rapidly grow to great heights in mid March and peak by June.\n\nDespite all this progress, the people have seen little change to their financial circumstances. Corvee labour and unemployed men provided much of the working force for the Guangxi reconstruction project, and many suffered from abuse. New surveys indicate that desperate poverty and backwardness is still rampant, and although the Guangxi government is aware, only time will tell if they intend to remedy the situation with drastic measures.
We will improve conditions for those poor workers!
Those men should be thankful to receive any work at all!
Granary System
The current Guangxi credit system, much like the province's terrain, is coarse, rough, and irritating. Guangxi farmers have to compete with the economic power of grain wholesalers, giving them little control over market price. Wholesalers could buy at harvest, wait until spring and sell for double the price. Typically, there are three types of credit arrangements. The first one is the borrowing of cash. However, cash loans are exceedingly rare. Another credit arrangement was the selling of agricultural futures. However, the most common type of credit arrangement was the borrowing of grains, typically seed grain. Regardless, the general consensus among farmers is that the interest rates are too high - some 20-50% per year. Six year loans charge a full year's interest at 30%.\n\nIn an attempt to control supply, the local government had tried to set up a granary system that bought grain during times of glut and sold during times of scarcity. In today's Guangxi, this system is archaic and poorly run. Provincial regulations stipulated that if a person wished to sell land, they first had to try to sell it to the village government. Granaries were supposed to be built near road systems, but encountered three major stumbling blocks.\n\nFirstly, the government lacks the funds to support such a system, as much of the allocated funds are redirected towards military spending. Secondly, the system lacks trained personnel to maintain the system, in part due to the low funding. Thirdly, the system is not properly understood by the general population of Guangxi, so they do not partake in the system. Perhaps in the future Guangxi will have a fair system of grain production, but today it remains only a far flung hope of the idealistic.
We will begin improvements on the Granary System immediately.
The system is ancient, we should allocate resources to better projects.

Similarly, the glorious revolutionary struggle waged by Song Qingling, Deng Yanda and the rest of the MinGan Insurgency was not the sole source of discontent against despondent warlord rule, for there were parallel hubs of radical peasant agitation throughout China.\n\nAfter the tragic catastrophe that was the 1932 Shanghai Uprising, the burgeoning Chinese Socialist movement was critically wounded, limping towards retreat and seclusion, in an effort to regain strength and numbers. Trade Fair in Liuzhou
When China came into increasing contact with the west, the Imperial Government in Beijing saw fit to enact a policy known as the Canton System for the purposes of controlling trade with the west. This occurred by funnelling all trade to the city of Guangzhou, leaving all trade in the city being controlled by Chinese merchants. These merchants became known as Hangs (Hongs), operating their trading enterprises from a neighbourhood outside Guangzhou known as the Thirteen Factories. During the reign of Qianlong, the Hangs were sanctioned as a monopoly known as the Gongxing (Cohong), after which the Guangdong Customs Supervisor (Hu Bu/Hoppo) and Viceroy of the Two Guangs supervised foreign trade under the Gongxing.\n\nThe Canton System was abolished upon the signing of the Treaty of Nanjing, which would see China open up trade with the west. Although this marked the end of the Cantonese System officially, Guangzhou remained as an important trading port. The city has retained economic relevance all the way through the latter half of the 19th century, and even past the Xinhai Revolution. In 1929, the Guangxi government opened a trade fair in Liuzhou, a city bordering the Pearl River. A Bureau of Exports was also established with 500,000 Yuan, balancing exports and imports. With the Three Year Plan in full swing, many have begun to debate the trade policies which would be best for the two provinces. Some say that the province should focus on exports, which would continue Guangzhou's trade dominance in the region. Others have theorised a new more autarkic future for the province's future in order to prevent western imperialists from leaching from our natural wealth.
The future of Canton hangs in the balance.

Guangxi Tariff Plan
In order to protect the fragile Guangxi economy, the New Guangxi Clique has emphasized self-sufficiency and petitioned their Guangdong counterparts to aid them in their quest for balancing the trade deficit. Of particular importance to the landlocked province is the agricultural sector, which make up the bulk of their exports, though they have also been keen on cultivating a local industrial base as well.\n\nA trade bureau has been established in Wuzhou to regulate imports and enforce quality control on exports. Heavy tariffs have been implemented on many imports and in some cases (like luxury items) they have been outright banned. Efforts of foreign concessionists to enforce treaty stipulations have been largely bypassed by central government inaction and also heavy taxation on Chinese distributors who sold foreign goods, causing outside cigarettes and alcohol to disappear from the shelves.\n\nIn order to bolster domestic production, protectionist economic policies have been mixed with private-public joint-enterprises that have provided tax concessions and subsidies to these businesses. Much of this programme has been funded by the mass export of tung oil, which makes up nearly a third of Guangxi's exports along with mining products. Every household in Guangxi has been ordered to plant ten tung seedlings per able-bodied man every year, with county tung committees ensuring compliance.\n\nNot every sector has been as thoroughly controlled as agriculture and industry however. Critics of this heavy-handed government intervention point out that the mining industry laments under such management, despite comprising a substantive part of regional revenue. The government has previously taken to a lighter hand, selling licenses to private companies for tin and manganese as well as largely leaving the rest to private ventures. Many have suggested modelling further economic reconstruction around this model instead.

Rebuilding Finances
As China marches on past the Zhili decade and chaos once again returns to the shores of the Middle Kingdom, Guangdong finds itself a modicum of stability with Governor Chen Jiongming's return to power. Chen's first reign as governor of Guangdong saw him establish many complex political programmes to promote his ambitions, and the Governor has wasted no time in working to rebuild the old order. For any warlord regime in this tumultuous period, having stable finances is a cornerstone of success, and Chen has made securing his financial situation top priority with a number of financial reforms. Much government bureaucracy has been streamlined and slashed in the name of efficiency and the audit department has been restored.\n\nChen has quickly extended a hand of cooperation to the Guangdong Provincial Bank, an institution established by him to help the transition from Imperial Taels to Republican dollars back in 1910. In order to secure stability for banking notes, Chen Jiongming has once again signed an agreement with the Hong Kong Banking Corporation, though this has caused notable unrest in the population. Chen has also called upon Chen Xiru to help him secure foreign loans from a slew of powers and openly published the agreements, a first in Cantonese history. Kuomintang agitators greatly protested the move, denouncing Chen as an agent for foreign interests.\n\nIn times past, Chen could count on the financial reliability of the British Government for support, but with their fall, the Federalist Governor finds himself sorely lacking in foreign backers. Without reliable foreign aid, Chen has increasingly considered cutting military spending, though such a move would undoubtedly cause unrest in the army, whose ranks already house many unreliable actors. Irrespective of military spending, Chen has also reformed the salt administration to increase revenue, which he plans to use to repay his government's loans.

Excising the King of Vices
One of the main hallmarks of Chen's first governorship was his total ban on gambling in 1920, met with cheers from the populace. Chen's administration was very strict on any attempt to set up a lottery, with the Governor forcing everyone to vote publicly and castigating everyone who supported it as being bought out. Later on in his first governorship, he formed the General Association for the Suppression of Gambling. The General Association established the death penalty for gamblers, closed brothels, and established heavy fines for opium. Although prohibition was never abolished even after Chen was forced out of Guangdong, it was really strictly enforced except when he was governor.\n\nWith Governor Chen back in charge and his financial reforms in full swing, he has wasted no time in restoring gambling prohibitions. China's Plunge into Modernity
One of China's most famous exports, silk, has recently been under increased scrutiny from the Governor. Hoping to promote the silk industry of Guangdong, Governor Chen has ordered all counties in Guangdong to establish a forestry management office, and reestablished the inspection office for raw silk and a Centre for the Production of Seed Silkworm. On top of silk, Chen has also looked to promote coffee, rubber, and tropical fruit farms. Recently, he had a canning factory opened in Guangzhou, and has seen to establishing Whampoa as a deep water port once again.\n\nChen even hopes to establish a major stock exchange in Guangzhou, inviting Chinese merchants from Hong Kong, Shanghai, and Guangzhou to participate in developing one. Formerly in disrepair in the decade of anarchy, Chen has restored the Industrial and Commercial Products Exposition. In order to police the growing commerce in Guangdong, he restored the authority of the Association of Chambers of Commerce of Guangdong, which he first set up in March 1922.\n\nOne of the dreams of the Governor is to establish a provincial railway system, and he has seen to the continual development of previously built highways from Huizhou and Zhangmutou and set up the East River District Highway Authority. In order to modernise the Canton-Hankou railway, he has sought out the Germans for another loan. On top of railroad modernisations, he has seen to the modernisation of Guangzhou itself. Many old walls have been torn down, and new business centres and public parks are being built in their wake. The Guangdong people will truly remember this as a period of prosperity.

Ending the Age of Anarchy
To avoid the utter chaos of the decade of anarchy once again, refining the administration has been top priority for Chen Jiongming's government. His regime has already outlined several plans to streamline the provincial government, restructure the county governments, and implement a system for evaluating the performance of county magistrates.\n\nChen laid out similar plans during his first governorship, but now with Guangdong fully under his control, he may finally enact his full vision without Kuomintang or Guangxi reprisal. In order to stabilize the paper currency, improve tax collection, control expenditure, and achieve a balanced budget, Chen has continued his post Sun-split strategy of courting the gentry and elites, so that they may collaborate with Guangzhou on matters of finance.\n\nOf course, no stability can truly be achieved without enforcement or the support of the peasantry, and Chen has seen to it that the Department of Industry adopt a new economic policy to investigate ways to develop the mining industry, put idle land into cultivation, promote the industrial arts to provide employment, and cooperate with local farmers. On the matter of public security, Chen has seen to the immediate extension of the police force to every corner of the province and established police academies to train qualified personnel.
May Guangdong remain ever stable!
Washington's Favoury
With Governor Chen's attempts to establish Guangzhou as a friendly port to all traders, many Americans have found themselves in the city, some for commercial opportunities, and others simply to escape from the chaos back home. Although Chen has not publicly declared his government to be inspired by the American Federal System, his administration has increasingly gained the reputation of being Americophilic, with many American journalists noting such. Conversely, his government is also regarded as being hostile to the Franco-British Syndicalist governments which funded his Kuomintang rivals.\n\nHis government is also noted as being increasingly hostile towards Tokyo despite the presence of pro-japanese figures such as Chen Lianbo in its higher echelons, no doubt a product of increasing Japanese influence in Dongbei and an increase of tensions across Asia. Although the United States has fallen into anarchy not too dissimilar to Guangdong's dark decade following the Northern Expedition, Chen has found American merchants and banks to remain fairly reliable and friendly, and many expect the US to find a friend in Guangzhou once the dust settles back home.
We in China, like you, want a better world and we must have it!

Education by a Social Democrat
An educator at heart, Zhang Junmai has spent most of his career teaching. The National Institute of Political Science which he set up was founded in 1924 with the help of local Zhili governors and figures including Sun Chuanfang, and staffed its faculty with many foreign educated staff. Zhang's philosophy was to achieve a middle ground between emulation and rejection of Western education. While at the National Institute, Zhang declared that there were two ideas in political philosophy, being government by men and government by law. To him, China mastered the first while the West mastered the second. Zhang argued that China must seek a middle ground in the two for its new government system, as neither will work on its own for the reorganisation of China.\n\nFor the goals of the National Institute, he adopted the policies of cultivating the ability to organise in its student body and an objective study of Western political systems. To promote organisational skills, he looked to teach students self-governance by granting them a measured degree of autonomy. To teach western political thought, he established four departments of politics, economics, sociology, and international relations. To him, the National Institute's aim was not only the teaching of knowledge, but \

Free Business in a Free China
Guangdong has long been a haven for businessmen and merchants, and Chen Jiongming's government has no shortage of business interests. General Weng Shiliang, whilst initially an officer in Chen's Guangdong Army, found a successful business career after semi-retirement from the military and politics. Presiding over the Huizhou Pingtan Sugar Factory, Weng has made his fortune and his interest in politics only dwindles as he ages.\n\nChen's old friend Zhou Xingnan's business exploits are also well known. During his time with Chen's army, Zhou found himself working on Fujian's infrastructure to help the military campaign. After returning to Guangdong in 1920, Zhou did much of the same in Huizhou as he did in Zhangzhou. When Chen's forces were repulsed from Guangdong, Zhou found himself in Xiamen again, and spent the next decade building roads and parks.\n\nAnother one of Chen's associates, Chen Xiru was heir to his father Chen \

March of a Tempered Radical
While much of the socialist movement in China has been influenced by Russian-style bolshevism, Kropotkin's anarchism, and French syndicalism, Zhang Dongsun found himself drawn to English guild socialism. Initially drawn to mass democracy, Zhang promoted these ideas in the new republic. However, Yuan centralisation of power and dictatorial tendencies pushed Zhang to instead look for a backbone for the republic so that it could foster a strong modern state. Zhang became interested in state socialism derived from the Fabian society, which refuted capitalism based on the living conditions of the working class and advocated collectivism through state ownership and social legislation.\n\nWhat drew Zhang particularly to Fabianism was its political elitism, which would stay with him for the rest of his political career. The chaos of the early republic led him, Liang Qichao, and Zhang Junmai to believe in political reformation led by modern intellectuals like themselves. Zhang finally took the plunge into guild socialism when Bertrand Russell came to China in 1920 on the invitation of Liang and Zhang. Writing that the socialist state could slip into tyranny should it amass too much power, Russell criticised the Bolshevik Revolution in Russia as a dictatorship only of the Communist Party and not of the proletariat.\n\nAlso a critic of the Russian Revolution, Zhang Dongsun praised the moderate socialism which Russell preached, and applauded the balance between industrial unions and the state to ensure the greatest amount of freedoms to labourers and encourage their political participation whilst also putting necessary constraints on them. Russell ended up abandoning his hopes of achieving guild socialism in China, leading Zhang to regard him as not properly understanding China. Emancipated from following orthodox Western thought, Zhang began formulating his own theories on guild socialism to prove its feasibility in China.
What a bunch of utopian nonsense!
Bolshevism ran so we could walk?

Socialist Theory Put in Action
With Chen Jiongming's rise to power in Guangdong and Guangxi, Zhang Dongsun has a unique opportunity to put his theories into practice. Chen, while allied with many conservative gentry and reactionary generals, has always had an anarchist streak that was coated with gradualism not too dissimilar from Zhang's own interpretation of socialism. Guangzhou is also one of the few regions of China that has already embraced capitalism compared to the more feudal interior, and thus need not wait for as much capitalist development to take place.\n\nGuangzhou is also home to many industrial workers who will prove to be an attractive audience for Zhang Dongsun's theories. Though Chen has discouraged the politicisation of unions, Zhang Dongsun has pushed his friend and party leader Zhang Junmai to propagate their party platform to various labour leaders. Through Xuehai University and the Democratic Socialist Party, Zhang propagates his interpretations of socialism.\n\nWhile Zhang Junmai and Zhang Dongsun have differing views on socialism, they share a belief in political elitism carried from their shared mentor, Liang Qichao. Through educational institutions, they promote their ideas to the next generation of intellectuals. Regarding the Grand Republican League as the political elite who will lead the push out western imperialism, Zhang is confident that Guangdong will serve as the template for the inevitable coming of Chinese guild socialism.

Socialism through the Handshake
To Zhang Dongsun, long-lasting Chinese guilds could be transformed into modern industrial unions through organisational and ideological reconstruction. To achieve this, Zhang recognised a need to go through the stage of capitalism so that numerous jobs could be created and worker welfare improved to a limited extent, and provide institutional channels for them to consult the capitalist classes. Meanwhile, education would be funded by the wealthy, which would improve workers' literacy and turn the working class into an enthusiastic audience for the ideas of socialism. While the bourgeoisie would provide the economic stepping stones into socialism, the intellectuals would bring salvation to the uninformed masses of the nation. To more radical Marxists in the Kuomintang and CSP, Zhang's ideas to wait for capitalist development and put off class struggle is merely a wait-and-see theory, which China cannot afford to wait for. Zhang could be characterised as not particularly trustworthy of the people, though he himself would refer to it as his interpretation of Marxist scientific socialism.

Kuomintang Revolution in Liangguang
Despite repeated failures over the last few decades and the death of their founder, the Kuomintang refuse to yield in their quest to realise Sun Yat-sen's vision. Nowhere was that more apparent, it seems, than their traditional homebase in Liangguang, which fell to invading revolutionary forces from MinGan and Guizhou as well as uprisings by cells from within.\n\nThis has brought to power a revolutionary junta led by the victorious generals: the leftist Chen Mingshu and rightist Chen Jitang both of Guangdong as well as General Li Zongren of Guangxi. How long this so-called South China Political Council can remain internally cohesive, much less bring their revolution to the rest of China remains to be seen.

Tensions in Guangdong
Guangdong was long the Kuomintang base of operations, and its recapture a milestone in reversing their failure to hold off German intervention during the Northern Expedition. Sharing the prize however, has not been easy. Even in the best of times, the Kuomintang's leaders seldom got along. Now, with their mutual enemies dead or on the run, the party has quickly returned to infighting.\n\nGuangzhou, the provincial capital, has been secured by the New Guangdong clique under Chen Jitang. He has leveraged it to secure himself as the nominal head of the three-man revolutionary junta. Chen Jitang was a relatively low ranking officer in the old Fourth Army who managed to survive in the post-1927 provincial government. Loosely affiliated with the right, his ambitions are said to be mostly regional in scope.\n\nHis challenger is Chen Mingshu, a fellow veteran of the Fourth Army sent south by the MinGan government. Setting up base in Shaoguan to the northeast, Chen has allied with peasant activists and syndicalist sympathisers. His willingness to join the junta has drawn some scorn from orthodox leftists but is nevertheless their best bet for taking power in Guangdong.\n\nFurther south, the New Guangxi clique has proven even more ambitious than previously thought. Their forces have advanced into Beihai, a port city in southwest Guangdong. Beihai is too small to meaningfully steer trade away from the Pearl River for the landlocked Guangxi, so the real objective seems to be the resource-rich island of Qiongya.\n\nChen Jitang has begged Li to leave the region to his forces' control - claiming their mutual best interest in containing socialism in the two provinces. He claims to fondly recall their past partnership in securing Guangxi, though is vague on any details for a future pact. Li, freshly arrived in Guangzhou to take part in proceedings, is well aware of the power in his hands, though is reluctant to give up such a powerful bargaining chip for free...

Rise of the New Guangxi Clique
The New Guangxi Clique was formed as the Old Guangxi Clique began to disintegrate following the devastating 1920s Yue-Gui Wars. Victory over Lu Rongting gave them control over the province and they played an integral role during the Northern Expedition as the Seventh Army of the NRA. With their triumphant return, most Guangxi clans since have sworn fealty.\n\nThe New Guangxi Clique is not the same as the one that went north to Wuhan. Once a regional force with little ideology of its own, the subsequent exile has firmly reoriented their identity. Li Zongren's newfound ambitions for national leadership are on full display now with his flashy presence in Guangzhou for junta meetings.\n\nThe clique internally is ruled as a triumvirate. Socialists in Guangzhou
Guangzhou has long been a hotbed for leftist activity. With the Guangdong Army severely weakened by recent upheaval and many units having been deployed outside the city to face down Chen Mingshu's men and attempt to secure Qiongya, some believe that Guangzhou itself is vulnerable to further infiltration. The local chapter of the Chinese Syndicalist Party, led by famed labour organiser Deng Zhongxia, union activist and senior party leader Su Zhaozheng, and radical insurgent leader Zhang Tailei, have come out of hiding. They have found their ranks swelling, easily recruiting industrial workers, sailors, and other labourers to their cause.\n\nTheir actions, unsubtle as always, have quickly caught the attention of Chen Jitang. Suspicious of their intentions and already wary of the growing leftist threat to his control of Guangdong, he has lashed out in party meetings against the Chinese Syndicalist Party. He denounced them as foreign agents, loyal primarily to Paris and London, and accused them of sabotaging the Kuomintang revolution through actions such as the subversive formation of the League of Chinese Syndicalists, the violent Canton-Hong Kong Strike (which sank Kuomintang relations with foreign powers), and other actions that he claims have leeched the strength of Dr. Sun's movement in favour of the global socialist agenda.\n\nHis desire to clamp down socialist agitation has met with some opposition, even within his camp. Not all members of the Guangdong army are necessarily right-leaning, and there has been some questions about the loyalty of his officers. However, with the power vacuum growing in Guangzhou as Kuomintang forces clear out their former opposition, violence has broken out as syndicalist-aligned militias attack former members of the Merchant Corps and the Guangzhou Chamber of Commerce. Hated by many for their submission to foreign powers, it has nevertheless attracted concerns by conservative elements that Guangzhou is hurtling towards anarchy.

The Intelligentsia of Guangdong
In an effort to present a more democratic face, contrast themselves to the iron-fisted rule of Beiyang, and to tame the Republican hotbed that was 1920s Guangzhou, previous Cantonese warlords preserved a certain degree of political freedom in the city. While not nearly as vibrant as it once was, plenty of republicans disillusioned with the cynical reactionaryism have settled down in Guangzhou or Hong Kong. The latter has been particularly attractive to democratic exiles fleeing the law.\n\nThe Grand Republican Alliance, which sought to rally many of these figures, has since collapsed. Its component organisations, including the Old Guangdong Army affiliated Public Interest Party of Chen Jiongming and the big-tent Chinese Democratic League of Zhang Junmai, have scattered in wake of Kuomintang advances.\n\nWhile the flight of their senior leadership was perhaps inevitable, the lower republicans that remain in Guangzhou grow uneasy. While we share a dream of a republic far different from Beiyang's decadence, many are wary of Kuomintang authoritarianism and dread the repression of our secret police. Federalists affiliated with Chen Jiongming remember the bitter fights between Chen and Dr. Sun, and plenty plan to join Chen's flight to the legations. Wealthier intellectuals, many affiliated with the League, resent the heavy war-time taxation the party imposes, claiming us to be even more extortionate and undemocratic than the warlords.\n\nThat is not to say it is too late to win them back. More moderate factions in the Kuomintang, namely the Reconstruction Faction and the Western Hills Clique are less inclined towards long-term vanguardism. The ensuing assault was swift and well coordinated, harmonising the disparate [CHI.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] loyalist forces together in an elegant symphony. Chen Mingshu and his Fourth Army, with the Tenth Division at its core marched southwards directly towards Guangzhou, a bold play that signalled to all without any doubt that it is do or die. Their opposition, arrayed in seemly impervious defence lines, evaporated almost overnight - weeks of skirmishes, insurgency, and defections sapped their strength.\n\nChen Mingshu's men, for their part seemed to know the route well. Well-placed spies and diligent scouting foiled most traps and ambushes along the route, preventing them from getting bogged down. As the Guangdong army's retreat turned into a rout, many of their units clogged the roads and were eradicated by the swiftly advancing Fourth Army. Guangzhou itself fell without a siege, a large general strike turned into urban rioting that allowed Chen Mingshu's men to storm the city with little fight.\n\nChen Jitang and his remaining loyalists, recoiling in utter shock and disbelief, have fled southwards to Qiongya. With similar uprisings breaking out on the island, the situation in Guangdong seems untenable for the right. He has sent frantic telegrams to Li Zongren and the New Guangxi Clique, offering a greater share of power for his intervention, but with the situation seemingly long gone it seems that Li is more inclined to negotiate with the left.

Chen Mingshu's Coup Succeeds!
The operation was swift, synchronised, and almost surgical in its efficacy. After months of tension, uncertainty, and fluctuating front lines, the Clash for Canton came to a stunning end. Chen Mingshu and the Fourth Army marched southwards towards Guangzhou, his Tenth Division at the forefront, boldly challenging the Guangdong Army.\n\nThe gambit paid off. By the time the Fourth Army vanguard reached Guangdong lines, their foes were already a paper tiger.\n\nGuangzhou itself showed its true colours when a coordinated general strike paralysed city defences, avoiding a prolonged siege. The Peasants Rally in Guangdong
It has long been accepted that the peasant's lot in life is to toil in the fields from dawn to dusk, but times are changing. Increasingly disillusioned with China's social order, many have fallen under the influence of charismatic speakers who rail against the landlords and warlords. Their most recent target for abuse has been none other than Chen Jitang and his cooperatives initiatives, who they claim represent the worst of Kuomintang rightist hypocrisy and empty promises.\n\nLed by Peng Pai, the Guangdong Provincial Farmers' Association have rallied abused peasants. They travel around the province, slinking away from Guangdong Army security forces and gentry backed militia, playing music and songs via gramophone to sway the people against perceived injustice.\n\nHis efforts to foment unrest, usually relatively contained despite his grandiose promises, have taken a much more belligerent, emphatic tone as of late. No doubt he has reconnected with the Kuomintang committee in [CHI.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName], and is utilising their resources to his fullest advantage. His efforts have inflamed the rural insurgency. Rural guerillas bands have begun striking isolated security units and infiltrating guard posts. The gentry have reported kidnappings and assassinations among their ranks, and in some cases entire villages have been seized and title deeds burned.\n\nThis fighting, often far behind the front lines of the Fourth Army-Guangdong Army stand off, represents another dimension of the Clash for Canton. In many areas, local police forces are unable to contain the rebels and faith is declining in Chen Jitang's ability to govern. But diverting his limited forces is no easy task either. Many officers have chosen to hide in fortified villages and camps rather than confront the revolutionaries, leaving the vast countryside to the rebels. To force them to go out to contest enemy movement will require direct, forceful management.

Fourth Army Reinforcements Arrive
Ever since the apparent Kuomintang victory in Liangguang, two Cantonese armies have faced off against each other in northern Guangdong. Digging trenches and building hastily constructed bunkers, the armies of the two General Chens have tried to consolidate their positions while destabilising their rivals. These efforts have been foiled by the realities of the situation: neither army has the numbers to cover the entirety of the province nor the discipline to prevent soldiers from coming and going as they please. The fact that both are ostensibly supposed to be allies under a shared provisional junta further complicates things, but hardly prevents patrols from getting into deadly clashes.\n\nThe balance of power in the region has see-sawed, with little end in sight. Hoping to swing things in their favour, [CHI.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] has authorised the deployment of another legion to reinforce the Chen Mingshu and his Fourth Army. They have largely eschewed secrecy in this move, preferring to make their propagandised presence known as concerns of a futile, prolonged stalemate have been dampening morale on both sides.\n\nThe Fourth Army boast this latest detachment include many veterans from the successful MinGan insurgency, though more experienced commanders note that while these men might have been successful guerillas (if the claims are true), that has not always translated well in pitched, conventional battles. Commanders from the Guangdong Army have advised Chen Jitang to undercut the Fourth Army's narrative of imminent victory by launching a series of probing attacks along the frontline, showing the tide is unlikely to turn in the near future. But whether or not we have the reserves for such an operation remains in doubt.
Chen Mingshu's army continues to swell...
Chen Jitang orders probing attacks along the front.
The Federalists Seize Liangguang
Despite many doubts, the Federalist demise proved to be exaggerated. Chen Jiongming rallied the Guangzhou government's disparate warlord forces, despite the lukewarm support of the city establishment, and overcame Guangxi's warlords and Kuomintang insurgents to restore order to not only his native Guangdong but also Guangxi. For his efforts, he has been rewarded with mastery over the two provinces and a chance to realise his old dream of a Federalist China.\n\nHis supporters in Guangdong are ecstatic, even if there are questions about what will happen next. Chen is able to draw support from a broad base that includes his Yue Army loyalists, the gentry and merchant elite, the Guangdong Provincial Assembly, various warlords, and liberal intellectuals. His election to the Governorship, though under dubious circumstances, is now assured as the assembly reconvenes and is expected to extend his term indefinitely.\n\nFor Guangxi, the mood is much darker. The Guangdong Army effectively won the war by seizing Nanning and allowing allied warlord bands (many of which were recent defectors) to take other cities as the Old Guangxi Clique collapsed. While all now kowtow to Guangzhou, the prevailing expectation is that Chen will not be able to maintain his hold over Guangxi for long. The sacking of major Guangxi cities by vengeful Guangdong troops and their allies has cast a cloud on their victory.\n\nAs Chen works to gather support for a new rival government in southern China, he is expected to confront a dilemma - the same Federalism that won him so much support will prove to be an obstacle for leaving Liangguang for elsewhere.
All Forward under the Lawyer General!
Federalist Revival in Liangguang
Reduced to a minor Cantonese politician in the 1920s, Chen Jiongming of Guangdong has made a political comeback in the aftermath of the Liangguang civil war. Rallying the flagging Guangzhou city government and outlying Guangdong warlord forces, General Chen once again routed Old Guangxi troops in a campaign reminiscent his performance in the Yue-Gui Wars.\n\nAs the new master of the twin provinces, Chen Jiongming has been offered a second chance at implementing his Federalist ideals. Assembling a coalition that includes his loyalists in the Yue Army and Public Interest Party, the Cantonese gentry and merchants, various warlords, and liberal intellectuals, Chen seeks to build an alternative to the Beiyang order and Kuomintang radicalism.
Proof federalism is hardly dead.
The first time was folly, this second time is treachery.
The History of Chinese Federalism
Federalism in China was tied to late Qing-era political decentralisation. The Xinhai Revolution saw the emergence of the rival Beiyang and Guangzhou Governments. However, neither were able to break the deadlock and both began to lose their grip on their allied provinces. Fears of being dragged into the violent civil war caused regionalism and distrust of central authority that manifested in a desire for provincial autonomy.\n\nThis phenomenon also had feudal roots. Ming and Qing dynasty scholars have argued that the concept of protecting local autonomy to check tyrannical central power could be traced as early as the ancient Zhou Dynasty, using it to justify protections for their own intellectual autonomy from imperial control. This concept reemerged during Kang Youwei and Liang Qichao's Hundred Day Reforms, which included local self-strengthening programs and the formation of 21 Provincial Assemblies in 1909.\n\nBy the 1910s, the concept of the Warlord (Junfa) emerged, perhaps derived from the Japanese word Gunbatsu - the Marxist interpretation of Japanese military cliques. An almost intrinsically derogatory term, given intellectual calls to separate military and civilian power, extensive dialogue between intellectuals, warlords, and politicians continues to blur the line. These new intellectuals embraced the traditional Confucian role of the literari, teaching and advising political leaders, but also engaged with the masses, labour relations, and public education, making them indispensable to local militarists.\n\nFederalism then, could be seen as a product of this dialogue. Proponents claimed it was an attempt to peacefully reunite the provinces stuck in a military quagmire. Opponents denounced it as an attempt by southern warlords to legitimise their power and divide the nation. The first wave of Federalism fizzled out in the 1920s, but with the nation once again divided, it seems that China is seeing a rebirth of Federalism.
An idealistic project for national unity and democracy.
A practical ideology for a divided, warring nation.
The Occupation of Guangxi
The end of the Liangguang civil war was a complete reversal of fortunes between Guangdong and Guangxi, the latest of a series of conflicts between the twin provinces. Months later, Guangxi remains devastated and under Guangdong occupation while Guangxi leaders negotiate a return to the self-government they were promised. In August 1921, Guangdong forces occupied Nanning, ending the Second Yue-Gui War after a stunning counterattack. Chen Jiongming issued a four-point proclamation to minimise reprisals against the Guangxi population.\n\n1. The war is directed against the Guangxi militarists, not against the people of Guangxi. Enemy troops are welcome to join our forces.\n2. We are helping the Guangxi people to establish democratic rule and therefore should treat them as brothers.\n3. Every plant and every tree in Guangxi belongs to the people of Guangxi and should not be touched. Fair prices should be strictly asked and paid in buying or selling goods and foodstuff.\n4. Our past successes depended on the help given us by the local populace. We will be welcomed by the Guangxi people if we act in the normal civilised way.\n\nDespite this, there was considerable looting and brutality during the occupation as Guangdong soldiers took their revenge. Sun Yat-sen appointed educator Ma Junwu as Governor and the Guangxi Provincial Assembly was restored, but Guangdong control was always effectively nominal outside of Nanning and the Guangdong Army retreated within the year amid tensions between Chen and Sun. Shortly after, armed bands nominally aligned with Guangzhou fought among themselves and Lu Rongting for power.\n\nChen Jiongming is determined to avoid a repeat of the situation, but this is no easy task. Discipline by the Yue Army has broken down the further away from home they have marched. Meanwhile, Guangxi Generals such as Liu Zhenhuan and Lin Hu, despite their rivalries, have exploited the moment to demand control over reconstruction.
Guangxi ought to be governed by their own leaders, lest we be branded hypocrites.
We will reconstruct Guangxi the right way this time!
Rise of the Chinese Centre Left
The Chinese centre left has long been overlooked relative to the more conservative Beiyang republic and the revolutionary Kuomintang. Made up of progressive and liberal intellectuals, they have spent most of the Xinhai Revolution and ensuing years on the sidelines, allying with various \

Chen Jiongming's Demilitarisation Plan
Chen Jiongming's victory in the Liangguang Civil War has brought some economic stability, but the war has also drained Guangzhou's coffers. This is a very familiar problem to Governor Chen, who spent much of his earlier stints as Governor cleaning up the financial mess left behind by Sun Yat-sen and often lost power as a result. If there is any consolation, this time the situation is not quite as bad - the war was over before too much devastation could be wrought.\n\nStill, the same dilemma that divided Sun and Chen in the 1920s has returned, but only he now remains to decide its resolution. In the past, Chen wanted to reduce military expenditures by disbanding half of the 120,000 or so troops they had at the time, arguing that only 4 divisions were needed for Guangdong's contribution to national defence. He sought to use the money for local reforms which he believed would ultimately allow for national reforms after negotiations with Beiyang. Sun on the other hand had a quixotic dream for national unification from the top down, and was desperate for money to fund his Northern Expedition.\n\nThe Kuomintang's Northern Expedition failed utterly, but that is of little solace to the burgeoning Liangguang government. Political negotiations with Beiyang fell through in the brief period it was attempted, and the Zhili proved it was possible to unite China by force. As Chen Jiongming weighs the merits of making cuts to military expenditures, it is understood that his loyal Yue Army would be spared from the bulk of it.\n\nHowever, his allied warlord armies (especially those that defected at the end of the war), nervously await their fate. The main units in question however are the prized divisions of the old Liangguang order, often built along Western lines. While their weapons may ultimately be replaced (though most will be taken home by departing soldiers or sold on the black market), the cohesion and training of these troops will be missed.
Sun was wrong to rob the people for his vain quest.
But having quality divisions is needed to protect the people.

After elections, the Guangxi Provincial Assembly will finally resume operations. When it does, it will elect a new Governor, who ostensibly will be an equal partner to Governor Chen.\n\nIn practice, it is understood that Guangzhou will be calling the shots in Nanning.
