Guangxi Drifts Away Under Lin Hu
Unwilling or perhaps unable to afford a prolonged occupation of Guangxi, the Yue Army largely pulled out of the province in favor of leaving allied Guangxi General Lin Hu to be elected Governor. The establishment favourite and a member of the old Young Guangxi triumvirate, little has changed in Guangxi over the years as a result.\n\nLin has been a career soldier, starting as a civilian participant in Lin Yongfu's Black Flag Army in Taiwan resisting Japanese occupation. A soldier under Huang Xing during the Xinhai Revolution, Lin steadily rose through the ranks of the Old Guangxi Clique. After a disastrous defeat in the First Yue-Gui war, Lin Hu was forced to resign and go into exile. A year later, he returned at Chen Jiongming's (his former enemy) invitation to help resist Sun Yat-sen but they were ultimately beaten.\n\nHe spent 1923-1925 briefly eclipsing Chen in Eastern Guangdong, becoming the nominal leader of the United Forces Office which sought to align with Beiyang against the rising Kuomintang, and was even named Governor of Guangdong for a spell. His forces were destroyed in the battle of Mianhu, forcing him to flee to Shanghai. Ma Ji's return to Guangxi gave him an opening to return to power and he once more found himself under Lu Rongting's command.\n\nWith his independent history, Lin largely does not need Chen Jiongming for power after his initial election, and he has mostly steered Guangxi inwards - only nominally taking directives from Guangzhou. This might be for the best in some regards: a docile, isolationist Guangxi may be less of a threat than an actively defiant one and puts Guangdong in the driver's seat. Still, Chen hopes to at least coax some cooperation out of Lin.
Lin Hu continues to rehabilitate old Guangxi figures to support his independence.

The Death of Governor Yin Ji
Governor Yin Ji, appointed by the Guangdong Clique to oversee the completion of Guangxi's Reconstruction, has died of illness. A bright and ambitious young man born to a poor Hunan family, he entered military service under Chen Zhaotang (a subordinate of Viceroy Cen Chunxuan) and fought rebels in Guangxi. He became a favourite of Chen, which allowed him to attend the Guangzhou Military Academy in 1905. There he befriended Deng Keng, but surrendered rather than fight during the Second Revolution.\n\nHe then became a soldier under Lu Rongting during the National Protection War. He and his troops entered Guangxi while marching behind Lu Rongting's forces on the way to Changsha, and after Yuan Shikai's death Yi was sent as Lu's emissary to negotiations with President Li Yuanhong. By then however, the Guangxi Clique began to distrust him as a non-Guangxi native and he was sidelined.\n\nYi then entered the service of the Guangdong Army, serving under Hong Zhaolin. An apparently popular officer while stationed in Chaomei, he nevertheless came into conflict with Hong and was forced to leave. During the 1920s, both the Beiyang and Guangzhou governments courted him and he decided to side with Chen Jiongming but his rivalry with Hong forced him to retire even before 1925.\n\nAs governor, he has been a reliable administrator, noted for his public works and charity projects which has helped him shore up his position despite being an outsider. He was still reliant on Yue Army support to maintain his rein, but this was decreasing by the time he suddenly died of illness.\n\nHis death leaves little excuse for the Yue Army to maintain their occupation, with reconstruction mostly complete the Guangxi establishment has sought to elect Lin Hu as his replacement. General Liu Zhenhuan would likely be the closest thing to extending the status quo, though Chen Qiyou and PIP leadership have proposed Lei Rongke, the civilian PIP mayor of Nanning installed.

The Guangzhou City Government
In 1921, Chen Jiongming penned the Guangzhou provisional city charter, placing the city directly under the Provincial Government. The city was to be led by a 7 person executive committee headed by the mayor and 6 commissioners for finance, safety, education, health, public works, and utilities. A 30 person municipal advisory council was also to be created with 10 appointed by the governor, 10 elected by the public, 3 by the chamber of commerce, 3 by labour organisations, and 1 each by educational, medical, engineering, and legal professional groups.\n\nThe mayor was supposed to be elected democratically, but the commissioners were to be appointed by the governor upon advice by the mayor - controversial among many democrats who wanted the committee fully elected. Chen Jiongming however argued that this system was hardly unprecedented and in any case the provisional charter was to be a five year teaching tool: afterwards they could always revise it.\n\nThe Provincial Assembly raised objections to charter however, passing its own version of the charter and expressed frustration over being treated as \

The Old Order Holds in Liangguang
In the end, the revolutionary zeal of the Kuomintang and the republican idealism of the Federalists were insufficient to topple Lu Yuguang and his allies. Rallying their forces, they repelled external invasions by KMT forces and crushed rebellions by socialists, bandits and Cantonese warlords alike. On the surface at least, the old status quo has been restored, with the Young Guangxi Faction remaining dominant in Guangxi, the Shen Army Faction holding onto Shaoguan and northeastern Guangdong, and Chen Lianbo and his Cantonese allies holding onto southern Guangdong.\n\nThere is no going back however. The collapse of the old Zhili Order continues to reverberate across China. Even in Liangguang, the power vacuum left behind by the brief period of disorder has shaken the foundations of the post-Northern Expedition system. Power is consolidating away from local, petty warlord factions into the hands of the three remaining strongmen: Lu Yuguang, Shen Hongying, and Chen Lianbo. The latter two are scrambling to secure control over Guangdong, resuming a long-standing battle in the east. The victor may even prove strong enough to challenge Lu Yuguang for all of Liangguang.\n\nFor now however, Marshal Lu and the Young Guangxi junta remain in control over the region. They will have to navigate uncertain winds as Liangguang enters the crosshairs of the mighty contenders seeking to unite the nation. Perhaps they will join a side, or perhaps they will create one of their own.

Regionalist Powers Hold Onto Liangguang
Despite the zeal of the Kuomintang and the enthusiasm of the Federalists, in the end regionalist forces were able to repel the invaders and crush domestic uprisings. Within a few months, all major cities and strongholds were in the hands of the old powers that be in the twin provinces of Guangdong and Guangxi.\n\nThe most dominant is the Young Guangxi Faction of the Old Guangxi Clique, led by Lu Yuguang, adopted son of famous warlord Lu Rongting. He shares power with other warlord cliques including Shen Hongying in Shaoguan and Chen Lianbo in Guangzhou. Seeking to protect their autonomy, they face difficult questions ahead as they navigate China's fractious politics.

North and South
Lu Rongting made several careful alliances with northern factions, both in hopes of peacefully ending the civil war and in hopes of preserving his own power and the region's autonomy. This policy carried over to Lu Yuguang's rule, however tensions between Lu, the League, and the Zhili central governments prevented more indepth cooperation between the three.\n\nThe fiery collapse of the League in 1936 has allowed Liangguang to achieve de facto independence once again, but this situation cannot last forever. While there is significant support for self-rule and distrust towards Beijing, there is no support for secession. Either Liangguang will have to align with a government or they will have to create one of their own.\n\nOf course, Marshal Lu has time to play his cards and the Old Guangxi Junta will be carefully eyeing the situation to maximise their advantage in negotiations. But the Zhili and Fengtian Cliques - and possibly others too - will begin applying pressure along with their foreign backers and their agents within the region to bring us into their spheres. As internal and external affairs begin to overlap, we would be wise to tread carefully.
We welcome these emissaries of peace.

Politics of Southern China
Southern China is an extremely politicised region, a revolutionary hotbed since the Taiping Rebellion. It has been home to several anti-Beiyang movements in the post-Xinhai Era, including the National Protection War, the Constitutional Protection War, and the Kuomintang and Federalist movements. Guangzhou in particular was the temporary capital of several of those governments, giving it a degree of legitimacy for those seeking to unite the nation.\n\nThe Beijing side won the civil war after the Northern Expedition and has sought to undermine the political importance of the city. They have used divide and conquer tactics to keep the south apart, and Liangguang even moreso, in hopes of preventing a new challenger from emerging. Nevertheless, Guangzhou remains an important target, both for factions seeking to stake their claim for national leadership and for Northern factions seeking to reassert their control over the south. It will not be long before someone seeks to make their move on our territory.
We must be vigilant against these traitors.
We need to present a vision of our own.
The Lesser Factions of Liangguang
It is almost a foregone conclusion that Liangguang will fall to one of the three major warlords in the province or to external invaders. However, there remain some minor factions searching for a chance to wedge themselves into the political scene. They are mostly rooted in the cosmopolitan city of Guangzhou, but have some affiliates deeper inland.\n\nThe Political Science Clique is the oldest, a remnant of a different time in Liangguang. A loose collection of politicians, scholars, businessmen, and warlords once associated with Guangdong Governor Cen Chunxuan, his political demise in 1918 and the overthrow of other aligned PSC figures in southern China broke their power. Cen died in seclusion in Shanghai in 1933 and PSC figures have scattered - grafting themselves onto other strongmen. In Guangzhou, their remnants gather around Lin Zhengxuan, who managed to carve a role as Speaker of the Provincial Assembly, and the disgraced warlords Liu Zhenhuan and Yang Ximin.\n\nThe younger factions share a fascination with the West with the PSC, though in different ways. The Jingxing Society was founded by students studying in Germany and grew in influence as Sino-German Cooperation deepened. In Guangdong and Guangxi, the most prominent Jingxing affiliates are Zhu Jiahua and Ma Junwu respectively. Another German educated scholar is Zhang Junmai, who has founded the National Renaissance Society, which seeks to use education to propel China towards a democratic future along a blend of Social Democratic and Liangist lines. Lastly, there is the Public Interest Party founded by Chen Jiongming and other Chinese Hongmen (described as the Chinese Free Masons) which seek to implement Federalism in China. Their master defeated in battle, its remnants have rebased to Hong Kong.
The PSC has the connections needed to survive.
The JXS mirrors the world's hegemon.
The Democrats give scholars hope for the future.
Ideology and the Regionalists
Though Warlords have often been pragmatic and idiosyncratic in their beliefs, it would be a mistake to think they are ignorant or uneducated. Chen Lianbo and other Guangzhou warlords from wealthier backgrounds received modern, Western-style educations from either elite schools in China or abroad. And even those from bandit or military backgrounds like the Old Guangxi Clique often surround themselves with well-educated advisors and keep up with modern political trends, both out of respect from the traditional roles of the Confucian-Scholar literati and also out of a pragmatic desire for legitimacy and survival.\n\nLiangguang is a melting pot of beliefs. Even with the Kuomintang and Federalists driven underground, debate over Confucianism, the New Culture Movement, liberalism, nationalism, modernisation, and other ideologies continue. Lu Rongting, for his part, took a mixed approach towards progress, embracing nationalism and the republican revolution but often retaining existing local political systems in Guangxi. His efforts to preserve order and build roads was commended, but his authoritarianism, distrust towards revolutionaries, and corruption by Old Guangxi Clique officers led to his downfall.\n\nHis successors in Liangguang are generally on the conservative end of the Chinese political spectrum though not necessarily reactionary. There is a growing understanding that change is needed, but how far and how fast is another question.
We are the force of modern change in China.
We will uphold China's traditional values.
Minority Groups in Guangxi
Guangxi is an extremely diverse province. Han Chinese make up about 60% of the population, having migrated to the region in several generations and often intermarrying with other ethnic groups. The north and west tends to speak a southern sub-dialect of Mandarin while Cantonese tends to be spoken in the central region and southwest. Scattered in the east and southwest are Hakka speaking Han groups in Guangxi.\n\nThe Zhuang people primarily live in western Guangxi around Yulin. They tend to speak Cantonese and live in different clans, each with their own leaders. These clans are often described as temporary groupings rather than distinct cultural entities, adding to the dynamism of Guangxi politics. Nearly 37% of Guangxi are Zhuang and they are said to be generally assimilated to the broader Chinese identity.\n\nSmaller ethnic groups in Guangxi include the Yao people, which speak a variant of the Hmong-Mien language group. Some groups apparently practise slash and burn agriculture and migrated to the area as late as 1870. The Miao traditionally claim to have originated from Siberia and also speak a tongue of the Hmong-Mien language group that is tonal, monosyllabic and lacks a written language. They tend to dislike large villages but are capable farmers when settled, proficient in farming corn despite an apparent lack of mathematics or calendar. They are also apparently excellent singers. Lastly the Yi people, also described as the Lolo, have some branches in Guangxi and have a written, pictographic language that appears to be part of the Tibeto-Burman family. They are known to be monogamous and chaste.\n\nThe Guangxi Clique is known for its relative tolerance for ethnic groups, granting them semi-autonomous tribal areas. Though the Qing dynasty worked to slowly eliminate them, these writs have largely been ignored.
Together we are strong.
Halls of Jade, Lorded by Tigers
The rulers of China ever since the failure of the Northern Expedition and their successes in the Zhili-Fengtian Wars, the Zhili Clique has, for all their pretenses, never truly been united internally. Serving more as a dissident force to fight against mutual enemies, the Zhili family tree is fractured and split, with subfactions upon subfactions of alliances and deals between family members.\n\nWith their seat of power lying upon the Central Plains, their reach in the South is limited to a few garrisons or loyal compradors; Tang Xiangming, Yang Sen, Liu Cunhou, or the Southern Zhili Governors of Jiangxi and Fujian. From 1928 to 1936 the Zhili attempted to demonstrate their capacity to rule the South through various means, but ultimately had to rely upon the Old Guangxi Clique.\n\nDivided between the North Zhili of the Tianjin-Baoding Group and the Luoyang Faction, alongside the Southern Zhili dominated by Sun Chuanfang, the \

Crushing the Kuomintang
For the fourth time, once more the Kuomintang lies devastated and defeated once more. While previous attempts at resurrecting the Chinese Nationalist Party were led by the party's charismatic leader Sun Yat-sen; the death of the esteemed early revolutionary and political leader led to a power struggle between the party's leadership in 1925. The failure of the Northern Expedition, the Shanghai Uprising of 1932, and now their attempts to secure Liangguang for the Party - have shown outside observers the clear writing on the wall - that the Kuomintang is now defeated for good. Books, pamphlets, and journals belonging or adjacent to the Kuomintang are publicly burnt and rebel leaders are routinely hung or executed in a gruesome suppression of the radical revolutionaries.\n\nWhile main rebel forces have effectively been crushed by the various factions vying for power and legitimacy across the province; rebel guerrilla forces including those belonging to Peng Pai or the former MinGan Insurgency base have fled into the mountains and hills of southern China in isolated pockets. With rumours of severely weakened but surviving arms shipment routes from Shanghai or Hong Kong; many within the government have advocated for simply outright crushing these isolated pockets. Any of their aspirations for an attempt at a provincial takeover can be regarded as dead in the mud but their continued resistance may prove to be an annoyance to local governance and an issue of constant banditry. On the other hand, there are those within the New Guangxi and New Guangdong factions of the Kuomintang that may be more amicable with our Republican ideals. With Marxist-tinted socialism absent from their ideology; these former rebels and politicians may be inclined to work with our ranks and secure their positions; having realised that the Kuomintang dream is effectively destroyed forevermore in Liangguang.

Dismantling the Yue Clique
Towering, charismatic, and ambitious - Chen Jiongming has long been a towering figure in local Guangdong politics. The Hakka native has been a player in the region from his early days as a revolutionary alongside Sun Yat-sen; to ousting the latter in a bloody coup and while he was once believed to have been amicable to the Zhili dominance of China - it's clear that Chen and his Yue Army had ambitions of their own. In their failed attempt to usurp our position in Liangguang; the fate of Chen and his Federalist movement effectively lies in our hands.\n\nThe Yue Army, led by Ye Ju and including prominent Guangdong Clique military officers such as Luo Xianxing and Ma Yuhang, lies at our mercy having been defeated in the skirmishes and battles across the province. Despite their opposition, many of their officers are perhaps some of the better trained throughout the province. Within their ranks amicable to our principles of provincial regionalism and republicanism; the Yue Army will likely be absorbed into Chen Lianbo's army.\n\nThe same applies to the provincial gentry which may have backed Chen but ultimately are interested in keeping their own lands and property safe from the radicalism of communism. Local landlords, village chiefs, and town mayors have pledged their loyalty in exchange for maintaining their posts. Warlord ties to the southwest for instance, may be exploited by Lu Yuguang's Young Guangxi faction in the hopes of filling the local power vacuum while those in the northeast may find themselves in Shen Hongying's camp.\n\nWith major republican figures out of the picture, the provincial democrats and hongmen really have no one else to turn to than to the military leaders of the NPA. With ideology ever fluid and changing in China; many of these urban scholar-intellectuals are able to recognise the rapidly changing political situation and adapt their goals and movements to the current state of politics.

The Southern Struggle
Liangguang has long been a thorn on our side, hosting various revolutionary governments seeking to overthrow the Beiyang order. The end of the Northern Expedition and the splintering of the region into various warlord fiefdoms aligned with the Zhili should have been the end of matters, but the cunning Lu Rongting was able to reunite the region and loosen the Zhili's grip after the Zhili-Fengtian Wars and the 1932 Shanghai Uprising.\n\nLu's successors have not been nearly as successful and upon the collapse of the League Liangguang also fell into civil war. But Lu Yugguang (Lu Rongting's adopted son) along with other regionalist, reactionary forces emerged on top returning the region to the old status quo. Well, except for one matter.\n\nLiangguang remains independent, with the various warlords under Lu and his allies weighing their options. Shen Hongying, long cultivated by the Southern Zhili in Guangdong as an aligned alternative to the Kuomintang and later Lu Rongting (who in turn aligned with the Northern Zhili against Sun Chuanfang), continues to seek Zhili support. The various Zhili factions have in turn sent emissaries to him, hoping to leverage his influence to bring all of Liangguang into their orbit. Their rivals in China have in turn looked towards Chen Lianbo and his Canton Merchant Government, hoping to use the city's (also known as Guangzhou) outsized influence to gain sway over the region.

Reports of Bribery by [FROM.GetAdjective] Agents
Recruitment methods in China is different compared to other countries that have professional armies, owing the army's fractious nature, with many local warlords forming their own personal army. From street recruitment, forced conscription, and even recruitment through familial connections, local armies are often fueled by the lack of alternative employment and plagued with corruption.\n\nDesertion is a common occurrence among soldiers, whether it be out of self-preservation, disgruntlement due to personal issues, or bribery, making local provincial armies prone to disintegrate when cracks start to show. Thus, the high alert shown by the Young Guangxi junta as a reaction to reports of bribes allegedly offered to rank and file soldiers within the Guangxi Army by [FROM.GetAdjective] agents is natural.\n\nPossibly part of a wider conspiracy to expand their influence within Liangguang, the wave of bribes spread across the military would be spell disaster for Marshal Lu Yuguang and the Young Guangxi Faction should conflict ever emerge between Liangguang and [FROM.GetNameDef], as soldiers would start switching sides en masse the moment battle begins.\n\nTo counter this rot within the army, a comprehensive purge has been suggested by advisors to remove elements that have become compromised. Although it might affect the fighting capabilities of the Guangxi Army in the short term, with the chaotic situation that encompasses the whole of China, loyalty is most important among other traits within a soldier.
Structures begin to rot as bribes are given out unhindered.
Marshal Lu conducts a thorough purge across the military.

[FROM.GetNameDefCap] Accommodates Local Warlords
Although the Kuomintang's invasion was handful for the regional authorities of Liangguang, spontaneous rebellions staged by bandits and warlords across the twin provinces made an already chaotic situation far worse. In spite of this, order returned to Liangguang as these rebellions were crushed alongside the repelling of KMT forces. Broken and defeated, these warlords seemed to have been defanged, without a base of support to rely on with the dissolution of their personal armies.\n\nUnfortunately, [FROM.GetNameDef] has been reported to be accommodating these warlords, providing them with an opportune base of operations to rebuild their support, unreachable to the authorities of Liangguang. With such blatant interference in an already delicate power struggle within Liangguang, a temporary show of unity has been floated around as an avenue to combat [FROM.GetNameDef] and their supporters' rising influence in the region.\n\nFor Marshal Lu Yuguang, the support of his regional opposition, no matter how brief or superficial it may be, is necessary in securing Liangguang's autonomy. However, even with such high stakes, mistrust still exists among the regional strongmen, as personal ambitions of regional dominance still reign in the minds of some.

[FROM.GetNameDefCap] Offers Peace Feelers
The national unification has been the goal of many ever since China fractured with the death of Yuan Shikai. Whether through negotiations or forceful means, all efforts failing to achieve such due to a multitude of reasons. With the collapse of the League of Eight Provinces when Marshal Sun Chuanfang was shot and Qi Xieyuan took over as League-Marshal, the illusion of Chinese unity under Zhili domination was shattered.\n\nAs conflict concludes in the South with the failure of the Kuomintang invasions into Liangguang, a semblance of stability and political unity returned to the twin provinces. In the pursuit of national unification under their leadership, [FROM.GetNameDef] has approached regional authorities in the South, sending peace feelers to gauge the possibility of the reunification between the North and South, long divided in the conflict between revolutionaries and strongmen.\n\nHailed by some with Liangguang as a stepping stone towards a united China, many including Marshal Lu Yuguang and the Young Guangxi junta are skeptical to [FROM.GetLeader]'s sincerity, suspicious that it is nought but an attempt at imprinting their own influence within the region.\n\nStoking public sentiments for peace and opposition to the shedding of any more Chinese blood for pointless regionalist conflicts, those sympathetic to [FROM.GetNameDef] are increasingly gaining the favour of the public in Liangguang, to the chagrin of [ROOT.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName]'s leadership.

Liangguang Stands Defiant!
For most of Chinese history, the South has shown itself to possess a rebellious spirit and constantly defied the North at any opportunity they could, from the Taiping Rebellion to the Xinhai Revolution. This remains true today, as Marshal Lu Yuguang has stood his ground and refused to bend to pressure for Liangguang's alignment with our government.\n\nDespite this temporary setback, our efforts in expanding our influence within the twin provinces has not been in vain, as scattered rebellions are breaking out across the region against the Young Guangxi junta, both from within Marshal Lu's Guangxi Army and those led by our followers in the region. Even so, it is clear that without our intervention, this rebellion would be crushed easily, completely solidifying the Young Guangxi's hegemony over Liangguang.\n\nHence, it would be wise to commence an invasion of Liangguang during this brief moment of instability to safeguard our control over the Pearl of the South. However, there are also concerns within our government that such a blatant act of hostility would incur the distrust of other warlords in other provinces. Whatever the case, the intrigue in Liangguang has become unravelled, and its fate is at hand.
The South was crushed once, we shall crush it again!
Let them keep their autonomy, we shall bide our time.
Rise of the Young Guangxi Faction
Lu Rongting was a mighty warlord in Guangxi, whose star shone so brightly during the National Protection War but slowly fell out of the spotlight during the Constitutional Protection Government. Despite his lowly bandit origin and ultimate defeat in battle, he remained a sort of folk hero to many and after the collapse of the Northern Expedition he was able to stage a political comeback, subduing his rivals (chiefly Shen Hongying) and reasserting control over Liangguang. After his death in 1934, he left his empire to his beloved adopted son Lu Yuguang, who was reluctantly recognised as the new Viceroy of Liangguang by the Zhili and Old Guangxi Clique.\n\nThe real power behind Nanning however is the triumvirate that forms the Young Guangxi Faction. Led by Lu Rongting's other adopted son Ma Ji, the Governor of Guangxi and an associate of Wu Peifu along with Generals Lin Hu and Liu Zhilu, they and their officers dominate the Old Guangxi Clique, and thus by extension the Liangguang government. Once the Young Guard of the Old Guangxi Clique, with most of the older generation having died or retired (including the three Great Heroes of Guangxi - Lu Rongting, Chen Bingkun, and Cen Chunxuan) and the Shen Army Faction having been pushed out, the YGF include a mixture of formally trained military men, ex-bandits, and gentrymen. Conservative, regionalist, and militaristic, the Guangxi Clans are a potent force.\n\nThat has not stopped them from being diplomatic however. The Young Guangxi Faction is noted for being generally respectful of the Guangxi Provincial Assembly and cooperative with Beiyang decrees even if they are suspicious of Zhili overreach. There are civilians and intellectuals sympathetic to their rule, including educators like Ma Junwu or the various Political Science Clique figures they have worked with over the years. Despite fiercely protecting local prerogatives, they also have an anti-imperialist streak, mirroring the rising nationalism in China.
Lu Yuguang and the triumvirs continue Lu Rongting's legacy!
The YGF works to unite all the Guangxi Clans.
Through cooperation, we build a civilian government.
The Shen Army in Shaoguan
Shen Hongying grew up a poor bandit before being recruited into Liu Zhenhuan's Army. During the Second Revolution, he sided with the Beiyang Government and betrayed General Liu, finding employment and promotions under Lu Rongting. After Lu fell from power during the Yue-Gui Wars, he made a play for leadership of Guangxi from his base in Guilin with some support from Wu Peifu and the Zhili Clique, only to be outmaneuvered by Li Zongren and the KMT aligned New Guangxi Clique. Defeated, he fled into exile, but was able to make a return as an Zhili Clique auxiliary after the Northern Expedition. Though the title was promised to multiple warlords at once, the Zhili ultimately settled on Shen as their Governor of Guangdong.\n\nShen never truly was able to rule all of his nominal province, much less all of Liangguang. Guangdong Warlords rallied in Guangzhou under their provincial assembly and refused to recognise him. Meanwhile, he was routed in battle with Lu Rongting and lost Guilin. The loyalty of his deputies is questionable - Li Yaohan and Gu Ringguang are Guangdong warlords who already attempted to break away from him once in 1923, but found themselves once again under his command thanks to pressure from the League. Similarly Yang Chisheng and Yang Ruxuan are Yunnan-born officers, defectors from Yang Ximin's camp who found themselves under the Zhili's employ. And thus, for now at least, they serve Shen.\n\nThe Shen Army Faction also has loose ties to the Political Science Clique and Jingxing Society. However, the PSC are long past their heyday in the 1920s and many of them have gravitated towards Shen's rivals in Guangzhou, most notably Liu Zhenhuan, the mentor Shen fell out with. The Jingxing Society, generally more liberal and Western than the warlords they work with, are only really connected to Shen because of German interests in the League, both of which Shen depends on for power. So long as Mayor Chen Lianbo controls Guangzhou, Shen will never truly rule Guangdong.
Shen Hongying and his close companions consolidate around Guilin.
Without the Zhili, he needs Lu Yuguang to help control his subordinates.
Governor Shen deepens his Guangzhou contacts to press his leadership.
Politics of the Canton City-State
The Canton System ended nearly a century ago, but Guangzhou remains a valuable entrepot. When the Germans occupied it during the Northern Expedition, they installed the ambitious businessman Chen Lianbo as Mayor, a position he retained even after they finally withdrew. Chen, hoping to avoid a Guangxi occupation, hastily rebuilt a Guangdong Army with his rebuilt Merchant Corps as its nucleus.\n\nMerchant Corps exist throughout China, an outgrowth of local Chambers of Commerce. The Governor and the Assembly
Power in Guangdong has been divided between the Shen Army Faction in the North headquartered in Shaoguan and the Chen Lianbo Merchant Government in the South centered around Guangzhou. Shen Hongying, having been driven out of his traditional home base of Guilin, claims to be Governor while Mayor Chen is backed by the Guangdong Provincial Assembly. This stalemate has been enforced by the Lu Rongting and later Lu Yuguang regimes, hoping to keep the wealthier Guangdong divided and submissive towards their Guangxi-based government.\n\nChen Jiongming and his Yue Army's defeat in battle several years ago has created an opening for one to seize the advantage. His forces, situated primarily in the east around Haifeng county, have since disintegrated and his allies hastily absorbed into either Shen Hongying or Chen Lianbo's armies. But the crisscross of Shaoguan-aligned and Guangzhou-aligned villages is unstable, and it often feels like at any moment conflict may break out.\n\nNanning will of course work to prevent either side from destroying the other, but an opportunity to gain an advantage is there, it is just a matter of who will seize it.
The German-trained Shen Army overruns Merchant Militias.
Yue Army veterans unite with their fellow provincials against Guangxi.
Power Struggle Over Old Guangxi
When Lu Rongting was driven out of Nanning by Sun Yat-sen and Chen Jiongming in 1921, it allowed rival Guangxi Clans to challenge him for supremacy across the next few years. While Lu remained dominant at first, Shen Hongying and his men allied with Li Zongren and his clique to defeat him, before General Shen was defeated in turn by the KMT backed New Guangxi Clique. Even after Lu Rongting was able to stage his comeback after the Northern Expedition, animosity lingered between Lu and his Nanning based government and Shen who entrenched himself in Guilin.\n\nWhen the Southern Zhili were distracted by the 1932 Shanghai Uprising, Lu Rongting took advantage of the chaos by driving out Shen Hongying's forces from Guilin and forcing him to recognise him as Viceroy of Liangguang. But with Lu Rongting's death, his adopted sons and their allies lack the same gravitas as the elderly Viceroy Lu had. Shen is plotting to sway the various Guangxi Clans to his side. It will take time, but a showdown feels inevitable between the Viceroy and his Governor.
Lu Rongting's memory keeps Guangxi united.
The Young Marshal is an unworthy successor.

The Qiongya Conflict
Though the worst impacts of Black Monday have begun to fade, there are still many unemployed in Guangzhou and other Guangdong cities, particularly those who lost jobs abroad and were forced to return home to a war-torn country with few opportunities or social services. The Provincial government, for their part, has tried to alleviate the homeless problem by authorising the mint, Department of Finance, and secretly the army to help them find employment.\n\nAfter deliberations, it was decided that they would deploy hired labourers to Leizhou Peninsula and Qiongya Island, territory contested between local warlords and bandits. Many workers were killed as they tried to clear forests due to poor conditions and bandit raids. The region is divided between territory held by rival warlords Lin Hu and Liu Zhenhuan on the mainland, and Qiongya by the autonomous warlord Deng Benyin, who drove out Old Guangxi Clique garrison commander He Fuchang many years ago before entrenching his rule over the island.\n\nLu Yungao, a retired Old Guangxi Military commander noted for his feat of raising 20,000 caddies of military rice and aiding in the recapture of Nanning in 1922, had been tasked with this project but has recently fallen ill. Both Liu and Deng are tied to Chen Lianbo's Guangdong faction and are said to be stepping up their resistance to Old Guangxi interference. Given the difficulties, it may be worth backing down.
It is time we cut our losses in the south.
The Old Guangxi Clique will enforce its rule!
Unity and Self-Rule
Liangguang, the premier province of Southern China, has long been a hotbed of cultural and political sovereignty from the rest of China's many provinces. Since the Song Dynasty's resistance to the Mongols, to the Ming Dynasty's liberation of China from the humiliation of barbarian rule, their long-standing resistance towards Jurchen incursions, to now in the present era - the birthplace of Chinese republicanism and opposition towards the oligarchs and autocrats of Beijing.\n\nThe leaders of the various cliques such as the Old Guangxi Faction and the Shen Army - pride themselves on their regional independence. Abstaining from declaring outright secession as many are indeed truly Chinese nationalists and fervent republicans; they are dedicated to the unification of the Chinese country on their own terms - rather than from party dictatorships like the Kumointang or unrealistic dreams of a federalist country. Rather, unification will have to come from their own terms and likely from the tips of their soldiers' bayonets. The odds are of course ever so stacked against these republicans but when has the cause for freedom ever been achieved easily?
We must unite under Marshal Lu first if we are to unite the nation!
How can we expect self-rule if we cannot rule ourselves?
November 4th and the Warlords
What started as a student movement amongst Beijing's universities (Peking and Tsinghua) in November of 1919, soon escalated into nationwide unrest and protests against imperialism. The leaking and release of various Entente telegram cables promising Japanese and German concessions in Shandong as a result of their declining fortunes in the World War - ignited a fierce anti-imperialist sentiment with many across the country fearing that China was to be carved up once more. The spirit of the Xinhai Revolution, intended to drive out imperialism and to modernise the country, seemed to be on the verge of disintegration following the brief reign of Yuan Shikai and the prospect of a centuries old civilisation torn to shreds at the whims of foreign imperialists.\n\nThe November 4th Protests however did not merely spark the flames for the greater anti-imperialist movement. Passionate revolutionary writers such as Hu Shi, Li Dazhao, and Cai Yuanpei took it upon themselves to embrace the \
It is only through modernisation that we save our nation!
We fight to save not only our nation but our culture!
The Chairman Returns
After enduring a tense voyage, Hu Hanmin, the enigmatic leader of the right wing of the Kuomintang, has finally returned on Chinese soil for the first time in a decade. His brilliant mind and towering figure casts a long shadow over the warlord junta, almost immediately dwarfing them in influence.\n\nA devout revolutionary Hu Hanmin remained largely aloof from the fanfare surrounding his return, greeting the crowds before quickly focusing on more pressing matters. Meeting with various Liangguang power players, including Chen Jitang, Li Zongren, and Chen Mingshu, he has behaved cordially, stating his gratitude for their support whilst keeping thoughts of their crudeness mostly to himself.\n\nOrthodox and studious in approach in interpreting the Three Principles, Hu Hanmin is perhaps the most premier expert on the true intentions of his late mentor, Dr. Sun Yat-sen. A spirited return, Chairman!
The Guangzhou Nationalist Government
When the Kuomintang first retook control over Guangzhou, there were questions about the direction of the revolutionary junta. The issue appears to be settled now, with the rightist officers Chen Jitang and Li Zongren successfully outmaneuvering leftist Chen Mingshu take take control of Liangguang.\n\nThey have invited Hu Hanmin and his exiled rightist central committee to return from Moscow. Hu, a preeminent disciple of Sun Yat-sen, ironically was defeated in a similar power struggle between feuding triumvirs after Sun's death, but he has no intentions of failing this time. He has already announced plans to rebuild the party-state in Guangzhou and prepare for another Northern Expedition to reunite China under the three principles.

Imperialism vs Anti-Imperialism
In an auspicious start to the First Congress of the KMT, Hu Hanmin has let his intellectual prowess flourish. Elaborating the points he made years ago in Linkage of the Three Principles, he has showcased his refined expertise and interpretation of the Three Principles, hitting back at imperialism all around the globe.\n\nPontificating about how the world is a battleground between the two antagonistic forces of imperialism and counter-imperialism, he argued that the capitalist system brewed a militarist and hostile society. Similarly to Marx before him, Hu Hanmin has deemed capitalism in of itself to be the arch-foundation of Western political institutions. Being eternally corrupted by the influence of bureaucratic worshipping of absolute statism, capitalism, and militarism, these states enlist the support of capitalism to advance their imperial glories. In turn, the capitalists feed off the bureaucrats, using them as spokesmen for their system to leech off of nations. In combination with this, both the forces of the bureaucrats and capitalists utilise the military to advance their collective interests and conquer other nations.\n\nFurthermore, Hu stated that this all began in the distant 1500s as the burgeoning colonial empires competed for land and influence, causing the rise of mercantilism. Deeming this a prototype of capitalism, it began to evolve with the political liberalisation and economic laissez-faire in the periods of peace afterwards, culminating in the modern global economic system. Neo-imperialist colonial forces fused domestic nationalism with imperialist goals to subjugate China into a semi-colonial status.\n\nRelating his theory back towards the National Revolution, he made a clear connection between anti-imperialism and Sanmin doctrine, with which it is imbued with knowledge from the past three centuries of revolution, arguing that the Kuomintang must join China with liberation movements worldwide to bring about imperialism's downfall.
Down with Imperialism!
Surely, he cannot be serious?
Nationalism in the East vs West
Like a whirlwind, Hu Hanmin followed his lecture on imperialism today with one about the First Principle of the People, Nationalism. He states that whilst nationalism is not a concept unique to the Principles, the West have corrupted their idea of it, using it as a force of imperialism and slavery. Dr. Sun, by contrast, has turned it into a mechanism of liberation and freedom.\n\nDrawing comparisons between the East and West, Hu stated that the West's primary unit of society is the individual, and its individualism created a vacuum between individual and the state, creating a discord between them. The West, as a result, lacks a \
Nationalism for a new China!

Democracy and Anarchism
Like clockwork, Hu Hanmin began discussing the Second Principle of the People, Democracy. Drawing inspiration from universalism and humanism, the Chairman believes that the Three Principles are the superior method in achieving the ideal state of human society. Universalism, anarchism, and syndicalism, state institutionalism, nationalism and capitalism, have all failed.\n\nWhile the Principle of Nationalism is aimed at attaining freedom and equality for nations all over the world, he stated that freedom and equality at home cannot be achieved without the fulfillment of the Principles of the People's Political Power and Livelihood simultaneously at home. Once all nations achieve this tripartite nationalism, universalism will consequently dawn.\n\nDrawing comparisons between the Principle of Democracy and anarchism, stating that it entails people's control of political power in one nation, and then followed by political equality. Whilst both ideologies share goals in ending the state, anarchism left a void in where political power would vest.\n\nUnder Sanmin, power would be divided between sovereignty and administrative power. The first would be directly vested and exercised in the people, consisting of the four powers of election, legislation, referendum and recall. The second would be indirectly invested in the people through the five yuan: executive, legislative, judicial, examining and supervisory. This five branch government will become the instrument of the people to achieve true self government.\n\nContrary to common misconception, Hu Hanmin argues Proudhon and Kropotkin envisaged repeal of the state, but not the government. With Sanmin, elected government officials and the people would work in tandem for public interest, and with its political power deprived, the state would wither away in favour of absolute personal freedom. Therefore the Three Principles share the ideals of anarchism but the KMT actually gives a feasible programme to achieve them.
Only the Principles can give us democracy.
Walking a fine line, Chairman...
People's Welfare and Socialism
Once more in his flurry of theory, Chairman Hu Hanmin has focused his sights on the Third Principle of the People, People's Welfare. Drawing comparisons between People's Livelihood and the nascent Socialist and Syndicalist movements around the world, he has noted the radical difference between the three in implementation.\n\nWhilst all intended to achieve the ideal society, only the Three Principles were equipped with the instruments to transform these common ideals into reality, of which included land equalisation and capital regulation. Once private ownership of the land and capital is abolished, socioeconomic injustice would go with it, and the cannibalising class struggle would be avoided. Instead of violence, the national operation of conglomerates of railroads alongside mining and steel companies would create profits directly for the people.\n\nTaking a brief aside in his oration, Hu Hanmin drew a comparison of anarchism and Marxism, praising anarchists like Bakunin and Kropotkin. This anarchic socialism retained the structure of society and individuality of the person, the Chairman even claimed that Bakunin-Kropotkin's school was close to Sanmin, as opposed to Marxian proletarian dictatorship and belief in anarchist opposition to any form of social order. Concluding, Hu declared that governments which enjoyed Marxian political dictatorship and thorough control of the nation's wealth were more inclined to totalitarianism.\n\nConcluding his speech on People's Welfare, Hu Hanmin quoted Dr. Sun, in that: \
To aid the People!
Need I remind you, we're not the Left.
Development of the Revolution
Having secured favourable, if perhaps sometimes reluctant official party endorsements for his prior statements, Hu Hanmin has shifted his attention towards the practical implementation of the Principles, barely giving anyone time to rest after discussions.\n\nThe Chairman began by stating that the policies of land equalisation and capital regulation would gear the national economy towards eventual public ownership of national wealth, Gongyou, and that the two arch-enemies of economic equity, both state alongside private capitalism, would be subsequently eliminated. Through joint government-people economic endeavours, with close cooperation between the two would finally lead to Gongyou.\n\nScientific Production would reduce the workload, and increase the productivity of the people further liberating their minds and energy from the drudgery of work. Through this the people could devote themselves to creative, cultural and public work, and eventually step up to take on local responsibilities which would pave the way for self government. Once this scientific production is achieved, common enjoyment of social wealth (Gongxiang) would be underway.\n\nHailing scientification as speeding up the process to achieve Gongxiang, in the ideal world envisioned by Hu Hanmin, the ancient ideals of \
Steward of the Revolution!

The Revolutionary Future
In a bold display of his renewed authority as Chairman of the Central Executive Committee, and nominal leader of the Right wing forces of the Kuomintang, Hu Hanmin has unequivocally declared that Dr. Sun's will was for the Three Principles of the People - Nationalism, Democracy, People's Welfare - to be enacted immediately, simultaneously and at every stage of the National Revolution\n\nDespising the pitfalls and vile deals that his rivals Chiang Kai-shek and Wang Jingwei made, Hu Hanmin insists that even during the stage of military rule, every principle should be enacted lest the loyal revolutionary soldiers turn into merely new warlords taking a more pleasant form. In fact, he has placed special emphasis that the party should never neglect the people's interests during the stages of revolution, most important of which being the period of tutelage.\n\nHammering in his credentials as having the most orthodox comprehension of Dr. Sun's ideals, Hu Hanmin has further stated that the new National Revolution will follow the National Reconstruction Plan laid out by Dr. Sun in unyielding absolutism. The Constitutional Stage then would only be enacted in provinces once its counties had achieved local self-government. Once more than half of the provinces have reached that stage, a national convention would be assembled to promulgate a national constitution.\n\nIn his concluding statement, Hu Hanmin expressed his desire to achieve Datong - the Great Harmony - saying that agape should be cherished as the foundation in man's endeavour for existence. While many of his warlord patrons had already begun to have second thoughts about inviting his return, for a shining moment the generals all felt small as Hu's words landed on the audience. With his eclectic blend of Chinese social ideals, Christian socialism and modern scientific advancement, Hu promises to combine them all into a true formula for man to achieve Great Harmony. Basking in revolutionary glory, the audience roared in applause.
To a bright future!
The Fate of the Fourth Army
Amongst the attendees of the First Conference sits a selection of individuals most disagreeable to the new alignment of our government. The former MinGan officers associated with Chen Mingshu and his old Tenth Division were invited to attend the conference as an expression of unity between the wings of the Kuomintang.\n\nAligned with the centre-left, Chen and his allies find themselves stuck between a rock and a hard place. Finding few friends and considerable ire within the conference, it is clear that they have been outflanked by Chen Jitang. At the same time, they fear repercussions from their former leftist allies, who have long condemned them for working with the right in any capacity.\n\nTheir apparent duplicity has finally caught up with them, with Hu Hanmin being presented with the option to oust them from the conference for their ties to Wang Jingwei. Many of their former rivals in Guangdong like Chen Jitang have pressed the Chairman for their expulsion already charting out plans for an aggressive military campaign and political purge.\n\nHu however has been more circumspect. An olive branch would weaken Wang's committee and counterbalance his dependence on Chen Jitang, and if successful could further showcase his capability to lead the Revolution. On the other hand, Chen Mingshu is known to be a vehicle for leftist infiltration and tolerating him may have reverberations that follow.

The Fourth Army Retreats
Reports have arrived on Wang Jingwei's desk of various leftist officers from the province of Guangdong having fled persecution from the incumbent government there. Their leaders are General Chen Mingshu and his lieutenants Jiang Guangnai and Cai Tingkai, all who carry valuable skill sets and knowledge that could be put to great use in the future.\n\nDespite this, Chen Mingshu has evidently proved himself to be a failure, having lost control of one of the most valuable provinces in China and his political moment crushed by Hu Hanmin and his warlord allies. His inability to complete his mission however is hardly the only obstacle in his reconciliation - many reports indicate his wavering loyalty to our central committee and his foolish flirtations with the right.\n\nThus, their prospects of redemption are tenuous. Reportedly our key agent in Guangdong, Chen Mingshu, has defected to the right, bringing much of the Fourth Army with him.\n\nAlready various figures within the party are rife with anger from the betrayal of what was supposed to be one of our most elite armies, and all support for Chen Mingshu and his movement being cut. News from Guangzhou
The Kuomintang's defeat in the Northern Expedition did not cause the Chinese Nationalist Party to collapse. While the Party's main body fled overseas to the radical hotbed of Paris, Hu Hanmin and the right-wing leading members of the KMT sought refuge in Moscow. A cadre of important KMT leaders professing their dedication and loyalty to Sun Yat-sen's revolution created a separate rival government-in-exile with a Central Committee.\n\nRussia offered a place of refuge for the Chinese exiles due to a shared hatred of Germany's imperialism. For us, Germany has torn apart our great country, plundered our resources, and seeks to keep Russia weak. For the Chinese, the Germans have carved up their country with their colonial enclaves and reinstalled a deplorable imperial dynasty while crushing their desires for independence. It was only natural that our peoples would come together and look towards realising the destruction of both Japanese and German imperialism to pursue a world free from the chains of Berlin and Tokyo.\n\nFurthermore, both the Chinese exiles and the Russian government are united in their own shared distrust of bolshevism. In 1928 the Kremlin chose to cooperate with the KMT government-in-exile in tacit approval. Support mostly came in donations and military aid procurement although relations between Russia and the Chinese nationalists faltered during the CER War in which Fengtian victory led to more extremist Russian factions to denounce the Chinese exiles in Russia.\n\nNonetheless by 1936, these angers have mostly subsided and we have received news that the KMT has seized victory in their former province of Guangdong led by the Russian exiles. We wish the best in their pursuits for national unification and only time will see whether or not the Kuomintang can truly rise again.
We will observe them with great interest.
Internal Machinations
Following the establishment of [ROOT.GetLeader]'s new Revolutionary government in [ROOT.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName], the Chairman has made great strives to maintain cohesion throughout the movement, and avoid blatant favouritism. Even in his exiled committee in Moscow, [ROOT.GetLeader] maintained strict orthodoxy in relation to government structure, never allowing an official - or unofficial - second-in-command to make themselves evident. Following Sunism down to the very roots, in the current stage of the revolution only the Chairman of the Party should be the primary influence on the direction it takes, as the Prime Minister and Premier of the Executive Yuan do not exist until the constitution stage following unyielding party doctrine.\n\nThat being stated however, does not mean the Chairman doesn't lean on a variety of individuals for advice and their unique perspectives, in times where his own proves faulty. Much has changed since [ROOT.GetLeader] last set foot on Chinese soil, and the power players in Canton are in some ways different whilst also remaining the same. The Divided Revolution
Perhaps the greatest irony of the Kuomintang is that despite their insistence on strict, one-party orthodoxy (founded as a reaction to the unwieldiness of the old Tongmenghui), the Party has always been plagued by ceaseless infighting. Or perhaps it is their authoritarian nature itself that lends to such factionalism. Either way, the heirs of Dr. Sun were fighting over his inheritance even before he died, with the party first fragmenting into regionalist blocs, and then into right and left factions under Hu Hanmin and Wang Jingwei. Chiang Kai-shek soon formed his Whampoa military based clique in the party centre, right and left further divided into smaller squabbling factions, and so forth - a division that continued even after a long exile.\n\nIn Liangguang, the party remains fiercely divided. Subduing the two provinces required the combined efforts of three armies, each with their own vision for China. Still, it is not too late for [ROOT.GetLeader] to heal rifts, even if the scars will remain.\n\nThe Guangdong left is a broad array of disgruntled urban syndicalists, radicalised peasants, and more moderate leftist revolutionaries. A targeted effort to court the last group may be what is needed to isolate the extremists and present a message of reconciliation.\n\nOutside of Liangguang, republicanism retains plenty of sympathisers, including many wealthy liberal donors who felt marginalised by the party's reorganisation and drift away from its more democratic, Western-inspired roots. The largest faction directly associated with the Kuomintang is the Overseas Chinese Commission, dominated by but not exclusive to Sun Fo and his allies. Their ties across Chinese diaspora communities across Southeast Asia make them invaluable.\n\nOf course, the Kuomintang's right is itself quite factionalised. Focusing on shoring up our own support base and fostering unity within our existing coalition perhaps is ultimately better than making hollow promises to those who deserted us.
A United Front with the left will mend divides.

Unscrupulous Influences
In the course of the National Revolution, there has always been the ever present question regarding finances for the Government's operations. In the past the Kuomintang have had a variety of financial backers of varying degrees of commitment, however in the present imperialist world that has an overwhelmingly unfriendly opinion of our movement, and without posturing ourselves as lapdogs to Paris or London, we are bereft of options to fund our government - not aided by the recent global economic crisis. An enterprising option that is unfortunately widely used by regional warlords within China is to influence the trade of opium within and without the province, and our warlord allies have taken two wildly different approaches to such matters.\n\nIn Guangxi, the New Guangxi Clique's Provincial Government Office of Opium Suppression has - as the name suggests - taken to heavily taxing and suppressing the opium trade in the province, raking in such absurd revenues as to account for 50% of total provincial revenue per annum. However in an opposing direction to this in Guangdong, Chen Jitang has employed the strategy of influencing and controlling the trade of opium, using a variety of backhanded tactics such as suppressing smuggling yet keeping the stocks of opium to distribute to the Opium Suppression Bureau when needed.\n\nSuch actions have - perhaps indirectly - lead to a signifiant loss of revenue for the New Guangxi Clique, as opium commonly flows between provinces, and Li Zongren has sent a flurry of demands to [ROOT.GetLeader] insisting that Chen Jitang must cease his brazen infringement on the Guangxi economy. On the other hand, besmirching Chen Jitang with such an action could result in a burning of invaluable bridges needed to maintain course in the National Revolution.

In such circumstances, it is natural that they are attracted to mass movements to direct their fury and frustration. The Sinews of War
Within the province of Guangdong, there was always an ever independent streak amongst its residents - as indicated by being a hotbed of revolutionaries - and this has gone as far as to extend even into local currency. Continuously, the provincial governments of Guangdong minted their own special currency functioning in parallel to the central currency controlled by Beijing. This is not a new practice however, as even as far back as before the turn of the century, Guangdong had minted its own local provincial silver currency.\n\nAlbeit with the unification of the country steadfast in its progress, an influx of counterfeit and regional currencies have started circulating in our economy, with many maintaining questionable value. We could order the Banks to accept all currencies as comparative in value, and in exchange issue a more centralised currency to eventually achieve equalisation in our economy, slowly phasing out the illegal regional currencies. However this would be a slow and gradual process, and could still present a headache for years to come.\n\nAnother option presenting itself to our government is to instead mint an entirely new set of central currency, reforming the currency centrally around one in which we can control to supply and demand. Whilst this would naturally lead to much outcry from the citizenry as their currency may soon become worthless, it might be the necessary step required to truly unite the nation.\n\nInternally the Kuomintang are much divided on such matters, with the regionalists within the New Guangxi and New Guangdong cliques both pulling their weight behind a gradual phasing out of regional currencies, as opposed to the slash and burn centralisation desired by the Elders Clique and its allies.
Maintain acceptance of all currency.
Mint a new set of currency, for the whole nation.
Health of the People, Health of the Nation
Since the early days of the movement, the Kuomintang have linked Chinese public health initiatives with the spiritual health of the nation. Fitting, as their founder was also educated as a physician before he strove to heal the sickness plaguing the nation. In Guangdong, the various party intellectuals and the provincial administration have continued existing health programs, a cause célèbre on both the left and right, many which began during the party's initial 1920s rule in Guangzhou.\n\nIn Guangxi, where there is a widespread lack of healthcare access, an even more ambitious project is underway. The New Guangxi clique has begun a centrally planned effort, dividing the province into public health regions. Each region was to have a provincially funded hospital, opening new ones if none existed, expected to provide inoculations, collect health statistics, erect quarantine stations, and provide various other services. To support this expanded system, public health committees has been organised to manage it, and Guangxi University has opened a new medical school to provide additional personnel, and a drug factory has been opened in Wuzhou to produce medicine, bandages, and cosmetics.\n\nThese hospitals would be the centre of the region's healthcare system, orbited by dozens of planned district clinics to serve the countryside. To work around a lack of trained doctors and funds, there are plans to place a para-medical officer in each village. In many areas, and even within Kuomintang intellectual circles, traditional Chinese medicine by local healers and apothecaries remains popular, adding another dimension to the healing process. Its practitioners claim their ways not only heal the body but also the spirit.\n\nDespite high hopes, the actual efficacy of these programs are ultimately contingent on the amount of funding they receive from the party-state's already strained budget. Moreover, in the near-term, the actual yield of these projects seem more political than practical.
Traditional Chinese practices help the people make do in trying times.

Cadres, Cooperatives and the Countryside
To mobilise the people, the nationalist government has promoted the expansion of cooperatives as a means of reforming China's local governments. While the KMT is hardly alone in bemoaning the demise of the Chinese village, their fears that the new cooperatives will drift from the Party's charted course has pushed them to take a strong hand, with close involvement at every stage. According to party officials, these cooperatives fulfil the six responsibilities of local government during tutelage: First, compiling a modern census. Second, to break down notions of private property. Third, financing public works and infrastructure development. Fourth, improve grain management, grain storage, and welfare services. Fifth, open new lands for cultivation and reforestation. And Sixth, promote local self-government.\n\nThe cooperatives themselves are designed with parallel structures to the provincial and national government, with Credential Committees modelled after the Examination Yuan, Management Committees acting akin to the Executive and Judicial Yuans, and Inspection Committees imitating the Control Yuan. The Kuomintang theorise that the people's participation in these organs would convert villagers into citizens, educating them on the Four Rights set by Dr. Sun.\n\nOn the ground, these efforts at establishing cooperatives have received a mixed reception, with many young idealists wholeheartedly embracing the programme with stride while more cynical comrades resist these programs, fearing the consequences prematurely handing power to illiterate bumpkins. On Class and Schools of Thoughts on Mores
In his skyrocketing popularity, [ROOT.GetLeader] is finally able to put action behind the ideas he has formulated during his exile, and even further before during the 1920s, and as such a revival of the Chairman's ideas has begun to popularise them amongst his followers and the broader Kuomintang movement. Some of the most notable, have been brought to the forefront.\n\nOne of the most peculiar concepts to spur from this time in his life, the Chairman argues that China was always a class society, with the ruling classes (Emperor, Father, Husband) imposing their ethics on the ruled classes (Minister, Son, Wife) to the former's advantage. Simultaneously, they exercised absolute authority, and so consequently there was a class struggle as the ruled classes attempted to overturn traditions that were irrational, arbitrary or inhumane. Unfortunately little has changed since the Qin dynasty as the production methods have similarly remained unchanged.\n\nMoving to a broader scope, the Chairman states that historically there were 3 stages of development. Pre-Qin, there were Five Codes, or the Five Teachings: the father should act with righteousness, the mother with benevolence, the elder brother with sibling love, the younger brother with respect, and the son with filial piety. Following into the Warring States period, Confucius and Mencius created the Five Ethics in order to govern the princes, however this was distorted into the Three Bonds, which demanded total submission to the Emperor, the Father, and the Husband, lasting for 2000 years.
Hu Hanmin's wisdom knows no bounds!
Linking Qin Legalism towards Women's Liberation
Discussing the connection between the economy and moral precepts in society, [ROOT.GetLeader] argues that they went through three phases: During the pre-late period, where Clan and family bonds tied people to common land and resources, in which the Five Teachings fit this best. After the Zhou, socio-economic dislocation and the development of a class of landless peasants resulted in the Five Ethics, hoping to restore peace and order in society. After the Qin, China returned to Clan orientation, paternal authority rising to absolutism, and so led to the creation of the Three Bonds Mores.\n\nTo solve such a travesty, the Chairman theorised that the rise of the Clan society was due to the agrarian economy of primitive society, and when private land ownership emerged combined, so too did a socio-economic gap emerge. Believing that the industrial revolution disrupted the patriarchy by devaluing male physical strength, he also believed that the Clan system - alongside the Modern Family unit - would unravel as Capitalism unravelled, and therefore the best solution would be for every member in society to take up a profession most compatible to one's talent and ability.\n\nWith a final footnote, the Chairman argued that a contradiction existed in the modern nucleus family, wherein women were bound to choose between tending to household chores and taking up a profession, and so the natural trend would be headed for women's liberation from confinement to their participation in the workforce.
Hu Hanmin's wisdom knows no bounds!
He is truly lost in his theories.
The Knight of Guangdong
Despite the fall from the throne that Hu Hanmin has experienced in recent times, Chen Jitang has continued working feverishly to ensure that despite the loss of his patron, the New Guangdong Clique shall not fall with him. In his efforts to rebound his popularity and continue to drum up support, Chen Jitang has undertaken a series of initiatives strengthening his clique's position within the nation.\n\nRecently, the governor has promised to his citizenry to ease the tax burden to soothe their troubles, however it becomes evidently clear that he has no intention of doing so; for his operations on the national stage require many funds to support them. Initially applied whenever provincial revenue was low, the tumultuous period of recent national politics has led to the governor transitioning the levies and taxes to a more permanent force within the province. In some cases, there has even been a tax applied to excrement.\n\nWorking with his close ally Qu Fangpu, the governor has begun to centralise the collection of the taxes, opening them up to bidding and ensuring that the provincial government would receive a cut. However, some issues have arisen as an additional fee is incurred in districts that host military units due to infighting. Luckily the governor has set up a committee to help oversee the distribution of revenues gained in such a manner. Controversially, the governor has enacted a strict opium and gambling tax.\n\nDespite the dire need for funds and revenue, Chen Jitang still has his obligation to end miscellaneous levies, and therefore has publicly enacted policies to reduce them - however most levies are just replaced by another, and so the burden has not truly been alleviated. Pawns and Puppetmasters

Moderates and Extremists
Having its roots in the 1924 Reorganisation of the Kuomintang, the Western Hills Clique can be linked back to the original Tongmenghui, with its more western-style revolutionary orientation carrying a significant mass of various political opinions. Truly however, the clique formally came into existence during the aforementioned Reorganisation, greatly opposing CSP integration. The most outspoken opponents at the time were softly removed post Reorganisation, although most within the clique held their tongues out of deference for Dr. Sun.\n\nWith Sun's death, the nominal members of the Western Hills Clique closed ranks, and formed resolutely in opposition to continued cooperation between the Kuomintang and Chinese Syndicalist Party. Factionalism grew within the clique itself however, as a split emerged between the radical and moderate wings, with the latter seeking rapprochement with Wang Jingwei and the Kuomintang left whilst expelling the CSP. The former won out, nevertheless, and the growing rift between the party's wings led to the titular Western Hills Conference with the intent to be held in Beijing.\n\nWhilst nominally superior in power and influence within the clique, the radicals have accepted the \

Free from the Bindings
Having risen to national prominence in recent times, the Western Hills Clique's role as a minor partner and ally of Hu Hanmin's Elders Clique is no longer as apparent as it seems. Whilst still pertinent to the Chairman's vision for the nation, many within the clique are clambering for them to become an independent force within the party, not requiring the goodwill and patronage of Hu Hanmin to have any impact on Chinese politics. This notwithstanding, the relationship between Hu Hanmin and the Western Hills Clique has been an amicable and steady one, originally forming an alliance due to Western Hills needing a strong leader to fight back against the syndicalist encroachment of the Kuomintang, their bond developed further as both the Elders Clique and Western Hills Clique began to comprise the majority of positions within the exiled Moscow Committee.\n\nNoted for their influences from the late Song Jiaoren's vision for the party and China; a nation in which constitutionalism is enshrined in its core values, and with a strong parliamentary system, the Western Hills Clique and their dynamic with Hu Hanmin seem to perfectly mirror and replicate Song Jiaoren's old personal clashes with his boss Sun Yat-sen, with the clique's leadership comprised of various close friends of the Chairman. In their new prominence, Ju Zheng, Lin Sen and Zou Lu are all struggling to come to terms with the new strength of their group, split between personal loyalties and conviction. Revolutionary Pallor
For the last few couple of days a strange atmosphere surrounds most provincial government buildings in our territory, even in [ROOT.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName]. They appear eerily desolate and judging by the clerks and officials' faces inside, the air feels freezing, even though the heating and the electricity - where it's available - seems to be in working condition. Our once proud, but now faded banner sways in the light wind, tangling together from time to time, but nobody is there to adjust it - the people of the street pass by it with bowed down heads, perhaps at an even quicker pace then one usually walks with. Obviously they couldn't care less about the flag or our government which promised them a new and fairer China, and failed them - for the second time.\n\nThe widespread cynicism of the population against the Kuomintang has already crept into every part of the functioning of the state. Li Zongren was forced to notice this during a recent military inspection of the Second Army headquarters. It is undeniable that the veteran general did not mind the movement's zeal waning, since it was all limiting Hu Hanmin's influence. But perhaps he has undermined Hu's authority too much? Maybe he should have been slower to build his own vision because now it's just going to collapse before he can do anything? It's hard to say what was going through Li's mind as he listened to the joint briefing of division commanders in a wooden hut, gazing into the distance at a white sun flag slowly fading into the morning fog.\n\nBut Li was not alone in reaching this conclusion. His rival in every sense of the word, Hu Hanmin has also noticed the general scepticism and doubt coming from the population, and not reacting to this very serious warning would be a political shot in the foot. Several populist measures and a campaign plan for the rapid social-political mobilisation of the populace has been drawn up, but it really begs the question if this isn't too little too late.
Is this some kind of a curse?
An urgent exorcism is needed!

A Loyal Opposition

The main points of criticism are the widespread corruption and high taxes, not necessarily unrelated to each other. One surprising fact is that the one of the main criticism came not from Li Zongren, who is openly antagonistic to the chairman, but from two senior figures in the Western Hills clique, Lin Sen and Ju Zheng. They argue that poor governance and corruption are the inherent evils of the early period but we must not overlook these problems. Both are actually loyal supporters of Hu, so it's quite a twist, but it's not necessarily a betrayal, rather perhaps a sign that Hu Hanmin's influence is not as widespread in the Kuomintang as he is trying to show to the wider party.\n\nAs we march everyday forward towards our goal of unified China, it's clear we can't and we won't reach it without the necessary sacrifices. The Party and the Army
A recent hearing on military budget was unexpectedly quite eventful. Hu Hanmin's relentless questions about the military's overspending put Bai Chongxi, Li Zongren's lieutenant and de facto aide de camp, in a rather awkward position, to the extent that Li himself had to intervene to get him out of the predicament. The chairman has always been sceptical about militarism and the army and has never seen it as a state-leading element. Ideally, he believes, civilians should manage the day-to-day affairs of the party and the state. As the separation of these roles is currently not clear enough, there is constant conflict which is more or less visible to the public, but this hearing clearly showed Hu Hanmin's strengthened position and influence within the KMT.\n\nAs the party grows more powerful and energetic day by day we shouldn't forget the ordinary people who rally to our flag. A Splintering Movement
The fruits of Hu Hanmin's and the Kuomintang's tireless efforts are clearly paying off. Our banner flies prideful not only in [ROOT.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] but also in many other cities in South China and proudly heralds the triumphs of our movement. The general mood among the common people is quite enthusiastic, and it would appear that our envisioned new China is no longer a question of if but when.\n\nHowever, the current situation is not without its problems. Perhaps out of over-enthusiasm, building on our past achievements, more and more voices are calling for various reforms, from within and outside government. This has put our leadership in a very difficult position because these reforms are in most cases quite a bit more radical than what our current vision would allow, therefore these voices are left unsatisfied. In turn this has resulted in a wilful population that is difficult to control, the most typical manifestation of which is the emergence of several self-organising revolutionary circles. These organisations are completely local, separate from each other and all believe and trust in the party, but it is questionable whether, if the trend of the growth of our revolutionary zeal continues, they won't give us more headaches.\n\nAnother downside is that it's getting harder and harder to get cooperation from the high command of the army. They, led by Li Zongren, are increasingly sceptical about this growth of zealousness, but there's not much they can actually do about it, outside of the structure of the military where several reorganisations are to be expected in the coming weeks.

Apex of the Revolution
In recent subcommittee meetings on more than a few occasions, Hu Hanmin and several other senior members were shouted down by the other members, which quickly resulted these meetings turning into a screaming match between the more radical camps.
