On the political councils he also prefers - rather uncharacteristically - to sit quietly, without criticising others or speaking ill of them, one would might think he doesn't care what happens there, but this couldn't be further from the truth. For several days now, he's been obsessing over the problem of excessive zeal within the party. Due to the extreme revolutionary energy, the Kuomintang has started to fragment and is showing signs of collapsing under its own weight. It is now difficult to define the course or to control the various local branches of the party mainly due to the self-organising revolutionary circles that are not following the party line in the strict sense and standing outside the party. There are already reports of local incidents where fellow party members lynched each other over ideological differences or patrolled the streets in groups to seek out individuals they perceived as not sympathetic to the KMT.\n\nHu Hanmin is now facing the enormous task of reigning in something that should never have been let out of control, and there will be a high price to pay for it. But he has to do something about it and do it fast, before the fires of revolutionary zeal consume from within all that we have achieved with blood and sweat in the past.

Guangxi Reconstruction Research Committee

the Guangxi Reconstruction Research Committee has decided present their findings publicly with Li Zongren's proud backing. The committee was established by the New Guangxi clique to organise a plan for wartime reconstruction and gather post-war reconstruction ideas as well, envisioned as a high level incubator of ideas.\n\nTheir ideas heavily emphasise the need to prepare the nation mentally for war through heavy doses of propaganda in school civics classes, speeches by magistrates, featuring of propaganda films, and solicitation of public support for the troops. The committee has also stressed the need to improve and maintain morale among the conscripts by treating them well (as opposed to the regular abuse most Chinese armies have been built on), providing them quickly with food, drink, blankets, uniforms, and mosquito nets as well as friendly veteran officers. Officers have been advised to scout ahead to prepare clean camp grounds, limiting marches to at most 70 li a day.\n\nOn the flip side, they have recommended aggressive measures against desertion, discouraging them with arrests and harsh punishments. The traditional penalty of imprisoning a family members for a month was deemed inadequate, though the practice of allowing conscripts to pay for a replacement should be allowed to continue. Additional funding has been requested for these measures on top of further initiatives to expand military production and training.\n\nDespite their humble and sometimes crude warlord heritage, the New Guangxi Clique has proven quite adept at politics themselves. They have been working to impress others in the military brass with these plans, working to present themselves as the proud defenders of the nation. While it is unclear if their the decision to share this plan was a hasty response to their rivals in the Shanghai Committee, what is clear is that Li Zongren has been happy to soak up the spotlight. It is clear that his ambitions have grown far beyond that of Guangxi, though the austere nature of his agenda has worried many.

The Headmaster's Ghost
The legacy of the storied Whampoa Military Academy is a complicated one. Founded by Sun Yat-sen and many leading party figures including Hu Hanmin, Wang Jingwei, Liao Zhongkai, Dai Jitao, among others as a means of creating an army purely loyal to the party-state (as opposed to the fickle loyalties of warlord armies), it quickly began to develop an independent identity of its own. Military men with political ambitions like Chiang Kai-shek, Deng Yanda, and Zhou Enlai began to carve out their own power-base within the school, binding the radical zeal of the cadets to their cults of personality through complex chains of loyalty, mentorship, and fraternity.\n\nWhile plenty can be said about the leftists of the academy, an iconic fixture of the academy's contemporary legacy is its role in spawning the Kuomintang's far right branch. The province that initiated the ultimately doomed Northern Expedition has long been one home to much strife, suffering, and warfare during the Warlord Era. Ripe with conditions to foment its own regionalist Kuomintang party members, the party cadres from Hunan have been some of its most loyal, and some of its most mixed. Stalwarts of the party such as Cheng Qian, who has long endured the path of revolutionary struggle, or the amiable Tan Yankai, one of Dr. Sun's most reliable allies, owe the province their allegiance. Similarly, radical firebrands based within the agrarian province's rural populace, such as Tang Shengzhi, have proven to be the backbone of the Leftist faction's own struggle.\n\nAnd so the question is raised, with the occupation of Changsha and practical dominion over Hunan Province, what shall be the fate of the local party members? The leftwing notwithstanding, many of the younger party cadres, and especially the New Guangxi Clique, have opposed any and all considerations of true amnesty to the Hunan Kuomintang. Insisting that their time has passed, or otherwise that they are a spent force willing to parley with Beiyang.\n\nIn opposition, much of the older party membership, such as Western Hills, or the Elders, have opened communication channels with Cheng Qian and his 6th Army, alongside Tan Yankai and his 2nd. Feasibly vouching of their continued loyalty to the ideals of the National Revolution, and their relative apolitical attitude in inter-party affairs, their calls for integration have rung true throughout much of the neutral party membership. Yet concerns of enabling Old Guard domination of the party apparatus remain, championed most fiercely by Li Zongren and Chen Jitang.
Does the old tiger have any bite left?
At best, splitters. At worst, traitors.
The Estranged Northerners
A most curious political development during the Warlord Era is that of the emergence of the Guominjun. Originally a faction within the Zhili Clique loyal to Feng Yuxiang, they would slowly chart their own course of mass-peasant populism, even if particularly heavy-handed. Betraying the Zhili in the Second Zhili-Fengtian War, the Guominjun managed to even seize Beijing. However, with a coalition built against them by the Beiyang warlords, they would be beaten back.\n\nRetreating to the Northwest, Feng Yuxiang would resign briefly, and they would lose the loyalty of the Ma Families in their own rebellion. Detested by the local Mongols, and unable to break out of the region, they would enter into an uneasy agreement and alliance with Yan Xishan for the duration of the interwar. Having exchanged positive affirmations of the KMT, Feng would take the Wuyuan Oath, proclaiming loyalty to Sanminzhuyi, and the Party. Albeit unable to stop the failure of the Northern Expedition, he has continued to champion what little aspects of the Kuomintang remain within the North.\n\nHaving occupied their homebase of Shaanxi, the Guominjun have come out in droves, bearing white flags and offers of sincere dedication and loyalty to the party, nation, and army. Many of those pessimistic and skeptical within the party have called into question Feng Yuxiang's sincerity in his proclamations, considering his tendency to betray allies at a moment's notice, or even accusing him of being an ally of the left. Others, more optimistic, or otherwise already attached to the GMJ, such as Yu Youren or the Western Hills Clique, have insisted upon their revolutionary dedication. Late converts as they may be, they championed the revolution in their own way, and fought their own revolutionary struggle arm-in-arm with the masses. Many have even brought up their role with hosting the Western Hills Conference, the Inner Mongolian Kuomintang, or their work with Political Science, as proof of their merits.
Against better judgement, welcome aboard!
Have we considered how many times they've betrayed an ally?

The Nanning Mutiny
The successful completion of the Northern Expedition has given hope to leftists across China that a new dawn is rising. The fall of Beijing, the traditional capital of the nation, is seen as a sign of the old order being toppled and has inspired many to rise up against oppressors nation-wide.\n\nSeveral New Guangxi Clique generals have taken initiative, staging an attempted putsch in Nanning against Li Zongren. Led by cousins Li Mingrui, Yu Zuoyu, and Yu Zuobai, and aided by leftist activist Wei Baqun, they have quickly seized control of the Guangxi Teaching Corps, the Guangxi Guard Fourth Brigade and the Fifth Brigade. The New Guangxi clique has scrambled to fight back, and violence is expected to escalate.\n\nWhile the truce between left and right has always been tenuous and resistance against New Guangxi clique rule has been ongoing, especially in rural regions, the sheer speed and tenacity of the rebellion has stunned even the most dedicated ideologues of the party. Few had expected that red sympathies in the province extend even to senior level officers. The city, the capital of Guangxi Province, has been placed under martial law as various units chaotically flood in. The incident, too big to hide, is expected to inspire unrest across Liangguang and will have major ramifications for the region.
Nanning falls to the rebels in a determined push!
The socialists are driven back to the countryside.
Guangxi in Flames
The successful completion of the Northern Expedition has given hope to leftists across China that a new dawn is rising. The fall of Beijing, the traditional capital of the nation, is seen as a sign of the old order being toppled and has inspired many to rise up against oppressors nation-wide.\n\nSeveral New Guangxi Clique generals have taken initiative, staging an attempted putsch in Nanning against Li Zongren. Led by cousins Li Mingrui, Yu Zuoyu and Yu Zuobai, and aided by leftist activist Wei Baqun, they have quickly seized control of the Guangxi Teaching Corps, the Guangxi Guard Fourth Brigade and the Fifth Brigade. The New Guangxi clique has scrambled to fight back, successfully killing Li Mingrui in the fighting, and violence has quickly spiralled into various uprisings in Baise and Longzhou. As conflicting orders and scrambled communications throw the Guangxi army into disarray, the entire province plunges into pandemonium.\n\nLocal leftist committee members have begged [CHI.GetLeader] to intervene, but any response would be complicated by the fact that [CHI.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] has already recognised Li Zongren as Governor of Guangxi. Withdrawing it would have dire consequences for the central government's legitimacy, though standing by him would dent our credibility among socialists.\n\nWith the central army overstretched and central authority limited in Liangguang, the task of actually supporting these rebels would likely fall on the neighbouring Guangdong Army. Indeed, many speculate that the Fourth Army may take action on its own initiative, sparing us any culpability for the outcome but potentially making [ROOT.GetLeader] appear impotent. The consequences of an intervention may be dire: involving Guangdong will destabilise all of Liangguang. Taking leadership therefore carries its own risks, but so does remaining silent on the matter and letting things shake out on its own.

Chen Mingshu Rides Out
General Chen divided his forces carefully, hand-picking which divisions to take with him into Guangxi and meticulously which ones he could afford (and trust) to leave behind to stand guard. Within a few days, the van was already past Wuzhou, hugging the river for safety and supplies. Scattered Guangxi formations stood ahead of them, but few put up any resistance - it will not be long before they arrive in Nanning.\n\nNevertheless, Chen's indecision has already cost him. Faith in him has been falling in Guangzhou and reports of savage fighting in Guangxi have stirred fears of a prolonged quagmire among many military families. Stories have also spread of the poor behaviour of the Fourth Army, slanderous tales of their cowardice and how they loot from the poor while protecting the gentry like thugs. Many of these stories invoke similar tropes of older warlord armies, in fact some are simply retellings of older propaganda.\n\nEither way, General Chen's army lumbers on fighting not just their opponents in Guangxi but also a battle for their reputation at home. Chen Mingshu has sought to keep up appearances by making multiple public appearances at stops along the way and encouraging his men to write favourable letters to their friends and families at home. But the rumours have been taking their toll...
The honest and true always recognise the truth.
No good deed goes unpunished...
Tan Pingshan's Pronouncement
Tan Pingshan, as acting head of Provincial Government while Chen Mingshu is on campaign, has seen his hand suddenly strengthened considerably now that the bulk of the Fourth Army is out of the province. His power is hardly unlimited, the council still remains effectively deadlocked, and Chen tried to force him to swear not to make any significant decisions without him, but still Tan is in charge. How long this will last, however, depends on the length of the Guangxi expedition.\n\nNot everyone wants to wait and find out. The CSP leaders he has allied with, along with some of the radical members of the PAC, have demanded that he seize the moment. Already many unions have begun wildcat strikes to demand Chen Mingshu's ouster, denouncing the Lame General as a revisionist and blaming him for not overthrowing General Li sooner. With the remaining security forces barely able to contain them, this might be the beginning of the end of Chen's rule.\n\nLiang Hancao has narrowly avoided being lynched at a Guangzhou party meeting, and First Lady Chen Bijun has ordered Li Shiqun and a detachment of Presidential Bodyguards to personally retrieve her brother Chen Yaozu from the city. The calls have flooded into Tan's office. An angry one from the President demanding order. Moderate PAC members, Chen Mingshu loyalists, RCA cadres, and RCF sympathisers have begged him to call for calm. The people in the streets cry for revolution. Tan Pingshan steps up to the podium, and in a moment he will address the strikers directly.

The War Council at Nanning
It is obvious by now to even the most naive that Chen Mingshu has been betrayed. Conflicting reports have reached his headquarters about the extent of the chaos, but what is clear is that riots have broken out in all major cities and the countryside is overrun by hooligans. Gentry, bourgeoisie, and gangster backed goons have made a resurgence, adding to the sense of anomie.\n\nWith Chen Yaozu having been recalled to [CHI.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName], Liang Hancao is the last standing committee moderate remaining in Guangdong, his letters increasingly frantic as he struggles to keep order in Guangzhou. He begs General Chen to march home as soon as possible, having barricaded his own office out of fear for his life.\n\nThe arrival of the Fourth Army in Guangxi has caused the New Guangxi Clique's Seventh Army to disintegrate almost overnight, with many defecting to the Fourth Army for protection. This has swelled his ranks and allowed them to quickly secure Nanning, though Chen Mingshu's hold over the province is even more tenuous than in Guangdong.\n\nA break in the storm has emerged - Li Zongren and Bai Chongxi have offered to surrender to Chen, resigning his governorship and offering their armies to help Chen Mingshu restore order. Accepting would be seen as a great betrayal among the left, confirming their accusations of Chen Mingshu being an opportunist, but it might just be the advantage he needs to carry the day.

The Fourth Guangzhou Uprising
The last few weeks have marked yet another turning point in Liangguang's tumultuous history. The fallout of the Nanning Mutiny against Guangxi Governor Li Zongren has forced neighbouring Guangdong Governor Chen Mingshu's hand. The General, highly unpopular among the radical left, sent his troops out to restore order in Guangxi, destabilising Guangdong in the process. Sensing the power vacuum, radical Kuomintang and Syndicalist Party unions led by Tan Pingshan called a general strike while peasant militias under Peng Pai uprose, both hoping to secure Chen's removal for their own benefit. Realising the situation, Chen Mingshu hurried home.

The Triumvirate Splinters
Chen Mingshu's reluctance to intervene in Guangxi has not gone over well in the Guangdong Provincial Committee. With all eleven members summoned, the discussion became a heated rumble as his rivals accused him of dragging his heels on the matter. Their insistence that he leave for Guangxi has quickly grown into a demand that he leave power altogether, with his opponents charging him with dereliction of duty, political cronyism, financial misdeeds, and all sorts of crimes against the revolution. Tan Pingshan and Peng Pai, the main instigators, have therefore formally called for Governor Chen's impeachment.\n\nLiang Hancao, representing the Reconstruction Faction, has spoken in defence of the General and Chen Mingshu naturally has opposed the resolution as well. The six non-standing committee members have broken down along similar ideological lines. Chen Yaozu, the Reorganisation Comrades Association spokesman, meanwhile has deferred to his brother-in-law, phoning President Wang and First Lady Chen Bijun for their input. In any case, he maintains, it is the President who decides the Governor by law.

The Cantonese Quarrel
The fallout of the Nanning Mutiny has caused great consternation in the neighbouring Guangdong province. Governor Chen Mingshu's reluctance to intervene in Guangxi has fractured his already stormy relationship with his rivals, who have accused him of dragging his feet. Their insistence that he leave for Guangxi has quickly grown into a demand that he leave power altogether, with opponents formally charging him with dereliction of duty, political cronyism, financial misdeeds, and all sorts of crimes against the revolution. Tan Pingshan and Peng Pai, the main instigators, have therefore formally called for Governor Chen's impeachment.\n\nLiang Hancao, representing the Reconstruction Faction, has spoken in defence of the Governor and Chen Mingshu naturally has opposed the resolution as well. Seeing a deadlock, Chen Yaozu, our local RCA spokesman, has deferred to his brother-in-law, phoning President Wang and First Lady Chen Bijun for their input. In any case, he maintains, it is the President who decides the Governor by law.\n\nSong Qingling, Chen Duxiu, Li Lisan and surprisingly Chen Gongbo have reluctantly spoken in favour of Tan Pingshan, the quarrelsome leader of the Southern PAC with ties to the CSP. Cai Yuanpei, Li Jishen, Sun Fo and Gu Mengyu have advised that Chen Mingshu is a practical source of stability and moderation. Few have spoken in favour of Peng Pai, the free-spirited agrarian radical who commands a concerning amount of influence in the region and threatens to deny Guangdong to both the RCA and PAC.

Tan Pingshan is Appointed Governor
Tan has some friends in high places. Although he has had a tempestuous relationship with Song Qingling and Deng Yanda, who he blames for pushing him out of his own Provisional Action Committee, he is still seen as one of them. Similarly, despite leaving the Chinese Syndicalist Party on largely bad terms, the national branch mostly threw their weight behind him. It is said that even his former co-worker Chen Gongbo interceded for him with Wang Jingwei, so apparently he has not burnt all of all bridges. So, in the end Tan got what he wished - with one telegram Chen Mingshu was dismissed and Tan became Governor.\n\nIf there is some lesson about humility to be found here, Tan has not learned it. If anything, this has reinforced his belief that he is the only one who can unite the warring socialist factions. And he has not been subtle about it. Although Chen Mingshu and Peng Pai remain nominally in government, the standing committee has been sidelined and power has been centralised into Tan's hands along with the new Provincial Secretary-General (and Guangdong CSP Party Secretary) Deng Zhongxia. Troops have been deployed into Guangxi, though by the time they arrived the Baise and Longzhou uprisings had proven far more potent than previously thought. Tan has appointed Yu Zuobai as Governor of Guangxi, making it clear who the true master of Liangguang is.

A Change in Partners
The Clash for Canton ended, like most Kuomintang battles, in a stalemate. Unable to break the upstart General Chen Jitang's grip on Guangzhou through General Chen Mingshu's intrigues, the leftist Central Executive Committee has decided to settle for a compromise. After intense negotiations between the two factions, the right aligned Kuomintang warlords of Guangdong and Guangxi, led by Chen Jitang and Li Zongren respectively, were recognised as the official governors of the province under [CHI.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName]. In return, both agreed to submit to the authority of President [CHI.GetLeader] and participate in the coming national revolutionary wars. By uniting Kuomintang forces in Nanjing and Guangzhou, left and right, the party as a whole has been greatly strengthened - a win-win for both sides.\n\nThe biggest loser of this arrangement, it seems, is Chen Mingshu and the other leftists of Liangguang. Used and discarded as failures, the New Fourth Army's influence has effectively dissolved overnight with the Central Government's orders to submit to the vindictive Chen Jitang's authority. The Baise Uprising

The Baise Betrayal
The successful completion of the Northern Expedition has given hope to leftists across China that a new dawn is rising. Our alliance with rightist Governors has always drawn scrutiny, and the wisdom of doing so has been called into question as harsh reprisals have eliminated hopes that these warlords would ever see the light of socialism. This has hardened opinions against further collaboration against our so-called comrades in the KMT Right, and many have called on the CEC to offer sanctuary for rebel leaders in our ranks. Nevertheless, an exodus of questionably trained insurgents into central government controlled territory can only spell further disorder and radicalisation. Hu Hanmin Returns to Play
The Liangguang Kuomintang's alliance with the left has not resolved tensions between the two. There is too much at stake, too many bad memories, too much ruthlessness for either side to fall for the false sense of security the pact has created. Governor Chen Jitang, ever wary of intrusions into his powerbase, has desperately sought ways to minimise socialist thought in Guangdong through various means. The Central Government in turn has sought to undermine his rule, looking for openings to oust the turbulent government and seize his valuable province.\n\nGovernor Chen has escalated today with a political coup. The governor meeting with delegations of old men would hardly be a new sight, except for the fact that these men have spent the better part of the last decade in Moscow. Their leader, Hu Hanmin - briefly the second Generalissimo of the Guangzhou Government and successor of Sun Yat-sen - is Wang Jingwei's arch-enemy in the party, perhaps even more so than the deceased Marshal Chiang. His followers include many civilian rightists in the party who broke ranks after the Fourth Plenum and fled overseas rather than participate in the Northern Expedition. And it seems that Governor Chen has invited them to stay.\n\nMore than that in fact. Chen has announced Hu Hanmin as the Chairman of the Southern Political Affairs Council, ostensibly to coordinate party matters in the region. This riposte goes beyond the normal rejections of [CHI.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName]'s authority in local provinces during the normal warlord back and forth - it is a direct challenge on [CHI.GetLeader] and the leftist committee's claim to authority on all of China! The borderline treasonous behaviour of the right will likely cause the party to come to blows, but for now neither side seems ready to fight, at least not until unification. For now, Sun Fo and the other party moderates have sought to calm tensions, but they will not be successful for long.

Rioting in Hong Kong
The Kuomintang, a Chinese revolutionary and nationalist party, suffered significant divisions but has always been united in their hostility towards foreign legations. Their perennial choice of base in Guangzhou (Canton) has bred hostility with the neighbouring concessions in Hong Kong and Macau. Incidents such as the Shakee Riots and the Hong Kong-Canton protests contributed to the German intervention in the Northern Expedition.\n\nThe expansion of Chinese concessions to include numerous major cities along the Eastern coastline has only worsened the antipathy of Chinese nationalists towards foreigners, and with the Kuomintang's return to Guangzhou Hong Kong is once more in their sights. Emboldened by the presence of a leftist government in Guangdong Province, Chinese Syndicalist and Kuomintang union organisers have coordinated a massive general strike in Hong Kong. Efforts to bring it to heel have provoked a riot which will take days to disperse. It seems that so long as a Red China exists, the concessions will remain a geopolitical hotspot.

How the Iron Army Got Its Name
The Guangdong Army was founded as the primary arm of the Constitutional Protection Government in 1917. Sun Yat-sen appointed Chen Jiongming as the commander of the Guangdong-Fujian Army, which fought off rival southern warlords before turning their sights northwards. Chen Jiongming's betrayal did not diminish its significance, and it was reorganised into the Fourth Army of the National Revolutionary Army in 1925.\n\nNow under Li Jishen's command, the fallout of the Liao Zhongkai assassination attempt resulted in the 4th Army becoming the counterbalance to rightist influence in the military. Marching into Hunan with other NRA armies, it was initially slated to continue with them to Wuchang but ultimately held in reserve as geopolitical tensions in the Pearl River delta escalated. The worst came to pass in October 1926 when German troops attacked Guangzhou, forcing them to fight a bloody rear-guard action. Despite the odds, the Fourth Army was able to resist the global hegemon's advance long enough to cover the CEC's evacuation from the city.\n\nTo commend their achievement, patriotic members of the local gentry and merchant class commissioned an iron shield in support of them, inscribed with a message: \
We charge to the front with hearts of iron!
The people we love and serve rely on us like iron.
We bear the burden of revolution with an iron shoulder.

The Haifeng Soviet
Peng Pai got his start as a young director of the Haifeng County Education Bureau with the support of Chen Jiongming and the local student body. Already inspired by the French revolution, he began to clash with his fellow gentrymen before being dismissed for organising a May Day ceremony. Undeterred, he founded the Haifeng County General Farmer's Association.\n\nThis association, with far-reaching social policies, exploded in popularity. Providing medical care, agricultural guidance, personal protection, and mediation for marriage, debts, and land ownership disputes - its comprehensive nature meant it could easily become the foundation of a parallel government. Spreading visual-oriented pamphlets and oral propaganda for illiterate peasants as well as defending farmers in court for rent arrears in several high profile cases, by the mid 1920s it represented over a hundred thousand farmers in Haifeng (nearly a quarter of the population) and rapidly spread across Guangdong.\n\nThe Northern Expedition saw Peng Pai's ambitions restrained back to his home base of Haifeng, and his (renamed) Guangdong Farmer's Association splintered off from the mainline Kuomintang. Despite most ties being severed after the 1932 Shanghai Uprising, the return of the Kuomintang has brought them back into the fold but it's clear that they are de facto separate movements by this point.\n\nPeng and his lieutenants have kept their options open. Converging back into the mainline socialist movement would be the noble act according to the party-line, but trusting them to protect our interests is a tough sell. There has been talk about extending our reach to the urban proletariat, since surely the peasants and workers are not so different, but so far they have had little traction in the cities thanks to the CSP. Whatever the case, Haifeng remains a potent base of operations and will likely continue to do so in the near future.
KMT, CSP, and GFA must all band together!
Fighting for the peasants, we forge our own way!
We must reach into the cities for the sake of the workers!

Politics of Guangdong Syndicalism
Guangzhou has always been one of the two centres of Chinese syndicalism, and now with the new leftist government it is much safer than Shanghai. Beneath the surface, the syndicalist scene in Guangdong can be quite complicated. The original League of Chinese Syndicalists was a broad tent movement, made up of any socialist that the CSP could find that did not want to be primarily affiliated with the KMT. What identity they should take beyond that has always been controversial.\n\nThe primary leader of the Guangdong CSP is Deng Zhongxia. Deng is a famed labour organiser and one of the founders of the LCS and the All-China Federation of Labour. This allowed him to play a major role in the Canton-Hong Kong strike along with Su Zhaozheng. Su is a prominent leader of the Sailor's Union, next in stature to Deng in the Guangdong CSP. The Reorganisation Comrades of Guangdong
Chen Yaozu has come south from [CHI.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] to rule as the new Mayor of Guangzhou. The brother of First Lady Chen Bijun, the obvious nepotism in his appointment belies the fact that he is no fool. Like his sister, he was born to a wealthy Malaysian Chinese merchant family, which afforded him an education in Guangdong and Japan. He has a long history of working in Kuomintang administrations in the city - indeed, the Wangs and the Reorganisation Comrades Association were originally predominantly Cantonese based.\n\nAnd perhaps that is why his true orders from his sister, who frequently visits, is to carve out a Cantonese fiefdom loyal to Wang Jingwei. Chen Bijun is a calculating woman and a power in her own right, quite cognisant that the shift of the party's centre of power to Nanjing has left a major power-vacuum in Guangzhou. Disloyalty, in her eyes, is rampant and she is determined not to lose Guangzhou.\n\nUnfortunately for her, Chen Yaozu is not as strong-willed and cunning as she is, and despite his lofty position his control over the city, much less the province, is limited to parts of the civilian administration and party apparatus. This has made him effectively dependent on Chen Mingshu and his sister, his efforts further complicated by the perennial disagreements within Wang's camp on a national level. Chen Bijun's Residence (disparagingly translated as Palace) faction is often at odds with Chen Gongbo's Reorganisation Comrades Association. Chen Yaozu is one of the few Residence Faction members to formally join the RCA, reflecting their desperation in reestablishing a foothold in the city they once called home. Despite his apparent weakness, his decisions may prove pivotal in the future.

The Hlai People
The Hlai people, also often referred to as the Li people, are an ethnic group that inhabit southern China and northern Vietnam, with a large concentration in the island of Qiongya. Traditionally animists, Hlai legends say each of their clans originate from the marriage of a woman and a divine animal. Possessing a unique language and culture, [GXC.GetLeader] and other Liangguang government leaders have made a point of visiting during this year's Oxen Festival, a major event during the spring. Oxen play a major role in Hlai economic life, so on the eighth day of the third lunar month the Hlai honour them to help ensure a good harvest that year.\n\nScholars speculate the Hlai originated in the Leizhou Peninsula before migrating southwards to Qiongya and much of the coast. Growing Han migration in the 1700s saw tensions arise as the Qing dynasty centralised power and tried to assimilate them, though many remained in the relatively impoverished highlands. This may have contributed to their support of socialist movements (though few secessionist movements), which made them targets of harsh repression by reactionary forces. Their villages were razed on several occasions and their food stolen by occupying forces. Nevertheless, with the leftist victory in Liangguang, their loyalty has been rewarded. The central government has approved several autonomous counties to be established on the island. One can hope this is a positive sign for relations to come.
May all within China be rewarded as such.
Ah, but the secessionist movements?

Guangzhou, the Revolutionary Outpost
The central government in [CHI.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] has ordered the reestablishment of its foreign ministry under Chen Youren. While the Nationalist government's claim over China remains only quasi-recognised even within the Internationale, it is still a major step forward in building a new government for the nation. Circling around the Kuomintang include many sister parties consisting of smaller Kuomintang inspired left nationalist movements hoping to liberate their homelands in East and Southeast Asia.\n\nLiangguang has always been a friendly base for these plucky freedom fighters, no matter the government. The Kuomintang, Chen Jiongming, and even Lu Rongting have always been rather sympathetic in particular to Vietnamese nationalists resisting French and later German colonialism. Many groups freely took advantage of Guangzhou to plot and organise, and even launch cross border raids from Guangxi. Their tendency to smuggle opium to make ends meet drew some attention from Chen, but for the most part they were tolerated or even supported by successive Liangguang governments.\n\nToday three major Vietnamese groups have some presence in the area. [GetYear] Land Reform Act
Part and parcel to the Kuomintang's political and economic reconstruction plan is the promise of land reform. Dr. Sun Yat-sen included Land Equalisation as part of his original National Reconstruction Plan, heavily influenced by Georgism. Over the years, various impromptu land reform efforts have been made both in Guangdong and during the MinGan insurgency, but there rarely was enough sustained effort or stability for any programme to make a significant dent in the problem.\n\nThe new government in Nanjing has decided to flex its muscles by passing a series of directives related to agricultural policy. The most significant is a pilot land reform programme in Jiangsu (near Nanjing) designed to be a model for others to follow. Naturally, PAC leaders in Jiangxi and Fujian have taken initiative on their own to begin redistributing land, an extension of prior efforts by the MinGan. As one of the largest and wealthiest provinces, there have been calls for us to launch a pilot programme of our own, helping our own struggling peasants from their plight. And, admittedly, this might be an opportunity to show up the national government while we are at it.\n\nTwo broad currents compete for our attention. A more moderate agenda has been proposed that would implement a long-desired 37.5% land rent cap which will be paired with forced purchases from landlords of unused or excess lands by the government with a mixture of direct compensation, government bonds, and stocks in state-owned industries. Purchased land would then be sold at a discount to the peasants already working on it.\n\nA more radical proposal is to orchestrate forcible seizures of land from the gentry. Some strategic regions will be totally nationalised and others allowed to remain in the hands of small landholders, but the bulk will be converted into peasant cooperatives. Of course, rather than pursue either option, it might be best to wait for the results of other pilot programs for further study.
We pursue a moderate land to the tiller programme.
We need a radical redistribution of the land!
We must be cautious and await directives.
Power to the United Front
The United Front has always been a divisive issue within the party, but widely embraced by the left during the tail end of the original Kuomintang party split. The Chinese Syndicalist Party was founded independently of the Kuomintang, ordered to join forces with the Kuomintang by Paris in exchange for the Internationale's support of the Chinese revolution, but the arrangement has always been uneasy. The CSP has tried to maintain further autonomy by forming the League of Chinese Syndicalists but the League has similarly been unwieldy.\n\nThe Kuomintang Central Committee's return from their exile in France has not reduced the complexity of the situation. Exposure by many of the exiles to life in France and Britain have made many exiles sympathetic though others have hardened against either what they perceive as the revolutionary excesses in Britain, the chaos of \
We should embrace the alliance wholeheartedly.

Revolutionary Culture, Revolutionary People
The ailing Qing dynasty and its weakness in face of foreign imperialism has soured many towards the Chinese traditionalism they represent. The New Culture Movement, ironically inspired by late Qing dynasty reforms, sprung up after the Xinhai Revolution, opening up discourse about the state of Chinese society that often borders on iconoclasm. Calling for greater use of Chinese vernacular, the family structure, egalitarianism, nationalism, and a forward thinking orientation, it demands that Chinese economic modernisation come hand in hand with social modernisation. It has also triggered significant backlash from conservatives for its excesses, especially when paired with revolutionary action.\n\nThe Kuomintang as a party has had a complicated relationship with the New Culture Movement. Uncontroversial within the party are issues like women's emancipation and equality - especially issues such as the elimination of women's footbinding and to a lesser extent polygamy. However, as the movement veers into uncomfortable territory related to sexual and social norms, there has been pushback by some of the very intellectuals that first articulated the principles of the movement.\n\nLikewise in the political sphere, the call for Mr. Science and Mr. Democracy to take the lead has many sympathisers within the left but activists have also criticised integral parts of the Kuomintang doctrine such as the need for tutelage and the vanguard party. Attacks on Chinese culture and Confucian values have also meshed poorly with the party's patriotic rhetoric, especially among the right.\n\nThe Central Government has tried to put their own spin on things, ordering their propaganda and education organs to help craft (some say synthetically) a revolutionary culture fit for a new China. How receptive the people of Liangguang (and our own government) will be is another matter.
Times are changing, and so must we!
We can blend revolution and tradition.
There's rebellion and there is...

Inviting Federalist figures from across the nation, Chen and his allies have made their ambitions clear to the entire nation: to rebuild the seemingly dead Federalist movement and establish a new national government in Guangzhou.\n\nThis Conference is somewhat of a spiritual successor to the series of Federalist conferences held in the 1920s, most notably during Hunan's Constitutional drafting in 1920, and the 1922 \

Chen's National Reconstruction Plan
The first major topic discussed was Chen Jiongming's Plan for National Reconstruction. Largely similar to the one he published in May 1922, on the eve of war between him and Sun Yat-sen, it outlines a moderate Federalist future for China - especially compared to radicals like Zhang Taiyan who sought the effective dismantling of the central government.\n\nModelled after the American government, Chen's republic would share power between the provinces (which would remain the same as the current ones). The provinces would elect senators to present provincial interests, and a house of representatives would be drawn from local bodies to represent the interests of the people. The central government will have exclusive domain over the military, national security, and foreign relations, but provinces will not be responsible for supporting armies and military and civilian administration will be strictly separated. Finances would be divided between the central government and provincial governments, and responsibilities over the judiciary, education, industry and communications are to be decided at a later date.\n\nThe most radical departure from his 1922 is how to achieve their goal. At the time, Chen proposed three steps for unification. First, that all provinces should commit to achieving that Federalist vision. They would then send representatives to a conference to draft a final version. Finally, reunification would then be achieved through the voluntary creation of a Federation. He also proposed Sun Yat-sen and Duan Qirui as the top candidates for President and Vice President (Translator's Note: Chen took pains to avoid using the title of President, preferring the use of Chief Executive, but for ease we will exclusively use President).\n\nThe failure of negotiations in 1923 and unification by force in 1927 largely discredited that approach, and all can agree force of arms will likely be required. And everyone knows who is the top candidate for President in [GetYear].
This plan will surely encourage the cooperation of the provinces!
It is high time that we woke up to the reality of China's situation.
The DSP's Ten Point Programme
The DSP, other groups, and various independents who recently joined the GRA (most of them veterans of the Federation of Chinese Democratic Parties which sought to encourage democracy from within the Zhili government in the early 1930s), drafted a Ten Point Programme for the new Guangzhou Federalist Government. Opening with a preamble about unity, the points include vows to:\n\n1. Resist foreign imperialism and totally recover China's territorial integrity - opposing compromise with foreign powers.\n2. Realise the spirit of multi-party democracy and prepare for the drafting of a constitution.\n3. Strengthen internal unity and resolve inter-party conflict.\n4. Assist the GRA in executing their Plan for National Reconstruction.\n5. Firmly establish unity of national authority and properly divide central and local power.\n6. Ensure the armed forces belong to the nation and are loyal to it rather than military or regionalist cliques, and prevent its use to resolve domestic disputes.\n7. Carry out rule of law and protect the people's freedoms of life, property, and person from illegal punishments.\n8. Respect intellectual and academic freedom, particularly of legal speech, publication, assembly, and associations.\n9. Emphasise non-partisanship in the military, schools, the government, and public finances. Officials should be selected on the basis of moral character and ability.\n10. Vigorously carry out economic, administrative, and supervisory reforms to promote economic reconstruction.\n\nThere has been some debate over its pending publication. Drafted by an independent, Zhang Junmai and other DSP leaders have hesitated in publishing the entirety of the 10 Points, fearing some of it may cause the Yue Army and other Warlords to view them as a threat. But many of the more idealistic independents demand it be published in full lest they walk out. A compromise has been proposed, which is to publish it anonymously under the defunct FCDP label.
The authorities be damned, let them know what we stand for!
Hiding behind anonymity, the democrats raise their points in full.
Pragmatic self-censorship allows for stability and diplomacy.
Federalist Flags and Symbols
While the Guangzhou Federalist Government officially flies the Five Races Under One Union Flag of the 1911 Republic, for easier identification, party flags often fly alongside it. Like most things with the Federalist movement, even the flag did not come easily.\n\nWhile in exile in Japan, Tongmenghui leaders began debating over a party flag. Two of the finalists were the White Sun, Blue Sky, and Wholly Red Earth flag (a modification of Lu Haodong's design) and the blue, white and red \

Constitutionalism Among the Federalists
Constitutionalism is another major pillar of the Federalist movement, though throughout Chinese it has not necessarily been synonymous with democracy. Chinese jurists have had a long, proud legal tradition including concepts such as \
A lasting constitution is the only way that China can be brought to peace.
Respect for the constitution is the bedrock for Chinese Democracy.
We fight for a constitutional future, but must be practical about it until then.
Warlords, Regionalists, and Federalists?
The third major component of the Federalist Movement gathered in Guangzhou are the Regionalists. While opponents of Federalism have long charged that the ideology is but a front for warlords to justify their defiance of the central government, the reality was and continues to be nuanced. Just as not every Federalist is a warlord, not every warlord is a Federalist.\n\nWhile the idea of respecting provincial autonomy may be appealing to many provincial warlords, building a Federal government is controversial. China has traditionally been a centralist state in theory, even if in practice the autonomy of distant provinces has given rise to the proverb \
May Chen Jiongming preserve the peace at home.
We hope that Chen Jiongming continues to respect our autonomy.
The Conference Closes
As the Guangzhou Federalist Conference comes to a close, it is clear that the Federalist movement rests on three pillars: Democracy, Constitutionalism, and Regionalism. For the idealistic democrats, the Guangzhou Federalist Government is their chance to finally achieve some semblance of representative democracy in China. For the Constitutionalists, they are pinning their hopes on Chen Jiongming, a lawyer and long-time member of the Guangdong Provincial Assembly, to implement and respect a constitution once in power. For the regionalists, Federalism will ensure the voice of the locals will not be drowned out on a national level.\n\nThe Federalist movement has a spectrum of figures, some extremely vocal about their ideals while others much more pragmatic in their dealings. At the end of the day however, the new Guangzhou Federalist Government is expected to operate more or less the same way the old Guangdong government did. Chen Jiongming has gone from Governor to President after a conference vote with little opposition, the aging Cantonese businessman and revolutionary Chen Xiru (his de facto deputy) will likely become Governor of Guangdong.\n\nWhile the Conference continues to try and define Federalism, beneath the varnish the government is held together by the might of the Yue Army, the Guangzhou bureaucracy, and aligned gentry magistrates. Like most warlords, Chen Jiongming is the indispensable centre of a web of personal connections and though he tries to cloak it with local assemblies and parliamentary process (whether for benevolent or manipulative reasons is up to interpretation), his word ultimately is the last one.\n\nStill, that probably is still better than many warlords in China. And with how easily the theorists and thinkers are distracted or become out of touch with the reality on the ground, perhaps a strong hand is what is needed to keep the movement together and moving forward.
The magistrates and bureaucrats are what keep Guangdong going.
The Yue Army and its officers keep the various warlords in line.
Chen Xiru Passes Into the Night
Though elected Governor by the Guangdong Provincial Assembly shortly after Chen Jiongming was sworn in as President of the Guangzhou Federalist Government, Chen Xiru never had the chance to take up the post. The elderly Cantonese gentleman passed away last night after a long battle with illness, having spent his last energies aiding Guangdong's reconstruction.\n\nChen Xiru was the heir to a wealthy overseas Chinese trader, and after growing up in Guangdong he started school in Hawaii before attending Yale. While in America, he became classmates with Sun Yat-sen and a friend of Tang Shaoyi, two men whose paths he would continue to cross into the Xinhai Revolution. He and his brother Chen Gengyu inherited his father's business empire, and they used it to sponsor various political factions in the new Republic.\n\nWell respected within Cantonese circles, Chen Xiru was appointed by Chen Jiongming as civilian governor during the June 16th incident. His tenure was brief, and he fled into exile with Chen Jiongming to Hong Kong. Disillusioned, he nevertheless stuck by Chen in the name of protecting Guangdong. His elder son Chen Yongshan was also a Yale graduate and he served in the Yue Army, becoming director of Shijing Arsenal. In 1924, he was assassinated by a sniper in Macau. Chen's empire will likely be inherited by his surviving children.\n\nWho will take his place as Governor of the Federalist government's most vital province is now an open question. With the DSP lacking the institutional power to run a credible campaign, President Chen is expected to nominate and easily get elected an insider. Vying for the job are two of his secretaries. Ma Yuhang has experience in diplomacy and finance, and is seen as the favourite of the Yue Army. Chen Qiyou has been tasked with organising the PIP. Less popular candidates include Qiu Jingyun, Chen Jiongming's advisor Chen Gongshou, a merchant leader seen as Chen Lianbo's proxy.
Ma Yuhang is one of Chen's older, better established protégés.
Chen Qiyou is younger and has more ties with the GRA.
Qiu Jingyun was the President's own mentor and continued counsel.
Chen Gongshou would allow an outside voice while keeping matters in house.
Chen Jiongming's Men
Chen Jiongming commanded a wide range of friends, allies, and personal connections - something that has propped up his government and given life to his Federalist movement. But even as more join and his government expands, his core support network remains largely the same.\n\nAt the centre of his powerbase is his control over the Yue Army. The successor of his Xun Army (named after Huizhou), the Yue Army has evolved to something more than just a military force. Many of his top officers were also among his earliest followers, and their fidelity over the decades has given him a degree of security others lack. Of course, their loyalty has not been without question. While officers like Ye Ju and Ma Yuhuang, stood by Chen to the bitter end, officers like Xiong Lue, Weng Shiliang, Luo Xianxiang, and brothers Zhong Xiunan and Jingtang were willing to seek employment elsewhere.\n\nAnd loyalty does not preclude independent action - after the June 16th Incident Chen Jiongming claimed the coup was plotted without his involvement: a dubious claim but supported to an extent by his multiple \
The Yue Army will always be first and foremost.
The Public Interest Party will be Chen's legacy.
Chen was born a gentryman-scholar, and remains one.
The Makeup of the DSP
The Democratic Socialist Party traces its roots (as do most non-Kuomintang, reformist parties) to the teachings of Liang Qichao, the late Qing dynasty reformist and Beiyang founding father. Some older members also are drawn from the various exile study associations at the turn of the century; many were former Political Science Clique affiliated moderates like Xu Fulin and Lin Zhengxuan.\n\nAt its centre is Zhang Junmai, a Liangist educated in Germany (where he first was inspired by the German SPD), and who has tried to consolidate power within the party around himself. Ideologically he has attempted to synthesise Eastern philosophy with Western political ideas (particularly Social Democracy), to limited effect.\n\nZhang views the party more as an intellectual elite (the successor to his National Renaissance Society) than a mass movement, hoping to influence policy through connections to intellectuals and warlords. The NRS's mission was to awaken China's national consciousness, harmonise society, and allow for the free development of the individual through the power of the nation and they have a history of collaborating with warlords like Sun Chuanfang, Chen Jiongming, and now they are said to be courting various regional warlords under Chen. Zhang also had a brief dialogue with the Young China Party (another Liangist splinter group) leaders such as Li Huang and Zeng Qi in the 1920s.\n\nHis fears of becoming a mere armchair intellectual and his authoritarian efforts to control his party has caused many of his peers to resent him and the party to shrink to mostly himself, his students, and a support base of foreign trained educators and middle-age moderates. Liberals like Luo Longji and socialists like Zhang Dongsun have bemoaned this trend, hoping for the DSP to adopt a more grass-roots friendly approach to propagating their beliefs. They have sought to capitalise on anti-concessionist trends, with leaders such as Zhu Qinglan and Luo Wengan speaking out against Japan and Germany.
Zhang Junmai, like Liang before him, is the movement's guiding hand.
By educating the warlords, the DSP can concretely influence policy.
A modern party requires mass appeals to nationalism and socialism.
The DSP's wide range of connections allows it to build a working coalition.
Old vs New Federalists
The Federalist movement can be broadly divided into two groups of factions, those mostly founded and active prior to the Zhili unification and those whose mindsets were molded afterwards.\n\nThe original federalist movement active in the early 1920s was shaped by the civil war and the seemingly intractable divide between North and South. With the provinces drifting apart from one another and with no military unification in sight, the emphasis of this early phase of Federalism was on finding a diplomatic solution to the crisis. Federalism in this era was focused on bringing constitutional democracy locally, with the aspiration of the provinces reuniting through a negotiated federation.\n\nThe Zhili's successful unification after the Northern Expedition changed that calculus, and with national reunification no longer in doubt (at least on paper) and the Cao Kun constitution promising a federal division of power and democratic reforms (also on paper), the later Federalist movement shifted towards liberalising Beiyang on a national level. Regionalism began to be heavily criticised as unpatriotic amid threats of foreign imperialism and synonymous with the warlord era, causing the provincial autonomy aspect of Federalism to be de-emphasised in favor of implementing democracy and constitutionalism.\n\nThe PIP and DSP were both founded after 1928, and their leaders were active in both eras of the Federalist movement. However, Chen Jiongming and his allies are more tied down to the powers that be and are more regionally based, causing the PIP to appeal more towards the first camp. For Zhang Junmai and his allies, their lack of institutional power and the geographic dispersal of their members has seen the DSP trend towards the latter. They also have a freer hand in calling for radical changes to the socio-economic system since they are not weighed down by the practical realities of ruling. Both sides are expected to find additional allies as the Federalist government expands.
The Old Federalists stay true to the original purpose of the movement.
The New Federalists reflect changing values among Chinese intellectuals.
Finding Funding for the DSP
While socialists have long dreamed of a society that no longer needs money to function, at least for this era the DSP will need funding to survive. Scraping together a living has always been a concern for the intellectual class; they often had to be either independently wealthy, prolific and famous enough as writers to gain a decent following or most likely receive the patronage of powerful people. As a consequence, political newspapers in China have frequently sprouted up and ceased publication as political tastes change.\n\nBeing an intellectual(s) publishing a (likely transient) newspaper is one thing, but being an organised political party is another. The Federation of Chinese Democratic Parties learned this the hard way, with the loose coalition struggling to finance themselves, much less pay for the Federation. Only Huang Yanpei of the CNDCA seemed to actually pay money for the FCDP, with the others being perennially cash-strapped.\n\nThat was during peacetime. With a renewed era of conflict, many intellectuals are struggling to make ends meet, much less pay party dues or purchase party magazines. The PIP is effectively supported by Chen and his wealthy supporters, and the GRA operates as an extension of the PIP. The DSP has become effectively the only other true political party in the Alliance (though various interest groups like the AACSG endure) in part because it does have enough funding to continue operating continuously.\n\nThis is certainly helped by Zhang Junmai's extremely wealthy family. His brother Zhang Gongquan serves as the general manager of the Bank of China - one of the two largest in the nation. His sister Zhang Youyi is also a prominent businesswoman who has aided her brother in managing DSP finances.\n\nOf course, the FCDP was also notorious for taking money from slush-funds from various warlords hoping to undermine the central government. Whether or not the DSP has continued that practice remains to be seen, though Zhang has been seen meeting with plenty.
Who would have ever thought socialists and bankers could get along?
There is nothing wrong with discussing politics with local politicians.
Labour Issues Linger in Liangguang
Though the Kuomintang and other revolutionaries were routed during the Liangguang civil war, the social and political tensions it caused endures. Socialist insurgents in the countryside have gone underground, organising armed cells that terrorise the local gentry. In the cities, radical unions have not stopped trying to disseminate anti-capitalist literature. Led by KMT and CSP agents, disputes between the workers and their bosses have led to accusations of deliberate sabotage.\n\nThere is some hope for Liangguang to turn to a new page. Many peasants and workers, exhausted by years of conflict, have begun to turn away from revolutionary agitation and accept the status quo. New, more moderate unions and peasant groups focused on solely economic benefits for their members have begun to organise and take the place of their radical predecessors.\n\nBut the healing process is slow, and some fear labour relations may take more than a generation to recover. For many of the gentry, merchants, and political elites independent unions are nothing but trouble and ought to be banned in favor of yellow unions. In better times, Chen Jiongming was able to negotiate with Guangzhou's (predominantly anarchist) unions to resolve a series of strikes in 1922. Chen, while supporting the formation of unions and peasant groups, insisted vaguely that they be a \

Four Diseases Plaguing China
Zhang Dongsun's political beliefs evolved over time. Initially inspired by the French Revolution, Zhang believed that civilisations undergo three phases of development. The first was one built on religion, custom, and superstition, which was then replaced by civilisations of liberty, competition, individualism, and nationalism. The third and final would be one founded on socialism and cosmopolitanism, which made society the basis for thought and morality. China, then, was trapped between the first and second phases, but he hoped the \
Zhang Dongsun's views take root among many other intellectuals.
Zhang is hated by conservatives and ridiculed by other socialists.
The Federalist Civil Society
A common belief across the Federalist movement is the need for civic groups independent of political parties and the government to represent specific societal needs and influence policy as outsiders. Indeed, the Beiyang political culture traditionally eschewed formal political parties, instead being organised around informal cliques that prized personal connections and individual respectability. Though some political parties such as the Progressive Party, Young China Party and in the Guangzhou scene the DSP and PIP have emerged - unlike Western parties they generally avoided mass movement in favor of serving as social clubs for like minded politicians. Many legislators continue to run as independents.\n\nThis has made scholarly and educational groups appealing, serving as a bridge between politicians and intellectual leaders. With no firm commitments required and individuals coming and going, groups like the Zhang Junmai's National Renaissance Society were able to exert their influence on culture, social discourse, and governance - a tradition the DSP continues.\n\nSimilarly, with many Chinese social and economic elites having ties abroad, the Chinese diaspora wields significant influence. Hongmen Societies funded the Tongmenghui and later aided in the formation of the PIP, in fact the PIP was formally established in San Francisco in the United States. Their exposure to foreign ideas and access to hard currency helps them shape developments at home.\n\nFederalist scholars also speak highly of professional groups. These include agriculture, fishing, business, mining, education, legal, journalist, accounting, medical, and technical industries. Class tensions exist within these associations of course; with wealthier and specialised individuals tending to have great influence. The merchants and gentry have proven to be well organised in particular, leveraging their power to directly influence the government.
Our nation's thinkers help open our eyes to new possibilities.
The diaspora helps expose our nation to new ideas.
Professionals remind us that society is more than soldiers and magistrates.
Nationalism and Federalism
Chinese leaders often found themselves trapped between their desire to protect and nurture their native regions with the growing popular desire for unity and nationalism in face of foreign threats. For Chen Jiongming, there was little contradiction in his love for Guangdong and the rising anti-concessionist nationalism in China. To him both were perfectly compatible, and he believed that the provinces ought to have primacy by default, especially when it comes to local matters.\n\nWhereas many of his contemporaries believed the traditional state of Chinese affairs has always centred around the despotic Imperial Court, Chen wrote that the \
By empowering the provinces, we can build a modern democracy.
Through the unity of the nation, we can rebuild China's strength.
The Two Zhangs of the DSP
While the DSP was initially founded by Zhang Junmai, Zhang Dongsun and others, Zhang Dongsun and Zhang Junmai have emerged as a dynamic duo in directing the party's platform. While the Zhangs co-lead the party without much conflict on account of their strong friendship, the two men have differing visions for a free China. Zhang Junmai for his part has become a strong proponent of German-style social democracy and Liangist statism.\n\nHowever, Zhang Junmai does not believe that bottom-up democratic reforms can be achieved, rather the Chinese elite must implement such reforms. To him, not even universal education would remove the need for an elite, as politics requires \

Democracy Among Democratic Socialists?
With every passing day, the DSP grows larger as more and more young idealistic Chinese join its ranks. While this is a good thing for the DSP, there is an attached negative. If there's one big thing a lot of leading DSP members believe, it's that the elite should be leading the country to their peaceful reform into socialism. While elitism is nearly universal among the intellectual and political class of China, such an outlook has led to negative ramifications for how the DSP is run.\n\nDespite Zhang Junmai's hatred of the KMT's Democratic Socialism and Vanguardist ways of running things and his numerous critique of warlordist autocracy, he has earned a reputation for being two faced. Much like his mentor Liang Qichao, Zhang has an authoritarian streak when it comes to leading the party, only consulting an inner circle and often making rash decisions on behalf of the party without really getting wider input.\n\nThis has triggered many founding members like Chen Bosheng, Luo Longji, Jiang Yong, Pan Guangdan, and Fei Xiaotong to drop out of party affairs (if not from the party altogether), finding Zhang difficult to work with. Only Zhang Dongsun and Xu Fulin are considered to be Zhang's peers, with the remaining leadership of the party increasingly staffed by Zhang Junmai's students which further enables his autocratic tendencies.\n\nSuch authoritarianism and rule by decree within the party has led to many young members becoming disillusioned with Zhang's leadership, instead opting to instead create their own small organisations or join other democratic parties. Zhang Junmai is faced with the choice to either continue his centralisation of power within his party or start sharing power.

Chen Jiongming, the Reformer?
A statesman like no other, Chen Jiongming's initial plunge into Chinese politics saw him embrace many seemingly-conflicting ideologies in the name of rejuvenating the Chinese nation. Advocating a bottom-up, democratic, and peaceful political transformation underpinned and sustained by the principle of self-governance, Chen pushed back against the increasingly autocratic nature of Sun Yat-sen, and his burgeoning Kuomintang. Although a man of the people, Chen recognised that others would take power should he not, Chen has rationalised his continued use of the title of Commander in Chief.\n\nIntrigued by radical utopian ideologies from the West, Chen became interested in Kropotkin's utopian anarchism. Using anarchist beliefs, he constructed his ideas of provincial self-governance through educational reform and rural reconstruction. It was through educational support that he met Wang Jingwei, with the latter striking up a strong friendship with Chen.\n\nChen only encountered more socialists through his educational work. He sought out Chen Duxiu in hopes of attracting reformist support for his educational policies, and placed him as the chair of the Guangdong Provincial Educational Association. Through his continued leftist alliance, Chen achieved renown as a progressive force. Although Chen Duxiu and Tan Pingshan ended up siding with Sun after the Chen-Sun split, the two men often thought about their choice and wondered if they sided with the wrong side.\n\nAfter the 1922 Coup against Sun, Chen Jiongming saw much of his progressive alliance splinter in favor of the KMT. Despite this, he has begun working with the reformist left including the GRA groups and the DSP following his return. However, a common criticism for Chen's new leftist alliance is that the GRA is a corrupt association tainted by warlords, and the DSP is an elitist club with no mass appeal. It remains to be seen if Chen can wield this new coalition, or see it fall to the wayside as it did a decade ago.
The GRA defends provincial autonomy from the autocrats in Beijing!
The DSP fights for democracy, no matter the cost!
The PIP Grows in Size
The party of federalism and democracy, the Public Interest Party, has had a history extending far past its founding in 1925. Its origins lay in the history of its founder, Chen Jiongming. As a young man, Chen pursued a law career, before becoming infatuated with revolutionary republicanism. Founding the Zhengqi Society with his friends, he would preach his political ideals and promote the anti-Qing cause to his 30 students in Haifeng. Chen's political core would remain strong entering into the Republican Era, with many of his allies having their political start in the Aid-Fujian War.\n\nThe Party stands for Federal, Constitutional Democracy primarily, but also calls for the Abolition of Unequal Treaties, Peaceful Economic Reform, Social Progress and government transparency in the name of anti-corruption. Currently, the party is experiencing rapid growth, thanks to the efforts of Governor Chen. However, this increased size comes with an increased need to determine its own identity. Many of the new members are regionalists, who have been accused of using the party as a vehicle for warlordism by the nationalistic democratic wing. There are also quite a few ex-communists like Yan Xichun and Huang Dingchen in the young guard, who are seen as traitors.\n\nChen Jiongming's inner circle has come under increasing fire from various camps in the party for centralising authority as well. Chen Qiyou, his secretary and co-founder of the New Asia League Party, Datong Party, and finally the PIP and Chen Yansheng in particular seem to be Chen Jiongming's chief lieutenants, wielding great power within the party, to the envy of many. Other early followers like Chen Yaohuan and Chen Zhongyue were also early followers of Chen Jiongming from Haifeng. The large number of Chens have led to this faction being dubbed the Chen Clique (not all of them are blood-related to Chen Jiongming, despite their common home), but perhaps more accurately ought to be called the Haifeng faction.

Cantonese Liberalism in Government?
After the Opium Wars, Hong Kong was ceded to the United Kingdom and has significantly influenced Cantonese political culture through the intermingling of British and Chinese political traditions. Though the colony lacked democratic representation under its Western masters, the wealthy Chinese elite were able to find ways to influence the local government through economic leverage which also bled into Guangzhou politics. Chen Lianbo and his circle of comprador elites represent the greatest of these mercantile powerbrokers, utilising their militias, political connections and conglomerates such as the HSBC Bank to control major arteries of Guangzhou political life.\n\nIn stark contrast is the Political Science Clique, a middling but present force in the South. With a mixed history developing as an independent strain of the main revolutionary-reformist diatribe, they have made multiple attempts to control successive Guangzhou governments. Maintaining a grudge against Chen Jiongming for his forced deposition of the Guangzhou PSC in 1921 (and the subsequent forcing of the rump assembly to vote him as Governor), their peak of power would be in the 1924 Beijing Coup, wherein Huang Fu became President of China.\n\nTheir influence declined significantly after 1925 but the PSC remnant maintains a significant presence in the North. However, their conservative-liberal grandstanding has left them without rudder or patron in the South. They cling on through the leadership of Lin Zhengxuan, a Speaker of the Guangdong Assembly that was deposed twice by Chen Jiongming - first in 1921 and more recently in 1936. They hope to survive through loose connections, both with the Yang Ximin-Liu Zhenhuan warlord alliance and famed banker (and brother of Zhang Junmai) Zhang Gongquan.\n\nWith these conservative Cantonese forces increasing in influence, both Chen Jiongming and Zhang Junmai are scrambling to find allies in the developing political sphere. The question is, who will come out on top?

Chen Jiongming and the Chens of Haifeng
The gentry have always played a major role in Chinese politics, managing and keeping order in the rural countryside and supplying successive dynasties with a steady supply of scholar-officials. Sons of the gentry who successfully passed the examination and became Mandarins were welcomed home with lavish praise and celebration by their village. Even when the Imperial Examination was formally abolished by late Qing reformers, in practice the classic gentryman scholarly bureaucrat ideal never faded, it merely evolved.\n\nIn Guangdong, the sympathy of the gentry to the revolutionaries played a major role in hastening the downfall of Qing Dynasty rule during the Xinhai Revolution. In the southeastern county of Haifeng, Chen Jiongming became their latest favourite son, rising in influence as a lawyer and reformer with his band of Haifeng Tongmenghui followers and being elected Deputy Governor in 1911. Throughout his career, Haifeng has been a major source of funds and followers, and a refuge when Chen was periodically forced into hiding.\n\nThe loyalty has been returned. The gentry of Haifeng have received favorable appointments under Chen Jiongming's rule. The extended Chen clan have often been the target of socialist propaganda - especially from their hated enemies in Peng Pai and his Haifeng Farmer's Association. Of particular note are the three influential Chen brothers who are likely cousins or kinsmen of Governor Chen.\n\nChen Dasheng was an early follower of Chen Jiongming and served as his Director of the Public Affairs Department before being assassinated in 1922. Chen Bohua was a Director of the Guangdong Education Department who later became a newspaper editor. Chen Yuebo was said to be the second most powerful man in Haifeng besides Chen Jiongming. He was the first to rally the gentry to form an association along with Chen Kaiting. As fierce opponents to land reform, their growing influence in the administration has damaged Chen's reputation among the peasants.

Chen Jiongming, Defender of Order
Many would love to view Chen Jiongming as a man of the liberal world, of dedication to democratic traditions, egalitarianism, and of the Federalist movement. Shaking hands with the landowners, and the merchants, he would find himself similarly attempting to court the anarchists, the socialists, and the syndicalists. Influenced by western ideals of liberal democracy and national federalism, Chen's grandstanding and rallying would find himself a major Federalist leader of the South in the early 1920s, championing the cause as one of its most dominant military-political forces.\n\nIn his experiment turning to the left, Chen made peace with the infamous agrarian socialist and peasant hero Peng Pai and the Guangdong Farmer's Association. Similarly, he offered an olive branch to the World Society, and various anarchists in Fujian; appointed syndicalists like Chen Duxiu to education positions within his administration. Yet ultimately they would deceive him, side with Sun Yat-sen, and work against Chen in 1922.\n\nDriven out of Guangzhou in 1923, Chen would subsequently drift towards the right. Strengthening his ties to the gentry, merchants, and Beiyang warlords, his armies centred around Eastern Guangdong would establish themselves as firm warlords. Aligning with the likes of Lin Hu, Hong Zhaolin, Deng Benyin, and the Canton Merchants' Corps, Chen's restoration to Haifeng would see his allies in the Haifeng Gentry return to power, and Chen's establishment as a force once more.\n\nContinuing to dominate Chen's government in his new era of governance, he has largely been shaped as a conservative reformer, despite all pretenses of liberalism. Whilst not necessarily beneficial towards the morale of his democratic federalist movement, any attempts to tone down the government's ties to its key warlord, gentry, and merchant allies could destabilise his government.

The Dominance of the Yue Army
The line of work of a warlord inevitably leads to contention, intrigues, and betrayals. Certainly Chen Jiongming is no stranger to such a fact. Having been a politician, warlord, commander, and statesman since the Xinhai Revolution, he has most certainly seen his fair share of strife. Fighting against the KMT, he has also clashed with warlords such as Long Jiguang, Fang Shengtao, and Lu Rongting. Conducted deals with the most powerful men in the country, such as Duan Qirui, and Wu Peifu. But the few he has acquired have formalised themselves into the Yue Army. Championing Chen's cause even in his darkest hour, the Guangdong Federalist's sword and shield has long been stewarded by one Ye Ju; courtesy name Ruoqing. Who stood by Chen when he went into self-exile, numerous times? Ye Ju. Who raised armies and organised coups even when Chen wavered? Ye Ju. And who has stood by Chen in every scenario? Ye Ju. Although of course, there are disputes about how independent Ye's actions have been from Chen's masterminding.\n\nThe Yue Army serves largely as Chen and Ye's political machine, the force exerting their ideological goals upon the nation. The Petty Warlords of Liangguang
As Chen increasingly relies upon less than sparkling individuals to strengthen his movement, many have come to question the exact goals of the Guangdong Federalist project. Staffed with various warlords of prophetic diligence, or even blatant self-interest, Chen's United Provinces have become akin to a glorified cover for continuing warlord practice. With the likes of Qiongya warlord Deng Benyin standing tall with Chen, he has also seen other old warlord allies join his ranks once more, even amongst all of the new friends.\n\nLin Hu of the Old Guangxi Clique has long been a revolutionary sympathetic figure albeit not necessarily a Chen ally. Classmates with the famous Li Liejun and Cai E, Lin would ultimately walk a different path from his former friends. Collaborating with Xiong Kewu and others, Lin would ultimately find himself a subordinate of Lu Rongting after fighting in the National Protection War. A warlord to rival Chen himself, Lin has fought against Chen in the past, but has aligned himself to Chen since his return to power.\n\nSimilarly, Hong Zhaolin, another warlord of his own calibre, has found himself again with the likes of Chen. The local warlord of Chaomei, he was one of the most stringent activists against Sun Yat-sen, and fought fiercely on behalf of his master. Albeit, Hong's privyness to the criminal underworld are far from obscure, and it is well known that he runs an opium ring, with Deng Keng's murder being heavily linked to its discovery. Others of note include the likes of Liu Zhenhuan, a PSC revolutionary with a half-hearted commitment to democracy and federalism, or even Huang Dawei, another petty warlord of the region who formed the United Forces regime.\n\nWith so many petty local warlords attaching themselves to Chen's movement throughout his tenure as a politician, many of his critiques have brought to question if this is merely a pattern of circumstance and pragmatism, or the very core moving factor of the Federalist movement's identity.
A Movement of Federalists, or a Movement of Warlords?
