Infighting Devours the Reformists
Sometimes it feels that every time the Federalist Movement takes one step forward, it ends up taking two steps back. And for the progressives and democrats who once flocked to this movement, [GXC.GetNameDef] is far behind where they should be.\n\nThe exact reasons why and solutions to this crisis of faith remains elusive. And for those looking to the past for solutions, they will find that history has not been very kind to reformists losing their momentum. Even in recent history, neither the United National Construction League nor the Federation of Chinese Democratic Parties had much unified direction in their brief existences, prone to infighting among their constituent groups which doomed them from having even a sniff at power.\n\nThe Grand Republican League Chen Jiongming assembled was supposed to change that, bringing together an alliance of democratic and constitutionalist factions to build a new Chinese republic. But no one seems to agree where it went wrong besides for that fact that for some imperceptible reason it has. Was it because President Chen failed to show strong enough leadership? Or because he was too strong? Was the opposition at fault because of their inability to check his excesses, or because they failed to let the government govern? Are the provinces failing to democratise for a lack of federal intervention, or an excess of it?\n\nThe cracks in the unity of the Federalist movement have begun to wear down the Grand Republican League, and more and more executive decrees by the President have begun to take precedence over the National Assembly. Perhaps more worrying is the democratic backsliding in the provinces, with local warlords and Yue Army military authorities often strong-arming provincial legislatures. One has to wonder if there is anything good in sight.

Gentry Abuses and the Yue Army
The [GXC.GetAdjective] army is supposed to be a force of liberation, and the Yue Army is supposed to be an example not only on the battlefield, but also at home. Their behavior in suppressing rural insurgents have proven to be a sobering reality check for many, dampening views on the movement for many freshly initiated.\n\nChen Jiongming and his men are in many ways heavily tied to the Guangdong (particularly Haifeng County) gentry. Chen was born to a scholar-gentry family, and so were many of his top officers in both the military and government (many of whom are also part of his extended clan). The gentry have helped Chen quickly raise soldiers for his campaigns and maintain his grip on power - through their funds, their well-educated sons, and their control of major institutions. In return, Chen has rewarded them handsomely through appointments to favourable offices (particularly as rural magistrates, which has helped them legitimise and consolidate their power) and protecting them from socialist revolutionaries.\n\nChen, to his credit, has tried to broker a fair peace with rural activists like Peng Pai, at the very minimum for the sake of bolstering his reformist credentials. But in the end there is only so much he can deviate from what is perhaps his second most important powerbase behind the Yue Army.\n\nHow intertwined the two are may be illustrated by the case of the Zhong brothers, both Haifeng-born officials said to be in-laws with Chen Jiongming. Zhong Xiunan is older of the pair and co-founded the Haifeng Tongmenghui with Chen. Said to be an ex-anarchist, he served in both military and civilian roles - including as Haifeng's magistrate. Zhong Jingtang studied at the Seijo School in Japan to become a military officer and helped Chen Jiongming found the Haifeng Autonomous Newspaper. While stationed in Haifeng, they were the target of socialist propaganda, accused of conspiring with the local gentry in a litany of abuses against the local peasants.

The Ivory Tower and Mass Movements
The Pearl River Delta has always been a relative safe-haven for political thought in China, with various intellectuals and activists who have run afoul Chinese authorities often deciding to flee to Hong Kong for shelter. The formation of the Legation Cities have tightened some controls on free press (particularly calls for colonial democracy and anti-concessionism), but relative to many areas in China Hong Kong and Guangzhou are considered a haven for political discourse. As a result, it was a popular centre of operations for various dissidents like those in the FCDP.\n\nThe proliferation of various newspapers, magazines and other publication companies (although often individually short-lived) suggest a lively political scene. The DSP for example is known to publish the weekly Zaisheng (Renaissance) and the outspoken daily Guojia Shehui newspapers. The FCDP had one called the Guangming (Light) newspaper, while the YCP's Hong Kong branch publishes the Tan Haideng. The Democratic Movement Energises
When the Guangzhou Federalist Government was first established, the gathered democrats, constitutionalists, and regionalists almost unanimously agreed that their goal would be the reformation of the promised 1911 Republic: corrupted by Beiyang reactionaries and revolutionary radicals.\n\nAs the Federalist Movement begins to grow, the unity of the different factions that make up it has begun to fray. For the various reform socialists, progressives, and even some of the left liberals, merely restoring Beiyang is no longer enough. With so many who have died and so many sacrifices being made, they have begun to envision a republic distinct from the old wheeling and dealing that characterised Beiyang at its best. They have begun to demand a democratisation not only of politics, but of society and the economy - a lofty goal for a government that has not even finished uniting China.\n\nTheir calls for change, and change now have begun to clash with the various conservative republicans, the warlords, the bureaucrats and magistrates, and even the leaders of their own parties who often have a top-down style of decision making. For these men, promoting constitutionalism, regional autonomy, and protecting China's fragile institutions take priority and they fear the damage radical change may do. To them, the status quo within Federal borders is what they are fighting for in their quest to unite China, and anything further than that is reckless at best, internal sabotage at worst.\n\nAs the first group begins to gain ground, it has begun to alienate the second, threatening a split that divides even within parties and cliques. The millions of Federalists will need to collectively make a decision sooner or later on what precisely their movement wants.
Is not a constitutional, federal republic a dream worth fighting for?

Li Jishen and KMT Defectors
Chen Jiongming's relationship with the revolutionary left, like the broader Federalist movement, was an uneasy one. His competition with Sun Yat-sen for influence over leftist groups including anarchists and syndicalists left him empty-handed, and he has since replaced those influences with the much more compatible reformist left, particularly Zhang Junmai and Zhang Dongsun. With [GXC.GetNameDef] having seized control of the Eastern provinces however, reminders of his past have cropped up.\n\nChen's early campaigns were in Fujian, where he served as commander in chief loosely on behalf of Sun's government. There he was noted for working with various anarchists, implementing educational, social, and governing reforms. There remains extensive debate over to what extent he was sincere about the alliance or if he was merely tolerating it, and the occupation was not always pretty - with various Guangdong Army officers cooperating with local warlords and engaging in corruption. The MinGan region has changed since the 1920s, having become a revolutionary hotbed under Kuomintang insurgents after the Northern Expedition.\n\nChen's relationship with Chen Duxiu, the future founder of the CSP, had similar ups and downs. Chen Duxiu was an acclaimed educator and protected by Chen Jiongming despite the backlash from the conservatives, however the former ultimately decided to resign. He ultimately decided to cast his lot with the Kuomintang in 1922, denouncing Chen as a warlord while many other intellectuals who were previously sympathetic to him like Tan Pingshan and Chen Gongbo were sidelined.\n\nThough Chen's relationship with the senior leadership of the Kuomintang are beyond repair by this point, many in the centre left have sought the entry of Li Jishen, their leading general, into our government. Seen as honest and on the reformist side of the party, he has sought to facilitate the entry of other defectors like Chen Mingshu and Tan Pingshan, however many see them with suspicion.

The Conference in Guangzhou
Lu Yuguang has sent out messengers across Liangguang to invite warlords, politicians, and intellectuals to Guangzhou for a conference, ostensibly for discussions regarding the reconstruction of the two provinces and also to discuss national unification. The significance of this act was readily understood by the attendees, who have begun to gather in the city.\n\nGuangzhou is the stronghold of the remnants of the old Guangdong Clique, to host a conference in this city is to assert dominance over Chen Lianbo and his allies. It was also the de facto capital of several southern governments and by gathering southern leaders there it signified his desire to follow in his father's footsteps in leading a rival government in the south.\n\nLu and the Young Guangxi Faction Triumvirate will present their case for leadership, using the two provinces as a platform to unite the southern provinces against northern tyranny. This is a bold play given the North has traditionally won most wars of unification and continues to be dominant. But Marshal Lu will not be deterred - either North and South will unify on even terms or there will be war.
Lu Yuguang truly is his father's son!
There are plenty of others involved too...
The National Protection Alliance
A new National Protection Alliance has arisen, seemingly deadset on making yet another attempt at unifying the nation. Led by Guangxi Warlord Lu Yuguang, adopted son of the legendary Lu Rongting of the original movement which sought to topple Yuan Shikai after his attempt at declaring himself the Hongxian Emperor, this new government seeks to rally the various southern provinces into a united coalition against northern tyranny. Who will join them and what type of posturing they will take against their rivals remains to be seen but for now spirits run high in the south.
But will they achieve what their predecessors could not?
Warlords banding with more warlords.
Lu Yuguang Presses for Unification
Lu Yuguang, the Young Marshal of the South, was straight to the point. Speaking to the assembled delegates, he proposed that the various southern provinces band together so that they will not be so easily pushed around by the Northern governments. This is hardly a novel concept: it was attempted several times by various warlords by internal divisions and the ultimate victory of the Kuomintang sundered these projects.\n\nMarshal Lu has the power to enforce this decree, at least over Liangguang. He is an unknown quantity in the nation, but he does retain his adopted father's name which remains recognisable. Similarly, there are multiple ways towards unification and he may benefit from that ambiguity.\n\nWhile he has declared his intention to stop recognising Zhili authority, this might be a negotiating tactic. Unification by force will be a difficult, costly affair and the formation of a rival government may simply be a ploy to get an advantage in future talks with either the Zhili or another faction. Another lingering question is whether or not he will be able to convince others outside of Liangguang to join him in his quest. Even those not ideologically opposed to Lu may come into contact with his government either due to personal enmity or ambition.
An aggressive course of action is our best bet for victory!
A defensive, diplomatic approach will ensure our survival.
Reluctance of the Regionalists
Despite Lu Yuguang's pompous proclamation, there remains considerable resistance to war among the warlords in Liangguang. The vast majority of his lieutenants and allies can be classified as regionalists - those more interested in local affairs and preserving local autonomy than fighting a war for unification. War brings devastation, heavy taxation, and political centralisation - none of which are appealing.\n\nThat said, all but the most narrow-minded of them would realise that unification in some way is inevitable and the only way to ensure it is on their terms is by leveraging military force into concessions. For Shen Hongying and his allies in Shaoguan, their decision has already been made. The Zhili and their German allies propped up his rule for almost a decade now, funneling weapons, money, and even pressuring his generals into his camp. Finding a new accord with one of the Zhili Governments is Shen's priority. Chen Lianbo and Guangzhou have a bit more flexibility but only so much. Their autonomy was protected primarily by foreign interests, though with the outbreak of the Second American Civil War, growing Japanese influence in the region, and German preference for the Old Guangxi Clique, they have been drifting into Anguojun circles.\n\nTheir positions on forming a rival government appear to have been made for them - neither has the strength to challenge Lu on making this proclamation. But he would be wise to be cautious about his next steps, because it is one thing to form a government, it is another to begin a war for it.
The regionalists flock to Shen Hongying and his clique.
The regionalists side with Chen Lianbo and his government.
Democrats and Unification
For the democrats in Liangguang, opinions are mixed on unification. Unification will bring peace, which many hope will allow for the eventual democratisation of the government, or at least the empowerment of civilian authorities and the relaxation of restrictions on civil liberties. But to form a rival government and wage war for one is bound to be devastating for the various democratic parties and civic groups who often transcend provincial borders and will chafe under wartime controls.\n\nThere is hope that the possibility of war will shake up the existing status quo and force the various sides to legitimise themselves by working with democratic groups, but the resumption of violence may end up sidelining them in favor of military men. Thus, the various democratic leaders are forced to navigate a treacherous road, demonstrating their loyalty and usefulness to the new government without potentially alienating their patrons in the north.\n\nThe two parties that may benefit the most from this are the Democratic Socialist Party led by Zhang Junmai, who has a few chapters in the north but generally has rebased around Guangzhou and the Public Interest Party which mainly draws its influence from Chen Jiongming's old allies in Guangdong and overseas Chinese donations. While not entirely supportive of any of the major factions, they have the least to lose by supporting the new government.
Lu Yuguang works to shore up his legitimacy through the DSP and PIP.
Lu would be better off mollifying his erstwhile allies.
The National Protection Government
Despite the challenging road ahead, ultimately Marshal Lu ultimately got his wish. The delegates, while some expressing reservations about the possibility of war, closed ranks behind him when he declared his intention to form a new National Protection Government.\n\nAssuming his new role as President, he vows to lead wisely and save the republic. For now, the situation remains effectively unchanged from how it was before: the triumvirate still rules Guangxi, Shen Hongying rules Shaoguan, Chen Lianbo Guangzhou. But the elevation of Lu Yuguang from being just another warlord perceived to be a puppet of his Old Guangxi peers to a potential regional, if not necessarily national, leader has been palpable. Wavering generals and military units have fallen in line, various leaders have lined up behind him, and intellectuals have begun singing his praises.\n\nHis first decision will be a simple but potentially decisive one. Moving his personal base of operations from Nanning to Guangzhou would help him shore up his legitimacy but will put him deep in \
President Lu will take office in Guangzhou.
The high command is based in Nanning.
New Tricks for an Old Warlord
In warlord era China, political popularity usually follows military power and Chen Jiongming was little different - his political fortunes often waxed and waned based on his battlefield successes. A well educated member of the Haifeng County gentry, he rose to influence through the Guangdong Provisional Assembly and later as a Xinhai Revolutionary General. He proved to be capable commander and logistician, raising multiple armies in his fights against the Guangxi Warlords and later the Kuomintang. But his fights against the KMT proved to be his undoing and he was forced to flee into exile until after the completion of the Northern Expedition where he carved out a role as a gentry commander aligned with Chen Lianbo's government.\n\nWhen he was bested in battle in 1936, most assumed that would be the practical end of his influence, forced to flee into Hong Kong in failing health. But alas, fortunes have swung back in his favor and his hardworking allies in his Public Interest Party in particular have allowed him to exert influence, pressing for national unification under a Federalist system. He seems mostly harmless but with their power growing, there remain some concerns.
Let us undercut his influence the best we can.
His views may prove useful for our purposes.
Resurgence of the Yue Clique
Despite their defeat in 1936, the remnants of Chen Jiongming's Yue Clique have achieved some enduring popularity in the new order. Much of the old Yue Army and even some of their commanders have been absorbed into our allied military forces while the gentry-backed Grain Industry Maintenance Association that was fairly tied to Chen Jiongming has swapped their allegiance towards us. The Public Interest Party and their Hongmen connections have also found themselves growing in influence within government circles.\n\nOur decisions have allowed a certain partnership to grow with the old Yue Clique, even if at times we need to keep our distance. Continuing to exploit this alliance may open up new avenues for us to benefit from this relationship.
Experienced Yue Army officers aid our military.
Alliances with the gentry stabilise the countryside.
Unity with the PIP and others bring us closer to unification.

In the halls of the Guangxi cliques, there are fewer names as convicted in supposed crimes committed against the Old Guangxi Clique than that of Liu Zhenhuan and his Guangxi Army. An early leader of the Guangxi Tongmenghui, Liu would be considered one of the chief representatives of the revolution for Guangxi, fighting in Xinhai, and the Second Revolution.\n\nReturning to fight in the National Protection War, he defected to the Old Guangxi Clique in 1917, but in 1920 under secret communications with the Guangdong Clique of Chen Jiongming, would defect again in-exchange for becoming commander of the First Division of the Guangxi Army. In the ensuing 1st Yue-Gui War, Liu would help extinguish the Old Guangxi Clique for a time, plunging it into the Age of Heroes.\n\nAs a pawn in the broader Sun versus Chen chess game, Liu would ultimately fight for Sun, be appointed as an alternate member of the Kuomintang's Central Executive Committee and Governor for Guangxi, before taking his Guangxi Army, and in conjunction with his ally Yang Ximin, would rebel in 1925 after Sun's death, ostensibly with the hopes of becoming Tang Jiyao's Governor of Guangxi. Defeated, he would be cast into exile in Hong Kong.\n\nAs a member of the Political Science Clique through connections to Yang Yongtai, Shen Hongying, and Yang Ximin, Liu's return to Liangguang politics under Chen Lianbo sparked much animosity from the Old Guangxi Clique; who decry him as a traitor, the second Wu Sangui, or as the \

Western Liberalism in Today's China?
A supportive force in Chinese politics, the Political Science Clique owes its origins to the parliamentary split between the Kuomintang following Song Jiaoren's assassination by Yuan Shikai. With rump members of the parliamentary Kuomintang, those that didn't fight the Second Revolution, forming the Democratic Constitutional Party in 1913, figures such as Zhang Yaozeng and Gu Zhongxiu would come together to practice constitutional politics and consolidate democracy under its banner.\n\nHowever, the Minxian Party would soon fade, with the radicals (Gu, Zhang, Yang Yongtai) dissipating. Following Sun's creation of the Gemingdang in 1914, with his demand for personal oaths of loyalty, the leading members of Minxian would refuse, forming the European Affairs Research Society to study the First Weltkrieg. Staffed by the likes of Li Genyuan, Cen Chunxuan, Yang, Gu, Zhang, Li Shucheng, and many others, the EARS would fight Yuan Shikai in the National Protection War, splitting into factions afterwards.\n\nGu, Li, Yang, Zhang and others formed the Political Science Society in 1916, debating the merits of Centralism and Federalism, Confucianism and the parliament, alongside constitutionalism. The PSS would see Beiyang perform limited reforms, albeit after Duan Qirui's dissolution of the Assembly, they would largely flee to join Dr. Sun in Guangdong.\n\nAttempting a coup, Yang, Cen, Li and Zhang would flee north to Zhili Beijing, and in 1923 would transform from a society into a Clique, joined by Huang Fu. With the Beijing Coup in 1924, Huang would become Prime Minister, with Zhang taking a key place in the cabinet. However, after Feng Yuxiang's deposition, Political Science would survive purely by political skill alone, managing to receive an amnesty by the Zhili once the dust had settled.\n\nNow an Anti-Zhili body, the Liangguang PSC has taken on its own unique nature. Led largely by Lin Zhengxuan, alongside Yang Ximin and Liu Zhenhuan, with ties to Lin Hu and Shen Hongying, it is more militarised, and inducted with warlords.
They fought for Democracy when no one else did.
Splitters and Traitors, the whole lot of them.
Chen Lianbo, the Merchant Prince
Chen Lianbo was born into a silk merchant family and at age 16 he was employed at the HongKong and Shanghai Banking Corporation (HSBC) while also managing a silk and insurance business on the side. Chen made a fortune during the 1910 Shanghai Financial Crisis, using his HSBC ties to manipulate the gold and silver market. The budding tycoon later expanded into shipping, mining, paper, tea, tung oil, pigs, tobacco, bamboo, straw mats, and other local products.\n\nAmid the 1911 Xinhai Revolution, he and other merchants had organised the Guangzhou Cantonese Merchants Public Security Maintenance Association (better known as the Merchant Corps) to protect their businesses and Chen Lianbo reluctantly was placed at its head. Warlords came and went, and Chen sought to collaborate with them to minimise their abuses though he never quite trusted them.\n\nHis relationship with the KMT was especially sour after they took power in 1922. When pressed to join them, Chen resisted, saying that \
His wealth proves to be a critical asset.
His access to foreign weapons is vital.
The AACSG Entrenches Themselves
The Association of All Classes to Save Guangdong was established by Chen Lianbo after the 1925 Merchant Corps Incident, hoping to rally a coalition to drive out the Kuomintang. But before that, the Merchant Corps had older, deeper roots in various Merchant associations that were formed in different cities.\n\nThe merchant class was traditionally looked down upon, but the modern capitalist system has helped them grow in influence. Their role as compradors to foreign businesses and their connections across the country give them a powerful supporting role for many warlord governments. In Guangdong however the Merchants were able to carve out a direct governing role of their own after the fall of the Kuomintang in 1927.\n\nThe Merchant Corps was officially an extension of the Guangdong Chamber of Commerce but it also included a variety of smaller independent groups such as the Commercial Maintenance Association, Dongguan City Business Group and Merchants Groups representing Xiangcheng, Xinhui City, Jiangmen, Shilong, and Beijing.\n\nSome groups had their own unique character. The Cen Zhiqing's Shuncheng Merchants Group was known for its signature yellow uniform and for being particularly vice heavy. After 1925 it survived by rebranding as the Daliang Citizens' Corps under Luo Bangyi. The Huicheng Merchants Association under Chen Dongzhang nominally had strict rules against smoking, gambling, drinking, bullying, and wearing their uniforms to brothels, boats, and winebars but it was poorly enforced. The Ronggui Merchants Group was led by the notorious blowhard Hu Peiyun while the Guanshan Merchants Group were from Chen Lianbo's home village and he hand-selected their leaders.\n\nThe Merchants were often intertwined with the gentry and also utilised charitable organisations like the Nine Charitable Halls as a mouthpiece for their politics, lobbying against joining the Great War, swaying the people, and even intervening in local warlord politics.
The power of the business class allows us to get ahead.
Their political influence cannot be understated.
The Young Marshal of the South
Lu Yuguang lost his father at a young age and his mother (a Vietnamese woman) fled to China where they met Lu Rongting in Guilin. The elder Lu was smitten and took her as a concubine. An orphan himself, he adopted Lu Yugguang as his son. The ex-bandit, seizing power in Liangguang across the 1910s, was already in his 60s during the apex of his power in the 1920s and was eager to begin planning his succession. Marshal Lu and the Triumvirs
The Young Guangxi Faction has grown tremendously in influence as it takes shape and develops its own identity after the death of the Old Guangxi Patriarch Lu Rongting in 1934. Founded as the young guard of the Old Guangxi Clique by Ma Ji (Lu's adopted son), Lin Hu, and Liu Zhilu, after the Zhili victory and Lu Rongting's restoration following the Northern Expedition they became the dominant faction of the Old Guangxi Clique.\n\nMa Ji is the most powerful of the triumvirate, and second only to Lu Yuguang. His father was a close friend Lu Rongting and had Ma Ji declare him his adopted father. A skilled martial artist and a graduate of Guangxi Military School, he served as Lu Rongting's personal assistant and as a field commander of the Old Guangxi Clique. It is said he fired the first shot of the National Protection War.\n\nAfter Lu Rongting's defeat in 1924, he served as Chief of Staff of Wu Peifu's Headquarters and fought on the Zhili's side during the Northern Expedition. His friendship with Wu Peifu began in 1918, where they met while negotiating in Hunan after being introduced by the Guangxi born Zhili staff officer Zhang Qijin. He also became very close to Lin Hu, one of the few other formally trained officers in the Guangxi Army. The two became sworn brothers, and Lin is known to be among the more politically engaged members of the Young Guangxi Faction - having associated himself with Sun Yat-sen, Chen Jiongming, and other ideological leaders. Liu Zhilu is the third of the triumvirate, an experienced commander who helped organise the Allied Forces Office in Zhangzhou along with some Yue Army Officers. Another important officer is Guo Chunsen, a close aide of Lu Rongting with a gentry (rather than bandit) background.\n\nAs Lu Yuguang's power grows, his relationship with the triumvirate has strained. Unlike his father, he is a generation younger than the triumvirs and cannot rely on filial piety. They will have to resolve their differences behind closed doors...
Lu Yuguang asserts his authority.
Ma Ji is the real power behind the throne.
From a Bandit to a Warlord
Shen Hongying was a Hakka born in Guangxi. He worked as a farmer, earning side-income selling herbal medicines and performing Shaolin martial arts before an audience. One day he and his brother were robbed by local soldiers and Shen sought revenge - killing their attackers before enlisting as a soldier. During the Xinhai Revolution his unit fought on the side of the revolutionaries, ultimately coming under the command of Lu Rongting.\n\nOver the next few years he would continue to rise through the ranks and eventually become a warlord in his own right, playing off different factions before he was ultimately defeated by the New Guangxi Clique. Nevertheless, he was able to bounce back with the help of the Zhili, and his Shen Army Faction dominated the northern part of Guangdong.\n\nThe Shen Army, despite often being bandits themselves, gained fame suppressing banditry under Lu Rongting. However they became notorious for their brutality and indiscipline while stationed in Guangdong, engaging in various vices, looting, and extorting locals. Despite their strength and equipment, they often lost battles due to out-dated tactics.\n\nWith Shen's power on the rise, it might be wise for him and his deputies to clean up their acts. Enforcing discipline is no easy feat, but a truly wise and strong leader is capable of passing such a test.
Shen is a great leader, and great leaders discipline their men.
A truly wise leader knows when to trust his soldiers.
Zhu Jiahua, the Grey Eminence
Zhu Jiahua was a student in the Tongji School in Shanghai, a university founded and funded by Germans. He joined a Dare-to-Die Squad during the Xinhai Revolution in 1911 before continuing his electrical engineering education at Tongji. Afterwards he won a scholarship to study metallurgy in Berlin where after some disruptions from the Great War, he completed his PhD. He became a prominent German-educated Professor in Peking University before leaving Beijing after the brutal suppression of the March 18th Protests in 1926 by the Fengtian Clique.\n\nAfterwards he went southwards where he taught at Guangdong University and briefly worked in the KMT government but refused to join the party. He proved to be a successful intermediary after the German seizure of Guangzhou, allowing him to play an important role in Chen Lianbo's government before seeking a position under Sun Chuanfang. He joined Li Linsi's budding Jingxing Society, which began as a vehicle for Sino-German Cultural Exchanges but developed into a political movement to improve Sino-German ties and promote Chinese modernisation along German lines.\n\nAfter the brutal suppression of the 1932 Shanghai Uprisings, Zhu resigned from the Sun government and returned to Guangzhou. Influential within Sino-German circles, the ascendant Shen Hongying has hired him as an advisor. But Zhu is a crafty and ambitious man of his own, and many believe that he is the true mastermind behind the previously shambling Shen Army's rise.
Zhu dedicates his efforts to improving Shen's image.
Zhu leverages his contacts to bolster Shen's position.

Some even mused the possibility of establishing a rival government of their own.

Li Highlander Uprising
As it stands currently, the majority of China's citizens are located in urban cities that are at high risk of siege or invasion. To counter this a plan has been put forward on relocating the denizens of Haikou and Qionghai to the Wuzhi mountains, located in central Qiongya, in order to provide a safe haven from any coastal invasion. Albeit such a proposed plan has been met with much contension by the local populace.\n\nThe ethnic Li population have become invigorated into an uprising over such a proposal due to fears of Han dominance in the region becoming a more apparent threat to their way of life and culture. The core ringleader of the uprising is one Wang Guoxing, leader of a local Li Village in Qiongya. Largely staffed by his family, the rebellion is evidently built on the unbreakably strong ties of village to village.\n\nWith matters of national security at stake, members of the government have advocated for a particularly hardline response, some even suggesting the planned execution of thousands of Li villagers in order to quell the uprising. Balking at such a heavy-handed and brutal idea, many in the fierce opposition have instead insisted upon the necessary investments made into the protection of Li culture, and the local area. Ultimately their proposals have been met with counter arguments that such frivolous spending would deprive necessary materiel for the war effort.

Naval Cliques
Despite our dominance in the navy, we are still on shaky ground with the numerous other cliques, namely the divide between the Whampoa Army and the Guangzhou Naval clique, both seek to modernise the navy but their division is based on how centralised the fleets should be. The Whampoa Army would have the navy under their wing whereas the Guangzhou Naval clique wants greater independence from the army, which grew from their training that it received from the British officers in Hong Kong.\n\nWe could enter friendly relations with one of these factions, however other options are still available outside of the aforementioned cliques who have already taken root in our government. The Fujian and Qingdao cliques, which are the representatives of the regionalist forces. One thing to note is their development of how their field of study developed, example being the Qingdao clique received their studies from the Japanese and German navies whereas the Fujian clique is a much more organically home line of thought that developed more and more with the conflicts that occurred after the failed northern expedition.\n\nAnswers on who we ally ourselves with is to be answered as soon as possible so that not just the protection of our coastal territories can be guaranteed but also become a patriotic example for China on the future world stage.

Changing with the Times
As the Guangzhou Government modernises its military along the lines of the former League of Eight Provinces, new ideas swirl around the officers. German ideas of warfare proliferate amongst the army. Although German officers had only a limited role in training the Liangguang military, they brought along the ideas of Max Bauer, a radical proponent of a totalitarian military dictatorship dedicated only to war. Bauer proposed state planned development of heavy industry, communications, and air transport, proper introduction in the tactics of modern warfare, including chemical warfare. While the government is less inclined to follow Bauer's ideas of totalitarian dictatorship, his ideas of industry development have been quite influential.\n\nIndeed, the Three-Year Plan embarked upon by the Guangzhou Government was influenced in part by Bauer. Officers in our military have been quite taken by Bauer's ambitious theories of warfare. The military has repeatedly petitioned [GXC.GetLeader] to prioritise railroad and communication modernisation. Despite the innovative new direction of the military, it remains to be seen how effective reforms will be. The bureaucracy of China has proven to not be as flexible as hoped, and many officers are still exercising the military strategies of our imperial past, when China pitted barbarian against barbarian. Regardless, [GXC.GetLeader] remains hopeful that China will march into the future against all odds.
Large Farmers can be military geniuses too...
Luring the Snake
As China and Japan once again enter into another conflict, it is increasingly clear that this war will be like no other. The Empire of Japan and their lapdog collaborators employ vicious and new strategies of war. Many of our plans have not survived first contact with the invaders. Defensive lines alone will not stall the enemy advances. Indeed, the Japanese have mastered mobile warfare far more than the Chinese military has. We cannot hope to engage the enemy head on; no, we must improvise new tactics for our current situation.\n\n[GXC.GetLeader] has instructed our generals to conserve as much fighting strength as possible, and to retreat whenever necessary. We must now lure the Japanese invaders deeper and deeper into China in order to overstretch their logistics and communications. Just as Lettow Vorbeck successfully evaded the British with limited resources and a weaker military force, we too will seek to outlast a superior enemy. When the Empire of the Rising Sun falters and the opportunity arises, our military officers will launch a counteroffensive and put an end to a century of unbearable humiliation!
China will never fall!
Lessons from West Point
In the years following the failed Northern Expedition of Sun Yat-sen's Kuomintang, the Chinese government in Beijing sought inspiration for new military methods to defeat rebel Marshal Zhang Zuolin and his Japanese backers. In the spirit of modernisation, a Chinese military mission went to the United States of America to observe West Point. Located in New York, West Point was the premier service academy for the Federal Government of the United States prior to the Second American Civil War. Although most officers of the Chinese military mission to the United States owed their allegiance to the Qing central government and the League of Eight Provinces, a few officers were aligned with the Guangdong clique.\n\nWith Qing hegemony now shattered and the order in China forever altered, it now falls upon the Guangzhou Government to rebuild the middle kingdom. Although the United States has now fallen into chaos, West Point is still regarded as a prestigious institution that may very well serve as the inspiration for the new Central Military Academy. Officers who took part in the military mission have been hard at work styling this new academy in the image of West Point, and [GXC.GetLeader] has great confidence that unlike the Kuomintang established Whampoa Military Academy, this academy will train the new generation in the modern arts of war, rather than revolutionary zealotry.
What a sad fate for such a beautiful country...
Eliteheer Achieved
Although the German Empire is an imperialist state deserving of scorn and partition, there is much to respect when it comes to the military competence. Indeed, the Chinese military reforms undertaken by the new central government have been primarily inspired by the German concept of the Eliteheer. German military theories are now widely discussed amongst Chinese military circles. Although the government stopped short of Bauer's totalitarian political system, his ideas of heavy artillery usage, heavy industry development, rail modernisation, and creation of modern communication networks have been adopted by the Chinese state. The list of prominent German officers that the military has taken a liking to does not stop with Bauer, however.\n\nLettow Vorbeck is widely respected in China due to his achievements in the Weltkrieg, and Heinz Guderian's ideas of Bewegungskrieg have been quite intriguing although armour technologies in China have yet to match our more powerful neighbours. The up and coming star of German officers stationed in the far east Erwin Rommel has also caught the attention of many in our military. Indeed, many generals are attempting to emulate his command style to earn the nickname 'Rommel of the East.' The most influential German officer in China though, has been Hans von Seeckt. His book 'Gedanken eines Soldaten' (The Thoughts of a Soldier) is very popular and quoted all the time in military circles in China. These reforms are just the beginning, China will continue the drive towards modernity, spearheaded by the Chinese Wehrmacht!
The Empire of the Rising Sun has a new rival in the East...
Surpassing Beiyang
Though ultimately a gross failure of China's earlier attempts of modernisation, the idea of a modern central army was not flawed conceptually. Today, with a nation in pieces and regionalist warlords seizing control of far flung provinces, it has become all the more important that the Central Government wield an army superior to provincial forces, and knowledgeable of modern military tactics.\n\nWith the fall of the Beijing Government of the Great Qing, it now falls to the Guangzhou Government to pick up the mantle of Beiyang and establish a new central army, powerful enough to keep the warlords in check, foreign invaders at bay, and spearhead military modernisation. With years of military reform and experimental tactics, the new Central Government has finally achieved that which the Beiyang Government could not. Despite our lack of German aid and economic collapse in all provinces due to the chaos of the League collapse and Black Monday, the Guangzhou Government triumphs through all obstacles.\n\nThe Chinese military and government must not grow complacent at the current success we have seen in the preceding years, for the climb back to world prominence is a long one and threats internal and external still exist and await an opportune moment to return the middle kingdom to the state of weakness it was in during the height of the Warlord Era. Let these threats come and test themselves against the New Model Army of the Republic, heir and supplanter of the National Revolutionary and Beiyang Armies!

The Dragon and the Eagle
Even though the United States of America has been plunged into chaos in the aftermath of the Elections of 1936, it remains a role model to many in China, including but not limited to Chen Jiongming, Wu Peifu, and Sun Yat-sen. Indeed, the United States service academy of West Point has been a great inspiration to the Central Military Academy. Just as West Point has trained many great officers with internalised republican virtue, so too has the Central Military Academy. Much like the United States Military before General MacArthur, the Republic of China Armed Forces swears to not interfere with the politics of the nation, acting as simply the apolitical guardians of the republic.\n\nThese military reforms have seen much success in our efforts during the Second Sino Japanese War and against warlord rebels. The Japanese came in, expecting a weak military to simply knock down and humiliate. Instead, they faced a modernised military, one which has loyalty not to a single political party or individual, but the nation itself. Where warlords expected a self-serving military force that would be content with its own slice of China, they face a nationalist force committed to total unification and an end to warlord exploitation of the common folk. No longer will China be restrained by selfish opportunists from within and foreign vultures, China will walk amongst the free nations of the world!

A Return to Warfare of Old
Finally, after an interregnum spanning nine hundred years and a multitude of dynasties, the Chinese soldier now finally practices the Chinese way of waging war. All across the nation, the soldier and peasant act as one organism functioning in symbiosis. In peacetime, the Chinese citizen tends to their occupation, to their family, to their livelihood, and in war the same citizen will take up arms to defend their nation and community. No longer is the soldier class derided as the rowdy, unintelligent brute no better than a bandit, but instead seen as a core component of average Chinese society. One does not need to look far to see the merit in the integration of the militia system, as our staggering national rise could not have been done without the loyalty and ferocity exhibited by the militia, striking deep into the enemy no matter their size.\n\nWith the new reforms of the military and citizenry in our hand, the Chinese nation is sure to never fall to the crippling weakness and exploitation it endured during the decline of the Qing, instead a new era has dawned on the Middle Kingdom, a phoenix from the ashes destined for greatness. With the militia ensuring perfect efficiency, cohesion and mutual respect throughout our nation, no one can doubt the new era will see China return as a dominant world power.
Four thousand years of warfare, perfected!

From Kuomintang to Federalist, Zhili to Fengtian and all inbetween, there have always been individuals placing their very lives on the line to fight back against the tyrannical structure of previous dynasties. Once again, like a moth to a flame, these individuals have answered the call to arms rang throughout the nation, excelling above and beyond their contemporaries in a cause they truly are devoted to.\n\nThis statement is not any more exemplified in the waves of devout, loyal and fierce cadres produced by our Whampoa academy, striking deep into the heart of all whomst oppose the liberation of the Chinese people. The revival of Whampoa has brought the Chinese army onto the forefront of the world's armies, and never again shall a century of humiliation befall their people, for the very fire of the revolutionary cause burns furiously in every soldier, never to be doused.

Dongjiang River Column

As China is unable to fight the Japanese man for man due to their technological superiority, the National Government has engaged in a strategy of Guerilla warfare, where the military is to utilise the land and China's home field advantage against the enemy.\n\nFollowing the Central Government's example, [GXC.GetLeader] has created the Dongjiang Column of the Guangdong People's Anti-Japanese Guerilla force. Led by Gu Dacun, Zeng Sheng, and Wang Zuoyao and comprising upwards of ten thousand partisans, this guerilla force will help push the Japanese invaders out of Guangdong. Already, the soldiers of the Dongjiang have assisted in defending China by eliminating Japanese strongholds along the Guangzhou-Kowloon railway, as well as defending the Shenzhen river. If China is to defeat Japan in this final hurdle to secure the National Revolution, we will need many more forces like the Dongjiang Column.

Favouritism in Whampoa
In light of the recent military reforms modelling our army on that of our forebearers, the legacy of Whampoa shines bright and strong throughout the armies, and its cadets gleam with revolutionary spirit. With undeniable dedication to both the party and National Revolution, Whampoa derived units are heralded as some of the most elite forces our nation has to offer, and receive extensive praise from both the government and public for their military feats.\n\nHowever, this has allowed a steady increase of corruption and rot to fester within our command structure, for Whampoa units have started to receive extensive favouritism by the high command. Personally spoiled by their commanders, receiving the latest and most premier equipment and funding first before all other units, there has even been a report that a cadre of unruly Whampoa cadets with actions worthy of a court martial were merely given a lecture by their commanding officer, and asked to apologise.\n\nLeading the charge against such blatant nepotism, face of the New Guangxi Clique and leading military figure, Li Zongren, has fired a volley of accusations at the government, accusing important figures such as [GXC.GetLeader] of endorsing and condoning such actions. He has pressed forth that the regional armies should be treated as equals of their Whampoa counterparts, and that rewards should be given on merit, not loyalties.\n\nMuch of what Li Zongren spouts rings true, for Whampoa units do not tend to excel in combat much more than the armies of New Guangxi or New Guangdong. Nevertheless, whilst not actively condoning the actions, the current state of affairs had been most beneficial for [GXC.GetLeader] as a loyal army ensured swift progress on the revolutionary front. The True Meaning of the KMT
With great swathes of China now under the control of the Nationalist government, the Kuomintang must now endeavour to begin an efficient and competent programme for economic reconstruction. While many opinions have surfaced on how said reconstruction could look, Hu Hanmin has recalled a paper he wrote while in exile in Paris to be a guiding theory of the KMT's economic policies. Titled 'The True Meaning of the KMT,' Hu asserts that the KMT as a party of the workers and peasants stood for the interests of all Chinese people. Stressing the interests of workers and peasants, the KMT gathered around it all Chinese in forging a revolution to overthrow the warlord dictatorship and restore political power to the people.\n\nHu stressed that all the Chinese constituted one class of people, who suffered from the bullying of foreign imperialists. Rebuking a criticism that the KMT was a party of petty bourgeoisie, Hu reiterated that the party was the sole revolutionary body representing the interests of all the Chinese.\n\nIn the paper, Hu continued to promote his one-class Chinese theory via addressing the question of the Three People's Principles. He regarded the Principles as an integral set of revolutionary doctrine and asserted that it would be impermissible for anyone to take a certain portion from it to serve one particular group of people. Quoting Sun, 'the Principle of People's Livelihood is socialism and is also known as communism,' Hu concluded that common ownership of property, connoted achievement of happiness by the entire nation, in rejection of Marxist class struggle theory.\n\nNever in his paper did Hu indulge in possible convergence of the KMT and the CSP for their ideologies were so far apart from one another, having urged the Central Committee to move further away from the Syndicalist League many years ago. Of course, Chairman Wang paid no heed to Hu's words, and led the party to disaster. Now, Hu has his chance to fix Wang's mess and follow Sun's vision to the letter.
The party will ensure economic prosperity for all!
National Finance Conference
With the Nationalist Government establishing the Reconstruction programme, a National Finance Conference has been called. In the name of assisting the peasants and workers, it was decreed that no new surtaxes were to be levied, and surtaxes would not exceed the basic land tax. In light of this, 5200 surtaxes were to be abolished. While this will reduce tax revenue by 49 million, KMT financial ministers agree that it is a necessary step for rural and urban reconstruction.
$GXC.lategame.251.d_intro$\n\nThe Conference also tackled the issue of standardisation, aiming to standardise both taxes and currencies in lands controlled by the Nationalist government and aligned warlord provinces. Farming and lijin duty on movement of goods would not be taxed. Leading luminaries including Sun Fo and Ju Zheng plan to account for the lost tax revenue by diverting money meant to go to the military, and have pressed Chairman Hu Hanmin on this issue. The generals of the New Guangxi Clique have strongly objected to these measures, asserting that without a strong military budget, the NRA would not be able to complete total unification, or fend off invasion from the Japanese or Germans.\n\nThe Conference was ultimately unable to decide on who the Chairman of the Central Bank ought to be, although the list of candidates has been narrowed down. The first candidate Huang Hanliang is unliked by many, but is a liberal banker with ties to Sun Fo and is unlikely to cause Hu Hanmin much trouble. The second candidate, Fu Rulin, is a safer choice, being a favourite of Ju Zheng and his Western Hills Clique. The third and fourth candidates, Ou Fangpu and Huang Xuchu, are not tied to Hu, Ju, or Sun, but many suspect that they may be tied to militarist elements in the army, especially Chen Jitang and Li Zongren. The Conference has requested that Chairman Hu choose the chairman in the name of unity.

Surplus Squabbles
Thanks to Boxer Indemnity Funds and Customs Surplus among other things, the Nationalist government now has a tidy sum of money not currently dedicated to any effort or project. Many in the government have already drafted formal proposals for the proper allocation of the funds, first among them Sun Fo, eager to utilise his knowledge as the new minister of rails. Despite his extensive plans, the Political Council of the Kuomintang is reluctant to give Sun funding.\n\nZou Lu staunchly opposes Sun Fo's reallocation of the Boxer funds, as they were originally promised towards the ministry of education by Sun Yat-sen, which Zou Lu vehemently defends as a leading advocate of public education. Backed by the WHC, Zou Lu's opposition poses genuine concern to many on the Political Council. Independent of the squabbling of the civilian government, officers Chen Jitang and Li Zongren have also outlined their plans for the use of the extra funds.\n\nChen, as governor of Guangdong province, hopes to utilise the funds to enrich Guangdong and shore up his administration via the creation of railways, the development of government industries, and increased funding of his provincial military. Chen has enticed Hu Hanmin with promises of support should the funding be sent his way. Many on the Political Council have denounced Chen for his regionalist tendencies, perhaps the only thing Sun and Zou can agree on.\n\nLi Zongren has proposed that all of the funds go towards the National Revolutionary Army, as unification is far from over. Li argues that money spent on anything will be a waste as future wars will disrupt those efforts anyways. While the Political Council remains unimpressed with both Chen and Li's proposals, they do recognise the considerable military influence both men possess, and are hesitant to have two enemies with control of the army. In the end, the decision falls to [GXC.GetLeader] to break the deadlock and choose where the funds shall go.

Sun Fo Intrigues
Although Sun Fo gained great influence in the government after being appointed as the minister of rails, he was still a new face at the head of a new ministry in the Nationalist Government. Unlike Zou Lu, Sun was not respected by the party despite his familial lineage, or perhaps in spite of it. Sun purchased four planes from the American-owned Curtis-Wright company and formed the Chinese National Aviation Corporation, and was made president. On top of being president of the CNAC and minister of rails, Sun also negotiated with Curtis-Wright for an airmail service between the major cities of China, including Shanghai, Wuhan, Nanjing, Beijing, and Guangzhou.\n\nHowever in doing so, Sun gained the ire of the communications ministry. Wang Boqun, the minister of communications, sued Sun for overreaching with his authority and encroaching on their domain. Wang also pushed for his ministry to be renamed the Ministry of Communications and Transportation. In the end, the Chinese Supreme Court sided with the communications ministry against Sun, forcing him to relinquish his position of president of the CNAC to Wang.

The Northern Kuomintang
The Kuomintang has always been a southern based party, with Dr. Sun being of Cantonese descent and many of his top lieutenants drawn from Guangdong. This problem worsened as conflicts arose between the Beiyang Government and Southern Revolutionaries, culminating in a decade-long Guangzhou-Beijing split.\n\nSuccessive generations of leadership reinforced this regionalism, with Kuomintang leaders, officers, and supporters often being drawn from southern provinces like Guangdong, Guangxi, and Hunan. The major poles of Kuomintang activity were Guangzhou and Shanghai, and thanks to complicated systems of patronage as well as internal party purges very few Kuomintang members or National Revolutionary Army officers can claim northern heritage.\n\nAs a consequence it was virtually unanimously welcome news to hear that the northern branches of the party have begun to blossom as the Kuomintang's legitimacy rises. Much of this has been credited to the spirited leadership of northern Kuomintang figures such as Ding Weifen and Yu Youren. Ding led multiple secret political organs called the Grand Alliance (Datong Meng) as cover organisations for the party right under the noses of Beiyang warlords, his words continue to carry considerable weight in Northern provinces. Yu Youren similarly carries great stature, described as optimistic, sincere, and forthright. With a powerful build and speech, he carries the \

The Creditors Claim Their Due
Li Zongren may be gone, but the ideas he represented are hardly dead. Li, like most nationalist warlords, had an idiosyncratic view of regionalism, often defending the need for provincial autonomy and protecting their right to a semi-independent army to keep central government encroachments at bay. At the same time, they often bemoaned the lack of national unity and were quick to admonish any notion that they were secessionist.\n\nWhatever the case, their active promotion of these principles has found considerable currency across aligned provinces, taking advantage of Kuomintang regional, provincial, and local level reconstruction and tutelage programs to enhance their own wealth and power. Even as General Li was escorted ignominiously from the Second Congress, few doubted it would be long before another took his place. Indeed, Guangxi is said to have quickly returned to its old status quo.\n\nThey need not look far. Chen Jitang, the Governor of Guangdong, is perhaps the strongest warlord in China and one of the most prominent proponents of regional autonomy (at least for Guangdong). His direct and consistent support for Hu Hanmin and his ostensibly democratic allies has become a liability in this regard, though not quite in the same way as the New Guangxi Clique was. Unlike LiBai, Chen seems to have little ambition outside of Guangdong, even going so far as to eagerly aid Hu and the central government in their march north if only just to get them out of Guangzhou.\n\nRooting out corruption in Guangdong is not as simple or clear-cut of a matter: he does not pose an immediate threat to the central government and in fact has resisted entreaties from other warlords to join them in rebellious coalitions against the government. At the same time, he jealously guards his authority, refusing to allow any central government directives to bypass his office. Most have contented themselves with \

The Chairman and the Party-State
Hu Hanmin is a peculiar and often mercurial personality, though if there is one thing consistent about him its that he is a strict adherent to Dr. Sun's plans for reconstruction. As a consequence he has faithfully adapted his mentor's plans for a transitional party-state, the Kuomintang being heavily enmeshed into the inner organs of the government itself. Hu Hanmin has strategically centralised power around his office, leveraging his control over the party apparatus to his advantage.\n\nHe has largely eschewed using the title of President (sometimes translated Chairman) of the National Government, seeing that as coming more into play during the constitutional phase of the revolution. For now, he primarily rules as Chairman of the Kuomintang, in part to stress the temporary nature of the tutelage but also as a means of strengthening his own legitimacy. Likewise, while avoiding any personal cult of personality for himself, he freely ensures that the Nationalist government idolise Sun Yat-sen as virtually a secular deity. The Third National Congress
At last, China has been unified under one banner: the banner of the Kuomintang. It has been a long road. Sacrifices were made, families were divided, brave martyrs were lost. But in the end, despite everything the world could array against the revolution, Dr. Sun's teachings won out. Inspired by the eternal wisdom of the Three Principles, the Kuomintang rallied and inspired the people to overcome all those challenges just like its late revolutionary prophet said they would.\n\nFinally then, Chairman Hu Hanmin, on behalf of the Chinese people, has issued a call for delegates from all across the nation to come to [ROOT.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] for deliberations as China can finally turn to the next page of its story: the nation-wide political tutelage. While many feared that like the provincial and local tutelages the process would be marred by the interference of military men, the victory of civilian factions in the party has shone a light at the end of the tunnel. For the first time in decades, the Chinese people can actually hope for a democratic future.\n\nThat is not to say there is not plenty of (well-earned) cynicism surrounding the Congress. Indeed, as Hu Hanmin steps up to the podium to address the amassed delegates at the start of proceedings, he has reiterated his words of caution against self-congratulatory indolence, instead extolling the need for continued revolutionary vigour. In Six Years, he boasts, the Kuomintang will complete the political tutelage and lay the ground work for the final phase of the revolution: a Constitutional Republic.\n\nOf course, despite Hu Hanmin's insistence that six years is a literal time-frame, even showing off his outline for how to achieve democracy within that window, many suspect that such a schedule is overly ambitious. Few think that a full democracy by the end of the decade is plausible, but times are clearly changing and many are growing bolder in their aspirations for the future of China.
With Hu at the helm, democracy is not far away.
Its clear that we have a long way to go.
The Constitutional Crusade
Ever since the Li Zongren crisis, few have been willing to mention Li or Bai in public, with many hoping to forget it ever occurred. But the incident remains fresh in Sun Fo's mind, the young idealist forever shaken by the threat of military men to his father's revolution. Despite the prevailing orthodoxy his father set by insisting that no constitution be drawn until the constitutional phase, Sun has begun to come around to the notion that a provisional constitution must be drafted to act as a safeguard against further instability or tyranny. Growing almost obsessed with the issue, spending much of his spare time personally trying to draft one.\n\nTo Hu Hanmin however, there cannot be deviation from the party-line set by Sun Yat-sen. The reason why a constitution must wait until the constitutional phase (and also why the 1912 constitution was thrown out) is so all parties and groups across China can contribute to its drafting together on equal footing. To draft a provisional constitution now, while the Kuomintang has been invested with temporary but nevertheless full and absolute power needed to complete the tutelage, would be illogical. Moreover it would unfairly usurp the power of the future democratically elected National Assembly, and be an open admission that the party does not believe can finish the tutelage in a timely manner.\n\nIt would seem that while many have lost faith in the ability for democracy to be achieved within this generation, Hu Hanmin has not. More cynical minds however point out that when it comes to politics, Hu and the Elders control the party while other factions have sway over the bureaucracy. A provisional constitution that establishes the promised five branches early would shift power away from the Chairman and the party-state towards the newly founded bureaucratic government. This issue has exposed deep ideological dividing lines between the civilian party, and with no compromise in sight it seems only one world-view will prevail.
It is far too early to begin drafting a constitution.
On the contrary, we have gone too long without one.
Reconstructing a Nation
Part and parcel to the Kuomintang National Reconstruction plan is the economic reconstruction, which is simultaneously extremely detailed yet also very open to interpretation, much like the other parts of Sun Yat-sen's doctrine. While a greater emphasis on economic affairs and relief for the common people was and remains the prevailing attitude among the civilian right, a growing sense of weariness has emerged among more conservative elements of the party.\n\nThe Western Hills faction has always been wary of the socialistic, iconoclastic nature of the post-reorganisation party, denouncing the left for their stoking of class warfare and anarchy. The staggering cost of Hu Hanmin's proposed national reconstruction agenda has also drawn many concerned eyes, combined with many Nationalists' aggressive anti-capitalist and xenophobic rhetoric many investors (especially foreign ones) are reluctant to give the Republic much needed capital to expand the economy. The alternative so far has been autarky and heavy taxation, which has attracted considerable resentment.\n\nFinally breaking their silence, Ju Zheng, Zou Lu, and Fu Rulin have drafted a resolution calling for the paring back of the scale and scope of the National Reconstruction Commission, hoping to concentrate Kuomintang financial energies towards only large-scale projects domestic investors cannot fund on their own. They have also advised the relaxation of taxation and economic controls, promoting a more market conciliatory approach to the Kuomintang \
Wise men know when and where to open their purse.
A full-throttle approach is the only way to see true democracy.
The Other Republicans
Despite growing dissent over the plausibility of a six year tutelage, Hu Hanmin finds himself well within his wheelhouse. In addition to being an experienced politician, gifted ideologue, and silver-tongued speaker, Hu is an exceptional legislator. With well-practised skill, he has begun to coax leading members of the congress towards his drafted six years plan of legislation, a half-decade long series of bills and ordinances that when implemented would lead the way in reshaping the nation towards a democracy.\n\nPromising key appointments to drafting committees to leading delegates, Hu has stressed the need for a stable tutelage in which the party-state fully mobilises its energies to accomplish its founding mission. The temporary nature of the tutelage, rather than being a source of instability is in fact a rallying point. Its short nature means that in proper democratic centralist fashion once the plan is drafted, deliberated, and decided all citizens will put their petty reservations and differences aside in favour of one shared, immediate goal, thus putting their full energies into seeing the plan through.\n\nHaving finally found a workable coalition of democratic Kuomintang figures, Hu insists that further compromise is not needed for the remainder of tutelage. Other parties and factions could wait until the soon-promised Constitutional phase where free elections in every single one of the thousands of counties in China can decide each group's proper influence. His prospective coalition partners are not so sure. Ju Zheng and the Western Hills faction have pleaded that the Kuomintang include other parties, pointing out that while the Beiyang Republic was incurably corrupted, it did have a budding centre-right democratic movement that should be separated and incorporated for the sake of stability and pluralism. Sun Fo similarly has called for the inclusion of trustworthy, idealistic parties - the fellow centre-left republican groups based in the south. Left unsaid is these inclusions would advance their own shared interests.
As one committee we can finally see tutelage through.
South and North must unite if China is to be one.
We were never alone in our fight for democracy.
The Democratic Awakening
The Kuomintang's party constitution states that the National Congress must elect its Chairman, though in practice the party's next chiefs rarely been in much doubt except in times of severe distress that is often accompanied by violence. But for the first time in perhaps its entire history, the party seems slated for a competitive (albeit insular) race. In addition to Hu Hanmin, the incumbent, both Ju Zheng of the Western Hills faction and Sun Fo of the Reconstruction Faction have announced their candidacies, seeking to steer the party through the remainder of the political tutelage.\n\nHu showed little signs of unease as he announced the candidates, still feeling assured of his inevitable victory in elections. After all, it was he that kept the party through its ignominious exile, kept the ravenous warlords at bay, fought off foreign threats and led the party from Guangzhou to national unification. To him, this election is the crowning moment of his career, a confirmation from representatives from across the entire nation that the people trust him to lead them across the final bridge to constitutionalism.\n\nDespite his confidence, his opponents are hardly unknown upstarts either. Ju Zheng is a faithful party stalwart, a revolutionary leader in Shandong during the 1911 Revolution and selected by Dr. Sun to be his interior minister in 1921. An effective jurist and founding member of the Western Hills Conference, his moderate conservative agenda has attracted considerable following from much of the party old-guard, northern delegates, and the bureaucracy.\n\nSun Fo likewise has become a household name as a gifted law-maker with an almost encyclopedic expertise of his father's work. His progressive agenda has sought a middle position between Hu and Ju. His support base among the party young guard, southerners, and intellectuals cannot easily be discounted. After each candidate finished using their allotted time to make their case for leadership, voting commenced.

A New Mandate for the Old Chairman
Quickly, but meticulously, the votes were counted. Hu Hanmin, as expected, has emerged the winner by a slim but still sufficient margin. A short round of celebration emerged spontaneously as the party commemorated its first peaceful, competitive, and relatively fair election, a promising sign for what many hope will come ahead. There were some grumbling of course, especially from those who did not receive sufficient endorsements to make the ballot, but both Ju Zheng and Sun Fo were quick to shake hands and congratulate the winner to quiet any concerns of another party fracture.\n\nHu Hanmin has taken the stage, congratulating them in turn for an election well run and thanking the party for having faith in him for another term. Waxing poetically, as he usually does, about the philosophical implications of tutelage and his fond memories of Dr. Sun and revolutionaries past, he then turns the discussion to the future. Much work remains ahead, and though Hu's position as the premier party leader is now undisputed, the contradictatory demands placed on him has led many to question his true position on the direction of tutelage. A choice looms, between Hu's utopian dreams that will require the thorough political, social, and economic revolution he has long described and the impossibly speedy rate of progress Hu vows he will complete it in. The party has made its choice today, but what tomorrow holds remains another question.

Ju Zheng Wins the Vote

An arrogant man, but even prouder of his principles, Hu Hanmin accepted his defeat with an icy grace, shaking hands and congratulating the victor before the Congress. In that moment, the transfer of power was complete: Ju Zheng was the new Chairman of the Kuomintang. A short round of celebration emerged spontaneously as the party commemorated its first peaceful, competitive, and relatively fair election, a promising sign for what many hope will come ahead. There were some grumbling of course, especially from those who did not receive sufficient endorsements to make the ballot, but Hu Hanmin's acquiesence and Sun Fo formally conceding the election shortly afterwards sent a clear message: there will be no party fracture.\n\nJu Zheng has taken the stage, congratulating his opponents on an election well run and thanking the departing Chairman for his long service to the nation. He turns to the audience with the steady, confident voice of a scholar-gentleman. The seed of democracy that the party has sacrificed for all those years has finally sprouted today, and Ju promises to continue to nurture it until it blooms. Quoting Chinese Classics and Sun Yat-sen's teachings with practised poise, his speech reassures the audience of a steady march to the repudiation of the interceding influence of socialism and the foundation of a new Chinese democracy led by gentlemen rather than uncouth warlords or radical rabblerousers. He closes to applause, but some wonder whether or not he is throwing away the party's chance to truly change the nation by moving too quickly.

Madame Li Zongren
Despite the relatively conservative nature of the new government, avenues remain open for some women to exert significant power. Guo Dejie is a strong and forceful woman, keenly interested in her husband Li Zongren's work. Unlike Bai Chongxi's wife, who is comparatively shy and out of the spotlight, Guo Dejie was a formal member of the Guangxi provincial government and actively served in a variety of roles including Director of the Women's Propaganda Team for the Seventh Army, Executive Director of the Guangxi Women's Association and overseeing multiple wartime charities for orphans.\n\nAn indispensable part of General Li's public image, and with Chairman Lin Sen being a widower, she has happily played the role of Nationalist China's de facto First Lady. She is not, however, Li's only spouse. Like most powerful Chinese men, Li took on a concubine in 1924. Initially on good terms, Li's wife and concubine soon found themselves at odds. In an reversal of expected roles, the wife bore Li's only child (a son) while the concubine emerged increasingly powerful as her husband's chief emissary. Guo was the concubine.\n\nThe acrimony within the Li's household has become a source of rumour-mongering in [ROOT.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName], tarnishing Guo's reputation within elite circles even as she enjoys a strong public image. The Yue-Gui Showdown

The Gambit of the Western Hills
Despite ostensibly being the ruling party in the Republic, the Western Hills Faction are caught in a bind, trapped between the warring Yue and Gui camps. Ruling from [ROOT.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName], their power hardly stretches outside of the Presidential Palace (which Lin Sen has refused to personally inhabit, preferring instead a modest rented home), and their leaders frequently shuttle from Nanning to Guangzhou to exhort unity.\n\nLi Zongren has claimed the lion's share of credit for the Nationalist government's unification of China, leaving the Western Hills government chronically short on credibility with the general public. Their ability to control the generals or enforce the negotiated terms after the Second Congress have largely faded, as Li Liejun's efforts can only travel so far. The Guangzhou rebels formally acknowledge Lin's Presidency but largely ignore him, unwilling to cooperate with a government with Li in office. They have pleaded for Lin to order Li's removal, a political impossibility during the war.\n\nDespite this, the Western Hills faction remains determined to resist the General's influence. Throwing the Gauntlet
News of Lin Sen's imminent resignation as President has reverbated across the nation, quickly escaping the nominally private Central Executive Committee meeting. By dawn it has reached the front pages of every major newspaper in cities all across China. His decision to nominate Ju Zheng as his successor has caught the Gui camp off-guard, but Li Zongren remains undaunted, accepting a nomination of his own from major allies like Bai Chongxi and Huang Shaohong. A few minor candidates have thrown their hats in the ring, but the field is expected to winnow out very quickly before the formal party vote.\n\nEyes turn to a humble home in Guangzhou, the unofficial base of the Yue faction. There lives the only real third contender for power within the Kuomintang apparatus: the disgraced Chairman Hu Hanmin. Seething in his original betrayal and ouster, he has refused any part in the new government but has refused to endorse any move to topple it either, despite frequent coaxing by former allies like Gu Yingfen, Sun Fo, and Chen Jitang. He maintained that Dr. Sun would want all his followers to faithfully adhere to the party's leadership to ensure national unification.\n\nWith that having been achieved, many expected him to emerge from his self-imposed exile. Today, he has finally done so to great fanfare among the Yue camp, formally taking on the mantle of Chairman of the Guangzhou Committee. In a booming address, he has denounced Li Zongren as a military usurper in the vein of Yuan Shikai. He has called upon Guangdong to take charge as the Piedmont of China, making a stand against Li Zongren and his clique. With regards to the coming election, he has not declared his candidacy, instead declaring that any election would be illegitimate so long as Li Zongren remains free to corrupt it.\n\nApplauded by many, accused of sour grapes by others, Hu's return to the public scene represents a grave threat to Li Zongren's ambitions and has undercut the Western Hill's efforts to rally the civilian opposition. The Fourth Central Executive Committee
With the light of Kuomintang control now shining on all corners of the Chinese nation, a most pressing matter has been presented to [ROOT.GetLeader]. Whilst a revolution lead from Guangdong and Guangxi, the party now encompasses all regions of China, and it must acknowledge this fact. Like Prussia and Piedmont before them, the party-state must bring forth their brethren into the new age, and incorporate them so as to partake in the National Revolution. To take a first bold step in enacting this, members within the party have sought the expansion of the Central Executive Committee to include a more diverse array of members.\n\nWith Hu Hanmin's exile in Moscow, his Second Central Executive Committee majorly comprised those close to him, and those who joined following the failure of the Northern Expedition. As a result of such, it was stacked with Elders Clique and Western Hills members almost absolutely - with the notable inclusion of Sun Fo, Wu Chaoshu and Wang Kunlun in absentia. With the homecoming and return of the Chairman onto Chinese soil, the committee was expanded to include the military factions who allowed such a return.\n\nWith the capture of more and more urban centers, the populace and intellectuals alike clamber for another expansion of the CEC, to properly represent all members of Chinese society. Key members within Guangdong and Guangxi suggest expanding the seats of the New Guangdong and New Guangxi cliques, others bring forth the idea of including members from other provinces to balance Southern dominance, some murmurs even indicate the inclusion of Dangwai to encompass a wide front of democratic ideals and regionalist interests.\n\nSuch decisions have precedence within party history, as previous committees had made attempts to balance the membership list by region, whilst also welcoming a multitude of warlord allies to ensure their compliance in military matters. Ultimately, the verdict is up to [ROOT.GetLeader], and their vision for the future.
