The New Civil Service
Our governmental bureaucracy is in dire need for expansion, for some fear it has reached its limits. To ensure stable, efficient, well functioning government, the party has decided to expand and renew the current civil service system to accommodate for the new lands and civilians under their control. Of course with a new and expanded service comes the question of who to staff the many positions required? Many central figures are suggesting that appointments be made based on loyalty to the party and chairman, to make certain corruption and a self-serving attitude does not fester.\n\nIn contradiction, the Western Hills clique have suggested that we open the positions to the previous Beiyang burueacrats that held the position, for their experience might prove invaluable in such a time. Alongside this, the Liberals have championed that the fresh, Western educated young breed of returning overseas students are more than fit enough to fill such positions, with their fresh outlook and burgeoning vigor. Finally, the New Guangxi clique have pushed for veterans of the army - in dire need of retirement - to be given the positions, having proved their loyalty with blood.

A Free Legislature?
In our efforts to maintain cohesion and a resolute following of Dr. Sun's ideals, the recent period of party-state has directly led to the consequence that all decisions, large or small, in some way went through the expansive party apparatus, naturally creating a bureaucratic system that objects to change and transformation. With such a current system, a new legislature is a prospect with shaky foundations, despite this many internal factions are expecting a diversification of the ideas compromising the legislature. Pioneering the initiative is naturally Sun Fo and his Reconstruction Faction, demanding an in increase in non-Kuomintang member participation. The Western Hills clique are not far behind, harkening back to Song Jiaoren's legacy, they have also put their weight behind the inclusion of external parties as legislators. Even the Elders Clique, cautious and apprehensive as always, have been open to the discussion and idea.\n\nIn reality, these positions are not actually elected, and instead would be appointed by the Kuomintang party-state to ensure increased democratisation of the nation. In practical application, this leaves three options. Firstly, the prospective outside legislators could participate as Dangwai, a cautious but much needed step towards proper political participation. Secondly, they could follow the Western Hills ideas, and form their own political parties in parallel to the Kuomintang. Thirdly, the Chairman could put his foot down, and maintain Kuomintang stewardship of the nation for the time being, democracy after all, cannot be rushed.
They may partake, as Dangwai.
Let them establish the parties.
We shall lead, as always.
The Female Patriot
Whilst the revolution pushes ahead onto a new age of prosperity, we cannot merely leave behind our female compatriots in such a time of social upheaval and change. With his noted writings and theories, Hu Hanmin continues to be concerned with women's fate in modern society, insisting that they should be granted economic independence in society. \n\nFollowing such thoughts, he himself seems rather optimistic on the future of women's role in society, noting that since the Industrial Revolution, equality in the work force has been approaching a balance, and women's social status has improved as their economic contribution has increased. To push forward such a process, he has proposed that China requires a reconstruction of its socio-economic organisation to create a well-coordinated, property-sharing, and domestic society. With humanity's instinct to love others as well as oneself, genuine pure-love in all human relationships would be bound to flourish.\n\nIn parallel to this, Sun Fo has naturally taken a similarly progressive outlook on his female compatriots. With the further participation of women in society and government, naturally their status will be elevated to be on par with their fellow man. However, Sun Fo in particular has rejected the notion of birth control being freely available for women, fearing that the Chinese nation would be short of manpower by the end of the 20th century if it were to be easily accessible.\n\nIn complete contraflow to the ideas of his contemporaries, face of the New Guangxi Clique, Li Zongren, has put forth a nigh puritan outlook on his fairer counterparts. Whilst considering them key participants in society as a whole, even pushing for the permanent mobilisation of women in the army, it would appear he desires a puritanical, militarised and literate sect of society ready to serve the nation. An advocate against what he deems immorality, he has called for controls on women's clothing to counter promiscuity.
Socio-economic reorganisation, for pure love!
Sun Fo's liberalist outlook.
Li Zongren has the right ideas.
Capturing the National Revolution
Imperative but often unappreciated is the integral art of capturing history as it happens. An art in itself, the Chinese nation is rife with dynamic photographers, witty journalists and endeavouring artists all of whom work collectively to encapsulate the terrifically tumultuous period of the Warlord Era. And the Party itself is no exception to this rule.\n\nPertinent member of Sun Fo's young guard Reconstruction Faction, illustrious diplomat, close friend of Wu Chaoshu, accomplished bureaucrat, and more vibrantly, a wonderful photographer. Fu Bingchang is one of the more youthful politicians within the Party's retinue, having only joined the party at a mere twenty two years old. Graduating with a degree in Civil Engineering from the University of Hong Kong, Fu would develop a keen interest in photography, founding the short-lived Jinshi society specialising in scenic photography.\n\nJoining Dr. Sun's 1918 Constitutional Protection Government through familial connections, Fu would find renewed purpose and service as Wu Tingfang's secretary, he would develop a close kinship with Wu Chaoshu, Wu Tingfang's son. Accompanying Wu Chaoshu on various diplomatic missions as his secretary, Fu would return to China to serve as the representative of the Finance and Foreign Affairs ministry to Hong Kong. Transferred to serve as the supervisor of Guangdong Customs in 1923, Fu would find himself cataloguing the various events of the power struggle as they happened, having recently acquired a new camera by 1924.\n\nIn a Pictorialist style, Fu manages to capture the highlights of the Kuomintang's power struggle and Northern Expedition. Ranging from portraits of Chiang Kai-shek and Hu Hanmin, to group photographs of Tan Yankai, Sun Fo, Gu Yingfen, Wang Jingwei, and numerous other party elders. Fu's collection of photographs have gained particular notoriety with the resurgence of the Party, as captivated onlookers from around the world look to the past of the often besmirched Kuomintang.
What an illustrious passion!
Dongli Mountain House
Back during the reign of the Daoguang Emperor of the Qing Dynasty, Jiang Lixiang founded the Liyin Garden in one of Dongguan city's composite villages. Fate would not be kind to the garden, however. For one reason or another, the garden was abandoned during the Century of Humiliation and remained so until recently. General Jiang Guangnai, grandson of Jiang Lixiang and one of Chen Mingshu's close allies, took it upon himself to renovate the garden in the aftermath of Chen Mingshu's seizure of Guangzhou.\n\nHowever, instead of restoring the garden to its original state, Jiang has decided to build a villa in the garden. Renaming it to the Hongli Mountain House, this impressive new two-story building with a reinforced concrete structure covers an area of 223 square metres. Boasting a porch and front facade constructed of stone metres, the house takes many inspirations from Western-style villas. Indeed, its red brick exterior walls on three sides and Western Ionic capital-esque columns show much appreciation for Western architecture. With its construction finished now with Chen Mingshu's consolidation of power, Jiang Guangnai has made Hongli his primary residence and the envy of many.
With this, we make a statement about healing past wrongs!
With this, we make a statement about building a future!
Speech against Imperialism
With the Lame General's consolidation of power in Guangzhou complete and the Baise uprising situation resolved, Chen Mingshu has wasted no time in implementing more of his own ideological rhetoric, unburdened by Peng Pai and Tan Pingshan's conflicting visions. In order to rouse public support and propagate his agenda, Chen Mingshu announced to the denizens of Guangzhou that he would be giving a speech at Sun Yat-sen's old residence.\n\nStarting his speech, Chen declared, 'China's greatest enemy has always been imperialism. It was the British Empire that brought the taint of opium to China's shores and started the Century of Humiliation, it was the Russian Empire which stripped China of Outer Manchuria and western Xinjiang and encouraged Mongol separatism, it was the Empire of Japan which seized Taiwan from China, and it was the German Empire which crushed the first Northern Expedition.\n\nNo longer will the Chinese people tolerate a government which kowtows to the imperial powers, the Chinese people must overthrow all imperialist collaborators and establish a revolutionary government composed of the producing people! Overthrow Imperialism! Overthrow [FNG.GetLeader]! Overthrow Japanese Imperialism, Restore the Lost Territories of Manchuria!'
Together we join ranks against Imperialism!
All must rally to our banner as we march!
The Anarchists of Guangzhou
Tan Pingshan's climb to power in Liangguang has made him a credible candidate for leadership of Chinese Syndicalism almost overnight. But much to his chagrin, the League of Chinese Syndicalists is somehow even less governable than the Kuomintang. With the Guangdong CSP, made up of various Marxist industrial socialists, now allied, Tan seeks to combine them with his Kuomintang loyalists to build a support base to contest the League. His next target is to recruit the anarchists in the LCS.\n\nMany anarchists such as Mo Jipeng remain independent of the LCS, trying to stay true to anarchist principles rather than bend to the hypocritical authoritarianism of both. In Guangzhou, the bulk of anarchist thinkers have converged around Liu Shifu's old circles. Liu, born Liu Shaobin, was a member of the Tongmenghui who later formed both the Conscience Society and the Cock-crow Society. They lived in a common household with failed plans for establishing an agrarian commune. Though there were other anarchists in the city, Liu's long running newspaper People's Voice and his powerful personality made him the centre of Guangzhou anarchism. He also became one of the first Chinese Syndicalists, organising masons, shoemakers, barbers, and restaurant employees into dozens of trade unions through his Resolution Club.\n\nLiu, dubbed the Proudhon of China and lauded by Wu Zhihui (who once claimed that if he was still around, Chinese anarchism could be achieved in merely 500 years rather than 3,000), died from overwork in 1915. His unions survived him under his brother Liu Shixin, but slowly lost ground to the organised industrial unions of the CSP in the 1920s.\n\nTan Pingshan's recent courtship of the reluctant Liu Shixin may be a test to see if he really is worthy to be the next face of Chinese syndicalism. His current supporters however warn that courting the infamously flighty anarchists may be a mistake, destabilising the vanguardist spirit of action in favour of ineffectual 'talk'.
Together we can move beyond the sins of the past.
Sorry but revolutions are not won in theory.
Tan Pingshan, Statesman of the Hour
Tan has always been close with the Third Internationale. Elected as the CSP's representative to the Third Internationale he became friends with M.N. Roy, an Indian delegate and later leading Mission Militaire Internationale en Chine (MMIC) representative. In 1926, Tan argued before the China Committee of the Internationale that China's national revolution was driven by workers, farmers, and the urban petite bourgeoisie but by nature a bourgeois revolution rather than belonging to the proletariat. He called for the Internationale to provide greater attention and guidance to the CSP, implicitly blaming the Kuomintang for the disastrous expedition.\n\nThe speech, made while fighting continued, was seen as impertinent by many and effectively sealed his sidelining by the Paris Kuomintang. The CSP similarly expelled him in 1932 after profound disagreements during the Shanghai Uprising. Still, for many in the MMIC, Tan is seen as a figure who could more practically bring the Kuomintang towards syndicalism, especially after the plentiful mishaps of the current LCS leadership. Since returning home Tan has been willing to switch tracks as needed.\n\nWith his opponents in Guangdong firmly vanquished and seeing an opportunity in the resentment many party outsiders (Dangwai) feel towards the Kuomintang party-state, Tan has begun extending olive branches to various progressive democrats and even liberal figures in hopes of landing their support. His poor treatment of Chen Mingshu and Liang Hancao has not been forgotten however, and many are reluctant to give Tan the benefit of the doubt. Still, [CHI.GetLeader]'s own popularity has fluctuated over the years.
Reassuring words and a smile wins friends abroad.
Reaching across the aisle wins us support at home.
Mayor Gu Dacun and the Unruly City
Gu Dacun had previously joined the Chinese Syndicalist Party in 1924 although was already involved in revolutionary politics since the November 4th Movement in 1919. Gu was also a participant in the Eastern Expedition, which saw Yue Warlord Chen Jiongming expelled from the province. Gu led the Dongjiang Army to many great victories in the Northern Expedition and organised the peasant mass movement in Wuhua County. He led many farmers and peasants to establish farmers' associations, designed to combat landlord tyranny and reduce rent.\n\nWhen the Northern Expedition failed, Gu led his men into Baxiang Mountain, establishing a revolutionary base. Although Peng Pai and Gu Dacun were originally on friendly terms with the Chinese Syndicalist League, the Li Lisan Line, which insisted that the Syndicalist Revolution focus on urbanised regions, was a major affront to the rural socialist movement. Breaking with the Syndicalist League, Gu and Peng continually harassed the Guangdong warlords in the countryside for a decade before the Kuomintang returned.\n\nGu Dacun, as Peng Pai's chief lieutenant, has fittingly been appointed Mayor of Guangzhou with instructions to tame the ever restless city. The city, filled with malcontents even in the best of times, has seen tensions inflamed by Syndicalist rabble rousers and the mismanagement of Liang Hancao and Chen Yaozu. The one thing both sides can agree on is they are not thrilled to be ruled by a gang of raggedly peasants.
We must redirect their anger towards those who failed them!
Slowly, but surely we can earn their trust.
The Taming of Guangxi
While reaction has been curbed in Guangxi, much unrest remains. Wei Baqun, one of the leaders of the Baise Uprising, has been at work trying to stabilise socialist rule there. There are tensions between those who were former NGX soldiers who defected later like Yu Zuobai and those like Wei who were always rural insurgents. General Yu insists that a more moderate programme and the integration of the old Guangxi army will win over the conservative province, stabilising leftist rule.\n\nWei however is under pressure from Peng Pai in Guangdong, who tends to advocate for a different, more eccentric way of growing close to the people. For one, he tends to insist that his followers dress like the peasants they intend to serve, pushing party cadres to leave behind the urban intellectual backgrounds that tend to dominate both the Kuomintang and Chinese Syndicalist party. For Peng, himself an educated son of the gentry, this was a necessary step to truly become a servant of the people. While sometimes criticised for his tendency to abandon socialist orthodoxy, few can deny it has served him well so far.\n\nHow practical these measures would be in Guangxi, with peasants deeply suspicious of sanctimonious outsiders, remains to be seen. Peng definitely has an advantage over his former rivals in Tan and Chen in this regard, but many local leaders insist a more pragmatic approach is needed.
Only by living as the people do, do we become worthy to lead them.
The men of Guangxi are brave, and with time they will serve us faithfully.
The Southwest Political Affairs Council
With Hu Hanmin now the leader of Guangzhou at the behest of Chen Jitang, many rightists have flocked to Guangdong. Right wing KMT members and sympathetic nationalists all around China hope that Hu may indeed be capable of saving China from both the clutches of imperialism and the socialist autocracy of Wang Jingwei. Hu Hanmin has also begun to build up his own array of allies. Sun Fo, leader of the Reconstruction Clique is in Nanjing, but he and his fellow liberals are viewed with suspicion and distrust for not being socialists.\n\nIf Wang will not lend his ear to Sun, perhaps Hu can entice him to his side if Guangzhou and Nanjing find their differences irreconcilable. There is also Chen Mingshu and the Fourth Army situated here in Guangdong and loosely aligned with the leftists in Nanjing. Although they likely have ambitions of their own, Chen Jitang's consolidation of power in the province has likely dashed their hopes. However, while Chen Mingshu has tried to court both major factions in the Nanjing Central Committee, his political leanings remain independent of both camps.\n\nHe could be uniquely useful to Hu, due to his ties with other Fourth Army veterans in Nanjing, notably Li Jishen and Zhang Fakui. Of course, the most important ally Hu needs to keep onside is Chen Jitang and the New Guangdong Clique. While Chen may not have grand political ambitions of his own, he guards the influence he has with zeal and Hu would be a fool to bite the hand that feeds him.
We'll need all the friends we can get.
Guangdong must be safeguarded against all threats.

The 2nd Repatriated Congress
With the Kuomintang rapidly ascending from a fringe insurgency to the leading contender for national leadership, the party has decided to send a clear message by hosting another party congress, this time in the ancient capital of Beijing itself. Party figures drawn from around the country and beyond, from Xinjiang to Singapore, have arrived for proceedings. Topics include the establishment of a new Ministry of Education and a National Health Administration, language reforms, national reconstruction, and even a new national anthem.\n\nHowever, underneath those talks a sense of unease pervades the Congress. Tensions between Wang Jingwei and his RCA allies and his opponents, primarily Song Qingling and the PAC, have only worsened as the prospects of unification grow and unity in turn has declined. Wang's authoritarianism and accusations of PAC insubordination have rocked the nation, even as the revolutionary government has plenty more obstacles to overcome.\n\nChief among these is the question of the Rising Sun. While the Kuomintang and the Japanese government have had some historic ties, Tokyo's opinions on Chinese nationalism have dimmed over the years. While the fall of the Zhili was to our mutual benefit, their disappointment in the Fengtian clique's inability to exploit the situation is palpable. On our end, their hold over northeastern China has grown ever more pervasive and war between the White and Red Suns seem inevitable now.\n\nCalls for action only grow, especially among those who feel the party is moving too slowly on the issue. Others however have advised caution, noting that despite the Kuomintang's quick pace of stunning victories, Japan is no paper tiger. For us in Liangguang, the prospect of freeing distant Manchuria has attracted considerable nationalist attention and is an increasingly realistic possibility. Some, however, suggest that the island of Taiwan might be within reach, if only we would dare to pursue it!
To Manchuria we will go!
We will liberate Taiwan!
Academia Sinica
The Academia Sinica is a long proposed Kuomintang scholastic project meant to help fulfil the party's promises of modernising the nation. This institute for higher education would report directly to the President's office, the crown jewel of the new Republic's planned overhaul of education in China. Meant to be the pinnacle of academic study and coordinate research across the country at its highest level, planners have outlined departments in over a dozen topics both in the sciences and humanities. Widely supported both within the party and by academicians like the Chinese Science Society, the approval of its charter by the Legislative Yuan was received with considerable applause.\n\nSatellite campuses are being erected across Kuomintang controlled territory, and thanks to some heavy lobbying on our part Guangzhou has been slated to be the location of the Institute of Language and History, especially prestigious given the nationalist character of our movement. Our scholars have already begun excitedly planning an expansion with a new Institute of Physical Anthropology, but they are getting ahead of themselves.\n\nFirst we will have to make a decision of where to actually house this new institute. Sun Yat-sen University in Guangzhou is a logical choice since it already has a fairly large campus and a solid concentration of scholars. Building it there is bound to please those already studying there as well as the faculty who will appreciate the new found prestige. Others however have advised that we construct a totally new set of buildings, majestic ones worthy of a new Kuomintang age.
Sun Yat-sen is a fine campus named for a worthy man.
A new age of learning deserves a new building.

Prepare for the Long War
After 1911, there have been multiple attempts to reconcile the Northern and Southern governments of China. Dr. Sun himself made one last ditch effort in 1925 to negotiate, passing away from cancer in the process. By now, over a decade later, we are long past the point of compromise. Chinese unification will require war, and wars require sacrifice. The Nationalist government has begun raising war taxes preliminarily, fearing a sudden jolt would cripple the war effort should war break out with imperial powers. Stockpiles have been created, draft rolls updated, men and money raised from the people.\n\nThese policies are naturally quite unpopular even among Kuomintang bastions. After decades of instability and civil war, most Chinese families are struggling to scrape by. They have been further disillusioned by recurring corruption scandals.
A necessary sacrifice for the preservation of the nation.
Our Revolution was to undo their suffering, not increase it.
The Liangguang Incident
With the nation on the brink of a civil war, [GXC.GetLeader] has rushed to reassert calm and leadership in [GXC.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName]. In a grand ceremony before a crowd of hand-selected supporters and journalists, [GXC.GetAdjective] officials denounced the [CHI.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] government as corrupt, tyrannical, and revisionist, brushing off accusations of treason by arguing that it was [CHI.GetLeader] who betrayed the ideals of Dr. Sun. The event concluded with a formal proclamation rejecting the [GXC.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] Central Committee and calls for a new one to be established by loyal comrades.\n\nThe festivities could do little to hide the worry creeping into even the highest ranks of the administration. [GXC.GetLeader] has made a bold gamble, rebelling against the Central Government. The recent crisis at the Third Congress has shaken [CHI.GetLeader]'s political legitimacy, but it is unlikely to fundamentally alter the calculus on the battlefield. Despite [GXC.GetLeader]'s exhortations, there have been no high level internal defections, even as [CHI.GetLeader] cracks down on dissidents from within. The [GXC.GetAdjective] rebels can only hope others on the fringes join in the revolt, but it is no guarantee. As the artillery roars and infantry scramble into position, the fate of China will likely be determined far from Liangguang's halls of power.

Canton Reclaims Its Leadership
The city of Guangzhou (known internationally as Canton), was the home of a series of southern Chinese governments after the 1911 Revolution, including various incarnations of the Kuomintang. Many of the party's early members were drawn from there, and so if there was any city that could topple Wang Jingwei's party-state from within, it would be Guangzhou. Some of course were shocked that the Kuomintang heartland could play such a pivotal role in the destruction of the old Central Committee, sweeping away Wang loyalists and opposition alike. But those who knew the workings of [GXC.GetLeader] and the [GXC.GetRulingParty] understood that they always held resentments towards Nanjing and had dreams of a new order within the new Kuomintang order.\n\nWhat is not surprising is that [GXC.GetLeader] has proclaimed a new government and organised a new Central Executive Committee, thus seizing leadership of the old Kuomintang movement. With the old National Revolutionary Government gone, there are few who can oppose them, but their leadership is still contested. Whether or not [GXC.GetLeader] has a blissfully quick path to national unification or if there will be further conflicts over Wang Jingwei's succession will shape China for years to come.
Back in the Captain's Chair.
A New Canton Government
Gathering disaffected revolutionaries in Guangzhou (also known as Canton), [GXC.GetLeader] has proclaimed a new [GXC.GetNameDef] with the aim of liberating the nation from warlord tyranny, foreign imperialism, and even treachery from other revolutionary factions. Despite the late hour for this political turn, [GXC.GetLeader] believes Liangguang will be a powerful enough base to overcome entrenched foes in China.\n\nWhether or not this will pan out the way he hopes is another question, though there is plenty of precedent for Guangzhou leading a revolution thanks to its wealth, ready access to foreign support, and a history of maintaining large revolutionary armies. Victory however, will ultimately depend on success on the battlefield.

Dawn of the Third Party
Tan Pingshan has long waited for this moment, and at last the opportunity presented itself. Though the moment was fleeting and risks abounded, Tan realised that if there was any hope for a socialist China independent of the corruption of the Kuomintang and Chinese Syndicalist Party leaderships, he had to take action. Mobilising the full force of Liangguang against the central government, he raised his banner against Nanjing - and to the shock of many, he won!\n\nWhat banner that entails, however, remained up to discussion even through the chaotic firefights between the Central Government and Liangguang forces. Different proposals were offered to Tan, who eagerly savoured the chance to make such a meaningful (albeit ultimately symbolic) decision for the revolution.\n\nThe first would be to embrace the Provisional Action Committee's stated mission of redeeming the Kuomintang. Guangzhou Overcomes Beijing
In the end, it was not negotiations but military might that brought Federalism to the forefront of Chinese politics. The Guangzhou government has managed to march their armies from Canton to Beijing, one of the rare south to north unifications in Chinese history. Some may say they have avenged the oppression the northerners wrought on the south.\n\nIf there was any gloating among the Guangzhou high command, it was muted as Chen Jiongming and his entourage toured Beijing. The city has been devastated by conflict, many residents have fled to avoid the fighting, and the marvelous capital suffered severe looting before more disciplined units could restore order.\n\nThe situation is little better elsewhere in China. Millions have been displaced, and while most will return home in the coming weeks as the political situation settles down, many more will have no homes to return to. The economy has plunged into a recession as doubts swirl about the new government's ability to pay back vital loans and maintain order.\n\nResistance continues, with some units refusing to lay down their arms and vowing to continue the fight. But the Guangzhou Army is exhausted - overextended after a long campaign northwards. Many of their best divisions have been thinned out, losing troops to casualties, attrition, desertion, or garrisons. Others have been swamped by low quality militias and integrated warlord units, diminishing their overall fighting effectiveness.\n\nThe Federalists have secured their hard won victory, but now the question is whether or not their system will be able to survive the peace in the challenging days ahead.
One step closer to unification.
The Second Federalist Conference
Beijing has fallen and with it, the old Beiyang Republic is on its last legs. While remnants of the old government may linger on in various redoubts or in exile, for many this is a clear sign that a new age is dawning on the nation. While resistance to [ROOT.GetNameDef] continues, Chen Jiongming has felt confident enough to host another Federalist Conference in [ROOT.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName].\n\nThis time, a much larger guest list is expected. While the first Federalist conference held in Guangzhou in the post-Zhili era desperately sought the attendance of various figures - targeting prestigious intellectuals and liberal figures as well as regional warlords - this time the various stakeholders around China are desperately clamouring to get in. Few would like to be left out of proceedings, and many old Beiyang National Assemblymen seek to retain their positions in the incoming government.\n\nFor Chen Jiongming - this is his moment of glory. Once a mere regional figure that piqued some national interest, he is now perhaps the strongest power in all of China. It was he who rallied the moribund Federalist movement, it is he who built and marched an army to Beijing, and now it is he who will effectively decide the makeup of the new provisional Assembly.\n\nWith all this power comes greater scrutiny. His promises of a new, fairer republic were never really put to the test until now, and many of those liberal intellectuals and federalist governors who he had courted are now expecting him to live up to his words. Whispers have been going around the republic as the politically minded mull whether or not there will be (or even can be) meaningful elections in these circumstances, and whether or not President Chen will go the way of his predecessors in authoritarianism.\n\nFor now however, the question of the day is whether or not the assemblymen elected under his predecessors' regime will be allowed to retain their seats - a meaningful question as the government balances new and old.

The China Democratic League
Zhang Junmai, the sometimes pragmatic Liangist, may be willing to hedge his bets when it comes to President Chen, but his allies in the Federation of Chinese Democratic Parties are much bolder. Though the FCDP fell apart after the 1936 Seven Gentlemen Incident, national unification has also brought the movement back together. They have dropped two characters (Zheng Tuan, which emphasised their party-dominated nature) from their name, rebranding as the China Democratic League - implying one unified coalition.\n\nLuo Longji has published \

The Northerners Respond
As the GRA and CDL exchange harsh words, their northern counterparts see both risk and opportunity. While some northern politicians have already joined a side (either out of opportunism or ideological affinity), most retain their prior party or clique identification. Moreover, many of their leaders have not yet committed to joining the new government, adopting a wait and see attitude.\n\nEver since the defection of nearly one hundred Beiyang Assemblymen to Guangzhou in 1917, kicking off the Constitutional Protection Movement, northern and southern politics have taken marked different tracks. Even the nominal Zhili Unification failed to fully heal the divide. Generally more conservative than the south, Beiyang's largest formal party was that of Liang Qichao - the latest iteration going by the Democratic Constitutionalist Party label.\n\nBut Beiyang politics was not party-based, most politicians ran as (and prided themselves as) independents and only informally joined wider networks. The Zhili organised the Harmony Association as their political machine, with the Young China Party forming a symbiotic relationship with it. Other larger factions included the National Salvation Federation, the Old Political Science Clique, the Rural Reconstruction Association, and Endeavor Society.\n\nTheir interest in the squabbles of the southerners would be marginal if it were not for the growing centralisation of power around the President's office. Though Chen has ordered local provincial assemblies to convene along Federalist lines, in practice he has amassed considerable control (deliberately or not) through the army's \

Guangzhou at the Crossroads
Zhang Junmai was prophetic in his prediction that unification would lead to tensions between centralisation and democracy. But his gambit, probably only intended to shore up his bargaining position in coalition talks, has spiralled into something far more. A democratic wave has swept across the intelligentsia - with writers denouncing all prior Federalist discourse on democracy as empty words. To stand against it risks being swept away by the tide, to stand with it might spell doom...or opportunity.\n\nIn Chen Jiongming's camp, the discourse has escalated far beyond what they had hoped when the conference was first called. Rather than a ringing endorsement of Chen's National Reconstruction Plan, it has drawn demands for further democratisation - extremely prematurely given the work that still needs to be done drafting provincial constitutions, restoring stability and bringing an end to foreign imperialism.\n\nThe narrative has slipped outside of his control - northern press outlets have pointed out that he was elected by an assembly that barely represented the south. If he does not allow for a new vote with the northerners involved, he would mark himself a hypocrite. The CDL's position is not very secure either - though they claim to speak on behalf of the masses, most of them are academics and professionals too out of touch to even realise how little they know of the common people. And the military is certainly not pleased at the attacks on their leader or the destabilisation of the republic they have fought so hard for.\n\nTo ratchet down tensions, Chen Jiongming and Zhang Junmai have scheduled a private meeting. They have both agreed that the assembly must hold a new vote on the President, but there is a growing demand by progressives to call new assembly elections as well. New elections would take time, spell risks for both men, and pose risks for the integrity of the republic as a whole. But their democratic credentials might be called into question otherwise.

A Wartime Political Truce
The outbreak of war would cloud democratic elections in any republic, but in one as fragile as China the prospect of national elections while bullets fly and artillery shells boom is daunting. Already there is talks of suspending the planned elections, with many commentators castigating politicians for focusing on political jockeying rather than uniting against their common foes.\n\nHoping to assuage these concerns, Zhang Junmai has published \
How can elections be held in these conditions?
The First Federal Elections
As agreed, national-wide elections are to be called (or at least the territories under Guangzhou's control), lauded in aligned press outlets as a victory for Chinese democracy. But the scope of this first election is expected to be a lot more limited than idealists hope. It has already been cast as effectively a referendum on Chen Jiongming's leadership, who will serve for the duration of the election. Although over a dozen parties are expected to participate in elections, the Presidency has become a two way race before it has even begun.\n\nWith no enshrined national constitution for the Federal government yet, elections have defaulted to the old Beiyang system with a Federalist spin. Voting is indirect, with local assemblies (some holding fresh and/or fair elections, others not) selecting representatives to go to the capital. This system would normally be expected to favor the status quo, however the excitement of these new elections has caused civic groups to mobilise to increase turnout where they can.\n\nZhang Junmai, despite numerous personal disputes with other members of the old FCDP, is understood to be their best chance of unseating President Chen. He was reluctantly voted in as Chairman of the China Democratic League and has become the de facto top of the ticket. The CDL, though ostensibly rebranded as a unified party, remains a loose bloc of centre-left progressives dominated by Zhang's old DSP comrades.\n\nThe Grand Republican Alliance has also been energised, a powerful liberal-conservative bloc dominated by the PIP. They are expected to easily nominate the incumbent President to run for re-election. However, Chen has yet to announce his candidacy. To run is expected, and probably the stabler path. Chen of course also has his ego, believing himself to be the savior China needs. But with his age beginning to wear down on him, he is said to be mulling stepping aside in favor of his protégé Chen Qiyou - a fresh start but a risky one. His legacy hangs in the balance.
Chen Jiongming proudly accepts his nomination for a second term.

Two Chens, Two Zhangs, Many Allies
As Chen Jiongming and Zhang Junmai take centre stage, their supporting cast have also begun to move into position. Though not as immediately prominent, these men might be just as important for the futures of both parties.
The Zhejiang-born Zhang Junmai has selected the Guangdong-born political elder Xu Fulin, perhaps reinforcing the outsized importance of the province in the coming election. It is widely expected that should the CDL win, Zhang will appoint his long-time friend and deputy Zhang Dongsun as Prime Minister - likely a controversial choice given his socialist views.\n\nBoth sides have consolidated the top of their respective tickets around their own core allies in the old PIP and DSP, but they will also be ultimately dependent on their nation-spanning coalitions in the GRA and CDL to win. The GRA is an amalgamation of various Federalist parties including Tang Jiyao's People's Government Party, Chu Fucheng's Zhejiang Autonomy Movement, Zhao Hengti's People's Renewal Society, Xiong Kewu's Provincial Federal Autonomy Preparation Office, and several smaller groups.\n\nThe CDL, while proclaiming to be a united party now, in practice remains a federation of parties including the Luo Longji's Crescent Moon Society, Huang Hanliang's China National Democratic Construction Association, Ma Xulun's China Association for Promoting Democracy, Shen Junru's National Salvation Federation, and several other civic groups.

The Other Parties Sidelined
With most of the nation's attention on the Presidential candidates, the attention the GRA and CDL have received has taken the wind out of the sails of other parties. With little room to operate and doubts that the army high command (still overwhelmingly in the hands of Cantonese officers) would tolerate any other candidates, most of the luminaries of the other major parties in China have decided to sit out of the process altogether - ceding the spotlight to Chen and Zhang.\n\nThough their leaders could afford to bide their time, the lower ranking party members and activists of these groups can ill afford the loss of patronage and influence not campaigning would entail. Many smaller parties have folded into the GRA or CDL, but the larger DCP and YCP can afford to field candidates to attempt to secure influence in the post-election political environment. Navigating the limits of Federalist democracy, they hope to secure enough seats to use their leverage in future talks.\n\nMany, many other local politicians and their associated political machines are also running, braving fears of corruption and political intimidation (and perhaps engaging in some of their own) in hopes of surviving the new era. The lack of big names in the race has created a sense of dynamism in a previously ossified political environment, opening up the arena a little bit for outsiders to participate as well.\n\nSome socialists have also sought to run for office, some out of protest and in hopes of challenging the new federalist government and others out of a genuine belief that they can reform the government from within. But they have been banned in virtually all races, either de jure or de facto. The Central Committee(s) have denounced participation in Beiyang elections in the past, and continue to do so with ours.\n\nThough the GRA and CDL have effectively carved up the political system they founded between one another, it is clear that those left on the outside will not go down without a fight.

Battle Lines Drawn
In many ways, the GRA and CDL are cut from similar idiosyncratic cloths, with vague populist agendas of political reform, a mixed economic system, and Chinese nationalism. This free-styling approach worked well on the road to unification, drawing supporters from all corners, but as elections unfold it has proven difficult to meaningfully distinguish the two or set cohesive party identities.\n\nBoth Chen Jiongming and Zhang Junmai historically have made overtures to the left (including dabbling in extremist ideologies such as anarchism and Marxism) but relied on conservative power structures to stay relevant - synthesising moderate, reformist, and what some criticise as diluted platforms. This legacy endures in their respective parties and the lack of clear direction that pervades both the GRA and CDL has been worsened by their absorbing of various smaller parties into their blocs.\n\nUltimately, the direction of the new political system will probably rest on the GRA rather than the CDL. In addition to being the party in power, the CDL is generally seen as comfortably to the left of the GRA - the PIP arguably being the furthest left of the GRA while the DSP sits closer to the left-centre of the CDL relatively speaking.\n\nVeering to the left to compete with the CDL for progressive and even socialist votes would probably help the GRA with the masses but will alienate the conservatives. The CDL would probably reposition themselves as a Democratic movement, attempting to court various liberals and constitutionalists in the centre-right - including alliances with the DCP and YCP. This would likely cast the situation as a referendum on Chen Jiongming's Presidency and alleged authoritarianism.\n\nTacking to the right would help the GRA secure the conservative vote (probably at the expense of existing right-wing parties) but leave the CDL free to operate across the left. This would likely create a more typical left vs right dynamic in Chinese politics.

The Grand Republican Strategy
Though the Grand Republican Alliance is dominated by the old Public Interest Party's Leadership, it still has to contend with other constituent parties that helped to found it. The largest of these are Tang Jiyao's People's Government Party, Chu Fucheng's Zhejiang Autonomy Movement, Zhao Hengti's People's Renewal Society, and Xiong Kewu's Provincial Federal Autonomy Preparation Office - which mainly operate out of their home provinces and have a regionalist, personalist slant towards their founders.\n\nStrengthening the PIP's hold over the GRA during elections would probably help maintain party discipline and allow them to build support for their more progressive, albeit sometimes idiosyncratic agenda. But the GRA is generally less popular than its constituent parties in the individual provinces, and alienating the others is likely to damage the GRA's political machine and risk defections to smaller parties and independents.\n\nThe GRA does benefit from being Chen Jiongming's party, with the various political patronage networks, charity and civic groups, and military ties Chen has cultivated. His legacy may be strong enough that the party does not need to offer a cohesive programme to the people - just a reminder of who built this republic.

Zhang Junmai and the Culture Wars
In intellectual spheres, Zhang Junmai has a dual reputation as both a progressive democrat and a cultural and moral conservative. While initially supportive of the New Culture Movement, as it began to drift towards leftist iconoclasm, Zhang began to criticise it both on humanistic socialist grounds and from a conservative perspective emphasising traditional Confucian life-philosophy.\n\nThis also shaped his outlook on Westernisation. Zhang extensively studied and communicated with Western scholars like Eduard Bernstein, Karl Kautsky, Philipp Scheidemann, Karl Korsch, Rudolf Christoph Eucken, and Harold Laski, but he also opposed the excessive westernisation and anti-Confucianism of liberal thinkers like Hu Shih and Ding Wenjiang. While distancing himself from traditionalist reactionarism, Zhang argued that there is a crisis of modernity both socioeconomically and spiritually.\n\nZhang argued that Confucianism and modernity are not merely compatible but synergistic. He believed that its ethos can be separated from flawed institutionalism, and that its universal benevolence can guide the people. China must be able to harmonise old with new, freedom with order, and science with culture and ethics in order for all of these concepts to flourish.\n\nTo Zhang, nationalism is both a pragmatic extension of realist international politics and a collectivist drive to transform a people. He has compared China to the ascendant Germany: a century ago, it was a failed state suffering from Napoleonic invasion. If China is to imitate their rise to dominance, it needs radical reform in education, morality, and national spirit.\n\nChina's future is not modernist totalitarianism or reactionary autocracy, but a pluralistic democracy guided by moral intellectuals who could balance traditional ethics with modern progress. Beiyang tyranny undermined the legal institutions needed for democracy, while New Culture iconoclasm destroyed the cultural foundations for it. The new republic must work to undo the damage.
Wise words of moderation.
Empty words by an ignorant blowhard.
China's traditions are not merely a Liangist plaything.

Zhang Junmai's admiration of German social democracy and his studies of the French and British revolutions turned him away from Marxism. Upon his return to China, he strongly opposed the CSP and KMT's alliances with the Internationale and embrace of vanguardism. \

Evolving Towards Utopia
In the 1920s Chen Jiongming contributed to the anarchist-inspired Minxing Bao, presenting an idealistic outlook for the future. In it, he described a new world that has evolved beyond social darwinism and has freed itself from corrupt old institutions in favor of spiritual regeneration and a new, egalitarian society. This new world would be one where boundaries between states, nations, and even individuals have been erased - recalling the traditional Chinese utopian ideal of Datong (the Great Harmony).\n\nDespite the world's brokenness, Chen rejected nationalism. He argued that nations are only transitory institutions, and nationalism a tool for ambitious men to fool their people and oppress others abroad. For strong nations, nationalism is an excuse for aggression and for weak ones it is a short term tool to awaken their people to the fight for survival. But it soon corrupts even the weak, turning them arrogant and cruel - he asks rhetorically which of today's great powers have not climbed up from being weak yesterday?\n\nChen argued that nationalism cannot be used in a restrained manner. It will either fail to be effective or grow out of control. In particular he condemns Nietzschean militarism, which he believes threatens to devastate the world and enslave mankind. Instead, Chen argues that men have a natural capacity for fraternal love, and that love should be used not merely to love a nation but to love all of mankind. He further argued that China has a special role to play in healing the \
We still hold out hope that one day we will arrive at this Utopia.
Times change and so do people. But blood and sacrifice always changes the world!
The Guangdong Educational Model
Chinese culture has always prized education, but Guangdong prides itself as being the first province to have mandatory public education for both genders. Its Constitution obligates the government to provide all boys and girls at least six years of free public education starting at age 7.\n\nGuangdong adopted a 6-3-3 system, one that has become standard nation-wide. This means 6 years of primary school learning morality, composition, arithmetic, handicraft, reading, music, gymnastics, science, history, geography, and both Mandarin and English. The 3 years middle school curriculum covers morality, Chinese, mathematics, English, history, geography, sciences, arts, music, gymnastics, and homemaking for girls and handicraft for boys. Lastly, highschoolers would receive 3 years of education along three career tracks: college preparation, homemaking, or primary school teaching.\n\nGuangdong historically funded study-abroad programs in Japan, America, France, Britain, Germany, and Russia - including partnerships with elite international universities. At home, Guangdong's education budget was also used for a bureau of translation, an oratory institute for the training of public speakers, institutes for the homeless, night schools for workers, and schools for arts, libraries, and museums.\n\nKeeping these programs funded in practice has always been a challenge and likely will continue to be one. Though the Guangdong Constitution mandated a large portion of the public budget be spent on education and explicitly not used for military expenditures, the needs of the military and the state have often taken priority. And while intellectuals and reformers of all stripes celebrate the expanded attention to public education, corruption and incompetence has frequently siphoned funding from reaching China's students.
The first step towards national reform.

The March on Beijing
On Christmas Day 1915, military forces loyal to the Republic denounced Yuan Shikai, the self-proclaimed Hongxian Emperor. After suffering major defeats on the battlefield against a coalition of forces led by Cai E, Tang Jiyao, Li Liejun, and Lu Rongting - Yuan abdicated but stayed on as President. The brief war lasted throughout the spring and into the summer until Yuan's abrupt death concluded hostilities.\n\nThough it ended the monarchy, the National Protection War did little to prevent China's descent into civil war. A second National Protection Alliance was called to liberate the country. Unlike the first war, they successfully marched onto the North.\n\nBeijing is now ours but with huge swathes of territory under the National Protection Army comes significantly more problems. Towns and villages lie in devastation from the furious fighting, civilians have reported their crops, livestock, and property plundered and looted, and banditry roams unopposed in regions absent military authority. Furthermore, [ROOT.GetNameDef]'s economy, designed to enjoy the richness of an independent Liangguang, now buckles under the weight of controlling the former national government of China.\n\nIt is a difficult time but also a dynamic one. For the first time in years, the Northern and Southern governments are finally united under one Republican banner. Politicians from across the country will be arriving in [GXC.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] to discuss the forthcoming plans for the new country. Foreign observers from major countries will likely also be present in documenting the event and presenting to their countries news of China's recent unification as reunification fever washes across China.

Dealing with Beiyang
Beijing has fallen to a coalition of warlords from the South and the tides of power have significantly changed, but there are many who are unwilling to see vestiges of the regime fall completely into oblivion. Where there are still ways to go before unification, [ROOT.GetNameDef] eager to establish their firm hold over China - they have gotten to work in stabilising the northern administrations.\n\nAs the seat of national governance, various parties and political groups have made Beijing their home for the last few years. The largest parties are the Democratic Constitutionalist Party (descendants of Liang Qichao's movement and recently rebranded), the nationalist Young China Party, the Political Science Clique, and many, many independents. The usurpation of years of Beiyang rule have now rendered them in need of either currying favor with the new establishment or falling into obscurity.\n\nOf these groups, the PSC and the Jingxing Society are the closest to reaffirming the status quo. As a loose association of politicians, many of them already have allies and mutual interests with their southern counterparts and thus they will be a natural fit in the new order. Already, Southern PSC politicians are already staffing the national bureaucracy with former members of the Northern PSC while [ROOT.GetLeader] has made overtures to members of the YCP and DCP; promising them lucrative positions and rewards in the new national government. As the Mandate of Heaven now shifts to the South once more in Chinese history, the new regime is eager not to rock the boat too much and to applaud a unification as bloodless as possible.

The FCDP Reforms
Despite the gravitation towards reactionary autocracy or revolutionary party dictatorship amongst many Chinese intellectuals following the Xinhai Revolution, there were many who continued to fight for constitutional democracy in China. Many of these figures gathered under the United National Construction League in 1929 after the destruction of the KMT and Zhili Unification.\n\nElections that year resulted in an overwhelming majority for the Zhili backed Harmony Association. While UNCL aligned politicians won many seats, the participation of KMT adjacent intellectuals like Zhang Bojun and Tan Pingshan drew scrutiny to the quasi-legal movement. When socialist and leftist uprisings rattled China in 1932, the Zhili banned the UNCL, forcing them underground. The Federation of Chinese Democratic Parties was thus born, excluding the UNCL's socialist members and continuing to lobby for change.\n\nWith the NPA now in charge of Beijing, the Federation has a chance at reasserting themselves once again. Spearheaded by the Democratic Constitutionalist Party, the Political Science Clique and the Democratic Socialist Party, the loose alliance also draws in smaller groups like the National Salvation Federation, Democracy and Science Forum, the Chinese Vocational Education Society, Rural Reconstruction Association, and the China National Democratic Construction Association. While weak and disorganised, they appeal to many hungry for change.
Of course the FCDP will be allowed to operate openly.

Sidelining Our Rivals
Lu Yuguang has been called many things. A bandit, the son of a foreign whore, an inexperienced general. But he has done what many could not - finally unifying the North and South. The Young Marshal has become the de-facto supreme military leader in all of China. Cantonese Opera
As one of the major categories in Chinese opera, Cantonese (Yue) opera is one of the Cantonese language's most prestigious and premier cultural institutions popular within Guangdong, Guangxi, Hong Kong, Macau, and other Chinese communities in South East Asia. There is debate as to when Yue opera began but it is generally accepted that it came from the North and slowly migrated to the southern province of Guangdong during the late Southern Song. As the Mongols and other Northern barbaric tribes dismantled the cultural supremacy of the northern courts, the Song developed and adapted to the new cultural capital of China in Hangzhou.\n\nModern Yue opera comes from Shanghai, as during the 1840s, a large number of Guangdong businessmen came to Shanghai for oppurtunities. As these businessmen coalesced into associations, the development of Cantonese opera in Shanghai developed significantly - finding popularity amongst the city's Chinese elite.\n\nCommentators and fans take pride in the idea that Yue opera differs in its language and unique visual elements. Make up is less exaggerated than in the north and costumes are ornate but reflect southern styles. The singing style of Yue opera is also more lyrical and melodic; with the gaohu, yehu, and percussion dominating the instrumental track as opposed to the northern jinghu and yueqin.\n\nThere are two types of Cantonese plays: Mou (which is themed around war, with characters being generals or warriors) and Man (literally \

But with Japan's much more advanced position in the conflict owing to their superior air, naval, and land forces - has also led the nationalist war hawks in the government to support the cause of national unity.

The Guangdong Coup
The balance of power in Guangdong has been shifting for some time. While Shen Hongying's power waned in Guangdong, Chen Lianbo and his Merchant Government waited for the perfect opportunity to strike. He built up his forces and consolidated his powerbase, knowing that any move against Shaoguan would also draw the intervention of Lu Yuguang in Nanning. The outpouring of nationalist anger and the massive disruption of the Sino-Japanese War finally gave Chen the opportunity he sought.\n\nStriking hard against Shen's poorly trained and demoralised militias, Guangzhou forces overran Shaoguan in a rapid campaign for control over Guangdong. They also repudiated Guangxi's domination over Guangdong, directly challenging Lu Yuguang's hegemony.\n\nThe Young Guangxi Faction are mobilising their forces for a showdown. They must strike quickly, before Guangdong forces can consolidate under one banner. Otherwise, the larger province's control over the Pearl River trade will eventually allow them to subsume their landlocked neighbour. Whatever the outcome, it is expected to be costly - adding to the existing nation-wide disruption being caused by the Sino-Japanese War.

The Grand Disenchantment

This however, would be doing a disservice to both the memory and ideals of the late Dr. Sun Yat-sen, alongside leading to what many within the party would perceive as a corrupt sham, mimicking the procedures of the Beiyang Republic.\n\nImmensely dissastisfied with the continued maintenance of the authoritarian hand on the nation, the Dangwai, alongside various liberal Western educated intellectuals, students and even scattered members of the Kuomintang itself — namely the more moderate minded members within the Western Hills clique and Reconstruction Faction — have merged together into a cacophony of disillusioned singing, drowning out any attempt from the [ROOT.GetRulingParty] to aid and soothe the people's suffering.\n\nIn order to restore order, revitalised the nation, and rebuild trust in the government from the people once more, the Premier must demonstrate that the period of tutelage is but a process to ensure a thriving democracy can develop in its aftermath.

A Revolutionary Social Contract
Whilst the people dissent and openly treat the government with suspicion, resentment and caution, the majority continue to treat the regime with ambivalence, disinterest or even support in a few occasions, for there is still a strong belief and hope that lingers in [ROOT.GetLeader]'s steady leadership at the helm. However this series of affairs only continues under an incredibly strict unspoken social contract between the government and the people: they will willingly submit and acquiesce their political, economic and social freedoms if only the Kuomintang can succeed and deliver on the promises of the National Revolution improving their squalid livelihood in some meaningfully clear way.\n\nSuch a stipulation carries a heavy burden upon the government, for they must heed the people's demands and keep the swift pace with the exceedingly ambitious proposals presented by the National Reconstruction Plan if they want to maintain both the system upon which they have instated, and a benevolent outlook from the people. Regardless, the revolutionary party must start from rock bottom, for they have inherited the mantle of a broken economic system, fleeing investors and currency, devastated cities and infrastructure adjoining severe debts that run the risk of further crashing the economy with hyperinflation.
A long road ahead of us...

The Chinese Piedmont
Being the roaring forge in which the revolutionary movement was born, Guangdong has always seen itself as the most premier province in the republican movement. Boasting an impressive economy to further reinforce its lofty ambitions, the province has drawn comparisons amongst international spectators to that of Sardinia-Piedmont during the wars of Italian unification, being the primer driver of reunification for the Chinese nation and its people.\n\nDescending from the province, Hu Hanmin, takes great pride in the exceptional status of Guangdong, however, an ironic dilemma presents itself: the Premier despises challengers to his rule, and naturally is hesistant to entertain treacherous ideas such as autonomy for other provinces, yet blatant displays of favouritism for Guangdong are not uncommon, with the province receiving hefty benefits. This treatment has even gone as far as to extend to governmental appointments, with most positions filled by Guangdong natives.\n\nAs they say, to the victor go the spoils, and none would disagree that Guangdong has earned its special treatment, however some voices within the government are advocating that the Premier lessen the blatant prioritising of his home province, and others have even suggested the idea of extending the current privileges to the other provinces under the Kuomintang's auspices.

An Exceptional Man in Extraordinary Times

Hu Hanmin and the Dawn of a New Era
Amidst the chaos of the recent struggles within China, Hu Hanmin shines ever brightly as a beacon of hope, championing an orthodox interpretation of the Guofu's Three Principles of the People, for long he has envisioned overseeing a future for the Chinese people free of the strife brought on by the Warlord Era. However, Hu's unwavering commitment to the Three Principles sets him apart from his less principled peers within the Kuomintang — namely Wang Jingwei and the late Chiang Kai-shek — as he adamantly opposes any deals with external forces outside the party, relying mostly on his capable political manoeuvring and inter-party alliances.\n\nKeen on seeing the Three Principles enacted at every individual stage of the revolution, the Premier has reinforced the phases of military rule, with tutelage to follow soon after, as without this strict procedure the brave revolutionary soldiers of the National Revolutionary Army could merely supplant the warlords of old. Believing that the party before his stewardship was neglecting the people's interests by not implementing the stage of tutelage completely, the Premier intends to follow Dr. Sun's National Reconstruction Plan by the letter to craft the Chinese nation that Dr. Sun envisioned.\n\nIn the shift from Tutelage to Constitutionalism, Hu Hanmin maintains the concept that a province is ready for democracy once all of its counties had achieved local self-government as dictated by local and national efforts. Once more than half of the number of provinces within the country have reached the Constitutional Stage, a national convention will be assembled to deliberate and promulgative a new national constitution, ensuring a thoroughly healthy democratic foundation for the nation.
A golden road lies before us.
The Revolution and the Land
Having been an ever present issue since the malignant Great Qing, the land reform question has always burned ever present in the intellectual minds of not just the Kuomintang, but all political movements in the nation as a whole. With his mind deeply rooted in his simultaneous socialist background and commitment to the welfare of the people, Hu Hanmin has put forth his radical land reform plan to be debated on by the Central Executive Committee.\n\nCentral to this plan is the concept that the lowly tenant farmers of China should be protected from their common abuse and exploitation by landlords, stipulating that in the case of drought, flood or other similar natural disasters, their rent would be reduced accordingly or even exempted completely. Pushing onwards, the Premier's plan proposes that landlords be restrained from evicting their tenants except for the purpose of self-tilling. Insofar to this end, the Peasantry are to be aided by \
The compromise agreement sways the Premier.
Onwards with abolition of the landlord!

The Army of the National Revolution
Having been a strongly resolute civilian leader for the entirety of his career, the position of the National Revolutionary Army in relation to Premier Hu Hanmin has always been a tenuous one. Without the National Revolutionary Army's successes in the Second Northern Expedition, the revolution might as well have been stamped out for a second time in the century, yet perhaps the greatest threat to the revolutionary movement as it stands currently is now the cudgel with which it yields.\n\nAlways the capable national leader, the Premier has rallied for the recognition of the great significance of restoring Party authority and unifying cohesion amongst the NRA not only in the military campaigns of the recent years, but of the current work in the National Revolution and even in the heavy responsibilities dawning on the new Nationalist government. If the National Revolution is to succeed, there must be a tough revolutionary army and a faithful and valiant Commander-in-Chief. To enable the revolutionary army to break through enemy lines, there must be a strong party as well.\n\nIn a speech preached during a military parade in [ROOT.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName], the Premier left his audience of willing listeners with this statement: \
The NRA and the revolutionary people, unite!
Labour Day and the Chinese Revolution
To Hu Hanmin and his Elders Clique, the meaning of Labour Day is that of great commemoration, for it is when the workers and labourers of the United States fought valiantly in an effort to establish the system of an Eight-hour work day. Notably in the Chairman's eyes is the fact that such a celebration has never taken place in [ROOT.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName], due to the fact that in the past, the city was held under the iron grip of warlords, hence no commemoration could be held.\n\nHowever, the very fact that it can be held today, is that not due to the protection of the National Revolutionary Army? Protection from the NRA means protection from the Kuomintang, with which every citizen should come to learn that the KMT protects its workers and strives for their welfare alongside their freedom.\n\nIn a broader scope, the Premier has put forth the idea that the National Revolution is the united front of all nationals, and in this united front, the workers shall be the vanguards, refraining from playing havoc with each other lest a specific class be promoted leading to the oppression of other classes. From now on, the workers can shake hands with the KMT in a friendly manner, coming together as true friends, cooperating mutually for genuine liberation.\n\nHu Hanmin's concept of the party cooperating directly with the workers serves as a foil to the stance of the Western Hills Clique, who posit that workers should avoid labour disputes in their entirety, and that the true meaning of Labour Day is to exemplify the cooperation between capitalists and workers in the common cause of furthering productivity, not revolutionary liberation.\n\nWith the party's official stance divided between the two outlooks, a debate has silently flared as to whether the Premier's radical stance on worker cooperation that borders on socialist thought is more preferable compared to the stability provided by the WHC's thorough and stable approach.
The KMT is the true worker's party!
Coalesce both the capitalist and the worker in perfect harmony.
Democratic Centralism and Consensus
Adamant in the following of the Sanminism and the Three Principles in an orthodox manner, Hu Hanmin has refused all attempts from the more democratically minded elements of the Kuomintang to push for a provisional constitution at this time. However he is not a man of pure uncompromising stubbornness, and has accepted proposals of a new organic law in which a prototypic version of the planned five yuan government would be implemented. Therefore, the government has begun the formal creation of the various governmental yuans, with which has come the discussion of the accompanying political appointments.\n\nWith Hu Hanmin and his allies dominating the Executive Yuan, Sun Fo and the Reconstruction Faction have been granted free reign over the Legislative Yuan, whilst Ju Zheng and the Western Hills Clique were given the Judicial yuan, with the Control and Examination yuan being split between various northerners alongside other smaller cliques.\n\nWith the various factions clawing at their bountiful harvests, all that remains is for Hu Hanmin to appoint a Premier of the Executive Yuan, akin to a Western styled Prime Minister. End of the Six Years of Necessary Tutelage?
It had been a long and arduous road for the Kuomintang's National Revolution. One wrought with roadblocks, winding passageways, and sudden verges both left and right. Most appropriately it was not an easy path to tread. Dr. Sun knew this of course, for to transform a nation of over 400 million people into that of a modern one is perhaps a most insurmountable task. The Kuomintang is a revolutionary party, one built on organisation and doctrine, one that has been steadfastly stewarded by China's most magnificent and enigmatic statesman.\n\nSix years was the timetable to follow. It was six years that the Tutelage phase was to be enacted, as ordained by Dr. Sun. And it had been six years of perennial guidance and struggle for the modernisation and progress of the Chinese nation. Of course, the natural tendencies of rulership began to set in early on. Members of the party began to see the party as nothing more than a means of dominating the people, of abusing the principle of party leadership to exploit the masses for their own gain, to safeguard their assets. However such sentiment has rather subsided, has rather been beaten back tremendously by China's Man of Principle.\n\nFor the all the faults of complacency that would beset any incumbent party that heralds a revolutionary movement, it has not yet managed to clutch the grasps of Hu Hanmin's Party. And yet time of idle pondering remains scarce and fleeting for the Chairman, for even as steadfast and diligent as he is, not even Hu Hanmin could overcome the towering shadow of the Six Year Tutelage. For such great strides of progress have been undoubtedly made under the auspices of Hu Hanmin, but there will be a time wherein the Revolutionary is no more. And what then? Who would continue the wishes of the Eternal Premier? A successor was no easy selection, Hu Hanmin understood that all too well. Yet it is a most pertinent question to be answered, one that Hu himself must be prepared for.
Who can live up to China's Greatest Statesman?

The Plot Against Chen Jitang
Headlines race across the nation today as they have been flooded with reports of the assassination of Zhu Zhuowen, found in his residence alone, lying emotionlessly for perhaps days. The death of an official would not have aroused so much attention, if it were not for the fact that Zhu was allegedly involved in a scheme against Governor Chen Jitang with Hu Hanmin's cousin, Hu Yisheng.\n\nHu Yisheng's career faced a great halt as he was implicated in first a political scandal, and later the assassination attempt on Liao Zhongkai. Drifting into obscurity and fleeing into exile, Hu Yisheng would continue to lie low, until recently. Despite his return, he remains bitter, regarding Chen Jitang as the main obstacle to his current woes.\n\nTo seek a better future career, Hu Yisheng joined Zhu Zhouwen in his ambitious plan. The Revolutionary Diarchy Exalted!

Previous events such as Zhu's assassination, the pervasive personality cult within Guangdong, and other power plays have shown that the divergence between these two great men is far more than just a rumour. They have thoroughly opposed each other in most cases, and some are even worried that another Guangdong spat was unavoidable. However, in a banquet held in Nanjing, most have recently been convinced that such worries are unfounded.\n\nAs always, Hu orated and pontificated to his comrades with great passion. He praised the success of the NRA, and the role of the military in the tutelage stage. In a rather forced manner, the Chairman clearly alludes to himself and Governor Chen as the basis of the government. Moving on, the Chairman begins another one of his lectures about discipline for his comrades, the proliferation of Sanmin and the nuances of the future of tutelage within the government.\n\nConcluding his speech with a firm and clear voice, reminiscent of his tone in the First Kuomintang National Congress after the victory of the Guangdong Coalition, Hu waved to his comrades amidst a round of applause. Rather humbly treading down the steps of the stage, Chairman Hu began to shake hands with his colleagues. Meeting Chen Jitang's gaze, the Governor saluted without hesitation.\n\nWitnessing such a harmonious scene, it became rather evidently clear to attendees that the two men don't particularly maintain the best relationship. What with Hu reminding Chen of his true position within the Party, the mere stepping stone for the Chairman.
