Guangxi and the Rural Reconstruction
Extensibly throughout the Chinese nation, there remains a severe magnitude of mismanagement, abuse, and inefficiency in the nation's land utilisation. Dr. Sun Yat-sen knew this, and devised his economic programme for National Reconstruction. Huang Xuchu, interprets Dr. Sun's economic programme in a far more visionary light than his peers. Devising it as a form of economy with public ownership of the means of production, management of all productive enterprises would remain with the government, with all private concerns that have an important relationship with the economic standards of the people, or with the defence of the nation being subject to enforced public regulation. However, a key distinguishing factor is that private ownership remains an opportunity for those enterprising enough to take it.\n\nIn terms of educating the masses, New Guangxi initially experimented with universities focused on physical and biological sciences, with expanded curricula and faculties, however that was deemed unnecessary for the needs of the nation, and so the current economic programme follows an extensive training of village cadres with a focus on banking, mining, law and government. With this practical outlook, the clique aims to prepare trained personnel corresponding to the needs of Chinese society.\n\nIn a more revolutionary flash compared to Huang Xuchu's thorough top down approach is the Rural Reconstruction Movement, a common associate of the New Guangxi Clique. Led by Liang Shunming, the movement seeks to implement and assist in a bottom up approach of National Reconstruction. Differing on the promotion of local development under the direction of official local government institutions, Liang desires the economic programme to be a project conjured from the peasants themselves. With another key power player in the Quadrumvirate, Bai Chongxi has yielded a tacit endorsement of Liang Shunming's movement, albeit the final decision remains with [ROOT.GetLeader].
Huang Xuchu's slow and steady implementation can't fail.
Liang Shunming's peasant born movement is what we need!
The Basic Programme of National Reconstruction
On their tireless crusade to bring the renowned and successful Guangxi model to the rest of the provinces, the New Guangxi Clique has set out to codify their National Reconstruction programme. Being nominally inspired by Dr. Sun's own reconstruction plan, the now National endeavour, has been modified by both Huang Xuchu and Huang Shaohong to suit the militaristic requirements of the New Guangxi Clique.\n\nFirstly, the programme deems Sanmin doctrine to be the only appropriate principles for the Chinese revolution and the whole populace. Secondly, it is imperative that during the current stage of the National Revolution, the nation and people come together to fight against imperialism and the power of feudalism. All universal rights of the people or improvements in livelihood must be considered on the basis of the struggle for national liberation.\n\nFurthermore, in order to produce people that contribute to society, it is necessary to increase organisation and training of the people. In order to implement this, the New Guangxi clique have created \
The Guangxi model, nationwide.
This is a breach of Provincial Security!
Puritanism in Education
Mirroring the very roots in which the clique grew from, the New Guangxi Clique's high leadership shares very reserved, strict and puritanical views on society. Having been the case in Guangxi for the years under their tenure, a campaign of continued suppression of love songs, wasteful instrument playing, the propagandisation of patriotic and military songs remains in place, with plans to extend it across the nation. Alongside music, there exists the suppression of tai, the concept of maternal grandmothers bringing expensive gifts following the birth of their grandchildren — deemed an unreasonable financial drain on households — amongst others. Further more, strong opposition to long braids commonly worn by peasant women exists, with the consideration that they attract disease and dirt, with the belief that short haired women were puritanical, militarised, literate and prepared to use their regained strength to serve the nation.\n\nWhilst these programs of stamping out expression have been deemed a success in Guangxi province, many critics have pointed that such initiatives hamper the development of intellectualism. In response to this, President Li Zongren has expressed his clear belief that education — alongside defence — were responsibilities of the government, and although they differed in form they shared the same reality and remained inseperable. The education of the people had to be determined by the needs of their society, with today's student being tomorrow's citizen, and the citizen was the nation's defender after all. Those lacking the ability to defend the nation lacked the requirements for citizenship.
A harsh crackdown on frivolity.
We are not an autocracy!

For Whom the Bells Toll
Hu Hanmin, ever proud, ever enigmatic, ever stubborn, refused to enter political exile for his own safety after his ousting, instead choosing to remain within his home in Guangzhou, basically daring Li Zongren to issue an arrest warrant. Li refrained from making any open move against the ex-Chairman, preferring to merely keep his house under surveillance. Only rare visits from the likes of Lin Sen, Chen Mingshu, and Zou Lu appear to flash light into Hu's dark abode.\n\nIn a rather ironic turn of events, the unfolding intellect and headstrong mind of the ex-Chairman seemed to have been his undoing. During one of Hu Hanmin's frequent chess matches, an act he has reportedly become quite renowned for as a local champion, it would appear that he had an unfortunately fatal brain aneurysm in the midst of the match, later being declared dead at the scene.\n\nMany close to the ex-Chairman reported Hu's clearly shaken, dishevelled, and sickly nature following what could only be interpreted as, in his eyes, the utter collapse of the National Revolution. Those within the New Guangxi's upper echelons posit far more nefarious enquiries as to the ex-Chairman's final moments. Whatever the case, most within the clique have deemed it a happy accident, despite claims that the dire conditions of his living space contributed to his decline — some even daring to make blatant accusations of foul play.\n\nWith Li finally rid of perhaps his greatest adversary, there have been suggestions that the President maintain his outwardly nonchalant attitude towards the lackeys of the Elders Clique, perhaps even being respectful towards the death of the once great statesman. After all, Li is not a monster, and Hu himself had many loved ones, even the adoration of the nation, all of whom will mourn his loss. However, one cannot always rest upon their laurels, and exploiting such an opportunity would allow Li to forever cast the internal party opposition to the dustbin of history.

Like many before him, Li Zongren came from humble (but not too humble) roots, before rising to the occasion to save his people. Through his military and political talents as well as that of his allies, General Li has emerged from a province that most wrote off as backwards and underdeveloped to become President of a reunited Republic of China.\n\nThis Republic is very different from the ones that came before it, and Li Zongren is a very difficult man than the young failed soldier turned gym teacher that first joined the Xinhai revolution. From a Xinhai revolutionary to a warlord soldier to an opportunistic general to a Kuomintang exile to a radical militia proponent to a sometime liberal, Li Zongren has worn many different identities with varying degrees of conviction. He captains his ship much in the same way he once governed his province and once generalled his army, insisting on the strictest discipline for his civilian and military lieutenants alike.\n\nThe Kuomintang too has changed, moulded in the image of its new Chairman. The Spirit of Reform
Nobody would have believed that the near insurmountable position of Hu Hanmin could ever be bested whilst he reigned on the throne, and yet after the much anticipated National Third Congress waxed and waned, such a figment of imagination has become reality. With the stable election of the constitutionalist [ROOT.GetLeader] to the Premiership of the Kuomintang, the spark of democracy bellows once more under the reignited hope that the fulfillment of elections, and the end of Tutelage, will be more than just a nascent dream, but a guaranteed reality for the Chinese nation. The slightest embers of a more broadened political front has mobilised a sheer magnitude of energised politicians from across all avenues of the political spectrum, intent on having their influence on shaping the future for China.\n\nYet such an overwhelming flood of sudden campaigning, burgeoning parties forming, Kuomintang influence waning, it has brought with it intense instability, with many within the party fearing that if [ROOT.GetLeader] and their faction don't manage to keep the lid on things, the party, the National Revolution itself might very much collapse under its own weight.\n\nTo stack onto the mounting issues for the fresh leadership, the crucial question of the Republic's constitution has bubbled to the surface, for Hu Hanmin's clique preferred to rule through Organic Laws as opposed to a written, codified constitution, yet this has been one of the very promises made by the [ROOT.GetRulingParty]. Whatever it may be, one thing is for sure...
Democracy beckons...

Warlords. The Call for Change
As the iron grip the Party-state once had on the Chinese nation relaxes, if only a little, the far more liberal atmosphere has led to an increase in what many in the previous administration would've considered anti-revolutionary practice. Students, teachers, journalists, all unite together in a harmonious symphony directed at rallying against their perceived injustices of the current revolutionary government.\n\nDrawing spotlights on critical issues such as corruption, social conservatism, and authoritarianism, many in the party have actually welcomed such attention on the flaws of the nation, for it is easier to exercise the political will to crack down on such issues if there is significant popular support from the masses. However this has swiftly spiraled into broader rallying cries for changes to the nation and administration that the Kuomintang is hesitant to support, and even would rather be silenced.\n\nFears of such movements evolving further into outright opposition towards the party has led to many of the present leadership considering this to be another of the new administration's critical issues, for how can we provide for the people if they oppose us at every turn?

The [ROOT.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] Horsetrade
With the inauguration of our newly anointed Five Yuan Government, each governmental organ has yielded a bountiful harvest of fresh administrative and bureaucratic positions to be delegated however it is deemed necessary by the current government. However, there is the precedent, and even the expectation that each position is carefully balanced between the current main Kuomintang Factions — Western Hills, Reconstruction and Elders — to maintain a cohesive, unified force from all perspectives of governance.\n\nMost coveted of all the newly inundated positions is that of Premier of the Legislative Yuan, a position expected to be handed to a figure from a different faction than the President, as a demonstration of good will between the disparate factions. Of the two leading figures in the race for Legislative Yuan, the loser is expected to be appointed to Premier of the prestigious yet less powerful Judicial Yuan, whilst the leaders of the smaller factions are expected to receive the Premierships of the Control and Examination yuans.\n\nIn a similar fashion, the other positions within the legislature, such as Vice Premier, and Secretary General of the Legislative Yuan will be distributed in a balanced way. Taking an introspective, some are considering if such a carefully decided set of affairs would even advance the Kuomintang's march towards democracy, or if it will just beget further and further gridlock of opposing ideals.

The National Salvation Conference
Not a rare sentiment shared amongst the more civilian membership of the Kuomintang — although most enthusiastically, and perhaps loudly, held by Sun Fo — many lamented how critical KMT figures would take on multiple party and governmental jobs simultaneously, effectively amounting power and concentrating it in overstretched, underqualified individuals who could not effectively provide assistance to the government.\n\nThus with the newly constitutional turn of the Kuomintang, many personnel within the party have consistently demanded a rule to be applied that no active military personnel could be President or Premier of any of the Five Yuans, a one-man-one-post policy as Premier, and even the stipulation that members cannot participate on multiple councils.\n\nIn a collaborative effort, the three dominant figures of the current government, Sun Fo, Ju Zheng and Hu Hanmin, have all co-written the \

The Political Consultative Council
Ever an emblematic light, a rallying point even, the drafting of a new constitution has been the consistent life goal for Sun Fo, for he has championed relentlessly against the authoritarian, and sometimes arbitrary decisions made by the ruling caste of the Kuomintang during its tenure. With the democratic influences having finally wrested control from the more authoritarian, he has led the charge in pushing towards the drafting of a new constitution for the nation, to reflect the consistent march towards equitable governance handled by consensus, not autocracy.\n\nAppointing a constitution drafting committee, Sun sits as chairman adjourned by Wu Chaoshu and Zhang Zhiben. Included in this committee are the likes of Fu Bingchang, Liang Hancao, Wu Shangying, and many others. Ju Zheng's Western Hills and Hu Hanmin's Elders Clique have begun applying pen to paper in the lengthy drafting process, ironing out all of the details between. However there have been increasingly louder calls from even more radical members of the KMT to allow the external parties, the Dangwai, to participate in the drafting process. Such a brazen invitation carries heavy implications, for a combined effort not just from the Kuomintang, but would imply that the constitution being drafted as it is now would be the final product.\n\nHowever, such a broad extension of acceptance towards the external parties could truly be the difference between a flourishing democracy, and one that is stagnant, with the Kuomintang merely using elections as justification for their continued reign. Nonetheless, Western Hills and the Elders Clique have pushed against such a notion. Sun and Reconstruction for their part, remain on the fence, for whilst Sun himself has been quoted in stating that \
We shall not disavow Dr. Sun.

Rights and Duties of the People
As one of the most significant acts in deciding the future of the National Revolution, and the Chinese nation, the penning of the new Republican Constitution will require arduous effort and strides made on general consensus between all cooperating parties in order to ensure that the labour, the sacrifices, the turmoil of the last decades will all have been worth it in the name of progress.\n\nLeading the charge, [ROOT.GetLeader] has decided to disregard the last implemented constitution by the Kuomintang, the 1912 Provisional Constitution, for it has been deemed unfit for purpose in the new age of the party. However, there remains the will to draw inspiration from certain aspects, such as the rights of the people.\n\nBeing a relatively uncontroversial section of the constitution, the second chapter of the constitution, \

Extent of the Chinese President
In yet another roadblock on the road to promulgation of the new constitution, the role and affairs of the President has come into question. Whilst all come to a common consensus that the role of the President should not carry any true autocratic powers, instead remaining a form of figurehead, many query as to where exactly the President should be situated within the governmental structure.\n\nBeing the Head of State, representing the nation in foreign affairs, having supreme command of all branches of the military, and effectively serving as the overarching face of the government, some within the council have suggested that the President be attached to the Executive Yuan as it functionally serves to exert the will of the executive.\n\nIn counterpoint to this, the more cabinet oriented members of the council have raised the concept that the President should merely serve as a mediator between the Five Yuans, bridging the gap and ensuring continued smooth governance in times of political turmoil. Existing in a state of overall authority within every governmental organ, the President would be an advisory figure of all branches.\n\nThe Elders for their part have backed attachment to the executive, hoping to retain some strength and authority within the role of Head of State. On the opposite end, Reconstruction has seen it fit to insist upon the advisory position of the President, sending the clear message of their belief in the primacy of the Five Branches. Situated within the middle in this dialectical debate, Western Hills has been coaxed by both sides of the Council. With certain members privy towards a strong president, whilst others privy towards Song Jiaoren's ideal of the \
A mediator, unbiased in principles, pragmatic in action!

Matters of the Vice Presidency
In another avenue of contention from the conception of the role of the President, the existence of a Vice President has been brought to question. Serving a role similarly akin to the President, the Vice President would also serve as a component in presenting the Chinese nation towards the rest of the world, and whilst not carrying any serious political power, would still have their words heeded by the rest of the government, being its second most senior figure. At least on paper.\n\nOf course, the existence of the Vice Presidency has raised multiple objections and accusations, insinuating that such a position is the institution of bureaucracy for bureaucracy's sake, and should be completely disregarded. Notably raising the point that if the President were to perish in office or otherwise not be able to function, surely the President of the Executive Yuan can serve as acting President until the crisis is concluded, as opposed to the creation of an entirely new, practically useless position to serve as a backup.\n\nProponents of the Vice Presidency insist upon the United States model, drawing comparisons that have gained them particular ire. Pushed for by Sun Fo and his Reconstruction Faction, he insists that the role of the Vice President would allow even the President to have immediate council, one that isn't privy to the interests of the various Yuans.
Even the President needs his Second-in-Command.
We'll do perfectly fine without another bureaucrat.
Fate of the Executive Yuan
As the matter of the Presidency and Vice Presidency is settled, the constitutional council has now swiftly changed focus onto administrative powers, ironing ou the specific limits and powers of the Executive Yuan. With a generally agreed consensus that the Executive Yuan shall be the highest administrative organ of the state, the Executive Yuan will have a President, Vice President, Ministers, Chairmen of Commissions, and a specific amount of Ministers without Portfolio.\n\nWith the persistent precedence that the President of the Republic shall appoint all critical positions within a governmental organ, and with the agreement that the President of Republic itself should be a fairly weak role — incomparable to the Presidents of the nation's past — with a resilient cabinet based government and a strong legislature, the issue of the extent of the Premier of the Executive Yuan's power has been brought to the forefront.\n\nA large group of Kuomintang members from the Elders Clique have proposed the idea that the Premier of the Executive Yuan should in fact be a hefty post, carrying similar powers to that of Prime Ministers within countries like Canada, whilst others from more cautiously minded cliques such as Western Hills have advocated for the Premier to similarly be a weak position, beholden to the consensus of the cabinet.
Decisive action requires a strong Premier!
Legislature and cabinets should take precedence, not individuals.
Deciding the Fate of the Premier
In another blow to the smooth penning of the constitution, those within the Kuomintang crusading for a strong legislature and weak executive have brought up their issues with the appointment of the Premier of the Executive Yuan in the first place. Effectively serving as the primary force in actual administrative decisions, having the cooperation of the President of both the Republic and Executive Yuan in agreement with the Premier of the Executive Yuan is integral to good, smooth governance.\n\nIn conjunction with this, the current constitution explictily states that the power to appoint the Premier is granted exclusively to the President of the Republic, with consent from the Legislature. However multiple objections have been brought up, instead insisting that the experts within the Legislative Yuan should be the prioritised force behind appointing the Premier, with the President merely providing the rubber stamp.\n\nTheir reasoning — perhaps drawn from the horrors of Beiyang's bureaucracy — is to prevent a nepotistic conjoined power sharing agreement wherein two key individuals, President and Premier, dominate the government's daily functions, although many have thrown accusations of paranoia and needless complication of the constitutional process in the direction of the proponents, majority of which happen to constitute the Reconstruction Faction. Running in counterpoint to this train of thought, the Elders Clique has instead proposed increasing the strength of the president by allowing the position to appoint all cabinet members, not just the premier.
We will not be beholden to corruptible individuals.
President picks Premier, reserved for their best judgement.
Go with Hu's suggestion, the President cannot be hamstringed by the Cabinet.
Affairs of Local Governance
In a surprising turn of events, the rest of the articles and chapters of the constitution were passed with nary a single protest in their specifics or implementation. The roles and structure of the Legilsative, Judiciary, Examination and Control Yuans, most featuring a similar President-Vice President structure, with each having the capacity to enact their specific functions as deemed necessary by their members.\n\nAlbeit, like a spectre from the past continuing to haunt the Kuomintang, the issue of provincial autonomy has reigned havoc on the recent discussion of the past days, as the true extent of the Central Government in contrast to Local Governments comes under fire from those more privy to Federalist ideals. In the current setup, the Central Government has the power of legislation and administration for a myriad of critical components of running the country, such as the judiciary system, national law, national defence and foreign affairs amongst others, however it has been granted the power to delegate administration of the education, banking, forestry, industry, commerce, police, public health and other local issues towards the provincial governments themselves.\n\nSome are proposing that instead of the Central government merely being able to grant the power of Administration to provincial governments on a whim, these rights should instead be guaranteed to the local governments on the basis that they could better manage and implement these regional powers based on local issues at hand. This would allow the Central government to focus on being the beating heart of governance.\n\nYet such a proposition has extensive backlash from the majority of the Council, practically earning its proponents political exile the very moment such a concept left their lips. To repudiate the Centralist Principles of Sun Yat-sen, to repeat the mistakes of the past, and allow the provincial governments too much autonomy to act semi-independently from the Central Government. Its practically sacrilege.
Enough with this provincial autonomy, the Central Government is final!
...the irony is palpable.
Compromise, provincial Chairmen centrally appointed.

Remembering the First Republic

However, not all who worked within Beiyang were traitors. Many were ardent Democrats trying their hardest to make the best of a bad situation, trapped by the corruption of those around them. An idealist, one that believed in the sincerity of human compassion, patriotism, and adoration for one's country. However he had never lost his idealism, as even in his last will and testament he had instructed his friends to telegram Yuan Shikai about the assassination, hoping that he would be a martyr to ensure Yuan protected civil rights.\nSong's relevance to Western Hills is critical, as the constitutional parliamentary ideals and \

The Crown Prince and his Party
The dust has settled, and a new Chairman has taken the reins. Sun Fo has finally achieved his dream leading his father's Party and being able to enact his father's will. A liberal dilletante to some, an iron-neck to others, or a mere product of his own father's good name and nepotism. Sun Fo's rise to the much sought after position of Chairman of the Party has blasted all of his detractors with a salvo of his dilligent and hardworking life ethos. Throwing himself into the revolutionary movement from a young age, Sun has continuously championed constitutionalism and democracy, even if he flip-flopped on their exact timetabling within the National Revolution.\n\nNonetheless his energy ever since he has become Chairman is palpable, as he's taken to his work with the vigor of youth not unbeknownst to his reputation. Representing a new page in the history of his Party, one of a more democratic vision, a Revolution tempered by liberalism, and built upon hope. Democracy is not an easy beast to tame, especially in a nation without a democratic tradition, and make no mistake. Sun is not nearly as radical as his fellows in his approach to the National Revolution.\n\nSun is, however, caught between the other two major civilian factions. A Liberal Revolutionary, squeezed between Ju Zheng's more constitutionally conservative minded Western Hills clique and Hu Hanmin's orthodox revolutionary Elders Clique. The Crown Prince of the Kuomintang is not without options, or wit. Pragmatic in choice of allies, Sun and Reconstruction are willing to dalliance with the Left, the Right, and the Dangwai. Remaining true to his own Liberal values and to his father's vision, Sun has large shoes to fill, and a democratic tradition to foster.

A More Equal Countryside
The question of Land Reform is, quite naturally, one of the most important questions the Kuomintang must address. Inspired by the ideas of his friend and loyalist, the American-educated Wu Shangying - Chairman Sun himself has written up an expansive Land Reform plan already, which comes with three tenets.\n\nFirst, they would reexamine the taxation and land contracts of peasants and landowners, and adjust the tax rate to make it more fair for all, and to unburden them from their heavy workloads. Second, they would put those funds towards the modernisation of agriculture, allowing greater production and bringing in more money for both the Government and the common man.\n\nThird, they would control the sale of rice, rather than the merchants, and therefore control the price of food, improving the lives of the people and bringing in more money for the Government. This would be done by having the farmers give their harvests to the Government directly, and when the landowners demand rice as rent to them, they could simply point the landowners towards the Government, who would give them half the rent in cash and the other half in bonds.\n\nThe plan is, of course, not without its detractors. Questions surrounding the expansion of Bureaucracy being major. We could, of course, reach across the aisle to the Western Hills clique, and pursue a Land Reform more in line with their vision, with less state control in the market and instead focusing on allotting lands and funding to the peasants. Building a land reform model out of educating the peasantry, as opposed to mandated from the government. However this is a dear issue to the Chairman, who strongly believes that his plan is best for all of China.
Compromise is necessary, reach out to Western Hills.
Chairman Sun's plan is best.

Ju Zheng gave a speech where he condemned Sun Fo for allowing violence to go unchecked. Democratising the Party
As one of the Three Principles of the People, the question of how to fully realise Minquan (Democracy) is often on the Chairman's mind. He believes that if China is to ever become a true Democracy, the Kuomintang must become one first, and lead the Country by example. However one cannot implement Minquan without considering the democratisation of one's own party. For the blind cannot lead the blind.\n\nTo this end, he has spoken extensively on the oligarchic power the Kuomintang holds, and how it must be eroded from within, that the leaders must accept that they cannot permanently be the ruling caste of China, lest the Kuomintang become the greatest threat to its own Revolution. The Party has to show the people, not just with words but with action, his action. And through this action one must present the party's earnest democratic spirit.\n\nThrough sweeping reforms of pluralism and egalitarianism, the enshrining of Freedom of Speech for all allowing anyone to criticise the Government and for their voice to be heard, both within the Party and without. Furthermore, he has strictly enforced honest conduct by the Kuomintang in the electoral process, such as the honest conduct in general elections. Mostly brought forth by Sun's own disappointment and dissatisfaction with the local elections sponsored by the party, in which bogus votes and tallies were commonplace.\n\nExpanding further, the Chairman has proposed a series of plans in order to enact democracy within the nation. The people must be able to elect their representatives in some form, and those representatives should be selected upon merit and not party loyalty. For his father's teachings are not merely just for the Kuomintang, but for the populace as a whole.
A brave leap towards Democracy.

The Father of the Nation
In the end, Sun Yat-sen's dream of a free, democratic, and reborn Republic of China finally came true. The revolutionary prophet who spent most of his life in exile must have had his share of doubts, but never lost faith even in the end that his Three Principles would be the ones that would bring an end to the Century of Humiliation. Perhaps it is only fitting that Sun Fo would be the one to see it through. Many have and will continue to call the elder Sun their mentor and inspiration in spirit, it is only fitting that the Father of the Republic has many admirers. But he only had one son, one faithful son who preserved despite all odds.\n\nFor President Sun, the second one of his name, this is more than just a dream come true. Since his days in school, separated by oceans and revolutions from his distant parent, he often had nothing but the readings and letters sent to him from the elder statesman to connect with him. Finding his father meant finding the revolution, becoming the world's premier expert in the Eternal Premier through an encyclopaedic knowledge of the latter's teachings combined with a personal ability to decipher the lessons others tried to corrupt. Now, though the Guofu tragically cannot be at his side for this moment, he lives on in spirit through his work and through his spirit and through the blood that runs through Sun Fo's veins.\n\nSun Fo is more than just a living receptacle for his father's work of course. While faithful in carrying out the Eternal Premier's last will, he also embodies something more. A fresh face for a new generation of the party-state, a young leader for a young republic. There will be plenty of stumbling blocks going forward into a new era of political tutelage, a large part caused by the tensions between Sun's lofty promises of change and his promises of democracy, but maybe this really can be the start of that promising future denied to generations past.
Honoured by his only son.
The Red Piedmont

Advancing from Guangzhou in light of the despotic Central Committee, our brave soldiers risked everything to see to it that the National Revolution, and the Chinese Nation State, was salvaged from the jaws of tyranny. Fulfilling the original dream as drawn out by our forefathers and the Guofu all those years ago, this should be a time of great merriment.\n\nAnd yet, there cannot be anything but great sorrow. Collapse of the Red Army
As the sheer weight of the civil war and National Revolution crashes down upon our new Socialist government in a tsunami, the sword and shield of the Kuomintang, the very harbinger of our new prominence, has finally buckled under the immense stress it has endured throughout its tenure. Waging a glorious war in the name of the Revolution against the counter-Kuomintang forces, Fengtian and their Japanese overlords, foreign imperialists and finally, their own brothers and sister in arms during the civil war, morale has absolutely crumbled, with the will to fight shattered by the seeming betrayal of Wang Jingwei's Central Executive Committee. Certain divisions of the NRA are even functionally outright mutinying against our government, conspiring with the counter-revolutionary resistance to strike against any official response at every turn.\n\nHaving been built as the primary socialist army for the National Revolution, the army functioned on the desire to see Dr. Sun's Three Principles enacted, and come to fruition, however having committed the ultimate demoralising act of fighting their supposed comrades shattering all elements of ideological coherence, leaving the NRA as a shell of its former self.\n\nMost critical to the stability and continued perseverance of the government, the military high command has insisted urgently to [ROOT.GetLeader] that the military be brought under heel, lest it tear the nation asunder in its fury.

Chaos of the Economy
The hammer has struck thrice. Not only stacking with the continued counter-revolutionary campaign waged against our government, but with the crumbling state of the National Revolutionary Army too, what was once relatively one of the stronger provincial economies within China — containing the Pearl of the South, Guangzhou — has but faltered to a shambling corpse, sluggish and in decline. Having served as the main centre for support and logistics for our developing government, the flow of trade through Guangdong, alongside the resources and manpower of Guangxi provided ample support to our burgeoning contingent of the National Revolutionary Army.\n\nDuring the civil war in the party, where comrade fought comrade in a struggle to fulfill the true dream of Dr. Sun, a large portion of national infrastructure and industry was savaged in the warfare, having seen extensive utilisation in the fighting. Likewise, after the conclusion of the bloodshed, many of the urban centers outwith our direct control transformed into hotbeds to wage resistance against [ROOT.GetLeader], drastically hampering their capability for economic productivity.\n\nWith central authority in shambles, banditry, violence and local warlords have started to flourish in the void left by our absense, with foreign investors fleeing the country due to the broad instability caused by governmental incompetence, all of this combines in conjunction to prevent the National Revolutionary Government's capability to enact its much revered welfare policy, Minsheng.

State of the Radical Opposition
Following the political and societal aftermath of the Kuomintang Civil War, our burueacracy has been shattered along factional lines, with the once united Kuomintang committees and councils now barren, empty seats left by those who joined the Central Committee, or otherwise having been arrested for their ties to [CHI.GetLeader].\n\nAcross the party board, those associated with Song Qingling and the Provisional Action Committee, those within Wang Jingwei's Reorganisation Comrades Association, the doting liberals under Sun Fo's Reconstruction Faction, and even the extremist radicals under the elusive China Revival Society have all been cast away into the depths by [ROOT.GetLeader], branded as counter-revolutionary traitors.\n\nAlbeit not all have fully turned their backs upon our government, for some of [ROOT.GetLeader]'s deputies having managed to secure concrete interest in being welcomed back into the political mainstream, an olive branch of peace to those whom are ultimately our misguided comrades. With their skillsets and expertise yielding particular benefit to the National Government, there is much to be gained from such an extension of mercy, however, the political battlefield is merciless and bloody, and none know this better than [ROOT.GetLeader], and so the option of being ruthless is always on the playing field.

Fate of our Revolutionary Siblings
Having been a primarily revolutionary force since its inception, the National Revolutionary Army has attracted a broad menagerie of revolutionary opinions and views, naturally combining this with fierce ideological priority and relentless ferocity has lead to a factionally charged army placing politics above the nation. Whilst this has yielded a force utterly unmatched on the battlefield, for they fight not just for personal salvation, but that of the nation itself, it has also led to the fraying of capable soldiers and commanders in times of political infighting, such as the recent civil war.\n\nNumerous bright lights of potential within the high command have found themselves on opposing sides to the national government, fracturing its capability to wage the revolutionary struggle. Whilst most are ideologically opposed to our government, refusing all offers of possible amnesty, there remain a key section of more amenable figures from across the political opposition, willing to put aside ideological differences in the name of serving the national government oncemore.\n\nWhilst their skillset and expertise would prove invaluable in the reconstruction of the NRA, it may also yield itself to political differences that [ROOT.GetLeader] and the party may not be privy towards, and so there always remains the option of promoting a new generation of subservient officers, devoted not just to the National Revolution, but also ideologically compliant with the new government.

Labour Through Reform
Ostensibly the nation is fractured, infrastructure demolished, homes vacant or destroyed, and yet its people remain, ready and pliant to aid in the great reconstruction of China. Various proposals have been floated by those more inclined to authoritarianism within the party to enact a campaign of reconstruction, utilising the more than available pool of workers on state mandated projects aimed to revitalise the nation swiftly and efficiently.\n\nWhilst deemed a considerable breach of the inherent rights of the workers, a blatant abuse of governmental authority by many within the party, proponents of such ideas feverously defend the necessity of such drastic measures in these critically dire times.\n\nYet there continues to be even fiercer opposition from the moderates within our party, citing such ideas as being a complete abrogation of any of the ideals instilled within the party membership by Dr. Sun's Three Principles. Such infighting may lead to an even worsening governmental situation, although it appears to many observers that this is less an issue of national rejuvination, and more that of political positioning.

Government for the Producing People
When the dust settled, the Productive People's Party emerged as the sole victor of the Chinese Civil War. From relative obscurity, Chen Mingshu and his allies now have free reign to shape China as they see fit. Despite their betrayal of the Central Committee of the Kuomintang, Chen and the PPP have already begun to tie their legitimacy to Sun Yat-sen's legacy. In government-associated newspapers, links between PPP policy and Sun's three principles are emphasised, with it commonly being stated that the PPP only seek to Many moderate nationalists have begun to flock to the PPP banner, recognising that the new regime will not go away anytime soon.\n\nChen Gongbo himself met Chen Mingshu where the Lame General offered the RCA leader a pardon. Graciously accepting, Wang returned home to Foshan, retiring from politics for good. In an effort to strengthen the PPP's Kuomintang credentials, Song Qingling was offered the position of Chairwoman in the new revolutionary government, but rejected the offer on the grounds that Chen Mingshu had betrayed her late husband's legacy. Chen also offered the position to Hu Hanmin and Cai Yuanpei, both refusing as well. Not everyone in the former Kuomintang has followed Song's lead in shunning the new government, however.\n\nHuang Qixiang, one of Deng Yanda's close colleagues, accepted an offer to join the government's revolutionary government council, the highest government organ of policy creation. Chen Youren, another member of Song's camp, has become the Minister of Foreign Affairs. Chen Mingshu himself took the position of Minister of Interior in addition to the position of provisional Chairman, with his key ally Jiang Guangnai filling the role of economic commission director. The new government has promised to deliver critical aid to the peasants of China, and ensure prosperity for all.
The Lame General in charge? What a strange turn of events...
And to only lead us to ruin...
Harmonious Land Distribution
Intellectuals have defined feudalism as the system in which the subsistence economy of China plays a dominant role in Chinese society. It is universally agreed among the party that the system of feudalism is one of the greatest injustices in China, with landlords forcing the peasantry to labour away with no gain. Although China's coastal urban centres have transitioned out of feudalism and entered capitalism, most of China still toils under the feudal system.\n\nMany in the PPP were born into feudalism, and thus are motivated to liberate their townsfolk from this oppressive system. With a Revolutionary Government of the Producing People finally established, the essential business of land reform may finally be conducted. Some leaders in the PPP believe that the government should redistribute all the land to the people equally, regardless of former status. The remnants of the Third Party as well as former RCA and PAC members who have emerged as the more socialist wing of the PPP have backed this course of action along with some additions of their own, seeking a more Sunist' approach, with some aspects of land to the tiller being enacted, which allows peasants to obtain their own land to till and that the government will take steps to eventually nationalise land gradually. However, more moderate members of the PPP believe in a far more moderated land reform. In their eyes, a more conservative approach to land reform would be far more likely to be met with success and avoid the potential consequences of the social unrest and chaos that could spring from more utopian measures.
Distribute the land fairly, regardless of class!
A moderated land reform sounds promising!
The United Front
The new United Front consists of a much larger assortment of parties than the old Kuomintang-led one. The first two junior parties to the Productive People's Party were of course the Kuomintang and Chinese Syndicalist Party. PPP leadership welcomed Gu Mengyu into the fold as head of the RCA, with Wang having disappeared. Huang Qixiang's participation in the revolutionary government ensured that the PAC would be second to the PPP in prominence within the UF, while the LCS has been sidelined.\n\nWhile Chen Shaoyu denounced the United Front as a counter-revolutionary farce, Chen Duxiu has attempted to salvage CSP influence, working alongside Tan Pingshan's loyalists to promote socialist policies in the government. Feng Yuxiang also joined the United Front with his Guominjun organised into the National Revolutionary Party, aligning itself with the Kuomintang. While many are sceptical of Feng's commitment to the three principles and socialism, his help was welcomed by Gu Mengyu.\n\nIn addition to the former nationalist bloc, Chen Mingshu extended a hand to several liberal parties who were willing to cooperate with the Productive People's Party. Recognising Chen's movement to be far more moderate than the Kuomintang's socialist wing, Zhang Junmai accepted Chen's offer, making the CDL the 5th member of the United Front. Although the CDL would have preferred an immediate end to tutelage, they hope to make sure the fledgling republic does not fall to socialist vanguardism or revolutionary excess. To everyone's surprise, the YCP under Li Huang and Zeng Qi also accepted Chen's offer to join the United Front. Distrusted by all other parties except for perhaps the flexible NRP, the YCP hopes to ensure that the new government is does not lean too far into class struggle With this large assortment of partners, Chen Mingshu hopes to promote the democratic reputation of the PPP and showcase his commitment to a pluralist government.
Only time will see if Chen can wield this grand coalition...
And to cooperate with the bourgeoisie!
The End of the Reorganisation?
Although Chen Mingshu and the Productive People's Party have promised a continuation of the revolutionary mission of the reorganised Kuomintang of 1924, many wonder if the Lame General truly intends to stick with such a course. It was the 1924 reorganisation which saw the reactionary tendencies of the Kuomintang culled, replaced with a disciplined democratic centralist party structure poised to sweep through China with the cleansing fire of revolution. Despite this, the party was not united in what the vision of a Nationalist China would look like.\n\nAlthough all members of the Kuomintang were committed to the idea of National Revolution and the necessary stage of tutelage, the party continued to suffer from the plague of factionalism. During his governorship of Guangdong, Chen Mingshu suppressed radical leftist elements, which has made him no shortage of enemies among both the RCA and the PAC.\n\nNow that Chen has emerged as a dark horse victor in the chaos of the preceding years, such a need for diplomacy is at its end. Many committed revolutionaries fear for the fate of Sun's dream and of the reorganisation of 1924. Under the leadership of Wang Jingwei, it was generally understood that the goals of the National Revolution were to achieve socialism and uplift the Chinese people. Under Chen however, many Kuomintang members doubt the Lame General's commitment to a socialist revolution, and some have even suggested that he seeks to overturn the Reorganisation itself.\n\nIndeed, many of the more liberal members of the New United Front have taken it upon themselves to petition Chen Mingshu to make a statement on the failures of Reorganisation, while much of the Kuomintang has demanded that he maintain the revolutionary course of the Guofu. Chen recognises that to denounce the Reorganisation would not greatly affect his government's policy whilst earning the respect of various liberal elements in his coalition, though he risks angering members of the KMT.
The New China has no need for the Reorganisation of 1924...
The Reorganisation was a necessary for the establishment of this revolutionary state!
Honouring our Military Heroes
Just a few short years ago, our nation was pitifully divided between apathetic governments and self serving warlords. Thanks to the NRA and the 4th army of Guangdong, decades of infighting have been put to an end, and even the Century of Humiliation may finally be over. Chen Mingshu, Jiang Guangnai, Cai Tingkai, as well as many other 4th army officers who have led the military effort to liberate China have rightly been honoured as China's saviours in a time of unspeakable darkness.\n\nOf course, such military men should be rewarded for their heroism. The PPP has already begun drafting further rights and privileges for all veterans and serving men of the 4th Army, as well as soldiers' pensions for all veterans in general. The government has rightfully seen this as an opportunity to subtly promote its own image among the populace, and thus generate political momentum to push forward more of its own agenda. On top of that, keeping a loyal military that is well respected among the people will ensure the stability of the government for years to come.
We must honour these revolutionary heroes!
The Democratic Inclination
Chen Mingshu has often spoken of the need for a revolutionary government of the producing people, but many wonder what will become of democracy in this new China. It is no secret to anyone that the Kuomintang pushed for a one party state which would govern the state during a period of tutelage, and after an unspecified point of time would relinquish power and call forth elections when the people were educated on how to be good citizens in a democratic republic. Although Chen Mingshu and the PPP have made greater strides in courting liberal parties who were less amenable to the revolutionary socialist government of the old KMT, it is still the PPP who retains the true political power in the government.\n\nMany believe the new United Front to be nothing more than a PR tool for the PPP to prove its democratic intentions. Indeed, the government is still primarily composed of PPP members, 4th Army officers, former KMT members, and a small minority of liberal politicians. All democratic institutions developed since the founding of the new government have then been unsurprisingly unsteady, and there is little faith in actual democracy being implemented in the short term future. Despite democracy's future being in doubt, most Chinese are content with the current state of affairs, for the PPP earnestly pursues the betterment of the Chinese nation and all its citizens, a drastic improvement from the chaos of the warlord era and Zhili dominance.
You cannot have true democracy without the principle of Minsheng!

The Revolution Saved
The revolution is like an unhatched egg - the chances for it to break before the chick hatches are dangerously high, and so must be safeguarded with zeal and nurtured with care. The first revolution began with the Wuchang Uprising of 1911, which would culminate in the overthrow of the decadent Qing Dynasty and establish republican rule in China. However, the revolution was betrayed by the Beiyang government, and the nation was thrown into chaos.In 1924, the revolution was given new purpose in the first reorganisation of the KMT. However, the revolution was destroyed on the battlefield, trampled by the German jackboot and hunted down by opportunistic traitors.\n\nWhen the revolution once again seemed to be threatened by the very KMT which was meant to embody it, the PPP had to step in to ensure its survival. Where the old KMT would have eliminated the bourgeoisie and subversive elements in Chinese society, the PPP has taken the velvet glove approach, subordinating cooperative liberals and socialists into its movement. Yes, China may not be truly socialist, but neither has the revolution been subverted by liberal influences. The PPP remains true to itself, pursuing revolution by its own design to ensure common prosperity for the Chinese people. Where once corrupt imperialist collaborators dominated the middle kingdom, the Revolutionary Government of the Producing People seeks to undo all injustices done upon China.\n\nTutelage of the PPP is a necessary evil for the revolution, but it will not last forever. The Productive People's Party, much like its founder Chen Mingshu, has endured countless hardships and emerged all the stronger for it. Like China itself, Chen has been unfairly treated by those around him and denigrated for trying to secure a better future. No longer will Chen or China be subject to the unfair laws of fate, no longer will the Chinese Republic be unchained by imperialism and let it be known that it was Chen Mingshu who led us to this glorious future!
Three cheers for the Lame General no more!
Healing the Divisions of the 4th Army
While all who serve the National Revolutionary Army share a cause in bringing true liberation to the Chinese people under Sanminzhuyi doctrine, the 4th Army remains unique among all the armies of the NRA. Composed of Cantonese officers, the 4th Army enjoys a level of fraternity that has stayed true regardless of the battle lines taken during the Chinese Civil War. It is true that Li Jishen, Zhang Fakui, and Xue Yue stayed with Wang Jingwei and the Nanjing Government while Chen Mingshu and Cai Tingkai realised the follies of the Central Executive Committee, but 4th Army brotherhood transcends such divisions.\n\nWas it not the 4th Army which stood true together in the mountains of MinGan for over a decade? Was it not the 4th Army united in revolutionary spirit which defended the Chinese people of Shanghai from the wrath of Imperialism? It was indeed the shared brotherhood of the 4th Army which saved China from its dark decade under Zhili and German domination, and who continued to serve in the 4th National Revolution. Zhang Fakui and Xue Yue remained stalwarts of maintaining the true interests of the National Revolution despite Wang Jingwei's misdirection of the Party in the service of self-aggrandisement, and Li Jishen led Whampoa with the dignity and honour such an institution deserved.\n\nIt was truly a tragedy that Chen Mingshu and the India Clique found themselves on opposite sides during the NingYue split, but both sides knew that their 4th Army brothers simply fought to redeem the National Revolution. With the age of brothers' wars at an end and an age of prosperity and equality yet awaiting China, it stands to reason that the 4th Army must continue to shepherd our healing nation, with all the sons of the 4th Army standing guard.
We sons of Guangdong stand together!

Land Reform in the New China

In such a system, the dominance of the landlords should've been no surprise, with their capacity to control both the land and labour which worked it, it allowed the gentry and landlords to amass unaccountable influence within the political battleground, leading to a monopoly of power. Whilst the more modernised coastal areas of China have entered a proto-capitalistic stage, the rural provinces of the interior still struggle and toil under this despotic feudalistic system, deemed a critical issue by [ROOT.GetLeader], it is up to his new Third Party to best devise the solution to the qualms of the Chinese peasant and worker.\n\nAs favoured by the more radical members of Tan Pingshan's flock, Chen Gongbo has suggested a radical land reform approach towards the landlord problem, with the government methodically passing various land laws that would enable the National Government to seize land lawfully, and radically redistribute it amongst its workers. Redefining Chinese Syndicalism
Following the resurgence of the Kuomintang in the fallout of the League War, the junior party in the United Front, the Chinese Syndicalist Party, has limped alongside their master under the foolish leadership of Chen Duxiu. With the Chinese workers and peasantry representing the true citizenry of China, they have yet again been lead astray by a false prophet, for the Chinese Syndicates intended to represent them merely answered either to the Kuomintang, or to Paris and London, no more than an extension of the Internationale's influence into the Chinese political battleground.\n\nWhilst forever grateful for the diligent work, and brave sacrifices made in man and material by his European comrades, [ROOT.GetLeader] has set forth on defining China's own brand of Syndicalism, for it is a country split along two faces, the capitalistic worker, and feudal peasant. The shortcomings of the Chinese Syndicalist Party and Kuomintang are on full display, rife with contradictions in their approach to the citizenry, and beholden to inapplicable ideals.\n\nWith such revelations, the Chinese syndicates and unions have reveled in their newfound true independence thanks to the Three People's Principles Comrades Federation, no longer reliant on the Kuomintang nor Internationale. However, the matter of defining Chinese Syndicalism is a far greater beast than it would seem, and [ROOT.GetLeader]'s party is at an evident crossroads, choosing between his revolutionary industrial socialist ties, emboldened by the likes of Chen Gongbo, Deng Zhongxia and the other Marxists, or his moderate agrarian ties linked with PAC figures such as Zhang Bojun, and other theorists like anarchist Liu Shifu.

Spectre of Provisional Action

True Revolutionary Means in the New Era
In a bold new step for the Chinese Revolution, [ROOT.GetLeader] has decided to fully embrace his Marxist credidentials, pushing forward with the radical Syndicalist wing of his Third Party. In particular, Tan has sought the alliance of the Machinery Workers Union, led by Li Dexuan and Zhu Jingli - one of Guangzhou's largest labour unions in order to solidfy the anarcho-syndicalist united front within China.\n\nFurther inspired by the writings of Chen Gongbo, the Three People's Principles Comrades Federation has set forth with a state capitalist programme focused entirely on mobilising the proletariat for the future class struggle. With the planned inundation of the Yellow Unions, the nationalisation of key industries such as railways and mining, alongside the nationalisation of foreign trade, many in the counter-revolutionary opposition have thrown accusations of totalitarianism towards [ROOT.GetLeader], however he has refused this slander, openly stating that the presence of him and his Third Party will ensure the equalised prosperity for all within China, and inevitable advance of socialism.

Land Reduction Taxes

Seeing a common consensus of agreement amongst both wings of his Third Party, [ROOT.GetLeader] has offered Liu Shaoqi full capacity in implementing such a programme, seeing it as the middle-ground between the ideals of his old Kuomintang and CSP allies, and the necessary medicine for the Chinese peasantry and workers.

A True Chinese Revolution
In the national capital of [Root.capital.getcapitalvictorypointname], Chairman Tan gives a grandiose speech to initiate the commencement of the National People's Congress of [GetYear]. In attendance are a wide variety of those from Tan's party, anarcho-syndicalists, communists, members of the LCS, and, of course, the most provocative of union members of the League - those belonging to the Chinese Syndicalist Party.\n\nIn 1925, when the Chinese Nationalist Party aligned with the League of Chinese Syndicalists on the eve of the Northern Expedition, few would have expected that the fragile alliance between the revolutionary parties would succeed. Indeed, it was the failure of the Nanjing Government that had almost driven this cooperation to an end. But it was Tan who persisted and prevailed in the plight of the Chinese proletariat, the revolutionary worker who would not just resist the chains of the foreign imperialists but would march as one revolutionary vanguard in achieving the destruction of capitalism.\n\nFor the first time in Chinese history, the act of the strike and the will of the workers have been enshrined in a newly adopted national constitution - drafted by none other than Tan and his followers. The Chairman has ordered the implementation of vast reforms, seeking to improve the livelihood and well-being of the workers and the peasants. Across the country, in major industrial cities like Shanghai and Wuhan, committees and union membership among workers have risen dramatically.\n\nOf course, the future awaits to see what the Chinese worker will be able to achieve in the social revolution. Tan has pledged that the Chinese revolution must not just be limited to East Asia but that China must aid in the world's revolution against the destructive ways of capitalism. And so Tan looks on to the cheering assembly; he knows that through him - he can draft a course distinct from the demagogues of the CSP and the KMT, a new third way for China and the world to embrace at last.

New Beginnings
As the last remaining regiments of the former Nanjing Government offer their surrender, the new Chinese government is now controlled by Peng Pai and his followers, who have distanced themselves from the former Kuomintang regime and tried to emphasise their own distinct identity. But they are hardly the only agrarians in China and as they have absorbed other populist movements, conflicts have grown.\n\nThe Chinese Peasants' Party of Sichuan calls for a modernisation of agriculture, seeing it as the true path to democracy and progress, but are also hesitant on embracing the more radical aspects of the Guangdong Peasant's Association. Instead they have clamored for fair prices for confiscated land and have rejected tactics of landlord intimidation. Their rivals in Sichuan, the Chinese Peasants' Liberal Party, have also been folded into Peng Pai's movement. While liberal in nature, their members have shown a greater incentive towards working with the new Chairman.\n\nThe left and centre of the Chinese political spectrum are not the only ones clamoring for rural reform. Raising a similar banner, right-wingers like Shen Dengyi (a prior socialist) and Liang Shuming of the Rural Reconstruction Association have also offered their services to the new government; finding the Chairman's ears to be particularly recepetive towards rural reform in health, education, and productivity. Given their conservative ideologies, it is likely - that while their voices may be heard; they may not necessarily be listened to.

The Land Revolution
The rich earthly soil for which the Chinese farmer and peasant toils his entire livelihood is finally in the hands of the country's truly revolutionary proletariat. For years, the Guangdong Farmers Association, under the leadership of its bold and revolutionary orator Peng Pai, has associated the Chinese feudal land system with a system of imperialistic capitalism. Now, with the recent unification of the country following the people's victory against the bourgeois and corrupt Nanjing Government and their warlord allies - the issue of land has come to the forefront of Chinese politics.\n\nTo the new Chairman of the Chinese socialist republic, class struggle is an inevitable part - if not companion of the national revolution. To Chairman Peng, peasants were divided into four classes: landowning peasants, semi-landowning peasants, tenant-peasants, and the rural labourer. The enemies of the peasants were the warlords, bureaucrats, compradors, landlords, and reactionary intellectuals - while revolutionary intellectuals were seen as a revolutionary vanguard and allies to the proletariat.\n\nBut as the revolution has now reached its critical apex with the success of the peasantry over the bourgeois and counter-revolutionary Kuomintang; the question has also come whether the revolutionary should be led primarily by the peasantry or should the peasantry and working class work together in a new class alliance. While urban syndicalists have a far different scenario of revolution than the peasantry, with the new revolutionary government - working with allies such as the syndicalist Yang Yin can help bridge the gap between the syndicalist and agrarian revolution. An invitation to the elements of the CSP, would create a renewed united front but this time solely between the workers and peasants.
To the tiller, goes the land...

The Innocence of the Peasant
True, many will acknowledge that the Chinese peasant is ignorant; he lacks more than just an understanding of the modern world - he even lacks the notion of Zhonghua. To many socialist intellectuals, the Chinese peasant is a class that needs to have its consciousness awakened - until then, there can be no hope for success in the national revolution.\n\nFrequent comparisons are drawn by peasant sympathisers such as Peng Pai and his followers in the Guangdong Farmers' Association between the levels of the proletariat within Chinese society - significant distinctions are drawn between the rural proletariat of the peasantry and the urban proletariat. Evil and darkness are associated with the cities; unbridled crime and moral degradation fester the dimly lit streets in cities like Shanghai, while in the rural cities, there is plentiful fresh air and happiness. The Necessity of a Revolutionary Vanguard
In acknowledging the histories of past rebellions in China, such as the Taiping Rebellion or the Boxer Rebellion, socialist intellectuals, and revolutionaries have dwelled upon both the success of the mass movement in rising against the tyranny of Western imperialism and their utter and catastrophic failures in the wake of reactionary suppression. To the Chinese socialists, especially those who see themselves as communists - peasants and workers must be unified in class and led by the revolutionary intellectuals to transform their consciousness.\n\nThe syndicalists believe that the workers must lead a worker-peasant alliance—that is, the unions—but in reality, there are hardly many organised workers within the party leadership of the Guangdong Farmers Association to lead a syndicalist revolution. Most of Peng Pai's highest cadre are intellectuals educated both in China and abroad who seek to bring their knowledge of Marxism, socialism, and revolution to the Chinese proletariat.\n\nIn leading Marxist theory, it is, of course - the revolutionary intellectuals who partake in this seemingly unequal relationship. The intellectual is to be the leader to guide the uneducated and abused peasant to revolutionary catharsis. In this precious time of revolutionary change, it is essential- that the Chinese people understand that questions and doubts must be silenced away in favour of obedience and loyalty to the cause. For it is the discipline of the Party, that shall help hinder confusion, disorganisation, and disagreement - and it is by following the one cohesive revolutionary vanguard that true democracy and love can exist once and for all.
The leader leads, the people follow.

The Rural-Urban Divide
The general argument in contemporary Chinese socialism addresses two main issues. The first issue is what determines the nature of rural China: production forces or production relations. Some scholars believe that according to Marxism, production forces form the base of a society and hence determine the nature of the production relations and the nature of society. Since many new agricultural methods, equipment, and technology have been imported into China since the industrial revolution - more and more of the rural peasantry have been reduced to wage labourers and are now engaging in what appears to be a capitalist society. As a result, China is an entirely capitalist country from its rural outreaches to its crowded urban centers; and as such; the foci of the revolution should be entirely focused on its rural centers.\n\nHowever, others especially within the Orthodox wing of the Chinese Syndicalist Party argue that China is not entirely capitalist - that production forces are important but not as decidedly significant as aspects such as the means of production. They see China as a feudal non-capitalist state and that it must be the workers and unions who are to guide the revolution to its next logical conclusion.\n\nBoth of these sides acknowledge that capitalism had permeated the urban economy of China, but the struggle rages on between socialists throughout the country; as to where to focus the goals and expressions of the social revolution. By leaning towards the side of the orthodox wing of the Chinese syndicalist party, Chairman Peng could see it as an advantage to reconcile with the trade unions of the cities and declare a fruitful relationship between the workers and the peasants.
In the fields of Haifeng, the revolution was born.
It is a revolution of both the workers and the peasants!
The Village Unshackled
In a small village in the middle of the Chinese heartland, the minute the school bell rings to signal the end of the day, a fifteen-year-old boy puts on his red scarf and rushes out the door along with the rest of his eager and enthusiastic classmates. A few years before, one could hardly fathom that a school would be available for the village children, as it had been for centuries - that in a poor village like theirs, the children would have played a role in the fields. But times have rapidly changed, and since then, the villagers' livelihood has seemingly altered dramatically for the better.\n\nHeading home, he arrives to help his mother out at the local commune's kitchen. Multiple families are able to gather at the commune's mess hall to share communal meals underneath a grandiose portrait of Comrade Peng. Following their meals, the local commune's commissar stirs its members in a fiery speech, praising the commune's weekly agricultural production and promising they will only triple the yields by the next month. The commissar praises the young man for his academic excellence and hard work in the field, earning him a brilliant citation.\n\nAs he wipes off the sweat from his brow, once he is back in the fields, he could not help but think of the whispers he had heard in the school that day, that one the teachers at his school, who has not returned to work in three weeks, with the school's principal declaring that he had merely taken leave to study the great works of Chairman Peng.\n\nHe shakes these thoughts aside, instead whispering a silent prayer of thanks to Peng Bodhisattva for his guidance in bringing peace to the local village. After all, they had seen in the past few years, endless strife and conflict, now, finally - there is peace, and the days of begging for scraps on the floor from the evil bourgeois landlords have long ended. He looks onwards towards the sun setting in the distance and smiles, knowing his true ambition is to be nothing more than a rustless screw for the Chairman's glorious revolutionary cause.
There is no end to our rising red dawn!
The Hour of Federal Democracy?
Who would've thought that the lowly intellectual-led Federalist movement would've actually seen it through? Their ambitious plans for unification by the ballot, despite all reservations, was instead concluded through the sword, and yet their project is truly only beginning. There remains much work to be done, nonetheless, the Federalists have risen from the grave of the 1920s, and stepped forward to defiantly stand against both revolutionaries and warlords as the only true representatitve force of the masses. Ranging from Democratic Socialists to Confucian Scholars, their myriad of interpretations of the Federalist movement is equally as colourful and diverse.\n\nWith concerns over Federation, Confederation, the role of the province versus the role of the state, electoral infrastructure, the Frontier and its role to play, and even the international position of China on the world stage, undoubtedly the young republic has its hands full. Nonetheless, there is still striking progress to be made in no short order. With various provinces having already drafted their local constitutions, or are almost on the eve of concluding the drafting process, the time has finally arrived, as is ordained, for the United Provinces to draft their own national constitution.\n\nBringing together the brightest minds of the nation, and representatives from all provinces that are currently free from military occupation, the scene is similar to that of the ordeal of the Hunan Provincial Constitution. The bringing together of bright Federalist doctrinaires such as Zhang Junmai, Zhao Hengti, Tang Jiyao, Zhang Shizhao, Hu Shih, Huang Yanpei, and many others, all for the ultimate goal of the constitutional drafting process, has raised spirits, somewhat.\n\nBut there remains the ever looming presence that this is perhaps a peak overlooking a chasm; can the various allies that have been made along the way truly be trusted to relinquish their hold on power? Can the warlords be subdued long enough? And what of the revolutionaries, running amok?
And so concludes the Zeitgest of the 1920s.
The Nationalisations of the Military
Perhaps considered by the most ardent militarist and provincial autonomist as pooling the wool from beneath their eyes, the official announcement of the nationalisation of the military under the Central Government has been announced, and is already well under way. With control of the upper echelons of the military firmly under that of the Yue Army and its allies, there has been little any form of resistance can do to truly stop their integration into the Central Army. And yet that does not cease the simmering tensions from warlords and governors who fear the loss of their military power, but it is not a sudden conclusion either.\n\nAs what was generally held to be the fait accompli of the Grand Republican Alliance and the China Democratic League, the nationalisations of the military has long been a core doctrinal component of their democratic theories. Under the formation of the United Provinces, the armies of China would be reorganised into the National Defence Army, consisting of only four divisions. Under the command of the Ministry of War, the army will be solely responsible to the ministry for pay, supplies, and assignment of station areas, with the provinces exempt of any responsibility.\n\nWith enforced apoliticisaion of the army, nationalisation of the provincial forces, and the mandate of separation of powers between the civilian and military spheres, many believe wholeheartedly in the death knell of warlordism. And yet, equally there are those protesting most fiercely against the reduction of provincial rights. Zhao Hengti and the Hunan Federalists have led a thorough campaign against the process, insisting that each province absolutely must retain its own provincial force for defence, akin to that of the national guard in the United States.
The dichotomy between Nation and Province grows deeper.
Rebuilding from Devastation
Suffice to say, the Chinese economy has never been particularly the strongest beast. Not even considering the international state, the Chinese market has long been an object of desire, but not for the benefit of its people. The masses have long been abused by colonial exploits to harvest China's natural resources and export them for the economic benefit of compradors and foreign businesses. Even worse, successive governments have hinged themselves upon frequent usage of foreign loans to stay afloat, or otherwise sold themselves to foreign partners. Combined with extensive militarisation, devastation from war, corruption, incessant taxation, and an assortment of numerous minor issues, it is a surprise that the nation has avoided complete abstract bankruptcy.\n\nAnd this was all before unification. With the conclusion of the military campaigns, and the beginning of encirclement campaigns, the national constitutional draft, and the hoisting of the provincial government, the economy has come crashing down upon the societal frenzy.\n\nForeign companies have begun to cash in their debts, with a certain level of uncertainty about whether or not the government can even pay fully. Inflation has skyrocketed due to blatant increases in military spending, a plethora of areas on the frontlines such as Dongbei have been annihilated in the warfare, and are further restricted due to military occupation. The already high taxation compounding with inflation has led to a thorough dearth in any financial projection, and in-order to survive many officials have turned to corruption to feed their families.
They have not even given us a moment to breathe!
Our Founding Father's Failing Health
Having already lived for quite a while on borrowed time, it has become evidently clear that the health of the Father of the Nation, Chen Jiongming, is far from stable. Whilst not living a particularly spartan or healthy life from the onset, Chen has experienced health scares before; during his time in Guangzhou in the early 30s. And for some time now, it has become an even more pressing issue. He has begun to delegate further and further duties to his staff such as Chen Yansheng, Chen Qiyou, Ma Yuhang, Huang Dingchen, Yan Xichun, and others. And they can do very little but watch as their leading figure, and friend, deteriorates in his position.\n\nWhilst not particularly old, Chen nonetheless has had to endure untold stress upon his physical body, leading the nation during wartime and before that in the 1920s, having to lead forces in the Warlord foray has compounded with his terrible immune system. And ultimately, the clock is up — announcing in confidence to a few of his confederates, he has flagged his intention to retire from politics altogether after the next elections.\n\nEvidently the news has spread like wildfire, and has contributed largely to the sense of political instability. Calls for a new constitution and new elections, only subdued by the force of personality of Chen, have intensified. A large portion of the fervour has been contributed by that of the young guard, whom have continously critiqued the state of Federal Democracy for lack of vigour; that their compromises and the incessant gradualism of the senior GRA and CDL leadership merely isn't enough.
