The [FROM.GetAdjective] Rebellion
Following the realisation of the Federalist dream of China and Chen Jiongming's subsequent retirement, the parties in China's new federalist democracy have begun to scramble for power and support across the country. Within regionalist sentiment lies a fear of growing government centralisation (especially following the mandated nationalisation of the military), corruption within the government ranks, and a fear of dominance from Cantonese political cliques. With [FROM.GetLeader]'s supporters willing to back their claims with arms, a civil war is likely underway.\n\nThe Central Government of course is expected to have a major advantage, controlling China's major cities and commanding international legitimacy (at least for now) which as given them greater access to loans as well as a larger tax and manpower base to draft an army. The centralised Federal Army however still holds many disloyal regionalists within its ranks. Furthermore, [FROM.GetAdjective] regionalists are confident in the abilities of their forces and expect that others will rise up against the tyrannical dictatorship of the Central Government. And China is no stranger to upsets. What both sides can agree on, however, is that they will not tolerate another warlord era.
$GXC.endgame.311.d.base$\n\n[FROM.GetLeader] and the [FROM.GetRulingParty] have been loosely tied to the Federalist movement as of late, with independent but seemingly compatible roots. Now, however, with the Central Government attempt to assert its authority over its former allies, it seems that [FROM.GetNameDef] is simply unwilling to accept Federal domination.
$GXC.endgame.311.d.base$\n\nYunnan, the Southwest bastion of Republicanism, has since the Xinhai Revolution emerged as one one of the primary holdouts of political exiles and hopefuls determined to realise the dream of the Chinese Republic. Invoking that the social compact between the provinces in the Constitution has been violated, Yunnan has effectively seceded from the United Provinces of China and has marshalled its military forces in preparation to fight with the Central Army.
$GXC.endgame.311.d.base$\n\nHunan, having long held a resolute and resilient provincial constitution, holds provincial autonomy to the utmost, priding themselves on the borderline Confederate nature of their federalism. Cantonese domination over constitutional drafting and their open ambitions to nationalise provincial militaries has provked them into open rebellion as they desperately seek to fend off [UPC.GetLeader]'s grip before it is too late.

The nationalisation of provincial forces appear to be the final straw for them, seeking to push them into a merely provincial role. Refusing to bow to the central government's directives, [FROM.GetLeader] has chosen to rebel in a bid to redefine Federalism on his own terms.

Chen Jiongming Retires
With the conclusion of the nation's long awaited round of elections, and the final promulgation of the constitutional draft, the dawn has begun to set upon Chen Jiongming's legendary political career. A true son of Haifeng born to a local gentry family, Chen rose to meet every single challenge with determination and resolute belief. Trained to be a scholar, and with a learning as a lawyer, Chen would find himself a revolutionary general, politician, and hero, at least to those individuals not particularly close to Dr. Sun Yat-sen. Even in his twilight years, he continued to work diligently both for the Federalist cause, and that of Chinese democracy.\n\nFinally, it can truly be said that his hard work has paid off. Having completed his life's mission, Chen has begun to make amends with his retirement from the political arena, hoping to pass the reigns onto a new successor to carry the torch of Public Interest. Be it Chen Yansheng, Chen Qiyou, Ye Ju, Xiong Lue, or Ma Yuhang, every figure within the party admonishes the rather large shoes to fill, in the vacancy of the Father of the Nation. Hoping to spend his glory days with his five daughters and eight sons, many believe that the young Chen Dingyan could perhaps carry on the political willpower of the Chen family in the far off future.\n\nNonetheless, the President has one final duty that requires his utmost attention. Pruning himself before the camera, Chen would perform another historic moment in the history of China; the nation's first televised Presidential speech. Albeit the nature of such an event is bitter, with the general notion of an era coming to a close. Uttering poignant formalities about the Federal government, the rule of democracy, and the passing on of a torch to a new generation, Chen would take a brief moment to ponder...\n\nIn the chaotic flurry of thoughts that dance across his mind, one shined bright as day. What note shall his tenure as the people's leader end on? What final message does he want to convey?

Chen Jiongming Passes Away
A ricochet of sorrow has splattered across the nation today. The Father of the Federal Republic, and eternal leader of its politics has ultimately succumbed to the unstoppable tide of time and age — perhaps the only beast that could take down such a titanic figure of Chinese history. Revered across the nation, Chen Jiongming's humble retirement to Haifeng after the conclusion of his monumental tenure of leadership saw little activity in the interim. Mostly content to spend much needed time with his children, with the occasional invitation of friends to his humble abode, Chen remained largely aloof of national politics. Retirement is indeed retirement, and it is very so rarely as savoured for a statesmen that still carries the demonstrable respect of the majority of the nation.\n\nOccupying somewhat of an informal Presidential role despite all of his best wishes to retire, he has largely lived on startling borrowed time. In a moment of erstwhile weakness, it would appear that the great equaliser struck.\n\nComing down with an intense bout of typhoid fever, Chen's brittle body could hardly endure. The First Family of the DSP
At a reception in 1923 held by Beijing cultural circles in preparation for the visit of Tagore, Zhang locked eyes with Wang Shiying, a graduate from the Chinese Department of Beijing Women's Normal College and fell in love at first sight. There was something about her aside from her physical beauty that captivated Zhang. Shiying's intellect and wit, her ability to carry long winded political discussions, and her literary talent in prominent student magazines enamored Zhang who began a constant correspondence with the passionate student thirteen years his junior.\n\nDespite initially being hesitant with Zhang's pursuits, the two continued to grow closer and by 1925, Wang then accepted Zhang's proposal and the two were married. They would go on to have five children, with unfortunately the fifth child dying early in childhood due to sickness. As the now President Zhang spends his days locked in his office and with his cabinet, Wang Shiying has taken the responsibility of rearing the First Family herself.\n\nZhang's brother Gongquan, is a prominent banker fluent in French and the nature of Shanghai politics. Having worked in the banking industry for two decades, Gongquan has been rewarded a post in Zhang's cabinet - dictating the growing industrialisation of the Chinese nation.\n\nZhang's beloved sister, Youyi, has been close to the President since her youth when Junmai promised to take care of her after their mother lamented that no one would marry Youyi for refusing to bind her feet. After a brief stint in teaching German at Suzhou, she took over the management of the Shanghai Women's Commercial Savings Bank and saved it from near bankruptcy. After the restructuring, she opened a Clothing Company and engaged in stock trading in which she has become one of China's most successful businesswomen. Now with her brother as the President of China, she has taken a party post in managing the DSP's finances.

The Work of Huang Qiang
Huang Qiang, also known as Mo Jing, is a renaissance man of sorts - a Swiss Army knife for Chen Jiongming and the Yue Army. A Baoding Army Academy graduate, he participated in the Xinhai Revolution. Afterwards, he studied abroad in Europe before returning home to Guangzhou to serve under Chen Jiongming overseeing Customs.\n\nHe became a confidant of Chen, following him during his tour of the Yangtze valley. Chen selected him as his representative in negotiations including in discussions with Cantonese figures over various crises related to Lu Rongting and Sun Yat-sen. After Chen lost power in 1925, he served with the Kuomintang's National Revolutionary Army during the Northern Expedition as a staff officer in Fujian before rejoining Chen Jiongming as he hastily assembled Cantonese forces to oppose total Guangxi dominance over Liangguang.\n\nDuring the Zhili Decade, Huang worked in Qiongya island and the Leizhou Peninsula as a liaison, aiding in various construction and development projects. He also began his foray as a sociologist, publishing about his survey work in Qiongya. Huang then followed the Guangzhou government as it began its campaign of liberation, serving in various senior roles including stints as an governing executive of various cities and regions, a military commander in the Yue Army, and a representative in the constitutional convention.\n\nA senior advisor to the President but without a large personal following, he has carved out a niche as a sort of neutral figure in intra-Yue Clique politicking. He has sought a more meaningful position than being a glorified Presidential aid however. One proposed project is a nationwide survey of China, though he has also angled for the prestigious position of ambassador to France since he is fluent in the language owing to his childhood education in a French Catholic School and university education in Lyon. Both would remove him from [GXC.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] and leave the President shorthanded however.

Winds of Compromise
In the aftermath of Xinhai, the burgeoning republic was budding with a wave of idealistic enthusiasm. However, the first election would not be a triumph of liberty and progress, but a mockery of freedom when President Yuan Shikai assassinated the electoral winner, Song Jiaoren. This bloody affair determined how all of China's future transitions of power would occur from there on out, and the republic fell into chaos. However, the new republic has restored some level of idealism across the populace. The first election saw the party of the democratic revolution, the PIP, rewarded by the population.\n\nHowever, when their largest rival the DSP won the next elections, the Old Federalists stepped aside in favour of the DSP without a drop of blood. This peaceful transition of power has laid a promising start for the new republic, though the future is far from secure. Many still fear that either of the two large parties will eventually seize power and establish one party rule, and others are unhappy with the de facto two party system which has been entrenched. Despite this, many are still hopeful that things will still continue to improve, and the current level of democracy is already seen as an achievement.\n\nThe DSP, under the leadership of Zhang Junmai and Zhang Dongsun, have brought with them their campaign promises and goals to the halls of power. While the CDL is a coalition of democratic parties sharing certain goals, it is the Democratic Socialist Party which leads the flock, a fact well known to President Zhang. Zhang Junmai sets out to establish a social democratic state in the name of Liang Qichao and the image of the SPD. Zhang Dongsun hopes to establish his guild socialist utopian vision for China, and form the bourgeois gentleman class that will serve as China's political elite and stepping stone to a reformist socialist society. The Zhangs carry the expectations of their supporters and the responsibility to shepard the nation, and time will see if they will live up to their promises.

In the aftermath of Xinhai, the burgeoning republic was budding with a wave of idealistic enthusiasm. The Party of Washington
In many ways, the Chinese Federalist movement has long sought to emulate what the adventurous forbearers of the American Revolution advanced. Considered the primary component of the larger national army, they bore the brunt of the fighting and strategic decision making during the Unification Wars, and the Second Sino-Japanese War. With untold devastation wrought upon the nation, these brave fighters stood defiant, resisting the enemy, and protecting their homeland. Now as a young peace begins to settle across China, the Yue Army remains in wait, as the whole nation looks onwards to the constitutional convention held in [GXC.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName].\n\nCirculating around the capital, there has been a gathering of delegates from all composite parties of the Grand Republican Alliance — namely constitutional scholars like Huang Yanpei, or Zhang Shizhao — alongside members from the China Democratic League — with notable attendants being Zhang Junmai, Zhang Lan and Shen Junru — and assorted Independents from the Constitutionalists, or the likes of Hu Shih. Fading Light
As the nation recounts its losses, and works ever gradually towards the implementation of national Federalism, one would think that the President would bask in the glory, lead the nation onwards through this time of reconstruction. And yet, the image cast by Chen Jiongming is far from that of a victorious wartime hero. Present at many of the National Constitutional Convention meetings, many of those attending have noted Chen's extensive reliance upon his aides and close allies, alongside his rather frail and pale appearance. Far from resembling the ambitious and young utopian at the beginning of the Warlord Era, time has not been particularly kind to the Federalist leader.\n\nOf course, the President's confidants understand his gradual deterioration. Overworking, stress, a brief stint of exile, it has all compounded into his own morbid realisation: he is living on borrowed time. And yet there is so much work to do! The nation has hardly seen the implementation of the Federalist Project, let alone that of multi-partisan democracy! If he were to pass into the night without so much as fulfilling any of his goals, what legacy was he to leave? Father of the Nation yet ultimately an abject failure?\n\nHis opponents, perhaps sensing weakness or opportunity, have begun to pounce. Pointing out the hypocrisy in striving for a free and fair democracy, yet relying extensively upon the Yue Clique. They have begun to suggest Chen has merely replaced the Zhili with the Yue; ruling as Cao Kun did before him. However, belittling comments and snide remarks can hardly diminish a man of his stature. Undoubtedly, he still has a job to do. Democracy must come. If not the perfect ideal? Well at least something close enough.
The clock is ticking...

An Urgent Appeal
Echoing all throughout the masses ears and the halls of the intellectuals and politicians has been the China Democratic League's most recent political salvo. An array of shells has made headlines in news articles, and has rapidly disseminated itself all throughout political life in the nation. Seeking freedom under the guarantee of civil liberties, and the freedoms of speech, press, assembly, organisation, travel, residence, and selection of vocation, it has largely been a statement of a general call for change.\n\nThe abolition of dominant party control, abolition of wartime measures and all regulations on speech and publication, a commission of inter-party cooperation to control the size of the military, a commission to adjust personnel in accordance with local conditions. And even a call for local Self-Government from the Province downwards to be strengthened, so as to achieve a balance between the Central and Local authorities, have all been found within the text.\n\nMost importantly however, has been the rather flagrant demand for an enforced coalition government with the CDL, which should run the country and supervise new elections for the National Assembly, and ultimately serve as a transitory regime leading to the inauguration of a fully representative constitutional government, albeit it remains aloof of the complexity of working out what such a coalition should constitute.\n\nServing largely as an olive branch to the Yue Clique, a positive government response could very well be the last chance for Chen Jiongming to embrace or reject the CDL's domineering position, albeit concerns have highlighted themselves within his inner circle. Public intellectual anger has been growing as of recent, and not a lot of people are willing to believe in either the GRA or the CDL. An even larger chunk of the masses are more akin to cattle, tired and ignorant. The Nanning Incident
Tragedy has struck the Federal Republic! Whilst on his way to Nanning, Chen naturally cleared all checkpoints, but upon actually setting foot into the city, he and all of his entourage were seized by local officers. Ranging from lieutenants to even generals, the rebels managed to capture President and his inner-circle with minimal casualties, as most of the demoralised Yue Army surrendered after the first few shots. Placing immediate demands upon the President then and there, they mandated the firing of Ye Ju, Ma Yuhang, Zhong Xiunan, Chen Lianbo, and plenty others all of whom they blame for corruption in the government and military.\n\nMoving the President and his entourage to a secure location the coup has sent [UPC.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] into utter abject chaos! Lin Hu and Liu Zhenhuan for their part have announced their complete opposition to the plot, insisting that the plotters responsible are acting against their orders, albeit such claims are surely hard to believe.\n\nIn the capital, aides dash in-and-out of Marshal Ye Ju's office. Growing increasingly erratic as of the disclosure of the Incident, he has begun to seclude civilian Yue figures from deciding upon the government's response. Bordering on civil war, the Marshal has made orders to various Yue army units to swiftly march on Nanning whilst simultaneously dispatching army units to the CDL's headquarters in the capital with intent of seizing Zhang Junmai on charges of conspiracy and treason.\n\nYet they arrived too late to capture the troubadour. Fleeing the capital beforehand, he appears to have covered his tracks; the CDL having recently release a statement demanding a peaceful resolution. Remembering Our Founder
With a heavy heart, the nation has entered into a state of national mourning. With Chen Jiongming's body being initially covered with the flag of the Five Races, a symbolic gesture to showcase his loyalty to the Republic, the Chen family received encouragement to have their own private grieving period. After eight days of personal mourning from immediate friends and family, a state funeral procession was announced nation-wide, to be presided over by the President.\n\nCrowds gathered all throughout the nation as Chen's procession travelled through Nanjing, Guangzhou, Wuhan, and Beijing, before finally returning and being laid to rest on Ziwei Hill in Huizhou, the city that saw the beginning and the end of his lifelong struggle for a democratic republic. Collaborating with various associates, a Memorial Record of the President was produced.\n\nReceiving eleven eulogistic essays, and over 700 eulogistic poems, it has received contributions from a slew of figures. Close comrades such as Zhang Lucun, Chen Yansheng, Ye Ju, Chen Qiyou, and others. Some were from public figures such as Wu Xianzi, Wu Zhihui, Wu Peifu, Huang Fu, Hu Shih, and surprisingly contributions from Shen Junru, Zhang Lan, Lin Hu, Zhang Dongsun, and even Zhang Junmai were collected.\n\nSurvived by his eight children, Chen's movement is practically leaderless. For the Haifeng Clique, it is particularly devastating as they have all known him since the start of their journey.\n\nChen Qiyou and Chen Yansheng, leaders of the liberal side of his movement, want his legacy to be that of a progressive leader. Zhong Xiunan, Zhou Xingnan, and Zhong Jingtang for their parts, have pressed to exhibit his sense of reason, and morality.

National Military Woes
With the recent completion of Chinese unification, [ROOT.GetLeader] has gone to work in establishing a new centralised military. For the first time, an armed forces loyal to solely the Republic of China has truly been created and already eager bureaucrats within the military are at work in creating specialised branches such as a National Air Corps or in regards to the integration of military academies and arsenals. The new National Government desires a total unification of armed forces in terms of curriculum, traning, and doctrine from Shenyang to Kunming.\n\nAs such, local provincial and regional warlords have certainly found the new reforms to be challenging to say the least. Long since the collapse of central government authority following the Xinhai Revolution have local military leaders held on to regional blocs of power. Now, faced, with the reality of national unification - these warlords have filed serious complaints against the new central government. In more extreme cases, some warlords have even proclaimed the secession of their province and counties; refusing to adopt the new central government's military reforms.\n\nFurthermore, during the wars of unification and the subsequent war with the Empire of Japan; the Chinese army grew to extraordinary heights. Chronic shortages of well-equipped and well-trained soldiers led to the conscription of tens of thousands of men. Now with war finally over in China - the new central government is tasked with reducing the size and expenses of a burdensome armed forces.
Rebuilding will take some time.
The Struggling National Economy
Since the declining fortunes of the Qing Dynasty, the economy of China has been in steep decline - with conditions exacerbated by the growing imperialist stranglehold of foreign trade dominance. While Western countries had initially sought China for its lucrative markets and resources; overtime increasing foreign trade domination through the unequal treaties led to a staggering national economy.\n\nFollowing Black Monday and the subsequent worldwide economic depression; the Chinese economy which had been so intrinsically tied to the German East Asian market - also faced greater collapse. As overspeculation and banks ran in the Legation Cities, the Chinese national economy only worsened and production levels have only begun to return to pre-depression levels. Local warlord regional economies focused primarily on fostering local industries with some warlords even openly selling opium as a means of generating revenue to fund their private militaries.\n\nYears of devastation from civil war and the war with Japan has left the economy in further tatters. Hyperinfliation and a national deficit has led to the weakness of the national currency especially as [ROOT.GetNameDef]'s government now is the national government. Large sectors of the country's agricultural and industrial sector lay in ruins and an arduous reconstruction process is needed in order to help China catch up wit the rest of the world. Banditry continues to remain a large problem especially within the rural countryside as the lack of government police infrastructure is not strong enough to combat well organised groups. Continuous issues of land reform and economic integration of the many provinces have consistently been pressed; with rural advocates and communities pressing for immediate relief and compromise with the recent unification.
Just make the stocks go up!
A Developing Democracy?
Many revolutionaries envisioned the modernisation of China to inevitably include the development of China from a monarchy to a republican or democratic government of some form. Revolutionaries looked to natural law, the writings of Locke and Montesquieu, and western countries - that had adopted democratic principles and experienced serious growth. To many, democracy is a necessary and inevitable byproduct of the Chinese revolution.\n\nThe past few decades in China have challenged this notion and world view. The subsequent return of the monarchy under Yuan Shikai was an utter mockery of the revolution that the martyrs of 1911 had fought for. While many governments openly claimed to be democratic or priortise the entry of democracy into China; it became clear to many that even the most revolutionary of the warlord era leaders such as Sun Yat-sen; gravitated closely towards authoritarianism.\n\nThe recent unification of the Republic of China under [ROOT.GetLeader] has resurrected national fears and doubts of the course of democracy. A lot of new government jobs in the growing national bureaucracy are staffed with loyalists under [ROOT.GetLeader] or are former Cantonese politicians/bureaucrats. At a local level, corruption continues to reign supreme with little central government oversight - chiefs and mayors are effectively running their own private fiefdoms undisturbed even with the unification of the country. Nepotism reigns supreme especially in regards to coveted military and political positions and crime continues to grow due to a lack of government infrastructure. Serious government reform is therefore needed to address the new national government and to strengthen local governments in order to better prepare them for democracy.
Maybe a dictatorship would be better after all...
The NPA Dissolves
The National Protection Alliance is victorious, having successfully marched from Guangzhou to Beijing and toppled all their rivals before them. They have far exceeded the performance of its original iteration, which brought down Yuan Shikai's attempt at crowning himself Emperor but ultimately fell to infighting and failed to destroy Yuan's Beiyang Army in battle.\n\nThe victory of the NPA has catapulted [GXC.GetLeader] to great fame across the nation, allowing him to declare himself President of a reunited Republic. But though he is at the height of his power, the warlord is also experiencing a uniquely vulnerable moment shortly after his victory. Unification brings great expectations faster than it provides tools to handle them, and [GXC.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] is facing a raft of economic, military, and political challenges that leaves them open to a challenger.\n\nIn the long term, the provinces can expect centralisation and integration into the new order. Whatever allies they made along the way to unification are no longer needed; their uncontested control over the central army and government bureaucracy allows them to rule without them. This leaves both sides in a perilous position as both wait to see who will betray the other.
$GXC.endgame.610.d.intro$\n\nFor Long Yun, unification brings conflicting feelings. It surely satisfies the nationalism within Yunnan but there are lingering concerns that sooner or later the central government will begin to infringe on Kunming's autonomy. To strike now would be out of character for him, but to simply roll over and surrender would be a bitter pill as well.
$GXC.endgame.610.d.intro$\n\nFor Hu Ruoyu, unification brings conflicting feelings. It surely satisfies the nationalism within Yunnan but there are lingering concerns that sooner or later the central government will begin to infringe on Kunming's autonomy. Hu has worked for decades to finally take power - to leave his fate in the hands of a distant government is dreadful, but even with German aid and Zhili connections he has no chance...or does he?
$GXC.endgame.610.d.intro$\n\nFor Yan Xishan, this is a moment he has been waiting for for some time now. His rivals in the north now quashed, he can capitalise on Beiyang fears of southern domination to advance his own goals of national leadership. This may finally be his long-awaited chance to announce his own ambitions of national leadership.
$GXC.endgame.610.d.intro$\n\nNo doubt our odds are long, but they will not grow any shorter by waiting. If we have any doubts about [GXC.GetLeader]'s leadership, now is the time to strike.
This is our moment, and we will take it!

An Alliance in Tatters
The unification of China was a joyous moment, but behind closed doors it was also a treacherous one. Victory pushed [GXC.GetLeader] directly into the national spotlight, something that was bound to draw the jealousy and ire of his erstwhile allies. It has also thrust onto him tremendous expectations, which his government has scrambled to meet as they sort through various military, economic, and political challenges.\n\nFearing the inevitable centralisation of the country, [FROM.GetLeader] and his [FROM.GetName] have refused to go from allies to subject. Denouncing the President they had helped install not a year ago, they have announced their desire to fight a new war to protect the nation. It would seem like a long shot, but they are striking in a uniquely vulnerable moment for [GXC.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] - a troubled government already beset by post-unification difficulties.
$GXC.endgame.611.d.intro$\n\nFor Long Yun, this is a much bolder play than anyone would have expected. Known for his insular, almost isolationist rule, striking outside of his traditional territory in the southwest is a risky gamble. But it goes to show how little faith he has in [GXC.GetLeader]'s rule.
$GXC.endgame.611.d.intro$\n\nFor Hu Ruoyu, this is a bold play. He has spent years clawing his way into power in Yunnan, something he may easily lose in such a desperate gamble. But perhaps the fact he took such a risk is an indication of how little trust exists between warlords.
$GXC.endgame.611.d.intro$\n\nFor Yan Xishan, this is a reflection of his sheer ambition. Now it is only a matter of whether or not he can back his ambitions with battlefield victories...
$GXC.endgame.611.d.intro$\n\nIt is unclear if any other provinces will rebel at this time, but for now at least initiative is in the hands of [FROM.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName]. How long that momentum will last is another question.
There is but one fate for rebels!
There can only be one master of China.
To Rule a Nation
With his victory in China's wars of unification, President Lu Yuguang's position in China's new order has been sealed in blood. He has dispatched the last of his enemies in battle, driving out rival contenders for power and putting down any rebels that dared to challenge him. And with that, he has become the face of national unification - there remain dissenters but uncontested power has this tendency to smoothen out the rougher edges of a leader's image.\n\nWith that power comes the need to demonstrate leadership for the long fragmented nation. The President needs to set a long term direction for his regime, and it begins with his selection of Prime Minister. With confirmation at this point merely a formality, many would expect General Ma Ji to be the natural choice: an experienced general with Zhili connections and the pillar of the Young Guangxi Faction. Selecting him would indicate the desire of the Guangxi Warlords to replicate the Zhili system, supplanting Cao Kun and Wu Peifu with a new strongman junta that the rest of the republic will orbit around. But despite their ostensibly close alliance, Lu may have some reservations as well - Ma may be powerful enough to eclipse him already and many see him as an old guard choice.\n\nThere are many who have called for change in the name of the \

The Guangzhou Conservatory of Music
After the dissolution of imperial China in 1911, the Chinese people felt the imminent need to strengthen and revitalise traditional Chinese culture. Musicians began to organise large ensembles of traditional Chinese instruments such as the bowed string “euhu”, the plucked string “zheng” and “pipa”, blown wind instruments such as the “dizi”, and many others. At the same time however, they also brought in western instruments and began organising sections akin to their European orchestral equivalents.\n\nXian Xianghai is one of these new innovative composers hailing from Portuguese Macau to Tanka parents from Guangdong. As his father died early in his childhood, Xian followed his mother around and eventually immigrated to Singapore where he enrolled in Yangzheng Primary School which was his first foray into musical education. He was later brought to Guangzhou for future education where he began learning clarinet in addition to classical music theory. After studying for two years in Singapore from 1924-1926, Xian went to the National Institute of Music at Peking University and then the National Shanghai Conservatory of Music to study violin and piano.\n\nXiang has become quick friends with another Cantonese musician; Ma Sicong. The son of Ma Yuhang (the confidant of Chen Jiongming), Ma Sicong picked up both the piano and the yueqin in his youth. After falling in love with the violin, Ma Sicong traveled to France where he studied at the Conservatoire de Paris. There, he met Xian Xianghai and along with his colleague Chen Hong, established the Guangzhou Conservatory of Music; Guangzhou's first private music school. Priding itself as the first in Southern China to train and educate at the highest level both Chinese and Western music; composers have particularly focused on nationalist and cultural themes - emphasising China's rich historical traditions with the elegance of modernity.
The Chinese cultural scene grows!
The Marshal and the FCDP
When the National Protection Alliance was founded, it brought together warlords and intellectuals alike to resist the tyranny of Yuan Shikai. Lu Rongting built his cult of personality around being a man of the people, casting himself as a Robin Hood-esque figure leading a revolt against a wicked monarch. Though plenty saw him instead as a brutal warlord, his legacy has been greatly improved by the success of his son who hopes to capture that same Xinhai Spirit.\n\nLu Yuguang faces a much different republic than his father did. The Xinhai Revolution was growing cynical even in its first decade. An entire generation has since suffered war and authoritarianism under multiple regimes. The Republic has reunited, first under the Zhili and now under the Young Guangxi Faction. But the unification of the nation has also brought the unification of the opposition.\n\nThe Federation of Chinese Democratic Parties has advocated for a parliamentary form of government, arguing for the limitations of executive power and the creation of a coalition government representing all parties. It has also demanded the legalisation of all political parties, the guarantee of civil liberties, the abolishment of the secret police and penal labour camps, transparency in government, and fairness in hiring. The Life of Jiang Kongyin
As its role as the premier port on the Pearl River and thus one of the earliest openings connecting the West to the Orient, Due to its proximity with the British trade port and colony of Hong Kong (with mainland China connected to the New Territories and Kowloon) - Guangzhou's gentry and merchant class grew rich. While ironically placed in the traditionally lower echelons of Chinese society; the increasingly globalised and an industrial world birthed a rich gentry that profited off shipments of tea, opium, and other riches from the East.\n\nAmong one of these Cantonese businessmen came none other than Jiang Kongyin. Born in 1864, Jiang saw an early political rise under Kang Youwei. After passing his imperial examinations, he was appointed to a position of management for rural relief; where he worked to suppress bandits. During his time in government management, he also held sympathies for the revolutionary movement, he improved local fruit varieties, built a small gauge railway from Nangang District to Luogangwei, and began several weather conservancy projects.\n\nDespite his imperial alignment, he has also demonstrated sympathies with Cantonese revolutionaries - working with Wang Jingwei, Chen Jinghua, and meeting with Sun Yat-sen following the failure of the Second Guangzhou Expedition. When confronted with the reality of the Xinhai Revolution and end of the Qing monarchy, Jiang helped convince Liangguang's Governor-General and the Admiral of the Navy to express their support for the revolutionary movement and secede from the Qing.\n\nJiang is also famous for his gourmet foods known as the Taishi Recipes. Leading military and civilian elites in Guangzhou would come to his mansion to try them out, of which the Taishi Five Snake Soup is the most famous. Now in his old age having witnessed the late Qing Dynasty, the Early Republic, the Warlords of Guangdong, and now national unification - Jiang spends his days in retirement either cooking or performing calligraphy.
An auspicious statesman!
Inspiring the Chinese Democracy
Circulating in the higher halls of the government of President [ROOT.GetLeader] is this constant, consistent rumour of compradorship. Of ties to the British, or tangentially to the Japanese, or the Germans. And it is true, that President [ROOT.GetLeader] fondly looks towards the industrialised nations, but not to sell out China. But instead, as a model of state governance to emulate. Bismarckian Germany, Meiji Japan, the rule of the Whigs in Britain, or in the United States. All states, and all parties, have had their fair share of success stories, and of troubling controversies, but all have been the stewards of Great Powers, able to exert themselves with confidence in economic and military affairs. And it is to these states and parties, that factions have emerged within the Assembly, Senate, and even Cabinet, of the direction, or the model, upon which the President and his loyal men should follow.\n\nOn one side, an increasingly developing group of officials, technocrats, and those with links to the industrial lobby, who seek to emulate the success of their neighbour in East Asia, that of Japan. With abounding industrialisastion, an Empire of their own, and a brave attempt at the democracy, many sincerely believe that Japan is the most comparable and suitable example to inspire China as an amiable opportunity for state development.\n\nOn the opposing end however, are those that believe to hold themselves to an even greater democratic ideal. Proponents of national harmony, egalitarianism, and liberal constitutionalism, they have joined together to advocate for policies similar to that of The Whigs and Tories in Britain, now the Liberals and Conservatives. Music and Culture in Guangxi
Guangxi is a melting pot of ethnic groups and cultures. The victory of the old Guangxi Clique has made the province an object of fascination by Chinese intellectuals, many of whom previously dismissed it as a backwater, who seek to explore reasons why they came out on top. While plenty have focused on the militaristic aspects of Guangxi society, there has also been a greater appreciation (and government support for) its people.\n\nThe Double Third Festival (Sanyuesan) is sometimes referred to as the Washing Festival and it is a nation-wide holiday celebrated in April where people honor their ancestors at the riverside and then bathe to clean their bodies of filth before enjoying the day with picnics. The festival is particularly prominent among the Zhuang, who celebrate with songs, dance, games, and food. While some cultural scholars have recently sought to use it as a means of distinguishing Zhuang culture, for the most part it has become a tourism draw.\n\nTourists in Guangxi often go to the old city of Guilin known for its excellent view of the Li River and the Jingjiang Prince's city. Guangxi cuisine is influenced by the lighter Cantonese cuisine and the spicy and sour Guizhou cuisines, often mixing glutinous rice with hot and sour condiments. The different regions of Guangxi often have their own unique styles of flavoring as well.\n\nA Guangxi Federation of Literary and Art Circles has also been established in Nanning among cultural workers, collecting folk music and promoting the humanities. Though comparatively obscure, Guangxi music can sometimes include distinct elements. The Jing are known for a stringed instrument known as the Duxianqin and the Zhuang are known for their Bayin instrument ensembles. Their most famous folk singer is Liu Sanjie, a 1100s Zhuang musician whose work is being rediscovered. Guangxi is also home to Guiju, similar to Beijing Opera but sung in the Guilin dialect.
A lovely province and wonderful people.
A World Turned Upside Down
It was not too long ago that the Zhili were in their prime - having defeated the Kuomintang with German support, they then routed the Fengtian Clique and their allies in the ensuing conflicts to claim Beijing and total leadership of China. In that era, the battered Old Guangxi Clique and their bickering generals were forced to play an auxiliary role, hired henchmen for the Zhili to subjugate the south.\n\nThe Zhili then of course ruled for a decade, entrenching themselves in the bureaucracy and army, forming the Harmony Association to herd politicians under their umbrella, making exploitative deals with the Germans, fighting amongst themselves for influence, and ultimately growing decadent and arrogant as their hired warlords grew stronger and more discontent until finally their house of cards collapsed.\n\nNow the Old Guangxi Clique are the ascendent power and the ex-Zhili are the ones desperately seeking to claw whatever influence they can muster. The Shen Army Faction was always an intermediary of sorts between the two, a splinter from the broader Old Guangxi Clique that received extensive Zhili patronage after the Northern Expedition.\n\nOf course it would not be too difficult to share power right now, with the ex-Zhili primarily concentrated in the North and East while the Guangxi Clique and allies primarily are a southern force. Withdrawing our military garrisons and allowing for civilian rule would likely stabilise the country. But one never knows when the next turn of fortune may occur, or what they are scheming.

The Sword of Progress
Perhaps nothing detested more in Chinese Society is the militant and self-interested warlord. To many, the warlord is but the bandit with the biggest stick, with only self aggrandisement and plundering as hobbies. Yet, certain warlords have been known to the Chinese people not as bandits, but as heroes.\n\nCai E will forever be remembered as the man who stood up to Yuan Shikai's autocratic empire, and defended the republic against overwhelming odds. Sun Chuanfang, the master of Eastern China for nearly a decade, will be remembered in Jiangsu as a man of the peasantry. Wu Peifu, his association with the failing Beijing regime notwithstanding, will be remembered as a pillar of anti-japanese resistance in the nation during its most trying years. Lu Yuguang, the Young Marshal of the South and heir to Lu Rongting of the Green Forest, hopes to rule the ailing nation not as an autocratic bandit and leave China worse than when he found it, but as a progressive-minded leader of Southern sensibilities.\n\nSuch an administration will certainly shake up the politics of the nation. China has long been ruled by Confucian morality, first under the Sons of Heaven and then by the traditionalist Beiyang militarists. Yet here stands Lu Yuguang, the first ruler of all China to hail from Southern China since the last of the Ming Emperors.\n\nWhile none of sound mind expect him to rule as a radical firebrand in the image of Sun Yat-sen's band of socialist revolutionaries, perhaps Lu will carve a path not too dissimilar from the spirit of Li Yuanhong's Progressive Party, or Liang's Research and Constitutionalist Parties. The young son of Lu Rongting has surrounded himself with progressive intellectuals, all hoping that the Young Marshal will lend his ear to their idealistic ideas. Of course, the Young Marshal knows better than to heed the advice of dreamers too closely, but perhaps the Chinese Republic will finally know a ruler who may alleviate the suffering of the people.
Three cheers for Lu Yuguang, man of the hour!
Zhili's Natural Successors
The Republic of China throughout its few decades of existence has gone through great change. In the high period of the Warlord Era, it seemed that every year harkened a new militarist regime sitting in the halls of Zhongnanhai in Beijing. The end of the warlord era ushered in a restoration of the Qing monarchy, but after a decade of laboured existence, it fell to the wayside, alongside the Zhili Clique.\n\nNow for the first time since the Great Ming, the Central Kingdom has been unified from the South. Perhaps though, this new Republic will follow the legacy of the Zhili Government of the 20s and 30s. Ma Ji and Lu Yuguang share power as the Jade Marshal and King of Baoding had, and together guide the National Assembly in leading China out of the turmoil of the earlier 20th Century and into an age of peace and prosperity. Certain democratic processes have been restored, but greater pluralism continues to elude the new government.\n\nHowever, the military continues to retain significant power behind the scenes, with the officers of the Guangxi Clique entrenching themselves with positions of great power in the Republic, likely to form the core nucleus of a new elite for generations to come. This has left many disenfranchised former military men of losing cliques and factions grumbling at the current state of affairs, but unable to bring about political change in their favour due to the heightened state of stability the nation finds itself in.\n\nThe Guangxi Clique comes to power at a critical point in the history of China, with the Empire of Japan's efforts to subjugate the nation thwarted and the end of the Century of Humiliation finally upon its doorsteps. Undoubtedly, the rule of Lu Yuguang and Ma Ji will shape the direction of the Republic for decades, if not centuries, and history will judge if the adopted sons of a bandit rule well.
The sons of Guangxi chart us to tomorrow through the lessons of yesterday!

However, as times have evolved, so too have the forms upon which the wealthy exhert their political influence and weaponise their monetary funds. Becoming politicians, or funding their campaigns, clever donations, brazen corruption, all manners of less than honourable pursuits of power. From a Revolutionary to a Statesman
As far as Chinese revolutionaries fare, Liu Zhenhuan's career may perhaps be the most remarkable. Joining the Tongmenghui in 1907, he would incorporate individuals like Shen Hongying into the Guangxi Uprising. Through many years of long, arduous, and deadly fighting, Liu's revolutionary activities displayed a genuine sense of conviction and belief in the ideals of the Tongmenghui and Kuomintang. Young and vigorous, he was a reliable soldier of the revolution, organising soldiers in Guangxi on numerous occasions, working to further the revolution in South East Asia, and even joining the Old Guangxi Clique, betraying them for Chen Jiongming and the Guangdong Army. However, transforming into what was in-effect a warlord during the 1920s, the once optimistic revolutionary would become a man marred in conflict.\n\nFlagrantly disobeying orders from Dr. Sun Yat-sen, Liu's Guangxi Army would become that of a liability, only taking action in-line with the Kuomintang if it was to Liu's own benefit. Hitching himself to the Political Science Clique, Liu would cooperate with Yang Ximin to defend their interests. However, after having disagreements with the Reorganisation of 1924, and with ambitions to take over Guangxi, Liu would attempt an ousting of the Guangdong government, ultimately being cast into exile in Hong Kong. Liu's return to the Liangguang political scene would coincide with Political Science's own return to prominence.\n\nElected President at the head of a cross-party coalition to prevent Merchant dictatorship, Liu's glories as a war hero, alongside his easygoing relations with Progressive democrats and other Warlords, managed to see him secure the Presidential Office. The Reich of the East
For Millennia, China stood unrivaled as the world's greatest empire. Thus, the Qing Emperors did not meet the rise of the European Colonial powers with the seriousness owed, and suffered the consequences of arrogance. The Century of Humiliation taught the Chinese nation that if China was to become great again, it must learn from the lessons of the West, and adopt its strengths while avoiding its weaknesses. While some viewed Britain or the United States as the pillars of the West that China ought to learn from, the British Revolution and Second American Civil War showed their inherent weaknesses.\n\nLi Linsi, founder of the Jingxing Society, toured Europe to promote Chinese culture in the West and study European developments, and found Germany to be the true beating heart of the Continent. While Britain and France degenerated into fanatic workers' states, it was Germany who continued to develop a stable and rational government that brought prosperity to its people. After Germany intervened in China to prevent the spread of Chinese Bolshevism, Jingxing continued to encourage further ties between Berlin and Beijing. Jingxing member Zhu Jiahua found himself in Guangdong in the early 30s, and through circumstance and luck, was able to secure the Presidency of the Republic in the midst of General Shen's passing.\n\nWith China consolidated for the first time in decades, it falls to Zhu to restore order across the Middle Kingdom and determine the outlook of the Republic. A staunch germanophile, Zhu looks to utilise the German connection China has been afforded with, and restore China's greatness on the world stage. Bismarckian State socialism, long supported by China's elite, will become the core economic thought of Zhu's China. German democracy, the proven superior to Anglo democracy, shall ensure that the Chinese people enjoy proper pluralism and liberty. The Dragon and the Eagle shall fly as one, until the Dragon soars above all once again.
President Zhu knows success when he sees it!
A Republic Helmed by Iron Will
The Zhili Clique, the natural governing force across China, continues to lead the charge in restoring our humbled nation back into the isles of greatness. Although the Beijing Government was not to last forever, Cao Kun, Wu Peifu, and Sun Chuanfang must be applauded for restoring stability across the nation after the sheer chaos of the High Warlord Era. Yet even though the mighty Jade and League Marshals were unable to stem the chaos of the late 30s, Marshal Shen Hongying was able to follow their pristine example and restore order across the nation.\n\nMarshal Shen gave his life for the Republic's revitalisation, and all the nation honours his sacrifice. As Marshal Shen followed the footsteps of Wu and Sun, so too shall Marshal Li Yaohan. An elderly man like Shen and Wu, Marshal Li is not expected to rule over China for long. However, Marshal Li's commitment to order and progress is exceeded by none, and he shall ensure that China's governance will be put on the right track before his time on this world comes to an end. The National Assembly carries out legislation for the economic and social reconstruction of the nation, and the military continues to restore order across the land.\n\nMarshal Li in his seven decades of life has seen what has failed and what has worked, and will undoubtedly guide the Assembly with his wisdom. The Li Yaohan administration has stated a commitment to continuing the prosperity and developmentalism of the old Zhili government of Cao, and with Li's forces consolidating control over all China, few doubt his ability to enact his ambition. Many recognise that the era of selfishness and opportunism has come to an end, and the elderly marshal shall guide all into a burgeoning Chinese Century.
He who governs China must have that iron in him!
The White Sun in Kuala Lumpur
During the last decades of the Qing Dynasty, many Chinese revolutionaries found themselves fleeing to Southeast Asia, where many overseas Chinese communities had formed, sympathetic to the republican cause. Standing out above the rest, Malaya became the hotbed of Chinese revolution, with future Kuomintang leader Wang Jingwei meeting his wife, the Malayan born Chinese Chen Bijun, in Singapore.\n\nMany prominent Malayan Chinese businessmen offered financial support to the burgeoning Tongmenghui, and rejoiced for their mainlander comrades following the success of the Xinhai Revolution. However, while China freed itself from the shackles of the decrepit Qing, Malayan Chinese continued to toil under British colonial rule. While many were hopeful for a free Malaya following the collapse of the British Empire, it would not be the Malayans who received the fruits of Britain's fall, but the ascendant German Empire.\n\nFortunately, when hostilities in Europe flared up once more, Germany found itself unable to hold its Asian Empire and was driven out for good. After decades of British and German colonial rule, Malaya finally finds itself free from imperialism, liberated by the might of the National Revolutionary Army and the indomitable spirit of the Malayan people.\n\nChairman [ROOT.GetLeader] has in particular noticed the contributions of Zhuang Mingli, chairman of the General Labour Union of Malaya. Born in Fujian, Zhuang grew up in Penang and became involved with the Chinese revolutionary organisations. Greatly sympathetic to the Reorganisation Comrades Association, Zhuang has been entrusted to lead the fledgling Malayan government. While certainly young, the Malayan people are confident that he will guide the nation into a new age.

A seasoned revolutionary with wildcard revolutionary protégés, Lai knows the blood price paid for a free Malaya, and will not squander the young nation's security.
During the last decades of the Qing Dynasty, many Chinese revolutionaries found themselves fleeing to Southeast Asia, where many overseas Chinese communities had formed, sympathetic to the republican cause. Standing out above the rest, Malaya became the hotbed of Chinese revolution, with future Kuomintang leader Wang Jingwei meeting his wife, the Malayan born Chinese Chen Bijun, in Singapore.\n\nMany prominent Malayan Chinese businessmen offered financial support to the burgeoning Tongmenghui, and rejoiced for their mainlander comrades following the success of the Xinhai Revolution. However, while China freed itself from the shackles of the decrepit Qing, Malayan Chinese continued to toil under British colonial rule. While many were hopeful for a free Malaya following the collapse of the British Empire, it would not be the Malayans who received the fruits of Britain's fall, but the ascendant German Empire.\n\nFortunately, when hostilities in Europe flared up once more, Germany found itself unable to hold its Asian Empire and was driven out for good. After decades of British and German colonial rule, Malaya finally finds itself free from imperialism, liberated by the might of the National Revolutionary Army and the indomitable spirit of the Malayan people.\n\nChairman [ROOT.GetLeader] and the Party have in particular recognised the contributions of Leong Yew Koh. Known in the mainland as Liang Yugao, Leong joined the Tongmenghui at the age of 16 and during Japan's invasion led the Overseas Chinese Anti-Japanese Army in guerilla warfare. With Chinese control over Malaya consolidated, Leong has formed a KMT sister party in the Malayan Chinese Association, and stands ready to guide the nation into a new age of prosperity and ethnic equality.
Malaya helped free China from tyranny, now it's time to return to favour!

§YWe will gain a unique dynamic modifier that will be strengthened by taking focuses throughout the political tree in order to help facilitate a truly modern, united, and strong Chinese Republic.§!
Unification and Reconstruction
There can be no unification of the nation without reconstruction, and no reconstruction without unification of the nation! These are the principles by which the China Democratic League holds the whole nation accountable towards, and it is under these principles by which the new pseudo-military pseudo-civilian government of Li Jishen functions under. Under this policy plan, the nation must work towards the fulfillment of democracy and civil rights, independence and equality of the nation on the international stage, unification under the principles of self-governance, development of production and improvement of the lives of the peasants and workers, advancement of China's national culture and development of its scientific spirit, and above all else, a focus on peaceful reconstruction in national progress.\n\nThrough the affirmed recognition of basic political freedoms and human rights in conjunction with rulership by those with legitimate social positions — producers, educators, and servicemen — the nation shall advance beyond partisan politics. All under the expedient and diligent President-Marshal, the nation shall be ruled not by warlords nor egoism, and it shall not fall into the trappings of a Party State nor dictatorship; so that in the future, however far, the nation may yet experience democracy.
§YWaning enthusiasm in the Federalist Dream reduces the strength in integrity of the state! You will lose stability proportional to how much §cSocial Conservative§!, §nNational Populist§!, §PPaternal Autocrat§! and §lSocial Liberal§! influence you had.§!
§LIn order to develop China's own nascent economic strength various regions of the country will be marked out as Special Economic Zones.§!
§YWe will gain a unique dynamic modifier that will be strengthened by taking focuses throughout the political tree in order to help revitalise the Chinese Republic.§!
Beiyang of the South
The Beiyang Republic undoubtedly has a complex legacy; under noble intentions revolutionaries sundered an autocratic and collapsing monarchy, instituting a fresh republican national order. And yet the abounding optimism of revolutionaries can hardly compare to the harsh realities of power brokering and establishment politics. Under the affairs of individuals such as Duan Qirui, Yuan Shikai, and Feng Guozhang, the Southern-led revolution became one that was contorted under the ambitions of military strongmen and force of arms. Shattered, broken, and then promptly reunited once again in 1928, the aspirations of the original Xinhai revolutionaries would be but a faint memory in only the oldest soldiers and politicians. But the legacy of the Five Races Under One Union still shines true in some of its most devout descendants and believers, of whom occupy the seat of power within the Chinese nation-state.\n\nAffirming the right of the Southern militarists over that of the North, the new Beiyang Republic has avowed to be an evolution of previous efforts to unite and centralise the Chinese Republican project. Despite national apathy to the new incumbents, the Chinese nation can at least breathe a sigh of relief, knowing that the nation has been reunified, and the time of rebuilding is sure to follow. Whatever form the reconstructed Republic will take, is ultimately up to the new political leaders of the Republic.
§REnshrining regional autonomy within the Republic will result in the warlords taking a natural predisposition against the Central Government.§!

Heir to the legacy of the Guangzhou Government, Marshal Lu Yuguang finds himself at the crossroads of fate. Although his Old Guangxi Clique is master of Guangxi and Guangdong, the adopted son of Lu Rongting finds his regime pressed from all sides. From the East, Federalist and disgruntled Cantonese elements seek to end a decade of Guangxi dominance. From the West, the Kuomintang stands ready to restore Nationalist control over the South. Will the Young Marshal of the South weather the coming storm, or will he find himself consumed by the tides of Revolution and Federalism?
GFX_GXC_intro
§RThe Hotbed of Revolution§!
Long the champion of anti-Northerner influence within the nation, the Pearl River Delta's history in the Warlord Era is marred by revolt, struggle, banditry, and revolution. With a looming anti-Qing struggle occurring within the region since the end of the §YTaiping Heavenly Rebellion§!, the Pearl of the South is naturally home to many of China's present day great revolutionaries. Forming revolutionary groups such as the §lTongmenghui§! and other likeminded revolutionaries, Liangguang (referring to the joint region of §YGuangxi§! and §YGuangdong§!) had its own precursor uprisings before §lXinhai§! such as the §lSecond Guangzhou Uprising§!. These groups finally played their part in the §lXinhai Revolution§! as a republican stronghold. With the seizure of Guangdong by §lChen Jiongming§! and §aHu Hanmin§!, and §YGuangxi§! by §PLu Rongting§!, the region would remain steadfast in the hands of the Republicans. With the declaration of the §YSecond Revolution§!, and its ultimate failure, §aHu Hanmin§! was ousted as Governor, and replaced by §lChen§!, only for him to be ousted similarly and replaced by §PYuan§! Loyalist §PLong Jiguang§!.\n\nWith the onslaught of the §lNational Protection War§!, §YGuangxi§! tepidly announced their support after the §aHuguojun§! saw initial outstanding successes, and marched into §YGuangdong§! to depose the §PYuan§! loyalists. After a brief interim Southern Government hosted by National Protection leaders such as §SLiang Qichao§!, §mLi Genyuan§!, §aCen Chunxuan§!, and others, the §POld Guangxi Clique§! effectively united §YLiangguang§! under their dominion, at least on paper. With the chaos of the Warlord Era now in full swing, §eBeiyang§! infighting would see the proclamation of the §aConstitutional Protection Government§! in 1917, with its capital in §YGuangzhou§!. Led by the legendary §aSun Yat-sen§!, it included the likes of §lChen Jiongming§!, §cXu Chongzhi§!, §aTang Jiyao§!, §PLu Rongting§!, §lTang Shaoyi§!, §aHu Hanmin§!, §cLi Liejun§!, and numerous others. Eventually being hijacked and ousting §aSun§!, the §aConstitutional Protection Government§! attempted peace talks with the North.\n\nHaving been driven into §YFujian§!, §lChen Jiongming§! and §cXu Chongzhi§! initiated the §lFirst Yue-Gui War§! in 1920, successfully ousting the §POld Guangxi clique§!, and instating §lChen§! as governor of §YGuangdong§!. With §aSun§! insisting upon a new extraordinary government that would grant him executive powers, a political schism with §lChen§! formed, and after fighting the §lSecond Yue-Gui War§! in 1921, which sent §YGuangxi§! into practical anarchy, the two clashed over the §YNorthern Expedition§!, Unification by Negotiation or by Subjugation, and Federalism over Centralism. Tensions ultimately culminated in the §YJune 16th Incident§!, with the attempted shelling of §aSun's§! Palace by §aYe Ju§! and the §aOld Yue Clique§!.\n\nEngulfed in consistent conflict, many individuals would clash, and then defect or otherwise. With the rise in influence of warlords such as §PDeng Benyin§!, §mLiu Zhenhuan§!, §aYang Ximin§!, §PShen Hongying§!, §aLin Hu§!, §PHong Zhaolin§!, and numerous others, §YGuangdong§! effectively became a patchwork of influences. With the region inflamed further by the §YMerchant Corps Incident§! of 1924, wherein §YGuangzhou's§! capitalists rose up to protect themselves in fear of §kKMT§! atrocities, the §PWhampoa Training Corps§! and §PAcademy§! were formed that same year, and would soon drive out both §lChen Jiongming§! and §mChen Lianbo's§! forces. No longer willing to endure alliances with warlords in order to see unification of the nation, §aSun Yat-sen§! initiated the Reorganisation of the Kuomintang. Meanwhile, in exile, §lChen Jiongming§! founded the §lPublic Interest Party§! in 1925.
§lParty-State Infighting and the Northern Expedition§!
Politically, the §kKuomintang§! transformed radically. As reformists and Federalists flocked to the various Federalist leaders in the nation, the Party was reorganised in 1924, establishing a democratic centralist form of organisation, an alliance with the §RInternational§!, and a United Front with the §rChinese Syndicalist Party§!. With §aSun§! heading north to engage in peace talks, the Party fractured further due to lack of his leadership in the South. After his death, acting Generalissimo §aHu Hanmin§!, §aSun's§! presumed successor, engaged in a power struggle with §RWang Jingwei§!, §PChiang Kai-shek§!, §RLiao Zhongkai§!, §cZhu Peide§!, and §cXu Chongzhi§!. Simultaneously, §aTang Jiyao§! attempted an invasion of §YLiangguang§!, only to be halted by recent §kKuomintang allies§!, the §PNew Guangxi Clique§!.\n\nWith many rightist party members moving to the §YShanghai§! Headquarters due to increasing §Rleftist§! activity in §YGuangdong§!, §aHu§! was ousted by a §YNational Government Council§!. While he had almost managed to clinch victory from §RWang§! and §PChiang's§! hands, the assassination attempt on §RLiao Zhongkai§! caused a mass purge of rightists within the Party by §aHu's§! enemies. Reinvigorated, the United Front would hold strong, and the schism between the §aOld Right§! and §RLeft§! wings of the party grew larger. Growing disillusioned, the §aOld Right§! held the §cWestern Hills Conference§! in late 1925, and were subsequently expelled by the §kCentral Executive Committee§!, later fleeing to §YShanghai§!. With the political situation somewhat stabilised in favour of the United Front, the §kKuomintang§! launched their §YNorthern Expedition§! from §YLiangguang§! under the stewardship of §RWang§! and §aChiang§! in July 1926.\n\nThe expedition was led with the newly formed §PNational Revolutionary Army§!, consisting of the reorganised and committed allied warlord armies alongside the newly elite §PWhampoa-born 1st Army§!, led by §PChiang§! and §PHe Yingqin§!. Alongside the §P1st§!, was §cTan Yankai's 2nd§!, §cZhu Peide's 3rd§!, §lLi Jishen's 4th§!, §aLi Fulin's 5th§!, §aCheng Qian's 6th§!, §PLi Zongren's 7th§!, and §rTang Shengzhi's 8th§!. Initially successful, reaching §YWuchang§! in September, the §YNorthern Expedition§! was soon crippled by the German Intervention in October, overrunning §YGuangzhou§! by November. While the §k4th Army§! attempted reasonable resistance, the §a5th§! disintegrated, causing §PChiang§! to reorient the §P1st Army§! south in an attempt to relieve the siege. Seen as abandoning the front, this move was incredibly unpopular, and their intense assault resulted in failure.\n\nWith §YGuangzhou§! in German hands, their relatively high casualties resulted in their refusal to move further, instead opting for their §zZhili§! allies to finish the job; appointing §mChen Lianbo§! as Mayor in the interim. §PChiang's§! failure saw his ousting by §RWang§! with the support of §lLi Jishen§!, §lZhang Fakui§!, §RTang Shengzhi§!, §cTan Yankai§! and §cZhu Peide§!. Meeting in private with §PHe Yingqin§! and §PBai Chongxi§!, §PChiang§! resigned and was replaced by §lLi Jishen§!. With the §P1st§! no longer responding to orders and only directly listening to §PChiang§!, §RDeng Yanda§! and the §Rleft-wing§! militias organised a strike in §YGuangzhou§!. In January 1927 the §YBattle of Jinhua§! was fought by the §l4th army§! with assistance from the §c3rd§! and §R8th§!, causing the practical disintegration of the §l4th§! and §R8th§! armies as they retreated to start a guerrilla struggle in §kMinGan§!.\n\nReturning to power due to §lLi's§! failure, §PChiang§! swiftly initiated a political marriage with §aSong Meiling§!, intimidated §PHe Yingqin§! back into cowed alliance, and with fear of the left's influence was backed by §cZhu Peide§! and a (reluctant) §PLi Zongren§!, alongside an incredibly apprehensive §aCheng Qian§!, to the position of Generalissimo. While Chiang ordered the §YLong March§! westward to §YGuangxi§!, §YGuizhou§!, and southwestern §YHunan§!, §RWang Jingwei§! and §RLiao Zhongkai§! would lead the §RCentral Executive Committee§! into exile.\n\nWith §nYuan Zuming's§! defection to §eBeiyang§! and an attempted invasion yet again from §aTang Jiyao§! prior, the §YLong March§! turned disorderly, and following §PChiang's§! assassination in early February, would result in a drove of defections, and the assumption of control of the §PNRA§! by §cZhu Peide§!. Now tepidly based in §YGuizhou§! (due to the §aYunnan Clique's§! own infighting preventing their ousting) and parts of §YGuangxi§!, the §kKuomintang§! was slowly pushed deeper into the former, and the §YNorthern Expedition§! officially came to an end in February 1927 with the Party-State continuing its struggle in §YGuizhou§!, §kMinGan§!, and §YShanghai§!.
§gThe Liangguang Interregnum§!
Leaving chaos in their departure, the advancing §zNorthern§! and §MSouthern Zhili§! armies began to use irregulars and auxiliary formations from local factions to further shore up control as §eFengtian§! initiated the §Y3rd Zhili-Fengtian War§! in February 1927. Instigated by clashes between §PZhang Zongchang§! and §cWu Peifu§!, the §zZhili§! would deftly deal with §PZhang Zuolin§! and the §eFengtian Clique§!. In the meantime, the §POld Guangxi§! and §aOld Guangdong Cliques§!, with members having either fled to §zZhili§! lines or retired, returned to power and influence within their localities. With the §MSouthern Zhili§! rather ambiguously settling on §PShen Hongying§! as Governor of §YGuangdong§!, §mChen Lianbo§! retained his German appointed and §zZhili§! affirmed Mayorship of §YGuangzhou§!, in-part due to negotiations required for the German withdrawal. Neither would control the majority of the province, and through the acquisition of former warlords on §mChen§! and §PShen's§! payrolls, Guangdong further fractured into fiefdoms.\n\nIn §YGuangxi§!, the various disparate clans competed for unification in the void left by §PNew Guangxi's§! retreat, ultimately resulting in §PLu Rongting§! managing to manoeuvre himself back into power, to the surprise of many. With his venerable age and reputation shoring out any issues of control, he was reluctantly recognised as Governor of §YGuangxi§! by the §zNorthern Zhili§!. To the south, in §YQiongya§!, §aDeng Benyin§! returned to power in the island after a concerted effort from §mChen Lianbo§! and the Germans, establishing a tepid alliance between §YQiongya§! and §YGuangzhou§!.\n\nSlowly expanding his sphere of control, §mChen Lianbo§! himself begun hiring former §aGuangdong Army§! (both §kKuomintang§! and Federalist) officers to further bulk up his §mMerchant Corps§!, resulting in the employment of individuals such as §aChen Jitang§!, §lChen Jiongming§! (albeit he refused a military position), §aYe Ju§! and others. In conjunction with this, he hired individuals such as §mLiu Zhenhuan§!, and §aYang Ximin§!. With the conclusion of the §Y4th Zhili-Fengtian War§! and the §YCER Conflict§!, the §zZhili§! announced scheduled elections, and in the meantime §PSun Chuanfang§! formally incorporated Hunan, §YGuangxi§!, and §YGuangdong§! into the §YLeague of Five Provinces§! in 1929, becoming the §YLeague of Eight Provinces§!.\n\nWith the new elections of 1929 resulting in a §aHarmony Association§! landslide, the §lUnited National Construction League§! was formed by democratically minded Chinese intellectuals from the §nYoung China Party§!, §cDemocratic Constitutionalist Party§!, §mPolitical Science Clique§! (recently legalised), §lPublic Interest Party§!, §cPeople's Government Party§!, and plenty others, although the inclusion of the likes of §kKuomintang§! adjacents such as §RZhang Bojun§! and §RTan Pingshan§! drew heavy scrutiny. With §PZhang Zuolin's§! refusal to entertain unification negotiations after the §zZhili§! landslide election, §PSun Chuanfang§! triggered the §YLeague Crisis§! of 1930 as he ambiguously responded to overtures from §PZhang Zuolin§! about restarting the Civil War.\n\nPromoting Federalist rhetoric to protect the §YLeague's§! autonomy, amongst other power plays, his actions would see the §YShanghai Uprising§! erupt in 1932. Resulting in catastrophic reverberations throughout the nation, it saw the fleeing of intellectuals into the interior, and the resulting interior policy from the §YLeague§! itself saw §PShen Hongying's§! backers retreat, crippling his position. Swiftly §PLu Rongting§! coordinated an alliance with §mChen Lianbo§! and §aDeng Benyin§!, pushing §PShen§! out of major positions in §YGuangxi§! and §YGuangdong§!. In return the §zNorthern Zhili§! recognised §PLu§! as §YViceroy of Liangguang§! to check §PSun Chuanfang§!. Similarly §PLu§! signed an under-the-table agreement of mutual defence with §cZhao Hengti§!; should §PSun§! attempt to re-assert himself within the Southwestern provinces.\n\nAs 1934 dawned, §PLu Rongting§! died of natural causes, leaving much of his work to centralise the provinces of §YLiangguang§! uncomplete. His adopted sons, §aLu Yuguang§! (his groomed successor, and the Young Marshal of the South), and §aMa Ji§! took his place. Rearing their §aYoung Guangxi§! faction of the §POld Guangxi Clique§! to the forefront, they form a triumvirate, even quadrumvirate, between §aLu§!, §aMa§!, §aLiu Zhilu§!, and §aLin Hu§!. §PShen§! remains, hardly holding on, whilst §lChen§! continues his nominally \
Political Diaspora in Yue-Gui
§MThe Guangxi and Guangdong Cliques§!\nIn the chaos and anarchy of the §kKuomintang§! collapse many parties and factions, both old and new, have returned or otherwise appeared at the forefront of §YLiangguang§! politics. Chiefly amongst them, the §POld Guangxi Clique§! and its disparate internal factions, the Federalists within the nation, gentry and merchants, and all other manner of individuals. As de-facto the most powerful group within the region, the §POld Guangxi Clique§! remains disunited, split between the §aYoung Guangxi Faction§! and the §PShen Army Faction§!, with minor clans in and amongst their ranks.\n\nThe §aYoung Guangxi Faction§! profess a broad stroke ideological tendency towards old-style Republican Revolutionary politics, with ties to the §mPolitical Science Clique§!, and various other political groups. Ultimately quite pragmatic, they remain focused on extending ther influence within the South of the nation. Their chief rivals are the §MSouthern Zhili§! backed §PShen Army Faction§!, based around the eponymous §PShen Hongying§!. Ailing and elderly, the bandit turned statesman has long been yearning to stake his claim as Governor of §YGuangdong§! even in the diminishing circumstances. With a tepid alliance with the §aJingxing Society§! and other Pro-German Pro-South Zhili interest groups in the region, his disparate band of bandits seek to confirm their power promised to them in 1927.\n\nOperating within the urban hub of §YGuangzhou§! are those keen on wheeling and dealing, with a firm handshake masquerading less than noble intentions. With Mayor §mChen Lianbo§! exercising authority through his §lAmerican-inspired§! §mCanton Merchant Corps§! and extensive business connections within the province, he has managed to hire and invite numerous intellectuals and independents to his flock. Primarily represented through Senator §lChen Jiongming§!, founder and leader of the §lPublic Interest Party§! (founded in 1925), championing Federalism and democracy in the region. On his flank is the §SNational Renaissance Society§! led by Zhang Junmai, formed in 1932, as a subsect of the §cChinese Constitutionalist Party§!. Focused on adapting German Social Democracy to China, it carries a notable socialist tint with members such as §RZhang Dongsun§! championing Guild Socialism.\n\nSimilarly, they are joined by independent members of the political battleground, such as §mLin Zhengxuan§!, Speaker of the Assembly and member of the §mPolitical Science Clique§!. Other individuals associated are the likes of §aLin Hu§!, §mLiu Zhenhuan§!, and §aYang Ximin§!. Similarly the gentry are represented through the local §cGrain Industry Maintenance Association§!, having long been backers and friends of §lChen Jiongming§!, they have renewed their strength to protect their interests. In addition local warlords such as §aYang Ruxuan§! and §aChisheng§!, alongside §aDeng Benyin§! amongst others, scatter to protect their interests. Hardly a democratic cornucopia, §YGuangzhou§! nonetheless remains a Pearl on the River Delta.\n\n§MThe Kuomintang§!\nWhilst the §kKuomintang§! have very little overt influence within the region, they still retain sizeable unnofficial influence. Skirmishing militias in §YGuangxi's§! east, §rPeng Pai's§! rural movement in §YGuangdong's§! peasant slums, and various §kMinGan§! irregulars flowing in and out across the border co-exist with the influence of \

The Comprador President
§L\
Dongjiang River Column
The Dongjiang River Column (sometimes translated The East River Column), is a band of a socialist anti-imperialist guerillas that sought to resist foreign occupation. Organised as a stay behind force, they have been trained in blending in with the local population and sabotaging efforts by the enemy to consolidate their hold on the valuable Southern Chinese trade network. Although their numbers vary, their efforts have been mythologised in official government propaganda.
Qiongya Autonomy
Receiving an influential position within the government, Deng Benyin has been able to almost fully carve off the island of Qiongya as his own fiefdom, near enough to asking for provincial status, Deng has instead opted to maintain parity with the government. As long as he has an ear within the upper chambers of power, he is content to support provincial and national ambitions.
The Five Year Plan
Under the ambitious plans of the Democratic Socialists, the whole Frontier of China and the regions north of the Great Wall have been earmarked for continuous and concerted economic development. To transform vast swathes of desert, mountain, and steppe into economically productive land is no small undertaking, but already the various regions have seen remarkable economic improvement.

Entrenched Yue Dominance

Special Economic Zone
On the road to strengthening the Chinese Republic, the nation can only achieve true independence and assert itself on the international stage if it can achieve a certain level of economic development independent of that of foreign enterprises. As nations abandon laissez-faire economic policies on the gradual move towards socialism, China itself must still maintain zones of capitalist economic development. Under these, it is hoped that their economic prosperity will spread all throughout the nation.

Balancing the Levers of Government
Harmonious balance is a virtue that all Chinese governments must aspire towards, and the current iteration of the Beiyang Republic is no different. Imposed by Old Guangxi aficionados, various necessary alliances and old friends have been brought together under the notion of continued administration, and the pursuit of a national strengthening campaign. The Central Government has become split into three faces: the Political Science Clique, the Jingxing Society, and the various compatriots of the Old Guangxi and Zhili Cliques. Together, they ward off any attempts from outside influences to intercede on their governance.
